records.line: Everything is water in one form or another.
records.reference: Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.

records.line: Everything originated from the ‘apeiron’ (the “infinite” or “indefinite”), rather than from a particular element.
records.reference: Evans, J. (n.d.). “Anaximander”. Encyclopaedia Britannica. https://www.britannica.com/biography/Anaximander

records.line: The inhabited portion of the Earth is the flat top face of a cylinder, hanging in space, equidistant to everything else.
records.reference: Evans, J. (n.d.). “Anaximander”. Encyclopaedia Britannica. https://www.britannica.com/biography/Anaximander

records.line: The first creatures originated from the moist element by evaporation; humans originated from some other kind of animal.
records.reference: Evans, J. (n.d.). “Anaximander”. Encyclopaedia Britannica. https://www.britannica.com/biography/Anaximander

records.line: The world is not eternal and will be destroyed back into the apeiron, from which new worlds will be born.
records.reference: Evans, J. (n.d.). “Anaximander”. Encyclopaedia Britannica. https://www.britannica.com/biography/Anaximander

records.line: Everything comes from air: when it is thinned it becomes fire, when it is condensed it becomes cloud, then water, earth, and stones.
records.reference: Graham, D. W. (n.d.). “Anaximenes”. The Internet Encyclopedia of Philosophy. https://www.iep.utm.edu/anaximen/

records.line: The earth is a flat disc and floats on air.
records.reference: Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.

records.line: Mathematical order pervades the physical world.
records.reference: Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.

records.line: The soul is immortal and it transmigrates into other kinds of animals.
records.reference: Huffman, C. (n.d.). “Pythagoras”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/pythagoras/

records.line: We remember a lot of what we know from a previous life.
records.reference: Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.

records.line: We may get nearer and nearer to the truth but our knowledge is our creation; it is in fact conjecture.
records.reference: Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.

records.line: Each group shapes their gods in the likeness of their own.
records.reference: Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.

records.line: Everything is a coming together of opposites: health and disease, good and evil, hot and cold define each other.
records.reference: Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.

records.line: Everything is in flux: on those stepping into rivers staying the same, other and other waters flow.
records.reference: Graham, D. W. (n.d.). “Heraclitus”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/heraclitus/

records.line: This world-order, the same of all, no god nor human did create, but it ever was and is and will be: everliving fire.
records.reference: Graham, D. W. (n.d.). “Heraclitus”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/heraclitus/

records.line: There can never have been nothing; everything must always have existed.
records.reference: Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.

records.line: It is not possible for anything to pass into nothing; everything is eternal.
records.reference: Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.

records.line: The universe is a single unchanging entity; all is one.
records.reference: Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.

records.line: What appears as change is something that occurs within an enclosed and unchanging system.
records.reference: Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.

records.line: Everything is made up of four elements that are perennial: earth, water, air, and fire.
records.reference: Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.

records.line: Man is the measure of all things.
records.reference: Poster, C. (n.d.). “Protagoras”. The Internet Encyclopedia of Philosophy. https://iep.utm.edu/protagor/

records.line: We have no means of knowing whether gods exist or not or of what sort they may be.
records.reference: Poster, C. (n.d.). “Protagoras”. The Internet Encyclopedia of Philosophy. https://iep.utm.edu/protagor/

records.line: Everything is made up of indivisible particles (atoms) that are too small to be seen.
records.reference: Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.

records.line: All change in the universe consists of atoms altering formations or locations.
records.reference: Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.

records.line: The universe is not a continuum, but consists of separate entities.
records.reference: Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.

records.line: A moving thing must travel half way before arriving at the goal, thus an endless sequence of fractions of the total distance; motion is impossible.
records.reference: Aristotle. Physics.

records.line: The time period of an arrow’s motion is composed of moments and all moments contain an arrow at rest, so the arrow cannot be moving.
records.reference: Aristotle. Physics.

records.line: An impeccably logical argument can lead to a false conclusion.
records.reference: Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.

records.line: Knowledge of the universe makes little practical difference; it does not affect our lives, our behaviour.
records.reference: Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.

records.line: We need to know how to conduct our lives and ourselves.
records.reference: Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.

records.line: Things like ‘justice’ do exist; not materially, but in some sort of essence.
records.reference: Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.

records.line: To a person who preserves their integrity no real, long-term harm can ever come.
records.reference: Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.

records.line: We should pity the perpetrator of injustice, not the victim of injustice.
records.reference: Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.

records.line: No one really knowingly does wrong.
records.reference: Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.

records.line: There can be no cut and dried answers; answers themselves are open to question.
records.reference: Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.

records.line: Pleasure is the end; it is what goal our actions aim at and what is valuable for its own sake.
records.reference: O’Keefe, T. (n.d.). “Aristippus”. The Internet Encyclopedia of Philosophy. https://iep.utm.edu/aristip/

records.line: We should not defer pleasures that are ready at hand for the sake of future pleasures.
records.reference: O’Keefe, T. (n.d.). “Aristippus”. The Internet Encyclopedia of Philosophy. https://iep.utm.edu/aristip/

records.line: Everything is an ephemeral and imperfect copy of a permanent Form (or Idea) outside space and time.
records.reference: Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.

records.line: Underlying our messy world, there is another reality with all the perfection of mathematics.
records.reference: Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.

records.line: The underlying reality is accessible to the mind and the intellect; the body (with inaccurate senses, illnesses, fears, desires, etc.) affords us distractions.
records.reference: Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.; Plato. Phaedo.

records.line: Our souls possess the knowledge of the Forms before our birth; learning after birth is just recollection.
records.reference: Plato. Phaedo.

records.line: In our visible world with space and time, everything is becoming, nothing is.
records.reference: Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.

records.line: Our bodies are ephemeral but our souls are our permanent Forms, timeless and indestructible; death frees us from the bodily distractions.
records.reference: Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.; Plato. Phaedo.

records.line: Our aim in life should be to reject the pleasures of the body and to penetrate to the level of underlying reality.
records.reference: Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.; Plato. Phaedo.

records.line: Knowledge is true belief (judgment, opinion) with an account of the reason why it is true.
records.reference: Plato. Theaetetus.

records.line: The moral Forms set the objective moral standards by which human conduct and character should be judged.
records.reference: Stevenson, L. (1974). Seven Theories of Human Nature. Oxford University Press.

records.line: Is the pious loved by the gods because it is pious, or is it pious because it is loved by the gods?
records.reference: Plato. Euthyphro.

records.line: Human beings are made up of three conflicting elements: passion (desire, appetite), intellect (reason), and will (spirit).
records.reference: Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.

records.line: Virtue is not solely a matter of knowing what is right; intellect should govern passions through the will.
records.reference: Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.

records.line: The only harm that can come to a person is harm to the soul.
records.reference: Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.

records.line: It is better to suffer wrong than to commit it.
records.reference: Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.

records.line: Everything and everybody should be questioned, taking nothing for granted.
records.reference: Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.

records.line: Democracy degenerates into tyranny because most people do not have the intellectual skills to think through difficult political issues.
records.reference: Plato. The Republic.

records.line: A philosophically aware class should govern society through an intermediate police class for the happiness of the whole community.
records.reference: Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.

records.line: Women share by nature in every way of life just as men do, but in all of them women are weaker than men.
records.reference: Plato. The Republic.

records.line: Girls should be brought up on equal terms with boys.
records.reference: Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.

records.line: All artistic creation is a form of imitation (‘mimesis’).
records.reference: Britannica, T. Editors of Encyclopaedia (2011, November 22). “Mimesis”. Encyclopedia Britannica. https://www.britannica.com/art/mimesis

records.line: Art is doubly deceptive, creating illusory semblances of illusory semblances of the Forms; artists have no role in good societies striving for truth.
records.reference: Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.

records.line: A thing is not beautiful because of its shape, color, etc. but because it has a relation with the Form of the beautiful.
records.reference: Plato. Phaedo.

records.line: There is only one world that we can philosophize about – the one we live in.
records.reference: Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.

records.line: If we stray beyond the ground covered by experience we wander into empty talk.
records.reference: Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.

records.line: Mind is potentially whatever is thinkable: thinking is like characters being written on an unscribed tablet.
records.reference: Aristotle. De Anima.

records.line: A thing is whatever it is by virtue of its form and structure – and not just by virtue of the materials it is made of.
records.reference: Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.

records.line: Individuals are members of a kind of substance because they share a property (an essence) with all and only the other members of that kind.
records.reference: Haddock, A., Dupré, J. (2005). “Natural Kinds”. Encyclopedia of Philosophy. https://www.encyclopedia.com/humanities/encyclopedias-almanacs-transcripts-and-maps/natural-kinds

records.line: The material cause of a thing is what it is made of.
records.reference: Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.

records.line: The efficient cause of a thing is what does or makes it.
records.reference: Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.

records.line: The formal cause of a thing is what gives it the shape by which it is identified.
records.reference: Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.

records.line: The final cause of a thing is the ultimate reason for it.
records.reference: Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.

records.line: An object’s form, though not material, is inherent in the this-worldly object.
records.reference: Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.

records.line: The soul is the form of the body: though not material, it is a system of abilities inherent in animate bodies of suitable structure.
records.reference: Lorenz, H. (n.d.). “Ancient Theories of Soul”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/ancient-soul/

records.line: The real point of everything is what it does, what it is for.
records.reference: Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.

records.line: Change occurs when the ongoing material that is part of something acquires a new form.
records.reference: Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.

records.line: There cannot be an infinite series of moved movers; it must come to a halt in some unmoved mover, which is God.
records.reference: Encyclopaedia Britannica. (n.d.). “Aristotle: The unmoved mover”. https://www.britannica.com/biography/Aristotle/The-unmoved-mover

records.line: Spoken words are the symbols of mental experience and written words are the symbols of spoken words.
records.reference: Aristotle. On Interpretation.

records.line: All men have not the same writings or speech sounds, but the mental experiences which these directly symbolize are the same for all.
records.reference: Aristotle. On Interpretation.

records.line: Thoughts are ‘likenessess’ (homoiomata) of things.
records.reference: Aristotle. On Interpretation.

records.line: We may assume the superiority, ceteris paribus, of the demonstration which derives from fewer postulates or hypotheses.
records.reference: Aristotle. Posterior Analytics.

records.line: ‘Eudaimonia’ (happiness, well-being, flourishing), being something final and self-sufficient, is the end at which all actions aim.
records.reference: Aristotle. Nicomachean Ethics.

records.line: In order to achieve happiness and self-fulfilment, we must be members of a society; humans are political animals and personal isolation is no good.
records.reference: Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.

records.line: A good, virtuous person is someone who has ideal character traits; these traits derive from natural internal tendencies, but need to be nurtured.
records.reference: Athanassoulis, N. (n.d.). “Virtue Ethics”. The Internet Encyclopedia of Philosophy. https://iep.utm.edu/virtue/

records.line: A virtue (e.g. courage) is the midway point between two extremes (e.g. cowardice and foolhardiness); our aim should be balance.
records.reference: Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.

records.line: The purpose of government is to enable its citizens to live the full and happy life.
records.reference: Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.

records.line: People with no autonomous rationality – their work is the use of the body, and this is the best that can come from them – are slaves by nature.
records.reference: Aristotle. Politics.

records.line: The male is by nature superior and the female inferior, the male ruler and the female subject.
records.reference: Aristotle. Politics.

records.line: Poetry is more philosophical and more serious than history: poetry speaks more of generalities, while history speaks of particulars.
records.reference: Aristotle. Poetics.

records.line: Tragedy is sophisticated and powerful imitation.
records.reference: Shields, C. (n.d.). “Aristotle”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/aristotle/

records.line: Tragedy is done by performers and not by way of narrative, bringing about through pity and fear the purification (‘katharsis’) of such emotions.
records.reference: Aristotle. Poetics.

records.line: Like a plant, a good work of art is an intricate arrangement of parts, all of which are necessary and contribute to the whole.
records.reference: Eaton, M. M. (1988). Basic Issues In Aesthetics. Waveland Press.

records.line: Starting with one grain, and adding grain after grain, no single grain can make the difference between a number of grains and a heap.
records.reference: Priest, G. (2017). “Eubulides and his paradoxes”. https://blog.oup.com/2017/08/eubulides-paradoxes-philosophy/

records.line: Someone says “What I am now saying is false”; if it is true, it is false; and if it is false, it is true.
records.reference: Priest, G. (2017). “Eubulides and his paradoxes”. https://blog.oup.com/2017/08/eubulides-paradoxes-philosophy/

records.line: A hooded man enters the room and you do not know who he is, but he is in fact your brother; so you do not know who your brother is.
records.reference: Priest, G. (2017). “Eubulides and his paradoxes”. https://blog.oup.com/2017/08/eubulides-paradoxes-philosophy/

records.line: The only thing that matters is the difference between true values and false values.
records.reference: Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.

records.line: All social conventions (public/private, naked/clothed, etc.) are nonsense.
records.reference: Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.

records.line: I am a citizen of the world.
records.reference: Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.

records.line: People over the world believe in opposite things; knowledge is subjective.
records.reference: Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.

records.line: We cannot assume the truth of one explanation rather than any other.
records.reference: Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.

records.line: We should just go with the flow – the customs of wherever and whenever we happen to live.
records.reference: Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.

records.line: All there is in the universe are atoms and space.
records.reference: Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.

records.line: Atoms are indestructible and eternal, but their combinations (objects) are always changing.
records.reference: Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.

records.line: We, body and mind, are among the ephemeral objects built of atoms.
records.reference: Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.

records.line: What enables us to be free is the existence of a certain randomness (swerve) in the motions of our atoms.
records.reference: Konstan, D. (n.d.). “Epicurus”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/epicurus/

records.line: Don’t fear death; so long as you exist, death is not, and when death is, you are not.
records.reference: Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.

records.line: Gods are far away and have no desire to be involved with us; it’s as if they don’t exist.
records.reference: Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.

records.line: Happiness in this world should be our aim.
records.reference: Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.

records.line: The way to be happy is to withdraw from public life into private communities of like-minded people.
records.reference: Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.

records.line: We should enjoy pleasures in moderation, though no non-injurious activity needs to be forbidden.
records.reference: Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.

records.line: The greatest pleasure is attainable through knowledge, friendship, and with freedom from fear.
records.reference: Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.

records.line: Justice was never an entity in itself; it is a kind of agreement not to harm or be harmed, made between people in communities.
records.reference: Epicurus. Leading Doctrines, in The Art of Happiness. Trans. George K. Strodach. Penguin Books. 2012.

records.line: The substance of God is the whole world and the heaven.
records.reference: Laërtius, D. (3rd century BCE). Lives of Eminent Philosophers.

records.line: That which reasons is superior to that which does not; nothing is superior to the world, therefore the world reasons.
records.reference: Cicero. (45 BCE). Tusculan Disputations.

records.line: The nature of the universe is such that it accomplishes what is right and prevents the opposite.
records.reference: Cicero. (45 BCE). On the Nature of the Gods.

records.line: Law of nature is divine, and it has the power to force us to what is right, and to restrain us from what is wrong.
records.reference: Cicero. (45 BCE). On the Nature of the Gods.

records.line: Individual souls are part of the same fire as the world-soul of the universe.
records.reference: Cicero. (45 BCE). Tusculan Disputations.; Laërtius, D. (3rd century BCE). Lives of Eminent Philosophers.

records.line: A virtuous life is life in agreement with nature.
records.reference: Laërtius, D. (3rd century BCE). Lives of Eminent Philosophers.

records.line: A bad feeling (desire, fear, pleasure, sorrow) is a disturbance of the mind repugnant to reason, and against Nature.
records.reference: Cicero. (45 BCE). Tusculan Disputations.; Laërtius, D. (3rd century BCE). Lives of Eminent Philosophers.

records.line: Every proof has premises which it does not itself establish; no ultimate ground of certainty exists.
records.reference: Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.

records.line: God is immanent in the natural order; he is fully accessible to rational human comprehension in the same way as all objects and events are.
records.reference: Graver, M. (n.d.). “Epictetus”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/epictetus/

records.line: Our rational minds are fragments of God’s mind; our rational nature qualifies us as his kindred.
records.reference: Graver, M. (n.d.). “Epictetus”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/epictetus/

records.line: It is the free volition that is the true self of the individual; our bodies, possessions, successes or failures are contingent facts about us.
records.reference: Graver, M. (n.d.). “Epictetus”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/epictetus/

records.line: Even one’s own death is of no particular concern if that is what the orderly workings of the universe require.
records.reference: Graver, M. (n.d.). “Epictetus”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/epictetus/

records.line: Action is determined by character (what seems right to an individual) and not by spontaneous impulses.
records.reference: Graver, M. (n.d.). “Epictetus”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/epictetus/

records.line: Autonomy is guaranteed not by the absence of antecedent causes but by the very nature of the reasoning faculty.
records.reference: Graver, M. (n.d.). “Epictetus”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/epictetus/

records.line: Our feelings are conditioned by our judgments of value; if we correct our judgments, our feelings will be corrected as well.
records.reference: Graver, M. (n.d.). “Epictetus”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/epictetus/

records.line: The choices made by others are of ethical significance only for the agents themselves; to anyone else they are externals and so of no consequence.
records.reference: Graver, M. (n.d.). “Epictetus”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/epictetus/

records.line: One should not be angry at someone for their bad decision; the proper response would be to help them to see their mistake.
records.reference: Graver, M. (n.d.). “Epictetus”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/epictetus/

records.line: We are naturally oriented toward our own well-being, but acting for our own sake often entails contributing to the common good.
records.reference: Graver, M. (n.d.). “Epictetus”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/epictetus/

records.line: God is one, pervading all things; all being is one, all law is one (the common reason which all thinking creatures possess) and all truth is one.
records.reference: Aurelius, M. (161–180). Meditations. Translated by M. Staniforth. Penguin. 1964.

records.line: What keeps the whole world in being is Change; Universal Nature’s task is to shuffle, transpose, interchange, remove from one state and transfer to another.
records.reference: Aurelius, M. (161–180). Meditations. Translated by M. Staniforth. Penguin. 1964.

records.line: Whatever happens, happens rightly; in the succession of events there is not mere sequence alone, but a rational order that is just and right.
records.reference: Aurelius, M. (161–180). Meditations. Translated by M. Staniforth. Penguin. 1964.

records.line: Pain or pleasure, death or life, riches or poverty cannot be good or evil since they are all bestowed by Nature on the virtuous and the sinful alike.
records.reference: Aurelius, M. (161–180). Meditations. Translated by M. Staniforth. Penguin. 1964.

records.line: Life is but a moment, body a prey of worms, soul an unquiet eddy, our fortune dark; the Reason, the divine spirit within us is our only guide.
records.reference: Aurelius, M. (161–180). Meditations. Translated by M. Staniforth. Penguin. 1964.

records.line: Grief, anger, or fear are all rejections of something which has been decreed by the power that directs the universe – in other words, by Law.
records.reference: Aurelius, M. (161–180). Meditations. Translated by M. Staniforth. Penguin. 1964.

records.line: Emotions must not affect reason; if they spread to the mind, reason must refrain from adding its own assumptions of their goodness or badness.
records.reference: Aurelius, M. (161–180). Meditations. Translated by M. Staniforth. Penguin. 1964.

records.line: If you are distressed by anything external, it is not due to the thing itself but to your own estimate of it; and you have the power to revoke it.
records.reference: Aurelius, M. (161–180). Meditations. Translated by M. Staniforth. Penguin. 1964.

records.line: All thoughts of blame are out of place: Gods cannot do wrong, people’s misdoings are unintentional, and chance cannot be reprimanded.
records.reference: Aurelius, M. (161–180). Meditations. Translated by M. Staniforth. Penguin. 1964.

records.line: The wickedness of one person cannot injure another; it harms none but the culprit themself.
records.reference: Aurelius, M. (161–180). Meditations. Translated by M. Staniforth. Penguin. 1964.

records.line: A good person welcomes every experience fate may weave for them, keeps the inner divinity in serenity and decorous obedience to God.
records.reference: Aurelius, M. (161–180). Meditations. Translated by M. Staniforth. Penguin. 1964.

records.line: We must wait with a good grace for death; it is no more than a simple dissolving of the elements whereof each living thing is composed.
records.reference: Aurelius, M. (161–180). Meditations. Translated by M. Staniforth. Penguin. 1964.

records.line: Death is a release from impressions of sense, from twitchings of appetite, from excursions of thought, and from service to the flesh.
records.reference: Aurelius, M. (161–180). Meditations. Translated by M. Staniforth. Penguin. 1964.

records.line: Injustice is a sin; nature has constituted rational beings for their own mutual benefit, each to help his fellows according to their worth.
records.reference: Aurelius, M. (161–180). Meditations. Translated by M. Staniforth. Penguin. 1964.

records.line: Our kinship is not of blood or human seed, but of a common intelligence in every person, which is an emanation from the deity.
records.reference: Aurelius, M. (161–180). Meditations. Translated by M. Staniforth. Penguin. 1964.

records.line: The good life is not to be found in wealth, celebrity, or worldly pleasures; it lies in truthfulness, justice, self-discipline, courage, and independence.
records.reference: Aurelius, M. (161–180). Meditations. Translated by M. Staniforth. Penguin. 1964.

records.line: Uniformity of opinion exists only upon things affecting the welfare of society; our consistent aim must be the benefit of our fellows and the community.
records.reference: Aurelius, M. (161–180). Meditations. Translated by M. Staniforth. Penguin. 1964.

records.line: What exists is ultimately mental; for something to be created is for it to be thought.
records.reference: Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.

records.line: There are three ascending levels of being: soul (humans), intellect, and good.
records.reference: Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.

records.line: We should be in an attempted ascent towards one-ness with the good.
records.reference: Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.

records.line: The arts do not simply imitate things, but they run back up to the forming principles from which nature derives; they also make up what is defective in things.
records.reference: Plotinus, Ennead 5.8.1‐8, A.H. Armstrong translation.

records.line: The flow of time exists for living creatures but not for God; it is created by God.
records.reference: Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.; Augustine. (397-400). Confessions.

records.line: The flow of time does not exist independently of experience.
records.reference: Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.

records.line: The future does not yet exist and the past no longer exists; the present is the inescapable mode of all existence.
records.reference: Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.; Augustine. (397-400). Confessions.

records.line: Our whole worldly being, including our intellect, is dominated by our will.
records.reference: Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.

records.line: Even if I am deceived, it is beyond doubt that I am not deceived in that I know myself to be: if I am deceived, I am.
records.reference: Augustine. (426). The City of God.

records.line: There may be other things than our existence that we can know with absolute certainty.
records.reference: Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.

records.line: Our soul is non-material and timeless, while our bodies are decaying material objects.
records.reference: Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.

records.line: True knowledge is of a realm of timeless and perfect non-material entities.
records.reference: Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.

records.line: Objects of the material world are ephemeral and decaying, and our knowledge about them are fleeting illusions.
records.reference: Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.

records.line: God did not create evil, for evil has no positive nature; the loss of good has received the name “evil”.
records.reference: Augustine. (392). Acta Seu Disputatio Contra Fortunatum Manichaeum.; Augustine. (426). The City of God.
records.keywords: privation theory of evil, problem of evil

records.line: A creature that sins through free will is more excellent than one that does not sin because it does not have free will.
records.reference: Augustine. (388–395). On the Free Choice of the Will, on Grace and Free Choice, and Other Writings. King, Peter (ed.) Cambridge University Press. 2010.

records.line: Evils exist by the voluntary sin of the soul to which God gave free choice; if one does not sin by will, one does not sin.
records.reference: Augustine. (392). Acta Seu Disputatio Contra Fortunatum Manichaeum.

records.line: People inherit a sinful nature: we do not always sin, as Adam did, by our own personal will, but contract original sin from him.
records.reference: Augustine. (411–2). Punishment and Forgiveness of Sins.

records.line: We cannot be saved through our own will alone, God’s intervention and grace are necessary; souls who go to hell are damned by God’s choice.
records.reference: Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.

records.line: Each person is a citizen of two different communities simultaneously: the eternal kingdom of God and the unstable kingdoms of this world.
records.reference: Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.

records.line: An unjust law is not actually a law.
records.reference: Augustine. (388–395). On the Free Choice of the Will, on Grace and Free Choice, and Other Writings. King, Peter (ed.) Cambridge University Press. 2010.

records.line: Philosophers have wished, with amazing folly, to be happy here on Earth; true happiness is only available in Heaven after we die.
records.reference: Augustine. (426). The City of God.

records.line: Reason and revelation are both valid ways of arriving at the same truth.
records.reference: Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.

records.line: Since God is unknowable, it is impossible for Him to know himself.
records.reference: Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.

records.line: The assumption that the greatest being possible exists only in the understanding and not in reality is contradictory because existence is greater than absence.
records.reference: St. Anselm. (1078). Proslogium.

records.line: If the greatest being possible (God) exists in the understanding, it must also exist in reality.
records.reference: St. Anselm. (1078). Proslogium.

records.line: Truth consists in rectitude: a thing (a sentence, a will) is correct whenever it is or does whatever it ought, or was designed, to be or do.
records.reference: Williams, T. (n.d.). “Anselm of Canterbury”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/anselm/

records.line: A statement is correct (has rectitude) when, and only when, it signifies that what-is is.
records.reference: Williams, T. (n.d.). “Anselm of Canterbury”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/anselm/

records.line: Freedom of choice is the power to preserve rectitude of will for its own sake, by self-initiated action.
records.reference: Williams, T. (n.d.). “Anselm of Canterbury”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/anselm/

records.line: That an agent wills what is right because it is right entails that they are neither compelled nor bribed to perform the act.
records.reference: Williams, T. (n.d.). “Anselm of Canterbury”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/anselm/

records.line: Freedom of choice neither is nor entails the power to sin; God and the good angels have freedom of choice, but they are incapable of sinning.
records.reference: Williams, T. (n.d.). “Anselm of Canterbury”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/anselm/

records.line: Human beings abandoned the will for justice and became unable to exercise their freedom; apart from divine grace, they cannot help but sin.
records.reference: Williams, T. (n.d.). “Anselm of Canterbury”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/anselm/

records.line: Reason and faith are very distinct; we believe in answers that are undecidable by reason.
records.reference: Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.

records.line: All our rational knowledge is acquired through sensory experience.
records.reference: Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.

records.line: A baby’s mind is like a clean slate (‘tabula rasa’) with nothing written on it.
records.reference: Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.

records.line: The ‘essence’ of a thing is its description, and it is a separate matter from its existence.
records.reference: Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.

records.line: The world’s essence must have preceded its existence, in God’s mind.
records.reference: Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.

records.line: God’s own essence cannot have preceded his existence; God must be pure existence.
records.reference: Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.

records.line: The universe needs a first mover, a first efficient cause, a necessary being, a perfect being, and an intelligence guiding natural bodies; those are God.
records.reference: Stefon, M. (n.d.). “The Five Ways”. Encyclopedia Britannica. https://www.britannica.com/topic/the-Five-Ways
records.keywords: Five Ways, cosmological argument, teleological argument

records.line: To know in a general and confused way that God exists is implanted in us by nature.
records.reference: Thomas Aquinas. (1265-1273). Summa theologiae.

records.line: We cannot know what God is in this life, but we can, by natural reason, come to know what God is not (not changeable, not in time, etc.).
records.reference: Thomas Aquinas. (1265-1273). Summa theologiae.

records.line: The soul is known by its acts; it realizes it exists through the fact that it understands and perceives.
records.reference: Thomas Aquinas. (1256–1259). De Veritate.

records.line: A judgment is said to be true when it conforms to the external reality.
records.reference: David, M. (n.d.). “The Correspondence Theory of Truth”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/truth-correspondence/

records.line: Sensation attains to things existing externally outside of the mind; the sense objects (the visible, the audible, etc.) exist outside the soul.
records.reference: Thomas Aquinas. Commentary on Aristotle’s de Anima. #375

records.line: Thoughts can be about something (exhibit intentionality) because the form of the object thought about is in the mind of the thinker.
records.reference: Lagerlund, H. (n.d.). “Mental Representation in Medieval Philosophy”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/representation-medieval/

records.line: God cannot command cruelty because he is omnipotent: to sin is to fall short of a perfect action, which is repugnant to omnipotence.
records.reference: Austin, M. W. (n.d.). “Divine command theory”. The Internet Encyclopedia of Philosophy. https://iep.utm.edu/divine-c/

records.line: Evil has no formal or final cause, it is a privation of them; it has a cause by way of an agent, not directly, but accidentally.
records.reference: Thomas Aquinas. (1265-1273). Summa theologiae.

records.line: The defect in one thing yields to the good of another, or even to the universal good; if all evil were prevented, much good would be absent from the universe.
records.reference: Thomas Aquinas. (1265-1273). Summa theologiae.

records.line: True well-being is otherworldly, but we can also achieve an imperfect spiritual well-being on Earth by living a morally good life.
records.reference: Michalos, A. C., & Weijers, D. (2017). “Western Historical Traditions of Wellbeing”. The Pursuit of Human Well-Being. Springer.

records.line: The rational creature has a share of the eternal reason, whereby it has a natural inclination to its proper act and end (natural law).
records.reference: Thomas Aquinas. (1265-1273). Summa theologiae.

records.line: If a human law deflects from the natural law, it is no longer a law but a perversion of law.
records.reference: Thomas Aquinas. (1265-1273). Summa theologiae.

records.line: The best form of government is a mixed government: part kingship, part aristocracy, and part democracy.
records.reference: Brown, C. M. (n.d.). “Thomas Aquinas”. The Internet Encyclopedia of Philosophy. https://www.iep.utm.edu/aquinas/

records.line: The more hypotheses you accept, the more you increase your risk of being wrong; plurality must never be posited without necessity.
records.reference: Kaye, S. (n.d.). “William of Ockham”. The Internet Encyclopedia of Philosophy. (n.d.). https://iep.utm.edu/ockham/

records.line: We are born blank slates: there are no innate certainties to be discovered in our minds, all knowledge comes from experience.
records.reference: Kaye, S. (n.d.). “William of Ockham”. The Internet Encyclopedia of Philosophy. (n.d.). https://iep.utm.edu/ockham/

records.line: The human mind comes fully equipped with a system for processing perceptions as they are acquired: an unspoken, mental language.
records.reference: Kaye, S. (n.d.). “William of Ockham”. The Internet Encyclopedia of Philosophy. (n.d.). https://iep.utm.edu/ockham/

records.line: We acquire knowledge in four quick steps: sensory cognition, intuitive cognition, recordative cognition, and abstractive cognition.
records.reference: Kaye, S. (n.d.). “William of Ockham”. The Internet Encyclopedia of Philosophy. (n.d.). https://iep.utm.edu/ockham/

records.line: In intuitive cognition, objects in the world cause us to be aware of their existence, and this explains and justifies our belief in them.
records.reference: Kaye, S. (n.d.). “William of Ockham”. The Internet Encyclopedia of Philosophy. (n.d.). https://iep.utm.edu/ockham/
records.keywords: direct realist empiricism, causal theory of reference

records.line: Concepts are not mental objects (“ficta”) resembling objects in the world like pictures, but mental actions caused by the objects.
records.reference: Kaye, S. (n.d.). “William of Ockham”. The Internet Encyclopedia of Philosophy. (n.d.). https://iep.utm.edu/ockham/

records.line: There are no universals existing in individual substances or in the essences of things; being one thing and many things at the same time is contradictory.
records.reference: Kaye, S. (n.d.). “William of Ockham”. The Internet Encyclopedia of Philosophy. (n.d.). https://iep.utm.edu/ockham/

records.line: Universals are concepts caused in our minds (via recordative and abstractive cognition) when we perceive similar individuals.
records.reference: Kaye, S. (n.d.). “William of Ockham”. The Internet Encyclopedia of Philosophy. (n.d.). https://iep.utm.edu/ockham/

records.line: Since we have no experience of God in our lives on earth, we have no knowledge of God; belief in God is a matter of faith alone.
records.reference: Kaye, S. (n.d.). “William of Ockham”. The Internet Encyclopedia of Philosophy. (n.d.). https://iep.utm.edu/ockham/

records.line: The Trinity is a contradiction and the proofs of God’s existence inconclusive; theology is not logical and must never be mixed with philosophy.
records.reference: Kaye, S. (n.d.). “William of Ockham”. The Internet Encyclopedia of Philosophy. (n.d.). https://iep.utm.edu/ockham/

records.line: There are no constraints, external or internal, to what God can will: God’s free will establishes right and wrong.
records.reference: Kaye, S. (n.d.). “William of Ockham”. The Internet Encyclopedia of Philosophy. (n.d.). https://iep.utm.edu/ockham/
records.keywords: divine command theory, Euthyphro dilemma, moral realism

records.line: The pope does not have power to punish secular wrongs; he and the emperor should be separate but equal, each supreme in his own domain.
records.reference: Kaye, S. (n.d.). “William of Ockham”. The Internet Encyclopedia of Philosophy. (n.d.). https://iep.utm.edu/ockham/

records.line: Purely philosophical (non-theological) assertions should not be condemned or forbidden; anyone ought to be free to say freely what pleases them.
records.reference: William of Ockham. (c. 1334–46). Dialogus. J. Kilcullen and J. Scott, et al. ed. & trans. British Academy. http://publications.thebritishacademy.ac.uk/pubs/dialogus/ockdial.html

records.line: People in general are ungrateful, fickle, hypocritical, cowardly, and greedy.
records.reference: Machiavelli, N. (1513). The Prince.

records.line: Rulers will perish if they are always good; they must be cunning as a fox and as fierce as a lion.
records.reference: Machiavelli, N. (1513). The Prince.

records.line: Rulers should appear to be merciful, faithful, humane, upright, and most importantly, religious.
records.reference: Machiavelli, N. (1513). The Prince.

records.line: Rulers should not mind being considered cruel, as long as their actions restore order, peace, and loyalty in the end.
records.reference: Machiavelli, N. (1513). The Prince.

records.line: People are so simple and so much dominated by their immediate needs, that a deceitful person will always find plenty who are ready to be deceived.
records.reference: Machiavelli, N. (1513). The Prince.

records.line: The scientific method consists of data collection, hypothesis generation, experiment confirmation, and prediction.
records.reference: Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.

records.line: Negative instances are as important as positive ones in guiding us to the right conclusions.
records.reference: Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.

records.line: Our senses, feelings, and ideologies can be obstacles in our search for reliable knowledge.
records.reference: Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.

records.line: Words are but the images of matter, to fall in love with them is to fall in love with a picture.
records.reference: Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.

records.line: The universe consists of matter in motion, and nothing else exists; it is a vast machine.
records.reference: Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.

records.line: Every moving object, including human beings, is some sort of machine.
records.reference: Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.

records.line: Objects, people, and societies continuously cast and acquire parts but they stay the same over time because of the unbroken nature of the flux.
records.reference: Hobbes, T. (1655). De Corpore.

records.line: All mental processes consist of movements of matter inside the skull.
records.reference: Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.

records.line: Psychological motivations are sorts of pushes: drives and repulsions, appetites and aversions.
records.reference: Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.

records.line: Each person acts for some good to themselves, and appetites define what is good for each of us.
records.reference: Hobbes, T. (1651). Leviathan.

records.line: We are free agents if we are able to do as we will; liberty is the absence of external impediments.
records.reference: Hobbes, T. (1656). Of Liberty and Necessity.

records.line: Appetites (liking, love, etc.) are inherently unsatisfiable; they cannot cease until death.
records.reference: Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.

records.line: The strongest aversion is the fear of death.
records.reference: Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.

records.line: At bottom, the reason why people form societies is their fear of death.
records.reference: Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.

records.line: In the state of nature there is no order or justice; violence and cunning rules – life is solitary, poor, nasty, brutish and short.
records.reference: Hobbes, T. (1651). Leviathan.

records.line: In the state of nature, every person has the natural right to everything, especially to anything necessary for the preservation of one’s own life.
records.reference: Hobbes, T. (1651). Leviathan.

records.line: Societal alliances should be such that it is not in anyone’s interest to break laws.
records.reference: Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.

records.line: Everyone should hand over power to a central authority with absolute power who imposes law and punishes law-breakers, for the good of all.
records.reference: Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.

records.line: The central authority holds its power not from God or from any higher authority, but from the people, to protect their freedom and safety.
records.reference: Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.

records.line: Any tyranny is better than social chaos.
records.reference: Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.

records.line: One should be contented with so much liberty against other people, as one would allow others against oneself.
records.reference: Hobbes, T. (1651). Leviathan.

records.line: In mathematics we start from indubitable premises and deduce irrefutable conclusions.
records.reference: Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.

records.line: To gain reliable knowledge, we should start from indubitable premises as in mathematics.
records.reference: Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.

records.line: Sensual observations cannot be trusted, as they deceive us frequently.
records.reference: Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.

records.line: I cannot be absolutely sure at any moment that I am not being deceived by an evil demon controlling my senses.
records.reference: Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.

records.line: The only thing I can be sure of is that I am having the experiences that I am having.
records.reference: Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.

records.line: I may not know my own nature or the nature of things, but that I exist is indubitable, because I am having experiences.
records.reference: Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.

records.line: I can think of a perfect being even though I am imperfect; that being must be the cause of this idea within me, therefore it (God) exists.
records.reference: Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.

records.line: If I think carefully, I can be certain of my conclusions, because God will not deceive me.
records.reference: Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.

records.line: There are two kinds of substance, interacting with each other: mind (which I am as a conscious being; ‘res cogitans’) and matter (‘res extensa’).
records.reference: Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.

records.line: Unlike the body, the mind is indivisible; it is a unified and integral thing, and willing, sensing, understanding, etc. are not parts of it.
records.reference: Descartes, R. (1639). Meditations on First Philosophy.

records.line: Some of our ‘ideas’ (e.g. God) are innate, some adventitious (sensations coming from external objects), and others inventions (e.g. hippogriffs).
records.reference: Descartes, R. (1639). Meditations on First Philosophy.

records.line: Sense perception, imagination, and corporeal memory require the brain and the body but pure thinking arises from innate ideas in the intellect acting alone.
records.reference: Hatfield, G. (n.d.). “René Descartes”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/sense-data/

records.line: The sensations (‘ideas’) of light, hearing, tickling, pain, etc. formed in our minds bear no resemblance to the objects causing them.
records.reference: Descartes, R. (1629–1633). The World.

records.line: Animals can act ‘in accordance with’ rules of reason, but they do not really act ‘for’ these reasons; they cannot apply these principles to novel circumstances.
records.reference: Lurz, R. (n.d.). “Animal minds”. The Internet Encyclopedia of Philosophy. https://www.iep.utm.edu/ani-mind/

records.line: The best explanation for the absence of speech in animals is the absence of thought; animals are nothing but mindless machines or automata.
records.reference: Lurz, R. (n.d.). “Animal minds”. The Internet Encyclopedia of Philosophy. https://www.iep.utm.edu/ani-mind/

records.line: No matter how small the probability that God exists, as long as it is non-zero, the expected utility of theistic belief will dominate the expected utility of disbelief.
records.reference: Jordan, J. (n.d.). “Pragmatic Arguments and Belief in God”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/pragmatic-belief-god/

records.line: No one without faith has ever reached happiness; the infinite abyss in us can be filled only with an infinite and immutable object, by God.
records.reference: Pascal, B. (1669). Pensées. AJ Krailsheimer, trans. New York: Penguin Books. 1995.

records.line: It is the heart which experiences God, and not the reason.
records.reference: Pascal, B. (1669). Pensées. AJ Krailsheimer, trans. New York: Penguin Books. 1995.

records.line: To understand the world we should apply the methods of mathematics to reality.
records.reference: Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.

records.line: If God is infinite, he cannot have boundaries; everything is part of God, all is one.
records.reference: Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.

records.line: From a given determinate cause, the effect follows necessarily; and conversely, if there is no determinate cause, it is impossible for an effect to follow.
records.reference: Spinoza, B. (1677). Ethics.

records.line: The totality of everything is the only thing that needs nothing outside itself in order to exist; it is the only true substance, it is God.
records.reference: Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.

records.line: Many imagine God as a ruler, lawgiver, king, merciful, just etc. whereas these are attributes of human nature, not applicable to the divine nature.
records.reference: Spinoza, B. (1670). Tractatus Theologico-Politicus.

records.line: Religious and scientific conceptions are two ways of describing the same reality.
records.reference: Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.

records.line: Mind and matter are one and the same thing expressed in two ways, through different attributes.
records.reference: Spinoza, B. (1677). Ethics.

records.line: Each thing strives to persevere in its being; nothing can be destroyed without the action of external causes.
records.reference: Spinoza, B. (1677). Ethics.

records.line: All things are alive and have minds, in different degrees.
records.reference: Spinoza, B. (1677). Ethics.

records.line: As God, or Nature, exists for the sake of no end, it also acts for the sake of no end.
records.reference: Spinoza, B. (1677). Ethics.

records.line: Concepts like good, evil, order, confusion, warm, cold, beauty, ugliness do not indicate the nature of anything; they are products of the imagination.
records.reference: Spinoza, B. (1677). Ethics.

records.line: We do not desire anything because we judge it to be good; we judge something to be good because we desire it.
records.reference: Spinoza, B. (1677). Ethics.

records.line: Reason demands that everyone love themself, seek their own advantage and should strive to preserve their own being as far as they can.
records.reference: Spinoza, B. (1677). Ethics.

records.line: There is no free will; the mind is determined to wish this or that by a cause, which has also been determined by another cause, ad infinitum.
records.reference: Spinoza, B. (1677). Ethics.

records.line: Understanding that we are not free can liberate us, enabling us to come to terms with things as they are.
records.reference: Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.

records.line: We should look at our lives through the eyes of eternity; our personal problems are insignificant in the totality of things.
records.reference: Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.

records.line: Our desire to understand Nature (God) is the very essence of our minds, and this drive to understand also characterizes the good for us.
records.reference: Grey, J. (n.d.). “Benedict De Spinoza: Moral Philosophy”. The Internet Encyclopedia of Philosophy. http://iep.utm.edu/spin-mor/

records.line: Although virtue is founded in rational self-interest, rational self-interest in turn urges us to desire the good of others.
records.reference: Grey, J. (n.d.). “Benedict De Spinoza: Moral Philosophy”. The Internet Encyclopedia of Philosophy. http://iep.utm.edu/spin-mor/

records.line: The true aim of government is liberty, by enabling people to develop their minds and bodies in security.
records.reference: Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.

records.line: Freedom of speech is necessary to secure the public order but seditious ideas are not to be tolerated by the sovereign.
records.reference: Spinoza, B. (1677). Ethics.; Spinoza, B. (1670). Tractatus Theologico-Politicus.

records.line: Everyone is bound to submit to the state; reason bids us carry out even irrational orders because the alternative (living without a state) is worse.
records.reference: Spinoza, B. (1677). Ethics.; Spinoza, B. (1670). Tractatus Theologico-Politicus.

records.line: The law against killing animals is based on superstition; reason dictates that we consider our own advantage and use them at our pleasure.
records.reference: Spinoza, B. (1677). Ethics.

records.line: The data from which we start to think should come through our senses, from the outside reality.
records.reference: Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.

records.line: When we are born the mind is like a blank slate on which experience begins to write.
records.reference: Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.

records.line: We should analyze our mental faculties and find out their limits; we cannot know everything.
records.reference: Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.

records.line: What we have direct experience of are the contents of our own consciousness (‘ideas’): sensory images, thoughts, feelings, memories, etc.
records.reference: Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.

records.line: We can only know the classificatory properties (‘nominal essence’) of a thing, not the material thing in itself (‘real essence’).
records.reference: Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.

records.line: Our ideas of “primary qualities” (length, weight, velocity, etc.) resemble those qualities and science can deal with these objective qualities.
records.reference: Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.

records.line: Our ideas of “secondary qualities” (taste, smell, colour, etc.) bear no resemblance to those qualities which are out of the reach of science.
records.reference: Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.

records.line: We passively acquire simple ideas from perception and sensation, and then actively combine them to generate complex ideas.
records.reference: Locke, J. (1689). An Essay Concerning Human Understanding.

records.line: I can only know the contents of my awareness, not the thing (self) that is having those experiences.
records.reference: Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.

records.line: Possible knowledge consists entirely of transactions between the mysterious subject and object.
records.reference: Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.

records.line: A person is a thinking intelligent being, that has reason and reflection, and can consider itself as itself in different times and places.
records.reference: Locke, J. (1689). An Essay Concerning Human Understanding.

records.line: Personal identity is a matter of psychological continuity (in terms of memory).
records.reference: Locke, J. (1689). An Essay Concerning Human Understanding.

records.line: Because knowledge/science starts from observation, there is always room for error.
records.reference: Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.

records.line: We must always be willing to change our beliefs in the light of changing evidence.
records.reference: Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.

records.line: It is mistaken and morally wrong for political and religious authorities to impose beliefs.
records.reference: Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.

records.line: Incogitative matter could never produce thought; there must be an eternal cogitative being, God, giving other knowing beings their powers.
records.reference: Locke, J. (1689). An Essay Concerning Human Understanding.

records.line: As made by God in His own image, human beings were not jungle beasts in the state of nature; God had given them reason and conscience.
records.reference: Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.

records.line: The social contract is made between free people because the presence of government is better.
records.reference: Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.

records.line: Sovereignty ultimately remains with the people; they retain their individual natural rights and the government has to secure those rights.
records.reference: Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.

records.line: People have three natural rights (independent of the laws of any particular society): the right to life, liberty, and property.
records.reference: Locke, J. (1689). Second Treatise of Government.

records.line: If the government abuses people’s rights, they have the moral right to overthrow it.
records.reference: Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.

records.line: One can acquire the property of something by mixing one’s labour with it, but only where there is enough, and as good left in common for others.
records.reference: Locke, J. (1689). Second Treatise of Government.

records.line: I can dispose of my property right as I wish; give it, sell it, etc., independently of government.
records.reference: Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.

records.line: There are three ‘species’: God, the source of all being; Christ, the ‘middle nature’; and ‘Creature’.
records.reference: Hutton, S. (n.d.). “Lady Anne Conway”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/conway/

records.line: A principle of likeness links God and creation; since God is good and just, his creation too is good and just.
records.reference: Hutton, S. (n.d.). “Lady Anne Conway”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/conway/

records.line: Created substance, like God, consists of spirit, but, unlike God, is constituted of particles (‘monads’).
records.reference: Hutton, S. (n.d.). “Lady Anne Conway”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/conway/

records.line: All created substance is living, capable of motion and perception; inert corporeal substance would contradict the nature of God, who is life itself.
records.reference: Hutton, S. (n.d.). “Lady Anne Conway”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/conway/

records.line: Created substance is differentiated from the divine, principally on account of its mutability and multiplicity.
records.reference: Hutton, S. (n.d.). “Lady Anne Conway”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/conway/

records.line: ‘Middle nature’ (Christ) is an intermediary being (a bridge and a buffer) through which God communicates life, action, goodness and justice.
records.reference: Hutton, S. (n.d.). “Lady Anne Conway”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/conway/

records.line: All things are capable of becoming more spirit-like – more refined qua spiritual substance.
records.reference: Hutton, S. (n.d.). “Lady Anne Conway”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/conway/

records.line: All things are capable of increased goodness.
records.reference: Hutton, S. (n.d.). “Lady Anne Conway”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/conway/

records.line: Evil is a falling away from the perfection of God, and suffering is part of a longer term process of spiritual recovery.
records.reference: Hutton, S. (n.d.). “Lady Anne Conway”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/conway/

records.line: There are two kinds of truths: (necessary, analytic) truths of reasoning and (contingent) truths of fact.
records.reference: Leibniz, G. W. (1714). The Monadology.

records.line: The sufficient reason for a truth of reasoning can be found by analysis, resolving it into simpler ideas and truths until we reach those which are primary.
records.reference: Leibniz, G. W. (1714). The Monadology.

records.line: The reasons for a truth of fact are to be found by looking at other such facts; this analysis can be continued into greater detail without limit.
records.reference: Leibniz, G. W. (1714). The Monadology.

records.line: The sufficient or ultimate reason for truths of fact must be outside of the sequence of these contingencies; it must be a necessary substance, God.
records.reference: Leibniz, G. W. (1714). The Monadology.

records.line: If there is a reality in essences or in possibilities, or in the eternal truths, it must be based upon something existent and actual.
records.reference: Leibniz, G. W. (1714). The Monadology.

records.line: God alone (or the Necessary Being) has this privilege that if he is possible he must necessarily exist.
records.reference: Leibniz, G. W. (1714). The Monadology.

records.line: There is an infinite number of possible universes in the ideas of God, but only one of them can exist.
records.reference: Leibniz, G. W. (1714). The Monadology.

records.line: God, as a perfect being, created the best possible universe with the greatest possible balance of good over evil.
records.reference: Leibniz, G. W. (1714). The Monadology.

records.line: If you could step inside an enlarged conscious machine, you would find only mechanical parts, and never anything by which to explain perception.
records.reference: Leibniz, G. W. (1714). The Monadology.

records.line: Everything, matter or mind, is made up of indivisible, partless, non-interacting, immaterial, mind-like centers of activity, ‘monads’.
records.reference: Jorati, J. (n.d.). “Gottfried Leibniz: Philosophy of Mind”. The Internet Encyclopedia of Philosophy. https://iep.utm.edu/lei-mind/

records.line: Bare monads (all monads in plants and inanimate objects; and subordinate monads in animal/human bodies) have unconscious perceptions.
records.reference: Jorati, J. (n.d.). “Gottfried Leibniz: Philosophy of Mind”. The Internet Encyclopedia of Philosophy. https://iep.utm.edu/lei-mind/

records.line: The central monads (‘souls’) of animals have perceptions that are distinct enough to give rise to memory, sensation, and consciousness (‘apperception’).
records.reference: Jorati, J. (n.d.). “Gottfried Leibniz: Philosophy of Mind”. The Internet Encyclopedia of Philosophy. https://iep.utm.edu/lei-mind/

records.line: Some monads (God and the central monads of humans and angels; ‘minds’) are capable of self-consciousness and rational perceptions.
records.reference: Jorati, J. (n.d.). “Gottfried Leibniz: Philosophy of Mind”. The Internet Encyclopedia of Philosophy. https://iep.utm.edu/lei-mind/

records.line: All souls are created at the beginning of the world and are indestructible; they change their bodies only gradually and there is never perfect death.
records.reference: Leibniz, G. W. (1714). The Monadology.

records.line: Every non-initial state of a monad (or ‘entelechy’) has as a real cause some previous state of that very monad, but not of other monads.
records.reference: Jorati, J. (n.d.). “Gottfried Leibniz: Philosophy of Mind”. The Internet Encyclopedia of Philosophy. https://iep.utm.edu/lei-mind/

records.line: Since monads cannot interact with each other, minds and their bodies do not causally interact.
records.reference: Jorati, J. (n.d.). “Gottfried Leibniz: Philosophy of Mind”. The Internet Encyclopedia of Philosophy. https://iep.utm.edu/lei-mind/

records.line: God does not act in the course of nature; it is against the laws of physics and it robs created things of all power and action.
records.reference: Jorati, J. (n.d.). “Gottfried Leibniz: Philosophy of Mind”. The Internet Encyclopedia of Philosophy. https://iep.utm.edu/lei-mind/

records.line: God actualized all monads at creation to stay in a pre-established harmony with each other; minds and bodies correspond without interaction.
records.reference: Jorati, J. (n.d.). “Gottfried Leibniz: Philosophy of Mind”. The Internet Encyclopedia of Philosophy. https://iep.utm.edu/lei-mind/

records.line: Since God discovered minds as parts of the best of all possible worlds and actualized them without changing their nature, their freedom is conserved.
records.reference: Leibniz, G. W. (1716). “Fifth Letter to Clarke,” in Correspondence [between Leibniz and Clarke], ed. by R. Ariew, Indianapolis: Hackett, 2000; Jorati, J. (2017). Gottfried Leibniz [on Free Will]. In Kevin Timpe, Meghan Griffith & Neil Levy (eds.), The Routledge Companion to Free Will. New York, USA: Routledge.

records.line: Since each monad is accommodated by God to every other monad, each is a mirror, a representation of the whole universe, its past and future.
records.reference: Leibniz, G. W. (1714). The Monadology.

records.line: If two objects have exactly the same properties, then they are the same thing; indiscernibles are identical.
records.reference: Forrest, P. (n.d.). “The Identity of Indiscernibles”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/identity-indiscernible/

records.line: Nature makes no leaps; nothing takes place suddenly.
records.reference: Leibniz, G. W. (1765). New Essays on Human Understanding.

records.line: All we can ever apprehend are the contents of our own consciousness.
records.reference: Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.

records.line: We know that we exist because we have direct awareness of being a subject of experiences.
records.reference: Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.

records.line: An idea can be like nothing but an idea; our sensory images cannot be “copies” of external objects.
records.reference: Berkeley, G. (1710). A Treatise Concerning the Principles of Human Knowledge.; Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.

records.line: We can never be sure that our experiences are attached to objects external to us.
records.reference: Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.

records.line: A consistent empiricism leads us to the conclusion that what exists are only minds and their contents.
records.reference: Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.

records.line: Total reality exists in God’s mind; things exist either in our minds or in God’s mind.
records.reference: Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.

records.line: Visible figures signify tangible figures but the connection between them is arbitrary (like words and objects), instituted by God’s language of nature.
records.reference: Berkeley, G. (1709). An Essay Towards a New Theory of Vision.; Berkeley, G. (1733). The Theory of Vision Vindicated and Explained.

records.line: Our practice must be always shaped immediately by the rule that is framed with respect to the public good of mankind.
records.reference: Berkeley, G. (1712). Passive Obedience, or the Christian Doctrine of Not Resisting the Supreme Power, Proved and Vindicated upon the Principles of the Law of Nature. Reprinted in D.H. Monro (ed.), A Guide to the British Moralists, London: Fontana, 1972, pp. 217–27.

records.line: Besides their five external senses, humans have internal senses including a sense of beauty, of morality, of honour, and of the ridiculous.
records.reference: Hutcheson, F. (1725). An Inquiry into the Original of our Ideas of Beauty and Virtue.

records.line: Moral sense is our most important sense.
records.reference: Hutcheson, F. (1725). An Inquiry into the Original of our Ideas of Beauty and Virtue.

records.line: Moral sense is implanted in people, instinctively approving virtuous actions and disapproving the vicious ones.
records.reference: Encyclopaedia Britannica. (n.d.). “Francis Hutcheson”. https://www.britannica.com/biography/Francis-Hutcheson

records.line: In addition to the uncultivated “state of nature”, there is the cultivated state in which we are following our benevolent nature designed by God.
records.reference: Vandenberg, P., DeHart, A. (n.d.). “Francis Hutcheson”. The Internet Encyclopedia of Philosophy. https://iep.utm.edu/hutcheso/

records.line: Wisdom denotes the pursuing of the best ends by the best means.
records.reference: Encyclopaedia Britannica. (n.d.). “Francis Hutcheson”. https://www.britannica.com/biography/Francis-Hutcheson

records.line: The action which procures the greatest happiness for the greatest numbers is the best.
records.reference: Encyclopaedia Britannica. (n.d.). “Francis Hutcheson”. https://www.britannica.com/biography/Francis-Hutcheson

records.line: Our rights are either alienable (rights to our goods and labours) or unalienable (rights to our life and liberty, to our private judgment).
records.reference: Hutcheson, F. (1755). A System of Moral Philosophy.

records.line: No endowments, natural or acquired, can give a perfect right to assume power over others, without their consent; slavery is horridly unjust.
records.reference: Hutcheson, F. (1755). A System of Moral Philosophy.

records.line: The foundation of aesthetic value lies in uniformity in variety.
records.reference: Eaton, M. M. (1988). Basic Issues In Aesthetics. Waveland Press.

records.line: All our simple ideas (images in thinking) in their first appearance are derived (copied) from simple impressions (sensations, passions, and emotions).
records.reference: Hume, D. (1739). A Treatise of Human Nature.

records.line: Nothing can ever be present to the mind but an image or perception (idea or impression); there is no immediate intercourse between the mind and the object.
records.reference: Hume, D. (1748). An Enquiry Concerning Human Understanding.

records.line: Ideas are faint and obscure compared to impressions; the creative power of the mind lies in the mixture of the materials afforded by the senses.
records.reference: Hume, D. (1748). An Enquiry Concerning Human Understanding.

records.line: The mind has a great propensity to spread itself on external objects, and to conjoin with them any internal impressions.
records.reference: Hume, D. (1739). A Treatise of Human Nature.

records.line: Substance is a fiction: the imagination attaches something unknown and invisible to our collections of perceptions.
records.reference: Hume, D. (1739). A Treatise of Human Nature.

records.line: When I enter into what I call ‘myself’, I always stumble on some particular perception or other; the self is nothing but a bundle of perceptions.
records.reference: Hume, D. (1739). A Treatise of Human Nature.

records.line: Relations of ideas are intuitively or demonstratively certain but the contrary of every matter of fact is still possible; we cannot demonstrate their falsehood.
records.reference: Hume, D. (1748). An Enquiry Concerning Human Understanding.

records.line: Causal connection cannot be observed; all we experience is the constant conjunction of two events, without a necessary connection or power.
records.reference: Hume, D. (1748). An Enquiry Concerning Human Understanding.

records.line: A cause is an object precedent and contiguous to another, and so united with it, that the idea of the one determines the mind to form the idea of the other.
records.reference: Hume, D. (1739). A Treatise of Human Nature.

records.line: The necessity in causal relationships is something that exists in the minds, not in objects; it depends on our inference, not the other way round.
records.reference: Hume, D. (1739). A Treatise of Human Nature.

records.line: However many Xs I observe having the property or effect y, that is no proof that the next X I see will have the property or effect y.
records.reference: Hume, D. (1748). An Enquiry Concerning Human Understanding.

records.line: Custom is the great guide of human (and animal) life: the reliable but unproven, instinctual supposition that the future will be conformable to the past.
records.reference: Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.; Hume, D. (1748). An Enquiry Concerning Human Understanding.

records.line: There should be a degree of doubt, caution, and modesty in all kinds of scrutiny and decision.
records.reference: Hume, D. (1748). An Enquiry Concerning Human Understanding.

records.line: We have to act and reason and believe when we cannot secure the foundations of these operations; reason is and must be the slave of the passions.
records.reference: Hume, D. (1739). A Treatise of Human Nature.; Hume, D. (1748). An Enquiry Concerning Human Understanding.

records.line: It is only (repeated) experience which can enable us to infer the existence of one object from that of another, including that of God.
records.reference: Hume, D. (1748). An Enquiry Concerning Human Understanding.

records.line: There is no observational evidence for the existence of God or the afterlife – only some inferential, indirect arguments (like the Argument from Design).
records.reference: Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.; Hume, D. (1748). An Enquiry Concerning Human Understanding.

records.line: There is no basis for inferring the existence of an infinitely powerful and good God in face of the contrary evidence of all the evil in this world.
records.reference: Russell, P., Kraal, A. (n.d.). “Hume on religion”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/hume-religion/

records.line: Religion is founded on faith, not on reason; it requires the subversion of all the principles of understanding, to believe what is contrary to experience.
records.reference: Hume, D. (1748). An Enquiry Concerning Human Understanding.

records.line: The idea of an infinitely superior deity sinks the human mind into the lowest submission and abasement, promoting mortification, penance, and passive suffering.
records.reference: Hume, D. (1757). The Natural History of Religion, H.E. Root (ed.), Stanford, CA: Stanford University Press. 1957.

records.line: Metaphysical claims which contain neither quantitative reasoning nor observational factual reasoning can contain nothing but sophistry and illusion.
records.reference: Hume, D. (1748). An Enquiry Concerning Human Understanding.

records.line: Free will is simply the power of acting according to the determination of the will; everyone who is not a prisoner and in chains is free.
records.reference: Hume, D. (1748). An Enquiry Concerning Human Understanding.

records.line: The doctrines of necessity (as regularity of motives and actions) and of liberty are not only consistent with morality, but are essential to its support.
records.reference: Hume, D. (1748). An Enquiry Concerning Human Understanding.

records.line: Deductions of “ought” statements from “is” statements seem inconceivable; they need careful observation and explanation.
