records.reference: Hume, D. (1739). A Treatise of Human Nature.

records.line: Morals and aesthetics are not objects of reason and understanding but of taste and sentiment.
records.reference: Hume, D. (1748). An Enquiry Concerning Human Understanding.

records.line: Moral and aesthetic values cannot be addressed except in the context of a general theory about our shared human nature.
records.reference: Gracyk, T. (n.d.). “Hume’s Aesthetics”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/hume-aesthetics/

records.line: Sentiments of moral approval and disapproval are caused by some of the operations of sympathy (empathy).
records.reference: Cohon, R. (n.d.). “Hume’s Moral Philosophy”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/hume-moral/

records.line: Beauty is no quality in things themselves: it exists merely in the mind which contemplates them and each mind perceives a different beauty.
records.reference: Hume, D. (1757). “Of the Standard of Taste”.

records.line: Vulgar taste is more idiosyncratic and capricious; refined taste is more properly rule-governed, better informed, and stable.
records.reference: Gracyk, T. (n.d.). “Hume’s Aesthetics”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/hume-aesthetics/

records.line: Standards of taste are constituted by the consensus of qualified (delicate, practiced, open, knowledgeable) judges over time and from multiple cultures.
records.reference: Gracyk, T. (n.d.). “Hume’s Aesthetics”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/hume-aesthetics/

records.line: Animal behaviors resemble the human behaviors that are caused by associations among ideas, so animals have thought and reason.
records.reference: Lurz, R. (n.d.). “Animal minds”. The Internet Encyclopedia of Philosophy. https://www.iep.utm.edu/ani-mind/

records.line: To perceive an object is to be aware of it in a particular way and to be convinced that the object exists and is as it is conceived to be.
records.reference: Nichols, R. & Yaffe, G. (n.d.). “Thomas Reid”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/reid/

records.line: Visible figure is not a representational intermediary but a relational property between objects and our eyes, with a systematic variation with the tangible figure.
records.reference: Nichols, R. & Yaffe, G. (n.d.). “Thomas Reid”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/reid/

records.line: Sensations are like artificial signs (like words) for the qualities of objects: they lack any intrinsic connection with or resemblance to what they signify.
records.reference: Nichols, R. & Yaffe, G. (n.d.). “Thomas Reid”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/reid/

records.line: Our sensations function like a language that nature has constructed: their signification is the effect of our original constitution, instead of habit.
records.reference: Nichols, R. & Yaffe, G. (n.d.). “Thomas Reid”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/reid/

records.line: Skepticism is on the same footing as the common sense belief about external objects; reason and perception both depend on our constitution.
records.reference: Nichols, R. & Yaffe, G. (n.d.). “Thomas Reid”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/reid/

records.line: Shapes, sizes, and motions are intrinsic properties of objects while colors, sounds, and smells are relational properties of objects (with us).
records.reference: Nichols, R. & Yaffe, G. (n.d.). “Thomas Reid”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/reid/

records.line: Constant conjunction is neither necessary nor sufficient for the presence of a genuine law.
records.reference: Nichols, R. & Yaffe, G. (n.d.). “Thomas Reid”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/reid/

records.line: Genuine causation, ‘efficient causation’, is not reducible to physical causation; the rules of architecture never built a house.
records.reference: Nichols, R. & Yaffe, G. (n.d.). “Thomas Reid”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/reid/; Reid, T. (1788). Essays on the Active Powers of Man.

records.line: An efficient cause is a being with a mind (with ‘moral liberty’) who has power to produce the effect, and exerts that power for that purpose.
records.reference: Nichols, R. & Yaffe, G. (n.d.). “Thomas Reid”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/reid/

records.line: People sometimes give in to their urges and sometimes act rationally against their urges; there are no laws linking motives with behavior.
records.reference: Nichols, R. & Yaffe, G. (n.d.). “Thomas Reid”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/reid/

records.line: I am not thought, I am not action, I am not feeling; I am something permanent that thinks, and acts, and suffers.
records.reference: Reid, T. (1785). Essays on the Intellectual Powers of Man.

records.line: Language is the express image and picture of human thoughts, and from the picture we may draw some certain conclusions concerning the original.
records.reference: Reid, T. (1785). Essays on the Intellectual Powers of Man.

records.line: Language is used to express judgments about all manners of topics; statements purporting to express ethical judgments are no exception.
records.reference: Nichols, R. & Yaffe, G. (n.d.). “Thomas Reid”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/reid/

records.line: The perception of duty by conscience resembles the perception of visible figures: they are both relational but their objects are mind-independent.
records.reference: Nichols, R. & Yaffe, G. (n.d.). “Thomas Reid”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/reid/

records.line: God has excellently fitted our conscience, common sense, reason, natural instincts, and bodily appetites to the benefit of the species.
records.reference: Nichols, R. & Yaffe, G. (n.d.). “Thomas Reid”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/reid/

records.line: A work, to be an artwork, must express the artist’s sentiments.
records.reference: Nichols, R. & Yaffe, G. (n.d.). “Thomas Reid”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/reid/

records.line: The beauty and sublimity of objects of sense is derived from the expression they exhibit of things intellectual, which alone have original beauty.
records.reference: Reid, T. (1736–1796). The Correspondence of Thomas Reid, Paul Wood (ed.), University Park: Pennsylvania State University Press. 2002.

records.line: Universals are not abstract or general ideas, but general terms – the linguistic manifestation of general conceptions created by human minds.
records.reference: Nichols, R. & Yaffe, G. (n.d.). “Thomas Reid”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/reid/

records.line: General terms represent by convention; forming general conceptions is typically a public process and a product of social practices.
records.reference: Nichols, R. & Yaffe, G. (n.d.). “Thomas Reid”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/reid/

records.line: Human beings in a state of nature are noble savages; they are born good, loving justice and order, acting on compassion in addition to self-interest.
records.reference: Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.; Rousseau, J.-J. (1754). Discourse on the Origin of Inequality. Translated by G. D. H. Cole. London and Toronto: J.M. Dent and Sons. 1923.

records.line: The origin of society, law, and property gave new powers to a few individuals and subjected the majority to perpetual labour, slavery, and wretchedness.
records.reference: Rousseau, J.-J. (1754). Discourse on the Origin of Inequality. Translated by G. D. H. Cole. London and Toronto: J.M. Dent and Sons. 1923.

records.line: Civilization corrupts people: social humans desire reputation, receiving the consciousness of their own existence merely from the judgment of others.
records.reference: Rousseau, J.-J. (1754). Discourse on the Origin of Inequality. Translated by G. D. H. Cole. London and Toronto: J.M. Dent and Sons. 1923.

records.line: We cannot return to the state of nature by renouncing the advances of our species; we must work to better our moral and political condition.
records.reference: Rousseau, J.-J. (1754). Discourse on the Origin of Inequality. Translated by G. D. H. Cole. London and Toronto: J.M. Dent and Sons. 1923.

records.line: When a group of people consider themselves as a single body (the sovereign), they have a single ‘general will’ also, always directed to the general welfare.
records.reference: Rousseau, J.-J. (1762). The Social Contract. Translated by Christopher Betts. Oxford University Press. 1994.

records.line: What makes the will general is the common interest that unites people: everyone necessarily submits to the conditions that they impose on the others.
records.reference: Rousseau, J.-J. (1762). The Social Contract. Translated by Christopher Betts. Oxford University Press. 1994.

records.line: To be driven by our appetites alone is slavery, while to obey a law that we have imposed on ourselves is freedom.
records.reference: Rousseau, J.-J. (1762). The Social Contract. Translated by Christopher Betts. Oxford University Press. 1994.

records.line: No one has a natural authority over their fellows, and strength does not confer any right; the basis for all legitimate authority must be agreed convention.
records.reference: Rousseau, J.-J. (1762). The Social Contract. Translated by Christopher Betts. Oxford University Press. 1994.

records.line: What one loses by the social contract is their natural freedom and an unlimited right to anything; what they gain is civil freedom and the right of property.
records.reference: Rousseau, J.-J. (1762). The Social Contract. Translated by Christopher Betts. Oxford University Press. 1994.

records.line: People may be unequal in strength or intelligence, but with the social contract all become equal through agreed convention and by right.
records.reference: Rousseau, J.-J. (1762). The Social Contract. Translated by Christopher Betts. Oxford University Press. 1994.

records.line: Equality, it is said, is a theorists’ vision, which cannot exist in practice; but if an abuse is inevitable, does it follow that it should not at least be controlled?
records.reference: Rousseau, J.-J. (1762). The Social Contract. Translated by Christopher Betts. Oxford University Press. 1994.

records.line: To properly ascertain the general will, there should be no partial society within the state, and each citizen should decide according to their own opinion.
records.reference: Rousseau, J.-J. (1762). The Social Contract. Translated by Christopher Betts. Oxford University Press. 1994.

records.line: The general will and sovereignty cannot be represented (by deputies); any law that the people in person has not ratified is void, it is not a law.
records.reference: Rousseau, J.-J. (1762). The Social Contract. Translated by Christopher Betts. Oxford University Press. 1994.

records.line: Individuals can see the good and reject it, the public desires the good and cannot see it; all equally need guides, talented legislators.
records.reference: Rousseau, J.-J. (1762). The Social Contract. Translated by Christopher Betts. Oxford University Press. 1994.

records.line: Except for the original social contract which demands unanimous consent, a majority vote is always binding on all the citizens.
records.reference: Rousseau, J.-J. (1762). The Social Contract. Translated by Christopher Betts. Oxford University Press. 1994.

records.line: Citizens owe the state everything that they can offer (including their life) whenever the sovereign asks for them for the good of the community.
records.reference: Rousseau, J.-J. (1762). The Social Contract. Translated by Christopher Betts. Oxford University Press. 1994.

records.line: One who has control of people ought not to control the laws, and one who controls the laws ought not to control people.
records.reference: Rousseau, J.-J. (1762). The Social Contract. Translated by Christopher Betts. Oxford University Press. 1994.

records.line: Legislative power can belong to the people alone; executive power belongs to the government, an intermediate body between subjects and sovereign.
records.reference: Rousseau, J.-J. (1762). The Social Contract. Translated by Christopher Betts. Oxford University Press. 1994.

records.line: The act by which a government is instituted is not a contract, but a law; those with the executive power are not the masters of the people, but its officers.
records.reference: Rousseau, J.-J. (1762). The Social Contract. Translated by Christopher Betts. Oxford University Press. 1994.

records.line: As the number of officers in the government increases, it becomes more rightful but less effective; larger states must have fewer officers (or monarchy).
records.reference: Rousseau, J.-J. (1762). The Social Contract. Translated by Christopher Betts. Oxford University Press. 1994.

records.line: Each citizen should have a religion which makes them cherish their social and moral duties but its dogmas do not concern the state or its members.
records.reference: Rousseau, J.-J. (1762). The Social Contract. Translated by Christopher Betts. Oxford University Press. 1994.

records.line: Religion is a matter of the heart, not the head; emotions of awe and reverance should dominate instead of reason.
records.reference: Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.

records.line: Education should not repress or discipline a child’s natural tendencies (with rules and punishment); it should encourage their development.
records.reference: Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.

records.line: The tools of education should not be books but practice and example: direct experience of people and of things.
records.reference: Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.

records.line: Society is so big and complex that a single mind cannot possibly contain and understand it.
records.reference: Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.

records.line: Society is like an organism living on its own, not a machine which can be built and modified at will.
records.reference: Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.

records.line: The only realistic mode of political change is organic, not revolutionary.
records.reference: Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.

records.line: Each generation inherits a treasure from the past which it is its duty to pass on.
records.reference: Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.

records.line: Humans are imperfect creatures, therefore the idea of a perfect society is fantasy.
records.reference: Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.

records.line: Governments have to deal with people as they are, extremely unequal in talent and ambition.
records.reference: Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.

records.line: The public prospers more under practical-minded people than under clever theoreticians.
records.reference: Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.

records.line: There is no use in discussing a person’s abstract (natural) right to food or to medicine; the question is upon the method of procuring and administering them.
records.reference: Burke, E. (1790). Reflections on the Revolution in France.

records.line: Art creates a distanced context: we know that what we are seeing/reading does not actually endanger us, so delight replaces unpleasant emotions.
records.reference: Eaton, M. M. (1988). Basic Issues In Aesthetics. Waveland Press.

records.line: Great art seeks the infinite, which cannot be clear; it has the emotional pull and the distanced experience of the unknown, the sublime.
records.reference: Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.

records.line: What we can apprehend (perceive, feel, think, etc.) is limited by the capabilities of our sensory organs and brains.
records.reference: Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.

records.line: The contents of consciousness, produced by our bodily apparatus, are not the external objects; they are just limited representations.
records.reference: Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.

records.line: Our senses give us accurate information about the world, but they do so as gauges, not as pictures.
records.reference: Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.

records.line: The mode of existence of things-in-themselves (noumena) has nothing to do with our impressions (phenomena) of them.
records.reference: Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.

records.line: We have no means of access to the realm of things as they are (noumena).
records.reference: Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.

records.line: We make synthetic a priori judgements, based on our a priori forms of sensibility (space, time) and categories of understanding (quantity, quality, relation, modality).
records.reference: Kant, I. (1783). Prolegomena to Any Future Metaphysics. Cambridge University Press. 1997.

records.line: Space and time are forms of our sensibility, and causality is a category of our understanding; they are not original characteristics of things.
records.reference: Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.

records.line: There is no inner awareness of the self as an object but the “I think” must be able to accompany all my representations.
records.reference: Smith, J. (n.d.). “Self-Consciousness”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/self-consciousness/

records.line: It is impossible for any being to understand its own nature.
records.reference: Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.

records.line: Existence is not a property; it is a metaphysically necessary condition for the instantiation of any properties.
records.reference: Himma, K. E. (n.d.). “Anselm: Ontological Argument for God’sExistence”. The Internet Encyclopedia of Philosophy. https://www.iep.utm.edu/ont-arg/

records.line: We cannot know/prove the existence of God/souls because it is out of the limits of our senses.
records.reference: Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.

records.line: Science is the key to understand the world of material objects in space and time.
records.reference: Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.

records.line: If we are objects moving in space and time according to the scientific laws, we cannot have free will.
records.reference: Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.

records.line: The pursuit of happiness and its principles are variable, unpredictable, contingent; they cannot be the foundations of a necessary and universal moral law.
records.reference: Moran, K. (2022). Kant’s Ethics. Cambridge University Press.

records.line: You should act only according to maxims which you can will also to be universal laws; this is the categorical (unconditional) imperative.
records.reference: Kant, I. (1785). Groundwork of the Metaphysics of Morals.

records.line: A will that is merely free from deterministic causation would be a will that acts randomly; a will that is free must also be guided by some principle.
records.reference: Moran, K. (2022). Kant’s Ethics. Cambridge University Press.

records.line: A will is autonomous if it is subject to the law of morality not because of an interest it has but because it has given the law to itself.
records.reference: Moran, K. (2022). Kant’s Ethics. Cambridge University Press.

records.line: A will bound by the moral law is a free will, and a free will must be a will that is also bound by the moral law, the categorical imperative.
records.reference: Moran, K. (2022). Kant’s Ethics. Cambridge University Press.

records.line: We know we are free because we are, undeniably, bound by the moral law: it forces itself upon us as a synthetic a priori proposition.
records.reference: Kant, I. (1788). Critique of Practical Reason.

records.line: Our empirical (phenomenal) self is bound by natural determinism but our rational self is free from such determinism.
records.reference: Moran, K. (2022). Kant’s Ethics. Cambridge University Press.

records.line: Morality is possible only for rational creatures having an a priori understanding of reasons for action and acting with a sense of duty instead of emotions.
records.reference: Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.; Kant, I. (1785). Groundwork of the Metaphysics of Morals.

records.line: Because our wills are pulled away from autonomous willing by inclination and desire, we experience morality as a struggle, an obligation, an imperative.
records.reference: Moran, K. (2022). Kant’s Ethics. Cambridge University Press.

records.line: A human being (and every rational being) exists as an end in itself, not merely as a means for the discretionary use for this or that will.
records.reference: Kant, I. (1785). Groundwork of the Metaphysics of Morals.

records.line: Punishment must be inflicted upon the criminal only because they have committed a crime, not because it will be useful to them or to society.
records.reference: Kant, I. (1797). The Metaphysics of Morals.

records.line: Animals are not self-conscious and are there merely as a means to an end; that end is man.
records.reference: Kant, I. (1760s). Lectures on Ethics.

records.line: Taste is the faculty of judging of an object or a method of representing it by an entirely disinterested satisfaction (“beautiful”) or dissatisfaction.
records.reference: Kant, I. (1790). Critique of Judgment. (J. M. Bernard, Trans.) New York, NY: Hafuer Publishing Company. 1951.

records.line: In all judgments by which we describe anything as beautiful, we allow no one to be of another opinion. (‘subjective universality’)
records.reference: Kant, I. (1790). Critique of Judgment. (J. M. Bernard, Trans.) New York, NY: Hafuer Publishing Company. 1951.

records.line: Enlightenment is people’s emergence from their self-incurred immaturity – the inability, out of laziness and cowardice, to use one’s own understanding.
records.reference: Kant, I. (1784). An Answer to the Question: What is Enlightenment?

records.line: It is more possible for the entire public to enlighten itself than for each individual; enlightenment is almost inevitable, if the public is left in freedom.
records.reference: Kant, I. (1784). An Answer to the Question: What is Enlightenment?

records.line: A public can achieve enlightenment only slowly; a revolution may bring about the end of a despotism, but never a true reform in ways of thinking.
records.reference: Kant, I. (1784). An Answer to the Question: What is Enlightenment?

records.line: Enlightenment only requires the freedom to use one’s own reason in addressing the reading public – this does not contradict obedience in civil posts.
records.reference: Kant, I. (1784). An Answer to the Question: What is Enlightenment?

records.line: When we inspect a complex thing like a watch, we infer that it must have had a maker; similarly, organisms must have a designer, and that is God.
records.reference: Paley, W. (1802). Natural Theology.

records.line: Moral virtue is the doing good to humankind, in obedience to the will of God, and for the sake of everlasting happiness.
records.reference: Paley, W. (1785). Principles of Moral and Political Philosophy.

records.line: The rightness or wrongness of an action is determined by its consequences.
records.reference: Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.

records.line: Good consequences of actions are those that give pleasure to someone (and bad – pain).
records.reference: Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.

records.line: A right course of action is the one that maximizes the excess of pleasure over pain.
records.reference: Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.

records.line: The greatest happiness of the greatest number is the foundation of morals and legislation.
records.reference: Bentham, J. (1843). “The Commonplace Book”. J. Bowring (Ed.), Works.

records.line: Everybody is to count for one; and nobody for more than one.
records.reference: Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.

records.line: There are no such things as natural rights – no such things as rights anterior to the establishment of government.
records.reference: Bentham, J. (1843). Critique of the Doctrine of Inalienable, Natural Rights.

records.line: A goal and the chosen means to it are directly intentional; a known side effect is obliquely or collaterally intentional.
records.reference: Bentham, J. (1789). An Introduction to the Principles of Morals and Legislation.

records.line: A law is an assemblage of signs declarative of a volition conceived or adopted by the sovereign in a state, concerning a course of conduct.
records.reference: Bentham, J. (1782). Of Laws in General, H.L.A. Hart (ed.), London: Athlone Press. 1970.

records.line: All punishment is mischief; punishment in itself is evil and it ought only to be admitted in as far as it promises to exclude some greater evil.
records.reference: Bentham, J. (1789). An Introduction to the Principles of Morals and Legislation.

records.line: The purpose of punishment is deterrence, and it is only intentional actions that can be deterred, so it is only these that we should punish.
records.reference: Bentham, J. (1789). An Introduction to the Principles of Morals and Legislation.

records.line: We are justified in painlessly killing animals for food or to protect ourselves; we are the better for it, and they are never the worse in terms of pain.
records.reference: Bentham, J. (1789). An Introduction to the Principles of Morals and Legislation.

records.line: The law should protect any sensitive being from cruelty; the question is not “Can they reason?” nor “Can they talk?” but “Can they suffer?”.
records.reference: Bentham, J. (1789). An Introduction to the Principles of Morals and Legislation.

records.line: Government and all human relations can be simplified, explicated, and rendered transparent.
records.reference: Tomaselli, S. (n.d.). “Mary Wollstonecraft”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/wollstonecraft/

records.line: God made all things right and the cause of all evil is man.
records.reference: Tomaselli, S. (n.d.). “Mary Wollstonecraft”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/wollstonecraft/

records.line: Rights entail duties; none could be expected to perform duties whose natural rights are not respected.
records.reference: Tomaselli, S. (n.d.). “Mary Wollstonecraft”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/wollstonecraft/

records.line: For a truly moral society, a complete change in the nature of the relationship between men and women is required.
records.reference: Tomaselli, S. (n.d.). “Mary Wollstonecraft”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/wollstonecraft/

records.line: Women are ill-prepared for their duties as social beings and imprisoned in a web of false expectations.
records.reference: Tomaselli, S. (n.d.). “Mary Wollstonecraft”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/wollstonecraft/

records.line: Women should be educated like men, to become rational and independent beings whose sense of worth doesn’t come from their appearance.
records.reference: Tomaselli, S. (n.d.). “Mary Wollstonecraft”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/wollstonecraft/

records.line: Women should be able to support themselves and their children, and never have to marry or remarry out of financial necessity.
records.reference: Tomaselli, S. (n.d.). “Mary Wollstonecraft”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/wollstonecraft/

records.line: Women should be granted civil and political rights; they should elect representatives of their own.
records.reference: Tomaselli, S. (n.d.). “Mary Wollstonecraft”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/wollstonecraft/

records.line: Scientific laws cannot be logically derived from empirical observations.
records.reference: Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.

records.line: Empirical observations can be deduced from scientific laws.
records.reference: Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.

records.line: The universe is the creation of the subject; it is derived from our ordered conception of it, in accordance with logical necessity.
records.reference: Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.

records.line: We possess an immediate acquaintance with ourselves; the self exists and posits its own existence by virtue of merely existing.
records.reference: Smith, J. (n.d.). “Self-Consciousness”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/self-consciousness/

records.line: We have experience of the existence of our selves, not in our capacity as knowing subjects but in our actions and decisions as moral agents.
records.reference: Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.

records.line: The contradiction between the self and not-self can be resolved by positing a third concept (divisibility) which unites the two sides.
records.reference: Maybee, J. E. (n.d.). “Hegel’s dialectics”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/hegel-dialectics/

records.line: We know that we persist over time because we know that we bear moral responsibility for our actions.
records.reference: Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.

records.line: The fundamental nature of all reality is moral; the empirical world is a field of reality that is not me through which I can exist as a moral agent.
records.reference: Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.

records.line: Synthetic method to resolve a dialectically-generated contradiction: Thesis + Antithesis → Synthesis demonstrating their limits
records.reference: Maybee, J. E. (n.d.). “Hegel’s dialectics”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/hegel-dialectics/

records.line: What philosophy one chooses depends upon what kind of person one is; a philosophical system is animated by the soul of the person who has it.
records.reference: Fichte, J.G. (1797). “Introduction to the Science of Knowledge”, trans. Adolph Ernst Kroeger. Journal of Speculative Philosophy, volume 1 (1867).

records.line: Nature is a unity: it is not a state of affairs but an ongoing process which started with lifeless matter, evolving into plants, animals, and then humans.
records.reference: Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.

records.line: We are part of Nature, not separate from or standing against it.
records.reference: Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.

records.line: Our creativity is different from the rest of Nature’s creativity; it is self-aware as we explore the depths of our own being with our art.
records.reference: Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.

records.line: Since we are part of Nature, in creative arts Nature is becoming self-aware.
records.reference: Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.

records.line: The whole process of Nature is a development towards self-awareness.
records.reference: Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.

records.line: Creative art is the summit of all existence, the raison d’être of reality.
records.reference: Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.

records.line: The final desperate question: Why is there anything at all? Why not nothing?
records.reference: Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.

records.line: Being is only something in opposition to nothing, and they appear identical as no criterion can be invoked which differentiates them.
records.reference: Redding, P. (n.d.). “Georg Wilhelm Friedrich Hegel”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/hegel/

records.line: The category of ‘becoming’ (transition from nothing to being) saves thinking from paralysis, accommodating being and nothing as negated moments.
records.reference: Redding, P. (n.d.). “Georg Wilhelm Friedrich Hegel”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/hegel/

records.line: Everything is the result of an intelligible (not arbitrary), dialectical process of change, and everything is changing.
records.reference: Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.

records.line: Repeating dialectical process: Stage 1 + Opposition → Stage 2 (+ Opposition → Stage 3 ...)
records.reference: Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.

records.line: Even the apparently most immediate contents of consciousness are in fact mediated by the concepts with which they are grasped.
records.reference: Redding, P. (n.d.). “Georg Wilhelm Friedrich Hegel”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/hegel/

records.line: One’s self-consciousness is dependent on one’s recognition of those others as similarly recognizing oneself as a self-conscious subject.
records.reference: Redding, P. (n.d.). “Georg Wilhelm Friedrich Hegel”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/hegel/

records.line: Complex patterns of mutual recognition constituting ‘objective spirit’ provide the social matrix within which individual self-consciousnesses exist.
records.reference: Redding, P. (n.d.). “Georg Wilhelm Friedrich Hegel”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/hegel/

records.line: The subjective spirit and the objective spirit are parts of ‘Geist’ (or God), the Absolute Spirit – the ultimate essence of being, ‘I’ that is ‘We’ and ‘We’ that is ‘I’.
records.reference: Redding, P. (n.d.). “Georg Wilhelm Friedrich Hegel”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/hegel/; Hegel, G. W. F. (1807). The Phenomenology of Spirit, trans. Miller, A. V. Oxford: Clarendon Press. 1977.

records.line: Reality is a historical process where Geist develops towards self-realization and self-consciousness through the actions of people, groups, and states.
records.reference: Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.; Duquette, D. A. (n.d.). “Hegel: Social and Political Thought”. The Internet Encyclopedia of Philosophy. https://www.iep.utm.edu/hegelsoc/

records.line: Individuals feel alienated as they fail to understand the social and political structures that they create.
records.reference: Leopold, D. (n.d.). “Alienation”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/alienation/

records.line: We project perfect qualities on to God (omniscience, etc.) leading to alienation whereas we share the same spiritual existence.
records.reference: Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.

records.line: The self-determination of the bondsman through submission to the lord is incomplete because of the asymmetry in this relation.
records.reference: Duquette, D. A. (n.d.). “Hegel: Social and Political Thought”. The Internet Encyclopedia of Philosophy. https://www.iep.utm.edu/hegelsoc/

records.line: The bondsman objectifies himself through his labor, his transformed environment serving as a reflection of his self-realizing activity.
records.reference: Duquette, D. A. (n.d.). “Hegel: Social and Political Thought”. The Internet Encyclopedia of Philosophy. https://www.iep.utm.edu/hegelsoc/

records.line: When Geist will reach self-consciousness, alienations will end, everything will be harmonious, and change will stop (‘the Absolute’).
records.reference: Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.

records.line: In the Absolute, everyone will work freely, serving the needs of the whole community rather than of masters, subject only to the discipline of reason.
records.reference: Duquette, D. A. (n.d.). “Hegel: Social and Political Thought”. The Internet Encyclopedia of Philosophy. https://www.iep.utm.edu/hegelsoc/

records.line: The perfected nation-state is the end/goal of history; it is a spiritual individual providing an optimal level of self-consciousness and rationality.
records.reference: Duquette, D. A. (n.d.). “Hegel: Social and Political Thought”. The Internet Encyclopedia of Philosophy. https://www.iep.utm.edu/hegelsoc/

records.line: Religion supports the realization of Geist in the world and thus cannot have supremacy over or be opposed to the state.
records.reference: Duquette, D. A. (n.d.). “Hegel: Social and Political Thought”. The Internet Encyclopedia of Philosophy. https://www.iep.utm.edu/hegelsoc/

records.line: Hereditary monarchy is the appropriate form of authority: strong central government along with a system of indirect representation through Estates.
records.reference: Duquette, D. A. (n.d.). “Hegel: Social and Political Thought”. The Internet Encyclopedia of Philosophy. https://www.iep.utm.edu/hegelsoc/

records.line: Giving everyone an equal vote in large elections makes the outcome devoid of rational form, impersonal, contingent, and alienating.
records.reference: Wood, A. W. (2015). “Hegel’s political philosophy”. S. Houlgate & M. Baur (Eds.), A companion to Hegel. John Wiley & Sons.

records.line: The economy, especially through the division of labor, produces fragmentation and diminishment of human life.
records.reference: Duquette, D. A. (n.d.). “Hegel: Social and Political Thought”. The Internet Encyclopedia of Philosophy. https://www.iep.utm.edu/hegelsoc/

records.line: The task of the state is not to thoroughly bureaucratize civil society but to provide rationality, oversight, regulation, and when necessary intervention.
records.reference: Duquette, D. A. (n.d.). “Hegel: Social and Political Thought”. The Internet Encyclopedia of Philosophy. https://www.iep.utm.edu/hegelsoc/

records.line: Art is where consciousness of the Absolute first takes shape but modern religion and philosophy have passed beyond the limitations of art.
records.reference: Hegel, G. W. F. (1835). Aesthetics: Lectures on Fine Art.

records.line: Philosophy has no other object but God and so is essentially rational theology, more developed than the imagistic representational form of religion.
records.reference: Hegel, G. W. F. (1835). Aesthetics: Lectures on Fine Art.; Redding, P. (n.d.). “Georg Wilhelm Friedrich Hegel”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/hegel/

records.line: The history of philosophy is the development of one philosophy, a necessary succession of stages, culminating in the self-consciousness of Geist.
records.reference: Hegel, G. W. F. (1833). Lectures on the History of Philosophy.

records.line: Without time and space, there can be no differentiation; the noumenal reality cannot consist of things, it is one thing.
records.reference: Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.

records.line: Causal connection can exist only within the phenomenal realm, therefore noumena cannot be the causes of phenomena.
records.reference: Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.

records.line: The phenomenal is not a different reality from the noumenal but the same reality known in a different way.
records.reference: Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.

records.line: Will and the bodily movements are the same events known in two different ways (from inside and from outside).
records.reference: Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.

records.line: The purposeless energy, the unconscious impersonal ‘will’ (force) that we see in the physical events is what noumenal is.
records.reference: Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.

records.line: In the phenomenal world, we seem to be separate individuals but ultimately we are one and undifferentiated in the noumenal.
records.reference: Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.

records.line: The basis of morality and love is compassion; we are one in the noumenal and we share each other’s sufferings and joys.
records.reference: Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.

records.line: The concept of a personal God is too anthropomorphic to be taken seriously.
records.reference: Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.

records.line: The misery everywhere and the imperfection of humans cannot be reconciled with the existence of an all-wise, all-good, and all-powerful Being.
records.reference: Schopenhauer, A. (1851). “On the Sufferings of the World”. Parerga und Paralipomena.

records.line: The concept of soul is useless; it supposes mind to be inseparably connected and yet independent of the organism.
records.reference: Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.

records.line: Violence and injustice are rife in both the animal world and the human world; each individual lives a meaningless and purposeless tragedy, and dies.
records.reference: Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.

records.line: The empirical world seems to be independent of ourselves, but in fact it is all subject-dependent – an illusion.
records.reference: Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.

records.line: We should not be taken in by the empirical world; the end stage of philosophical understanding is the turning away of the human will from the world.
records.reference: Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.

records.line: We are slaves to our desires, never fully satisfied; existence is suffering.
records.reference: Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.

records.line: We can only escape existential suffering through aesthetic contemplation or asceticism.
records.reference: Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.

records.line: The productions of genius (art, philosophy) serve no useful purpose; they alone exist for their own sake, they are the flower of existence.
records.reference: Schopenhauer, A. (1818). The World as Will and Representation.

records.line: Through arts we experience being taken out of time and space, and out of ourselves, and being in touch with something outside the empirical world.
records.reference: Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.

records.line: Music transcends the other arts in metaphysical significance; through its abstractness we can enjoy being out of space and time.
records.reference: Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.

records.line: Nature has furnished men and women with different attributes; their bodies and minds are adapted to their different roles in the propagation of the species.
records.reference: Schopenhauer, A. (1851). “On Women”. Parerga und Paralipomena.

records.line: Women are the second sex in many respects – childish, foolish, extravagant, short-sighted, weak, pretentious, cunning, and unaesthetic.
records.reference: Schopenhauer, A. (1851). “On Women”. Parerga und Paralipomena.

records.line: European social, civil, and political affairs would improve if women were socially and legally assigned their natural position as subordinates, like in Asia.
records.reference: Schopenhauer, A. (1851). “On Women”. Parerga und Paralipomena.

records.line: God is an imaginary representation of the human species-essence; theology is anthropology.
records.reference: Gooch, T. (n.d.). “Ludwig Feuerbach”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/ludwig-feuerbach/

records.line: When human beings in the course of their history acquire new and different wishes, they worship new and different divinities.
records.reference: Gooch, T. (n.d.). “Ludwig Feuerbach”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/ludwig-feuerbach/

records.line: Religion is an alienated form of human self-consciousness: it involves the relation of us to our own essence as though to a being distinct from ourselves.
records.reference: Gooch, T. (n.d.). “Ludwig Feuerbach”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/ludwig-feuerbach/

records.line: Theological claims understood in the usual sense (referring to a non-human divine person) are self-contradictory.
records.reference: Gooch, T. (n.d.). “Ludwig Feuerbach”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/ludwig-feuerbach/

records.line: To posit matter as God is to affirm atheism and materialism.
records.reference: Gooch, T. (n.d.). “Ludwig Feuerbach”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/ludwig-feuerbach/

records.line: We need to renounce the theological essence of religion to affirm its true anthropological essence: the divinity of man.
records.reference: Gooch, T. (n.d.). “Ludwig Feuerbach”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/ludwig-feuerbach/

records.line: The hidden “truth of Christianity” will finally be realized as an atheistic humanism which embraces the historical tasks of human self-realization.
records.reference: Gooch, T. (n.d.). “Ludwig Feuerbach”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/ludwig-feuerbach/

records.line: Thought comes from being, but being does not come from thought.
records.reference: Gooch, T. (n.d.). “Ludwig Feuerbach”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/ludwig-feuerbach/

records.line: I am a real, sensuous being and the body in its totality is my ego, my essence itself.
records.reference: Gooch, T. (n.d.). “Ludwig Feuerbach”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/ludwig-feuerbach/

records.line: The whole of our conduct should be guided by the principle of utility, insofar as it has not been determined by Revelation.
records.reference: Austin, J. (1832). The Province of Jurisprudence Determined, W. Rumble (ed.), Cambridge: Cambridge University Press, 1995.

records.line: An independent political society must be in a habit of obedience to a sovereign (person or group) which does not habitually obey anyone else.
records.reference: Austin, J. (1832). The Province of Jurisprudence Determined, W. Rumble (ed.), Cambridge: Cambridge University Press, 1995.

records.line: Laws are commands set by the sovereign, prescribing kinds of acts (not specific acts), clothed with legal sanctions.
records.reference: Austin, J. (1832). The Province of Jurisprudence Determined, W. Rumble (ed.), Cambridge: Cambridge University Press, 1995.
records.keywords: command theory of law, legal positivism

records.line: ‘Positive law’ (set by the sovereign) differs both from the laws of God and from ‘positive morality’ (rules of conduct and conventional morality).
records.reference: Austin, J. (1832). The Province of Jurisprudence Determined, W. Rumble (ed.), Cambridge: Cambridge University Press, 1995.

records.line: The existence of law is one thing; its merit or demerit is another; a law, which actually exists, is a law, though we happen to dislike it.
records.reference: Austin, J. (1832). The Province of Jurisprudence Determined, W. Rumble (ed.), Cambridge: Cambridge University Press, 1995.

records.line: When we are considering our policies, we should seek the greatest happiness of the greatest number.
records.reference: Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.

records.line: Higher (mental, aesthetic, and moral) pleasures should be taken to be preferable (in kind, rather than by degree) to lower pleasures.
records.reference: Heydt, C. (n.d.). “John Stuart Mill”. The Internet Encyclopedia of Philosophy. https://iep.utm.edu/milljs/

records.line: To have a right is to have something which society ought to defend me in the possession of, for reasons of general utility.
records.reference: Mill, J. S. (1861). Utilitarianism. John W. Parker & Son.

records.line: Human liberty comprises liberty of thought and feeling, liberty of tastes and pursuits, and liberty of combination among individuals.
records.reference: Mill, J. S. (1859). On Liberty. John W. Parker & Son.

records.line: The only part of the conduct of any one, for which one is amenable to society, is that which harms others; over oneself, the individual is sovereign.
records.reference: Mill, J. S. (1859). On Liberty. John W. Parker & Son.

records.line: There is no parity between the feeling of a person for their own opinion, and the feeling of another who is offended at their holding it.
records.reference: Mill, J. S. (1859). On Liberty. John W. Parker & Son.

records.line: There ought to exist the fullest liberty of professing and discussing any doctrine, however immoral it may be considered.
records.reference: Mill, J. S. (1859). On Liberty. John W. Parker & Son.

records.line: Our beliefs have no safeguard to rest on, but a standing invitation to the whole world to prove them unfounded; this is the amount of certainty attainable.
records.reference: Mill, J. S. (1859). On Liberty. John W. Parker & Son.

records.line: Even a true opinion will lose its power/meaning and become a mere formal profession unless it is vigorously and earnestly contested.
records.reference: Mill, J. S. (1859). On Liberty. John W. Parker & Son.

records.line: Having a true opinion in the mind as a prejudice, as a belief independent of argument, is not knowing the truth.
records.reference: Mill, J. S. (1859). On Liberty. John W. Parker & Son.

records.line: The indulgence of hope with regard to the existence of God and the afterlife, while we recognize that we have no ground for more than a hope, is philosophically defensible.
records.reference: Mill, J.S. (1874). “Theism” in Three Essays on Religion, New York: Henry Holt & Co.

records.line: Different persons should be allowed to lead different lives (different experiments of living); whatever crushes individuality is despotism.
records.reference: Mill, J. S. (1859). On Liberty. John W. Parker & Son.

records.line: It is important to give the freest scope possible to uncustomary things, in order that it may in time appear which of these are fit to be converted into customs.
records.reference: Mill, J. S. (1859). On Liberty. John W. Parker & Son.

records.line: No community should be prevented from living under the laws they please, provided they commit no aggression on other nations, and allow freedom of departure.
records.reference: Mill, J. S. (1859). On Liberty. John W. Parker & Son.

records.line: Despotism is a legitimate mode of government in dealing with barbarians, provided the end be their improvement.
records.reference: Mill, J. S. (1859). On Liberty. John W. Parker & Son.

records.line: To produce children without a fair prospect of being able to provide for their physical and mental development is a crime against them and society.
records.reference: Mill, J. S. (1859). On Liberty. John W. Parker & Son.

records.line: A general State education is a mere contrivance for moulding people to be exactly like one another, so as to please the predominant power.
records.reference: Mill, J. S. (1859). On Liberty. John W. Parker & Son.

records.line: Ideal government: the greatest dissemination of power consistent with efficiency; the greatest centralisation of information, and diffusion of it from the center.
records.reference: Mill, J. S. (1859). On Liberty. John W. Parker & Son.

records.line: The inequality of men and women is a relic from the past; it should be replaced by a system of perfect equality.
records.reference: Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.

records.line: What is now called the nature of women is an eminently artificial thing – the result of forced repression in some directions, unnatural stimulation in others.
records.reference: Mill, J. S. (1869). “The Subjection of Women”.

records.line: To ensure a truly exceptionless regularity (and the law of causality), we must include a large portion of the universe into the cause (the “whole cause”).
records.reference: Broadbent, A. (n.d.). “Causation”. The Internet Encyclopedia of Philosophy. https://iep.utm.edu/causation/

records.line: With our mediated experience, we can have no warrant for believing in non-mental entities; matter is a permanent possibility of sensation.
records.reference: Macleod, C. (n.d.). “John Stuart Mill”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/mill/

records.line: The sensation of color cannot be explained by any law of motion (of the particles in the body) even though there is correlation between the two.
records.reference: Mill, J. S. (1843). A system of logic. John W. Parker.

records.line: The basic principles of logic and mathematics are generalizations from experience rather than known a priori.
records.reference: Heydt, C. (n.d.). “John Stuart Mill”. The Internet Encyclopedia of Philosophy. https://iep.utm.edu/milljs/

records.line: There are no such things as numbers in the abstract; numbers are properties of aggregates and denote aggregates with those properties.
records.reference: Macleod, C. (n.d.). “John Stuart Mill”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/mill/

records.line: Proper names are not connotative: they denote the individuals who are called by them without indicating any attributes as belonging to those individuals.
records.reference: Mill, J. S. (1843). A system of logic. John W. Parker.

records.line: All the organic beings which have ever lived on this earth have descended from some one primordial form.
records.reference: Darwin, C. (1859). The Origin of Species. John Murray.

records.line: There is one general law in the evolution of organisms: multiply, vary, let the best adapted live and the worst adapted die. (‘natural selection’)
records.reference: Darwin, C. (1859). The Origin of Species. John Murray.

records.line: Although altruism gives little advantage to each individual over others in a group, a group of altruists would have an immense advantage over other groups.
records.reference: Darwin, C. (1871). The Descent of Man. John Murray.

records.line: The sufferings of millions of animals throughout almost endless time are irreconcilable with the idea of a God of unbounded benevolence.
records.reference: Darwin, C. (ca. 1872). Autobiographies.

records.line: It is hardly possible to define clearly what is meant by the organisation of a living thing being higher or lower.
records.reference: Darwin, C. (1859). The Origin of Species. John Murray.

records.line: ‘Species’ is an artificial concept made for convenience; the search for the essence of the term ‘species’ is in vain.
records.reference: Darwin, C. (1859). The Origin of Species. John Murray.

records.line: The gradual formation of different languages is similar to that of species; the survival of favored words is natural selection.
records.reference: Darwin, C. (1871). The Descent of Man. John Murray.

records.line: The study of nature and humans will become far more interesting when we regard every production of nature as one which has had a history.
records.reference: Darwin, C. (1859). The Origin of Species. John Murray.

records.line: We have to look at individual entities, not ideas and abstractions, to understand the world because that is all there is.
records.reference: Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.

records.line: The individual human being is the ultimate moral actor; our subjective, personal lives, our decisions are important.
records.reference: Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.

records.line: The attempt to make the state the highest ethical authority is an unethical flight from the category of individuality into that of the race.
records.reference: Kierkegaard, S. (1846). Concluding Unscientific Postscript to Philosophical Fragments.

records.line: We build our lives and ourselves through the choices we make.
records.reference: Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.

records.line: The relationship of the individual soul to God is what matters most.
records.reference: Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.

records.line: Whoever learns to be anxious in the right way has learned the ultimate.
records.reference: Kierkegaard, S. (1844). The Concept of Dread (/Anxiety).

records.line: The supreme paradox of all thought is the attempt to discover something that thought cannot think.
records.reference: Kierkegaard, S. (1844). Philosophical Fragments.

records.line: Reality is a historical dialectical process operating with alienations.
records.reference: Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.

records.line: Only when alienations end will we be free and in control, as parts of an organic society instead of selfish individuals.
records.reference: Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.

records.line: The world and the dialectical process of change consist of material (not spiritual) forces; history is nothing but the activity of people pursuing their aims.
records.reference: Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.; Marx, K. & Engels, F. (1844) The Holy Family. Foreign Languages Publishing House, Moscow. 1956.

records.line: The human essence is no abstraction inherent in each single individual; it is the ensemble of the social relations, and all social life is essentially practical.
records.reference: Marx, K. (1845). Theses On Feuerbach.

records.line: The defining characteristic of each individual is their relationship to the means of production; this gives rise to the existence of socio-economic classes.
records.reference: Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.

records.line: The dialectic works with the development of the means of production and the conflicts between socio-economic classes.
records.reference: Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.

records.line: The ideas of the ruling class are in every epoch the ruling ideas; the class which is the ruling material force of society, is its ruling intellectual force.
records.reference: Marx, K. & Engels, F. (1845–46) The German Ideology. In Marx-Engels, Collected Works, Vol. 5, Lawrence & Wishart, London.

records.line: Institutions, religions, philosophies, the arts, etc. (‘superstructure’) are determined by the economic base (‘substructure’).
records.reference: Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.

records.line: Religion is the opium of the people; to abolish religion as the illusory happiness of the people is to demand their real happiness.
records.reference: Marx, K. (1843). Critique of Hegel’s Philosophy of Right.

records.line: The Industrialist Capitalist society is the last stage before the end of history, of the dialectic.
records.reference: Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.

records.line: Unlike animals, humans produce even when they are free from physical need and truly produce only in freedom from such need, as conscious beings.
records.reference: Marx, K. (1844). Economic and Philosophical Manuscripts.

records.line: Estranged labor (factories, division of labor, etc.) alienates the workers from the product, the process, the species-being, and themselves.
records.reference: Marx, K. (1844). Economic and Philosophical Manuscripts.; Marx, K. & Engels, F. (1848). The Communist Manifesto.

records.line: When commodities are in the relation of exchange, their ‘exchange-value’ is something totally independent of their ‘use-value’.
records.reference: Marx, K. (1867). Capital: A Critique of Political Economy.

records.line: The capitalists exploit the workers by paying them less than the value of their labor and appropriate the ‘surplus-value’ thus created to make profit.
records.reference: Marx, K. (1867). Capital: A Critique of Political Economy.

records.line: Commodities appear as independent beings endowed with life, entering into relation both with one another and the human race. (‘commodity fetishism’)
records.reference: Marx, K. (1867). Capital: A Critique of Political Economy.

records.line: Private property has made us so stupid and one-sided that an object is only ours when we have (possess, eat, drink, wear, inhabit, etc.) it.
records.reference: Marx, K. (1844). Economic and Philosophical Manuscripts.

records.line: It is inevitable that the workers will overthrow the capitalists and create a class-free society where the means of production belong to all.
records.reference: Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.

records.line: Philosophers have hitherto only interpreted the world in various ways; the point is to change it.
records.reference: Marx, K. (1845). Theses On Feuerbach.

records.line: We have no experience of that which truly exists; physical phenomena (light, sound, heat, location, etc.) are only inadequate representations.
records.reference: Brentano, F. (1874). Psychology from an Empirical Standpoint. Trans. L. McAlister, A. Rancurello, and D. B. Terrell. London: Routledge and Kegan Paul. 1973, 1995.

records.line: All phenomena, whether physical or mental, are appearances, objects of mental phenomena, “things of the mind”.
records.reference: Montague, M. (2017). “Intentionality: from Brentano to representationalism”. In Amy Kind (ed.), Philosophy of Mind in the Twentieth and Twenty-First Centuries: The History of the Philosophy of Mind, Volume 6. New York: Routledge.

records.line: Every mental phenomenon is characterized by the intentional (or mental) inexistence of an object, and reference to a content, direction toward an object.
records.reference: Brentano, F. (1874). Psychology from an Empirical Standpoint. Trans. L. McAlister, A. Rancurello, and D. B. Terrell. London: Routledge and Kegan Paul. 1973, 1995.

records.line: Intentionality is characteristic exclusively of mental phenomena; no physical phenomenon exhibits anything like it.
records.reference: Brentano, F. (1874). Psychology from an Empirical Standpoint. Trans. L. McAlister, A. Rancurello, and D. B. Terrell. London: Routledge and Kegan Paul. 1973, 1995.

records.line: Every mental phenomenon is directed towards itself as a secondary object; inner perception makes us become aware of our mental phenomena.
records.reference: Huemer, W. (n.d.). “Franz Brentano”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/brentano/

records.line: Consciousness and intentionality are constitutively related, and cannot be treated in isolation from one another.
records.reference: Montague, M. (2017). “Intentionality: from Brentano to representationalism”. In Amy Kind (ed.), Philosophy of Mind in the Twentieth and Twenty-First Centuries: The History of the Philosophy of Mind, Volume 6. New York: Routledge.

records.line: An object has ‘real existence’ when it is presented to us as it is in itself; only mental phenomena, in immediate inner perception, have real existence.
records.reference: Montague, M. (2017). “Intentionality: from Brentano to representationalism”. In Amy Kind (ed.), Philosophy of Mind in the Twentieth and Twenty-First Centuries: The History of the Philosophy of Mind, Volume 6. New York: Routledge.

records.line: We can know truths about our mentality with certainty; psychology is a first-person empirical science.
records.reference: Montague, M. (2017). “Intentionality: from Brentano to representationalism”. In Amy Kind (ed.), Philosophy of Mind in the Twentieth and Twenty-First Centuries: The History of the Philosophy of Mind, Volume 6. New York: Routledge.

records.line: Consciousness always forms a unity: we can only perceive one mental phenomenon at a specific point in time.
records.reference: Huemer, W. (n.d.). “Franz Brentano”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/brentano/

records.line: Inner observation (introspection) is impossible because it requires us to have two distinct acts in the same moment.
records.reference: Huemer, W. (n.d.). “Franz Brentano”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/brentano/

records.line: There cannot be unconscious mental phenomena, but only conscious mental phenomena of very low intensity.
records.reference: Huemer, W. (n.d.). “Franz Brentano”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/brentano/

records.line: There are three basic kinds of mental phenomena: presentations, judgments, and emotions.
records.reference: Huemer, W. (n.d.). “Franz Brentano”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/brentano/

records.line: Psychology contains the roots of aesthetics, logic, ethics, and politics, as ‘immediately evident’ judgments or emotions.
records.reference: Montague, M. (2017). “Intentionality: from Brentano to representationalism”. In Amy Kind (ed.), Philosophy of Mind in the Twentieth and Twenty-First Centuries: The History of the Philosophy of Mind, Volume 6. New York: Routledge.

records.line: Knowledge is an activity: a process of doubt/need-to-know, evaluation, and understanding/explaining.
records.reference: Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.

records.line: A full understanding of a concept must include the whole of the effects, which might have practical bearings, we conceive the concept to have.
records.reference: Peirce, C.S. (1878). “How to Make Our Ideas Clear”, Popular Science Monthly, 12(January): 286–302.

records.line: The properties of things (e.g. a diamond’s hardness) cannot consist in anything but in their ‘behaviour’ in an agent’s interactions with them.
records.reference: Peirce, C. S. (1903). The Collected Papers of Charles S. Peirce, 8 vols., C. Hartshorne, P. Weiss, and A. W. Burks (eds.). Cambridge: Harvard University Press. 1931–1966.

records.line: Knowledge is an instrument of survival in this world; we acquire it as participants, not as spectators.
records.reference: Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.

records.line: The only way to take inquiry to its limit is through the adoption of a scientific method; philosophical questions remain within the realm of scientific inquiry.
records.reference: Atkin, A. (n.d.). “Charles Sanders Peirce: Pragmatism”. The Internet Encyclopedia of Philosophy. https://iep.utm.edu/peircepr

records.line: The progress of science cannot go far except by collaboration; no mind can take one step without the aid of other minds.
records.reference: Peirce, C. S. (1903). The Collected Papers of Charles S. Peirce, 8 vols., C. Hartshorne, P. Weiss, and A. W. Burks (eds.). Cambridge: Harvard University Press. 1931–1966.

records.line: Scientific activity isn’t adding new certainties to existing ones; it’s replacing old explanations with better ones.
records.reference: Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.

records.line: The opinion which is fated to be ultimately agreed to by all who investigate is what we mean by truth, and the object represented in this opinion is the real.
records.reference: Peirce, C.S. (1878). “How to Make Our Ideas Clear”, Popular Science Monthly, 12(January): 286–302.

records.line: Some of our mental faculties are not only localized in our brains but also in the tools we use; I cannot have a discussion without my inkstand.
records.reference: Peirce, C. S. (1905). The Collected Papers of Charles S. Peirce, 8 vols., C. Hartshorne, P. Weiss, and A. W. Burks (eds.). Cambridge: Harvard University Press. 1931–1966.

records.line: ‘Abduction’: The surprising fact, C, is observed; but if A were true, C would be a matter of course; hence, there is reason to suspect that A is true.
records.reference: Peirce, C. S. (1903). The Collected Papers of Charles S. Peirce, 8 vols., C. Hartshorne, P. Weiss, and A. W. Burks (eds.). Cambridge: Harvard University Press. 1931–1966.

records.line: An ‘Icon’ is a sign which refers to the object that it denotes merely by virtue of characters of its own.
records.reference: Peirce, C.S. (1867). “On a New List of Categories”. Proceedings of the American Academy of Arts and Sciences, 7, pp. 287-298.

records.line: An ‘Index’ is a sign which refers to the object that it denotes by virtue of being affected by that object.
records.reference: Peirce, C.S. (1867). “On a New List of Categories”. Proceedings of the American Academy of Arts and Sciences, 7, pp. 287-298.

records.line: A ‘Symbol’ is a sign which is constituted a sign merely or mainly by the fact that it is used and understood as such, whether the habit is natural or conventional.
records.reference: Peirce, C.S. (1867). “On a New List of Categories”. Proceedings of the American Academy of Arts and Sciences, 7, pp. 287-298.

records.line: A proper name, when one meets with it for the first time, works like an Index; with repeated acquaintance, it becomes an Icon and then a Symbol.
records.reference: Peirce, C. S. (1903). 1992-1998: The Essential Peirce (EP), 2 vols., ed. Nathan Houser & Christian Kloesel (vol. 1) and Peirce Edition Project (vol. 2), Bloomington and Indianapolis: Indiana University Press.

records.line: Statements and theories that do all the jobs required of them (fit the known facts, generate insights/predictions, etc.) are true.
records.reference: Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.

records.line: If a statement cannot be disproved and a person benefits from believing in it, they are justified in doing so (e.g. religious beliefs).
records.reference: Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.

records.line: Before we invest much time or effort in seeking the meaning of anything, we should consider what practical difference it would make if we could find out.
records.reference: Pomerleau, W. P. (n.d.). “William James”. The Internet Encyclopedia of Philosophy. https://iep.utm.edu/james-o/

records.line: All thought is owned by some personal self.
records.reference: Pomerleau, W. P. (n.d.). “William James”. The Internet Encyclopedia of Philosophy. https://iep.utm.edu/james-o/

records.line: All thought is constantly in flux and never static; nevertheless, there is an ongoing continuity of thought for every thinker. (‘stream of consciousness’)
records.reference: Pomerleau, W. P. (n.d.). “William James”. The Internet Encyclopedia of Philosophy. https://iep.utm.edu/james-o/

records.line: New ideas are produced as accidental variations in the instable human brain and then selected by the person’s consciousness and the social environment.
records.reference: James, W. (1880). Great Men, Great Thoughts, and the Environment.; James, W. (1890). The Principles of Psychology.

records.line: Introspective observation is what we have to rely on first and foremost in psychology; it is difficult and fallible but this is true of all kinds of observation.
records.reference: James, W. (1890). The Principles of Psychology. Cambridge, MA: Harvard University Press. 1981.

records.line: The only safeguard is in the final consensus of our farther knowledge, later views correcting earlier ones, until the harmony of a consistent system is reached.
records.reference: James, W. (1890). The Principles of Psychology. Cambridge, MA: Harvard University Press. 1981.

records.line: Our only intelligible notion of an object in itself is that it should be an object for itself; our physical perceptions are effects on us of ‘psychical’ realities.
records.reference: Perry, R. 1935. Thought and Character of William James. Boston: Little, Brown and Co.

records.line: The notion of an all-encompassing Absolute Spirit undermines the values of human individuality, freedom, and responsibility.
records.reference: Pomerleau, W. P. (n.d.). “William James”. The Internet Encyclopedia of Philosophy. https://iep.utm.edu/james-o/

records.line: Four postulates of rationality are value-related, but unknowable, matters of belief: God, immortality, freedom, and moral duty.
records.reference: Pomerleau, W. P. (n.d.). “William James”. The Internet Encyclopedia of Philosophy. https://iep.utm.edu/james-o/

records.line: Realms of reality: objects of sense experience; science; philosophy & mathematics; illusion & prejudices; mythology & fiction; subjective opinions; madness.
records.reference: Pomerleau, W. P. (n.d.). “William James”. The Internet Encyclopedia of Philosophy. https://iep.utm.edu/james-o/

records.line: Determinism, though logically tenable, is pragmatically unacceptable: it trivializes evil and renders our natural reaction of regret pointless.
records.reference: Pomerleau, W. P. (n.d.). “William James”. The Internet Encyclopedia of Philosophy. https://iep.utm.edu/james-o/

records.line: Life can become worth living if we believe that it is and act on that belief, our commitment giving it meaning.
records.reference: Pomerleau, W. P. (n.d.). “William James”. The Internet Encyclopedia of Philosophy. https://iep.utm.edu/james-o/

records.line: Concepts arise from the equation of unequal things, overlooking what is individual and actual; nature is acquainted with no forms and no concepts.
records.reference: Nietzsche, F. (1873). “On Truth and Lies in a Nonmoral Sense”.

records.line: To be truthful means to employ the usual linguistic metaphors, to lie according to a convention which, after long usage, seems to be fixed, canonical, and binding.
records.reference: Nietzsche, F. (1873). “On Truth and Lies in a Nonmoral Sense”.

records.line: The success of science in offering an objective picture is an illusion since science builds on our language and our representations of time, space, etc.
records.reference: Nietzsche, F. (1873). “On Truth and Lies in a Nonmoral Sense”.

records.line: We cannot establish any fact ‘in itself’, there is only a “knowing” from a perspective; the more eyes we use, the more complete will be our “objectivity”.
records.reference: Nietzsche, F. (1901). The Will to Power.; Nietzsche, F. (1887). The Genealogy of Morals. Translator: H. B. Samuel. Dover. 2003.

records.line: We need to critique our moral values and fix their hierarchy, by considering their history and also using medical science.
records.reference: Nietzsche, F. (1887). The Genealogy of Morals. Translator: H. B. Samuel. Dover. 2003.

records.line: The desire for free will and ultimate responsibility involves nothing less than to be self-caused, to pull oneself up into existence by the hair, out of nothingness.
records.reference: Nietzsche, F. (1886). Beyond Good and Evil, trans. Walter Kaufmann, Random House. New York. 1966.

records.line: There is no neutral substratum, no “being” behind doing, working, becoming; “the doer” is a mere appanage to the action.
records.reference: Nietzsche, F. (1887). The Genealogy of Morals. Translator: H. B. Samuel. Dover. 2003.

records.line: The biological theory of “adaptation”, a second-class, mechanical, reactive activity, fails to realize the real essence of life, its activity, its will to power.
records.reference: Nietzsche, F. (1887). The Genealogy of Morals. Translator: H. B. Samuel. Dover. 2003.

records.line: To talk of intrinsic right and wrong is nonsensical; life essentially functions by injuring, oppressing, exploiting, and annihilating.
records.reference: Nietzsche, F. (1887). The Genealogy of Morals. Translator: H. B. Samuel. Dover. 2003.

records.line: Our success as a species was due to the perpetual elimination of the weak, incompetent, and stupid by the strong, competent, and clever.
records.reference: Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.

records.line: The origin of the “State” lies in conquerors and masters dominating populations, not in contracts.
records.reference: Nietzsche, F. (1887). The Genealogy of Morals. Translator: H. B. Samuel. Dover. 2003.

records.line: Language can be seen as the expression of the power of the masters; they seal every object and event with a sound, and thereby take possession of it.
records.reference: Nietzsche, F. (1887). The Genealogy of Morals. Translator: H. B. Samuel. Dover. 2003.

records.line: The Judeo-Christian tradition created a slave-morality based on resentment, pity, guilt, and self-denial, exalting the weak and stigmatizing the strong.
records.reference: Nietzsche, F. (1887). The Genealogy of Morals. Translator: H. B. Samuel. Dover. 2003.

records.line: The aristocratic morality acts independently (with the concepts “good” and “bad”); the slave-morality requires an external world with “evil” to react to.
records.reference: Nietzsche, F. (1887). The Genealogy of Morals. Translator: H. B. Samuel. Dover. 2003.

records.line: The obstructed and inverted cruelty of civilized humans invented the bad conscience so as to torture themselves with the thought of owing something to God.
records.reference: Nietzsche, F. (1887). The Genealogy of Morals. Translator: H. B. Samuel. Dover. 2003.

records.line: The bad conscience turns against the origin of the human race (original sin), Nature (its diabolization), or existence generally (Nihilism, Buddhism).
records.reference: Nietzsche, F. (1887). The Genealogy of Morals. Translator: H. B. Samuel. Dover. 2003.

records.line: The ascetic ideal has dominated all philosophy and science, because Truth was fixed as Being, as God, because Truth was not allowed to be a problem.
records.reference: Nietzsche, F. (1887). The Genealogy of Morals. Translator: H. B. Samuel. Dover. 2003.

records.line: When the belief in the God of the ascetic ideal is repudiated, the problem of the value of truth arises, even for science, atheism, and morality.
records.reference: Nietzsche, F. (1887). The Genealogy of Morals. Translator: H. B. Samuel. Dover. 2003.

records.line: We should embrace an ethics of life-assertion; “good” and “true” are things that are on the side of life and fulfilment, not self-restraint.
records.reference: Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.

records.line: Art is a saving enchantress, offering the sublime as the artistic subjugation of the awful, and the comic as the artistic delivery from the nausea of the absurd.
records.reference: Nietzsche, F. (1872). The Birth of Tragedy. Translator: W. A. Haussman. T.N. Foulis. 1910.
