records.line: We must become human beings who are new, unique, incomparable, who give themselves (non-“universal”) laws, who create themselves.
records.reference: Nietzsche, F. (1913). The Gay Science. Translator: J. Nauckhoff. Cambridge University Press. 2001.

records.line: Everything that has happened will happen again in cosmic epicycles of time; this can give motivation to live more fulfilling lives.
records.reference: Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.

records.line: The aim of morality is self-realization – pursuing to realize one’s ideal self in every situation.
records.reference: Candlish, S., & Basile, P. (n.d.). “Francis Herbert Bradley”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/bradley/

records.line: The importance of happiness as the point of morality can be retained but the means by which happiness is attained are also important.
records.reference: Candlish, S., & Basile, P. (n.d.). “Francis Herbert Bradley”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/bradley/

records.line: The concept of duties in morality can be retained if their obligatoriness arises from the nature of each duty rather than from some formal principle.
records.reference: Candlish, S., & Basile, P. (n.d.). “Francis Herbert Bradley”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/bradley/

records.line: Realization of the ideal self is unattainable through morality because morality depends on the existence of evil, but it could be possible in religion.
records.reference: Candlish, S., & Basile, P. (n.d.). “Francis Herbert Bradley”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/bradley/

records.line: The ideas by which we try to understand the universe (cause, motion, self, space, time, etc.) lead to contradictions in their implications.
records.reference: Candlish, S., & Basile, P. (n.d.). “Francis Herbert Bradley”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/bradley/

records.line: The notion of the thing-in-itself is self-contradictory; if we really know nothing about it, then we do not even know that it exists.
records.reference: Candlish, S., & Basile, P. (n.d.). “Francis Herbert Bradley”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/bradley/

records.line: If a relation were a further kind of real thing along with its terms, then a further relation would be required to relate it to its terms, and so on ad infinitum.
records.reference: Candlish, S., & Basile, P. (n.d.). “Francis Herbert Bradley”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/bradley/

records.line: Relations exist only in and through a whole which cannot in the end be resolved into relations and terms.
records.reference: Bradley, F. H. (1883). The principles of logic. Oxford University Press.

records.line: The impression of reality’s consisting of a multiplicity of related objects is a result of the separations imposed by thought.
records.reference: Candlish, S., & Basile, P. (n.d.). “Francis Herbert Bradley”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/bradley/

records.line: The final truth is identical with the whole of reality; it transcends thought and logical categories altogether, it is inexpressible.
records.reference: Candlish, S., & Basile, P. (n.d.). “Francis Herbert Bradley”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/bradley/

records.line: Reality is one; its contents are nothing but sentient experience, and it is a single and all-inclusive experience, embracing every partial diversity.
records.reference: Bradley, F. H. (1893). Appearance and reality. Swan Sonnenschein.

records.line: The Absolute articulates itself in a plurality of lesser sentient wholes, finite centres of experience, coordinated as parts of the overarching Reality.
records.reference: Candlish, S., & Basile, P. (n.d.). “Francis Herbert Bradley”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/bradley/

records.line: Reality itself admits of degrees: the more a phenomenon is a fragmentary aspect of the whole, the less real it is.
records.reference: Candlish, S., & Basile, P. (n.d.). “Francis Herbert Bradley”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/bradley/

records.line: The Absolute transcends good and evil, and religion – it is in a sense a Supreme Being, but not a personal God.
records.reference: Candlish, S., & Basile, P. (n.d.). “Francis Herbert Bradley”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/bradley/

records.line: Logic is independent of human thought; it is a normative science whose laws prescribe universally how one should think.
records.reference: Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.; Frege, G. (1893/1903/2009). Grundgesetze Der Arithmetik. Paderborn: Mentis.; Frege, G. (1897/1979). “Logic”. Hans Hermes, Friedrich Kambartel, and Friedrich Kaulbach (eds.), Posthumous Writings. Oxford: Basil

records.line: Mathematical truths are not empirical truths and numbers cannot be properties of aggregates of objects.
records.reference: Kusch, M. (n.d.). “Psychologism”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/psychologism/

records.line: Arithmetics can be derived from logic.
records.reference: Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.

records.line: Numbers can be construed as sets.
records.reference: Frege, G. (1979). Posthumous Writings, H. Hermes et al., eds., P. Long et al., trans. University of Chicago Press, Chicago

records.line: The ‘reference’ of an expression is the actual thing corresponding to it – its truth value if it is a proposition.
records.reference: Frege, G. (1892). “On sense and reference”.

records.line: The ‘sense’ of an expression is the ‘mode of presentation’ by which one conceives of the reference, whether or not it exists.
records.reference: Frege, G. (1892). “On sense and reference”.

records.line: Expressions with different senses can have the same reference.
records.reference: Frege, G. (1892). “On sense and reference”.

records.line: A thought is something for which the question of truth arises; it is the ‘sense’ of a declarative sentence.
records.reference: Frege, G. (1918). “The thought: A logical inquiry”.

records.line: Thoughts are immaterial and timeless – neither things of the outer world nor subjective ideas (they need no bearer); a third realm must be recognized.
records.reference: Frege, G. (1918). “The thought: A logical inquiry”.

records.line: Thoughts are apprehended by thinkers and bring about changes in the inner and outer world; thinkers do not create the thoughts.
records.reference: Frege, G. (1918). “The thought: A logical inquiry”.

records.line: Only in the context of a sentence do words have any meaning.
records.reference: Frege, G. (1884). The Foundations of Arithmetic: A Logico-Mathematical Enquiry Into the Concept of Number.

records.line: “I see blue” has the same content as “It is true that I see blue”; nothing is added to the thought by my ascribing to it the property of truth.
records.reference: Frege, G. (1918). “The thought: A logical inquiry”.

records.line: “Do I see the same colors as you do?” is an unanswerable, nonsensical question; color terms characterize sense-impressions belonging to one’s consciousness.
records.reference: Frege, G. (1918). “The thought: A logical inquiry”.

records.line: The principle of complete induction, which is not provable via analytical inferences, is a genuine synthetic a priori judgment.
records.reference: Murzi, M. (n.d.). “Jules Henri Poincaré (1854—1912)”. The Internet Encyclopedia of Philosophy. https://iep.utm.edu/poincare/

records.line: Arithmetic cannot be reduced to logic (which is analytic); it is a synthetic science whose objects are not independent from human thought.
records.reference: Murzi, M. (n.d.). “Jules Henri Poincaré (1854—1912)”. The Internet Encyclopedia of Philosophy. https://iep.utm.edu/poincare/

records.line: Mathematicians can use the methods of logic to check a proof, but they must use intuition to create a proof.
records.reference: Murzi, M. (n.d.). “Jules Henri Poincaré (1854—1912)”. The Internet Encyclopedia of Philosophy. https://iep.utm.edu/poincare/

records.line: The creative combinations of ideas are formed randomly in the subliminal ego and those that are harmonious become conscious.
records.reference: Poincaré, H. (1908). Science et Methode, Paris: Flammarion. Translation in 1913.

records.line: Although scientific theories (and geometries) deal with the same reality, each has its own language or syntax, which is chosen by convention.
records.reference: Murzi, M. (n.d.). “Jules Henri Poincaré (1854—1912)”. The Internet Encyclopedia of Philosophy. https://iep.utm.edu/poincare/

records.line: There is an objective, non-conventional criterion to judge the soundness of a scientific theory: the accuracy of its predictions.
records.reference: Murzi, M. (n.d.). “Jules Henri Poincaré (1854—1912)”. The Internet Encyclopedia of Philosophy. https://iep.utm.edu/poincare/

records.line: Laws are like smooth interpolated curves missing some of the data points; a scientific theory is not directly falsifiable by the experience alone.
records.reference: Murzi, M. (n.d.). “Jules Henri Poincaré (1854—1912)”. The Internet Encyclopedia of Philosophy. https://iep.utm.edu/poincare/

records.line: Hypotheses which have the maximum scope, common to all scientific theories, are the last to be changed.
records.reference: Murzi, M. (n.d.). “Jules Henri Poincaré (1854—1912)”. The Internet Encyclopedia of Philosophy. https://iep.utm.edu/poincare/

records.line: Seeing scientific theories abandoned one after the other, one may predict that the theories in fashion today will in a short time succumb in their turn.
records.reference: Poincaré, H. (1905). Science and Hypothesis. Dover reprint of 1905 translation.

records.line: Objects in scientific theories are merely names of the images we substituted for the real objects which Nature will hide forever from our eyes.
records.reference: Poincaré, H. (1905). Science and Hypothesis. Dover reprint of 1905 translation.

records.line: Relations between objects, expressed by equations in scientific theories, remain true in theory-changes; they are the only reality we can attain.
records.reference: Poincaré, H. (1905). Science and Hypothesis. Dover reprint of 1905 translation.

records.line: There is a dynamic unconscious part of the mind which exerts pressures and influences on what we do and say.
records.reference: Stevenson, L. (1974). Seven Theories of Human Nature. Oxford University Press.

records.line: The ‘unconscious’ is different from the ‘preconscious’ (some memories, facts, etc.) which can become conscious.
records.reference: Stevenson, L. (1974). Seven Theories of Human Nature. Oxford University Press.

records.line: The human mind has three structural systems: the ‘id’ (instincts), the ‘ego’ (the reconciler), and the ‘super-ego’ (conscience).
records.reference: Stevenson, L. (1974). Seven Theories of Human Nature. Oxford University Press.

records.line: All the “energy” in our minds comes from the instincts alone.
records.reference: Stevenson, L. (1974). Seven Theories of Human Nature. Oxford University Press.

records.line: The ‘Life’ instinct (Eros) contains erotic and self-preservative instincts; the ‘Death’ instinct (Thanatos) contains sadism, aggression, self-harm, etc.
records.reference: Stevenson, L. (1974). Seven Theories of Human Nature. Oxford University Press.

records.line: Our adult characters are determined by our early childhood experiences which have many sexual (body-pleasure-related) elements.
records.reference: Stevenson, L. (1974). Seven Theories of Human Nature. Oxford University Press.

records.line: The little boy has a desire for his mother and a fear of castration by his father (Oedipus complex); the little girl develops penis envy.
records.reference: Stevenson, L. (1974). Seven Theories of Human Nature. Oxford University Press.

records.line: When we repress our inner conflicts, they continue to exist in the unconscious and create neurotic symptoms.
records.reference: Stevenson, L. (1974). Seven Theories of Human Nature. Oxford University Press.

records.line: Neurosis can sometimes be treated with psychoanalysis: a process that brings back the repressed material into consciousness.
records.reference: Stevenson, L. (1974). Seven Theories of Human Nature. Oxford University Press.

records.line: Our whole civilized life might be neurotic because of the sacrifice of instinctual satisfaction.
records.reference: Stevenson, L. (1974). Seven Theories of Human Nature. Oxford University Press.

records.line: The teachings of religion are illusions, fulfillments of our oldest and strongest wishes; just as they are unverifiable, they are also irrefutable.
records.reference: Freud, S. (1927). The Future of an Illusion.

records.line: To use private judgement about religious arguments would be a wanton undertaking; such questions are too significant for that.
records.reference: Freud, S. (1927). The Future of an Illusion.

records.line: The God that many philosophers define is no more than an insubstantial shadow, no longer the mighty figure of religious teaching.
records.reference: Freud, S. (1927). The Future of an Illusion.

records.line: The truths contained in religion are so distorted and symbolically disguised that most people are incapable of recognizing them as truth.
records.reference: Freud, S. (1927). The Future of an Illusion.

records.line: Our mental apparatus was in fact developed in the effort to map the outside world, so must have a certain amount of expediency in its structure.
records.reference: Freud, S. (1927). The Future of an Illusion.

records.line: The individuals are the mortal vehicles of a possibly immortal substance (the germ-plasm), only the temporary holders of an estate which survives them.
records.reference: Freud, S. (1914). On narcissism.

records.line: Artists turn their fantasies and repressed unconscious desires into objects that the rest of us enjoy because we share those fantasies and desires.
records.reference: Eaton, M. M. (1988). Basic Issues In Aesthetics. Waveland Press.

records.line: A language (‘langue’) is the virtual system possessed by all those who form part of the same speech community.
records.reference: Joseph, J. E. (n.d.). “Ferdinand de Saussure”. Oxford Research Encyclopedia of Linguistics. https://oxfordre.com/linguistics/view/10.1093/acrefore/9780199384655.001.0001/acrefore-9780199384655-e-385.

records.line: ‘La parole’ is the utterances, the texts, that individuals produce and understand making use of the system that is ‘la langue’.
records.reference: Joseph, J. E. (n.d.). “Ferdinand de Saussure”. Oxford Research Encyclopedia of Linguistics. https://oxfordre.com/linguistics/view/10.1093/acrefore/9780199384655.001.0001/acrefore-9780199384655-e-385.

records.line: It is not things, but our conception of things, that are part of our language: schemas in the brain being evoked by certain combinations of sounds.
records.reference: Joseph, J. E. (n.d.). “Ferdinand de Saussure”. Oxford Research Encyclopedia of Linguistics. https://oxfordre.com/linguistics/view/10.1093/acrefore/9780199384655.001.0001/acrefore-9780199384655-e-385.

records.line: A language is a system of signs, each composed of two entirely mental values: ‘signifier’ (sound pattern) and ‘signified’ (concept).
records.reference: Joseph, J. E. (n.d.). “Ferdinand de Saussure”. Oxford Research Encyclopedia of Linguistics. https://oxfordre.com/linguistics/view/10.1093/acrefore/9780199384655.001.0001/acrefore-9780199384655-e-385.

records.line: Each signifier and each signified is a value produced by the difference between it and all the other signifiers and signifieds in the system.
records.reference: Joseph, J. E. (n.d.). “Ferdinand de Saussure”. Oxford Research Encyclopedia of Linguistics. https://oxfordre.com/linguistics/view/10.1093/acrefore/9780199384655.001.0001/acrefore-9780199384655-e-385.

records.line: The conjunction of signifier and signified is a sign which is of a positive order, and is concrete rather than abstract.
records.reference: Joseph, J. E. (n.d.). “Ferdinand de Saussure”. Oxford Research Encyclopedia of Linguistics. https://oxfordre.com/linguistics/view/10.1093/acrefore/9780199384655.001.0001/acrefore-9780199384655-e-385.

records.line: Every word or term or unit within the system is connected to an entourage of other units; no linguistic sign exists in isolation.
records.reference: Joseph, J. E. (n.d.). “Ferdinand de Saussure”. Oxford Research Encyclopedia of Linguistics. https://oxfordre.com/linguistics/view/10.1093/acrefore/9780199384655.001.0001/acrefore-9780199384655-e-385.

records.line: The link between a signifier and a signified is radically arbitrary; there is no logical connection between them.
records.reference: Joseph, J. E. (n.d.). “Ferdinand de Saussure”. Oxford Research Encyclopedia of Linguistics. https://oxfordre.com/linguistics/view/10.1093/acrefore/9780199384655.001.0001/acrefore-9780199384655-e-385.

records.line: Every element of a language is subject to change, to evolution, but no individual can change it.
records.reference: Joseph, J. E. (n.d.). “Ferdinand de Saussure”. Oxford Research Encyclopedia of Linguistics. https://oxfordre.com/linguistics/view/10.1093/acrefore/9780199384655.001.0001/acrefore-9780199384655-e-385.

records.line: Both the mutability and the immutability of language result from the arbitrariness of the sign.
records.reference: Joseph, J. E. (n.d.). “Ferdinand de Saussure”. Oxford Research Encyclopedia of Linguistics. https://oxfordre.com/linguistics/view/10.1093/acrefore/9780199384655.001.0001/acrefore-9780199384655-e-385.

records.line: Before language, human thought was an amorphous, indistinct, nebulous mass, a floating realm; and human sound was no different.
records.reference: Joseph, J. E. (n.d.). “Ferdinand de Saussure”. Oxford Research Encyclopedia of Linguistics. https://oxfordre.com/linguistics/view/10.1093/acrefore/9780199384655.001.0001/acrefore-9780199384655-e-385.

records.line: Language and writing are two distinct systems of signs; the second exists for the sole purpose of representing the first.
records.reference: Saussure, F. (1916). Course in General Linguistics.

records.line: The one thing that exists for sure is our consciousness; we should start from there to build our picture of reality.
records.reference: Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.

records.line: My awareness is of objects – not of myself having experiences of objects.
records.reference: Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.

records.line: We should bracket the questions about the independent existence of the objects that we perceive (‘epokhe’) and investigate them as objects of consciousness.
records.reference: Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.

records.line: We should bracket considerations concerning the contingent and accidental, and concentrate on the essences (‘eide’) of the objects and acts of consciousness.
records.reference: Smith, J. (n.d.). “Phenomenology”. The Internet Encyclopedia of Philosophy. https://iep.utm.edu/phenom/

records.line: We have a passive phenomenological process based on our interactions with things and a linguistically/conceptually mediated active one.
records.reference: Walsh, P. J. & Yoshimi, J. (2017). “Philosophy of Mind in the Phenomenological Tradition”. In Amy Kind (ed.), Philosophy of Mind in the Twentieth and Twenty-First Centuries: The History of the Philosophy of Mind, Volume 6. New York: Routledge.

records.line: Perception is founded on a pattern of counterfactual sensori-motor relationships between our current sensory experience and our immanent anticipations.
records.reference: Walsh, P. J. & Yoshimi, J. (2017). “Philosophy of Mind in the Phenomenological Tradition”. In Amy Kind (ed.), Philosophy of Mind in the Twentieth and Twenty-First Centuries: The History of the Philosophy of Mind, Volume 6. New York: Routledge.

records.line: The totality of my perceptual expectations forms a ‘horizon’ of understanding, which is updated when expectations are frustrated.
records.reference: Walsh, P. J. & Yoshimi, J. (2017). “Philosophy of Mind in the Phenomenological Tradition”. In Amy Kind (ed.), Philosophy of Mind in the Twentieth and Twenty-First Centuries: The History of the Philosophy of Mind, Volume 6. New York: Routledge.

records.line: Non-intentional sensations (‘hyle’) and the perceptual (interpretive) sense can vary independently: one can change while the other remains the same.
records.reference: Walsh, P. J. & Yoshimi, J. (2017). “Philosophy of Mind in the Phenomenological Tradition”. In Amy Kind (ed.), Philosophy of Mind in the Twentieth and Twenty-First Centuries: The History of the Philosophy of Mind, Volume 6. New York: Routledge.

records.line: The lived body is the locus of directly felt sensations and is capable of self-movement, opening a rich range of practical possibilities (“I can”s).
records.reference: Behnke, E. (n.d.). “Edmund Husserl: Phenomenology of Embodiment”. The Internet Encyclopedia of Philosophy. https://iep.utm.edu/husspemb/

records.line: Intersubjective (empathic) experience occurs when we consciously attribute intentional acts to other subjects.
records.reference: Beyer, C. (n.d.). “Edmund Husserl”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/husserl/

records.line: In intersubjective experience, I must presuppose that the objects forming my own world exist independently of my subjective perspective.
records.reference: Beyer, C. (n.d.). “Edmund Husserl”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/husserl/

records.line: Intersubjectivity is crucial in our constitution of ourselves as objectively existing subjects, other subjects, and the objective world.
records.reference: Beyer, C. (n.d.). “Edmund Husserl”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/husserl/

records.line: The present must contain a ‘retention’ of the immediate past and a ‘protention’ of the immediate future, or else it would disappear into nothing.
records.reference: Husserl, E. (1928). The Phenomenology of Internal Time-Consciousness.

records.line: The foundations of logic can be clarified with the help of psychology.
records.reference: Mohanty, J.N. (1977). “Husserl and Frege: A New Look at their Relationship”. Mohanty, J.N. (Ed.), Readings on Edmund Husserl’s Logical Investigations. Springer, Dordrecht.

records.line: Laws of logic are a priori, they are justified by apodictic self-evidence, and valid rather than probable; they are not psychological.
records.reference: Kusch, M. (n.d.). “Psychologism”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/psychologism/

records.line: Human beings should be explained from the perspective of evolution.
records.reference: Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.

records.line: Life is like a current passing from germ to germ through the medium of an organism which is like an excrescence.
records.reference: Bergson, H. (1907). Creative Evolution.

records.line: Our senses do not give us objective representations; they stimulate behavior for survival.
records.reference: Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.

records.line: Evolution is directed by a drive (‘élan vital’) towards greater individuality and complexity.
records.reference: Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.

records.line: Everything changes; the flow of time is fundamental to reality, to life.
records.reference: Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.

records.line: We have direct intuitive experience of time, independent of conceptual thinking and sensual input.
records.reference: Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.

records.line: We know we have free will because we have direct intuitive knowledge about our decisions to act.
records.reference: Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.

records.line: Our intellect artificially breaks up time and space into manageable units to handle them – very useful for everyday life, business, and science.
records.reference: Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.

records.line: The partitioning of the world does not show us reality; real time (‘duration’) and space are continuous and irreducible.
records.reference: Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.

records.line: The present contains nothing more than the past; without the survival of the past into the present there would be no duration, but only instantaneity.
records.reference: Bergson, H. (1903). An Introduction to Metaphysics.; Bergson, H. (1907). Creative Evolution.

records.line: We live in two worlds: our inner experience of the continuum, and our intellect’s partitioning.
records.reference: Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.

records.line: Knowledge is an adaptive human response to environing conditions, with practical instrumentality in the control of that interaction.
records.reference: Field, R. (n.d.). “John Dewey”. The Internet Encyclopedia of Philosophy. https://iep.utm.edu/dewey/

records.line: Hands and feet, apparatus and appliances are as much a part of thinking as changes in the brain: what matters is what they do, not what they are made of.
records.reference: Dewey, J. (1916). Essays in Experimental Logic. Chicago: University of Chicago Press.

records.line: Scientific knowledge is the most reliable and useful type of knowledge.
records.reference: Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.

records.line: ‘Warranted assertibility’ is a better way of capturing the function of both knowledge and truth insofar as both are goals of inquiry.
records.reference: Capps, J. (n.d.). “The Pragmatic Theory of Truth”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/truth-pragmatic/

records.line: There is no fundamental fact/value distinction; nature embraces all aspects of life, including values.
records.reference: Baldwin, T. (2001). Contemporary Philosophy. Oxford University Press.

records.line: The worse or evil is a rejected good: in deliberation and before choice no evil presents itself as evil – until it is rejected, it is a competing good.
records.reference: Dewey, J. (1922). “The good of activity”, Human Nature and Conduct: An Introduction to Social Psychology. New York: Modern Library.

records.line: The problem-solving approach of the scientific method should be the pattern for all other types of enquiry.
records.reference: Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.

records.line: The scientific method is a social activity because it requires criticism.
records.reference: Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.

records.line: The education of children should be based on the problem-solving approach (‘learning by doing’).
records.reference: Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.

records.line: A beautiful object is not art if there is no artist who made it; art is a product of a creative process involving intentional actions and desires.
records.reference: Eaton, M. M. (1988). Basic Issues In Aesthetics. Waveland Press.

records.line: Having an ‘experience’ is different from just being alive; an experience is a coherent and structured unit of features of our interactions with the world.
records.reference: Eaton, M. M. (1988). Basic Issues In Aesthetics. Waveland Press.

records.line: Artists provide us with ‘experiences’ by producing coherent and structured objects/events that bring together various aspects of disjoint perceptions.
records.reference: Eaton, M. M. (1988). Basic Issues In Aesthetics. Waveland Press.

records.line: A theory of physics is not an explanation; it is a system of mathematical propositions to represent as simply as possible a group of experimental laws.
records.reference: Duhem, P. (1906). The aim and structure of physical theory. Princeton, NJ: Princeton University Press. 1954.

records.line: The highest test of a theory is to ask it to indicate in advance things which the future alone will reveal.
records.reference: Duhem, P. (1906). The aim and structure of physical theory. Princeton, NJ: Princeton University Press. 1954.

records.line: When a theory is successful, it means that the relations established by our reason among abstract notions only correspond to the relations among things.
records.reference: Duhem, P. (1906). The aim and structure of physical theory. Princeton, NJ: Princeton University Press. 1954.

records.line: Scientific hypotheses only work given further auxiliary hypotheses, some of which are about the experimental equipment.
records.reference: Baldwin, T. (2001). Contemporary Philosophy. Oxford University Press.

records.line: When a prediction fails, the scientist may reconsider the theoretical postulates or check the equipment.
records.reference: Baldwin, T. (2001). Contemporary Philosophy. Oxford University Press.

records.line: Observations and experiments cannot be by themselves decisive in refuting scientific hypotheses.
records.reference: Baldwin, T. (2001). Contemporary Philosophy. Oxford University Press.

records.line: Scientists choose among competing theories by eliminative reasoning; this can only be reliable if there is the true one among the alternatives.
records.reference: Seifert, V. (2023). Chemistry’s Metaphysics (Elements in Metaphysics). Cambridge: Cambridge University Press.

records.line: What we can ever have is an imperfect and provisional theory with innumerable groupings, hesitations, and repentances.
records.reference: Duhem, P. (1906). The aim and structure of physical theory. Princeton, NJ: Princeton University Press. 1954.

records.line: It is hard for scientists from different schools or time periods to understand each other and have meaningful discussions; they speak different languages.
records.reference: Duhem, P. (1906). The aim and structure of physical theory. Princeton, NJ: Princeton University Press. 1954.

records.line: Metaphysical/religious doctrines touch on objective reality, whereas physical theories use mathematical signs stripped of all objective existence.
records.reference: Duhem, P. (1906). The aim and structure of physical theory. Princeton, NJ: Princeton University Press. 1954.

records.line: Since metaphysical/religious doctrines and physical theories do not have any common term, they can neither contradict nor agree with each other.
records.reference: Duhem, P. (1906). The aim and structure of physical theory. Princeton, NJ: Princeton University Press. 1954.

records.line: Knowledge is composed of instances of awareness that in themselves do not contain the prerequisites for knowledge (e.g. concepts, universals, essences).
records.reference: Saatkamp, H., & Coleman, M. (n.d.). “George Santayana”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/santayana/

records.line: Philosophy must begin in the middle of things, in action itself, where there is an instinctive and arational belief in the natural world: ‘animal faith’.
records.reference: Saatkamp, H., & Coleman, M. (n.d.). “George Santayana”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/santayana/

records.line: Ancient conditions of life on Earth determined which ‘psyches’ (natural structures of physical bodies) should arise and prosper.
records.reference: Saatkamp, H., & Coleman, M. (n.d.). “George Santayana”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/santayana/

records.line: There are certain inevitable beliefs; they are inescapable given nature and our physical history (e.g. belief in external objects, inductive reasoning).
records.reference: Saatkamp, H., & Coleman, M. (n.d.). “George Santayana”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/santayana/

records.line: The four realms of being are ‘essence’, ‘matter’, ‘truth’, and ‘spirit’; these are the qualities of reality and they are irreducibly different.
records.reference: Flamm, M. C. (n.d.). “George Santayana”. The Internet Encyclopedia of Philosophy. https://iep.utm.edu/santayan/

records.line: An ‘essence’ is a universal, an object of thought, not a material force; ‘matter’ remains as the origin of existence and the arena of action.
records.reference: Saatkamp, H., & Coleman, M. (n.d.). “George Santayana”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/santayana/

records.line: All conscious beings must ascertain belief about ‘truth’ based on the success of actions that sustain life and permit periods of delight and joy.
records.reference: Saatkamp, H., & Coleman, M. (n.d.). “George Santayana”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/santayana/

records.line: The nature of truth is correspondence with what is but the test of truth is pragmatic because of the limits of our nature.
records.reference: Saatkamp, H., & Coleman, M. (n.d.). “George Santayana”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/santayana/

records.line: ‘Spirit’, or consciousness, is momentary, fleeting, and depends on our physical bodies; it is a result and not a cause, a comment and not an agent.
records.reference: Saatkamp, H., & Coleman, M. (n.d.). “George Santayana”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/santayana/

records.line: Ethical statements cannot be true or false like scientific statements; they are only expressions of preferences we feel.
records.reference: Nath, R. (n.d.). “Bertrand Russell: Ethics”. The Internet Encyclopedia of Philosophy. https://iep.utm.edu/russ-eth/

records.line: There is no one good for all, or even for an individual; it depends on our nature and the circumstances, and in different times and environments.
records.reference: Saatkamp, H., & Coleman, M. (n.d.). “George Santayana”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/santayana/

records.line: Self-knowledge is the distinguishing moral mark; the extent to which one knows one’s interests will determine whether one can achieve a good life.
records.reference: Saatkamp, H., & Coleman, M. (n.d.). “George Santayana”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/santayana/

records.line: While science may investigate the nature of the universe, only philosophy investigates its ‘ultimate nature’.
records.reference: Thomas, E. (n.d.). “John McTaggart Ellis McTaggart”. The Internet Encyclopedia of Philosophy. https://iep.utm.edu/mctaggar/

records.line: The universe contains multiple substances, dissimilar to each other; they admit of exclusive, sufficient descriptions.
records.reference: Thomas, E. (n.d.). “John McTaggart Ellis McTaggart”. The Internet Encyclopedia of Philosophy. https://iep.utm.edu/mctaggar/

records.line: Substances are infinitely divisible into parts unlike their wholes which are also substances; ‘simple’ partless substances are not possible.
records.reference: Thomas, E. (n.d.). “John McTaggart Ellis McTaggart”. The Internet Encyclopedia of Philosophy. https://iep.utm.edu/mctaggar/

records.line: Because any two substances compose a further compound substance, the universe is a single substance composed of all other substances.
records.reference: Thomas, E. (n.d.). “John McTaggart Ellis McTaggart”. The Internet Encyclopedia of Philosophy. https://iep.utm.edu/mctaggar/

records.line: Instead of deducing a sufficient description of a substance in virtue of its parts, we should be deducing descriptions of the parts in virtue of the whole.
records.reference: Thomas, E. (n.d.). “John McTaggart Ellis McTaggart”. The Internet Encyclopedia of Philosophy. https://iep.utm.edu/mctaggar/

records.line: The only way the nature of substance could comply with whole-to-part descriptions is if substance is spirit, not matter.
records.reference: Thomas, E. (n.d.). “John McTaggart Ellis McTaggart”. The Internet Encyclopedia of Philosophy. https://iep.utm.edu/mctaggar/

records.line: The primary parts of the universe are selves, and the secondary parts their perceptions which are differentiated to infinity.
records.reference: Thomas, E. (n.d.). “John McTaggart Ellis McTaggart”. The Internet Encyclopedia of Philosophy. https://iep.utm.edu/mctaggar/

records.line: The reality of time requires the temporary (not permanent) properties of being past, present, and future.
records.reference: McTaggart, J. E. (1908). “The unreality of time”. Mind: A Quarterly Review of Psychology and Philosophy, 17, 457-473.

records.line: Past, present, and future are incompatible determinations; every event must be one or the other, but no event can be more than one.
records.reference: McTaggart, J. E. (1908). “The unreality of time”. Mind: A Quarterly Review of Psychology and Philosophy, 17, 457-473.

records.line: Every event has all three characteristics of being past, present, and future; if it is present, it has been future and will be past, etc.
records.reference: McTaggart, J. E. (1908). “The unreality of time”. Mind: A Quarterly Review of Psychology and Philosophy, 17, 457-473.

records.line: Time cannot exist; it is not real.
records.reference: McTaggart, J. E. (1908). “The unreality of time”. Mind: A Quarterly Review of Psychology and Philosophy, 17, 457-473.

records.line: All but only lived human experience, taking place concretely and without reduction, is real.
records.reference: Kemp, G. (n.d.). “Croce’s Aesthetics”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/croce-aesthetics/

records.line: We cannot determine the boundary between the present and the past; history represents the only all-encompassing account of reality.
records.reference: Kemp, G. (n.d.). “Croce’s Aesthetics”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/croce-aesthetics/

records.line: Mental activity is either theoretic (aesthetic or logic) or practical (economic or ethical).
records.reference: Kemp, G. (n.d.). “Croce’s Aesthetics”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/croce-aesthetics/

records.line: The aesthetic deals in particulars (individuals or intuitions) whereas logic deals in concepts and relations, or universals.
records.reference: Kemp, G. (n.d.). “Croce’s Aesthetics”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/croce-aesthetics/

records.line: The intellect presupposes the intuitive (aesthetic) mode but the intuitive mode does not presuppose the intellect and concepts.
records.reference: Kemp, G. (n.d.). “Croce’s Aesthetics”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/croce-aesthetics/

records.line: The intuition is an organic whole; to analyze it into atoms is always a false abstraction.
records.reference: Kemp, G. (n.d.). “Croce’s Aesthetics”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/croce-aesthetics/

records.line: Science and mathematics use pseudo-concepts postulated for convenience; ‘empirical laws’ are not fit subjects for truth and knowledge.
records.reference: Kemp, G. (n.d.). “Croce’s Aesthetics”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/croce-aesthetics/

records.line: Art is everywhere, and the difference between ordinary intuition and that of “works of art” is only a quantitative difference.
records.reference: Kemp, G. (n.d.). “Croce’s Aesthetics”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/croce-aesthetics/

records.line: To intuit is to express; works of art are mental objects, and spectators (and critics) are actually recreating them in their minds.
records.reference: Kemp, G. (n.d.). “Croce’s Aesthetics”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/croce-aesthetics/

records.line: The technical, physical manifestation (the painting of pictures, etc.) is only contingently related to the work of art, that is, to the expressed intuition.
records.reference: Kemp, G. (n.d.). “Croce’s Aesthetics”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/croce-aesthetics/

records.line: Expression and beauty are not two concepts: beauty is nothing but the precision of the image, an appropriate expression is also beautiful.
records.reference: Croce, B. (1912). The Essence of Aesthetic. Translated by Douglas Ainslie. London: Heinemann. 1921.

records.line: At the aesthetic level, there is no identification of content independently of the form, and none of form independently of content.
records.reference: Kemp, G. (n.d.). “Croce’s Aesthetics”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/croce-aesthetics/

records.line: Aesthetic pleasures and pains exist, but they are the practical echo of aesthetic value and disvalue, not the aim of art.
records.reference: Kemp, G. (n.d.). “Croce’s Aesthetics”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/croce-aesthetics/

records.line: In having an intuition, one succeeds entirely insofar as aesthetic value is concerned; there cannot be a real question of a ‘standard’ of beauty.
records.reference: Kemp, G. (n.d.). “Croce’s Aesthetics”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/croce-aesthetics/

records.line: The variability in critical verdicts in art is largely due to variances in the psychological conditions and the physical circumstances of spectators.
records.reference: Kemp, G. (n.d.). “Croce’s Aesthetics”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/croce-aesthetics/

records.line: Works of art only produce effects upon souls prepared to receive them; natural signs do not exist, all are conventional – historically conditioned.
records.reference: Croce, B. (1909). Aesthetic: As science of expression and general linguistic. (D. Ainslie, Trans.) New York, NY: Noonday. 1922.

records.line: Drawing, sculpting, writing of music and so on are just as much ‘language’ as poetry, and all language is poetic.
records.reference: Kemp, G. (n.d.). “Croce’s Aesthetics”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/croce-aesthetics/

records.line: Expression is an indivisible whole: noun and verb are abstractions made by us, destroying the sole linguistic reality, which is the sentence.
records.reference: Kemp, G. (n.d.). “Croce’s Aesthetics”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/croce-aesthetics/

records.line: What matters aesthetically cannot be the content of a work of art; two works with the same content can be aesthetically very different.
records.reference: Eaton, M. M. (1988). Basic Issues In Aesthetics. Waveland Press.

records.line: The ‘significant form’ composed of formal qualities (lines and colors) that elicit emotional response is the most important element in works of art.
records.reference: Eaton, M. M. (1988). Basic Issues In Aesthetics. Waveland Press.

records.line: To appreciate a work of art we need bring with us nothing from life, no knowledge of its ideas and affairs, no familiarity with its emotions.
records.reference: Bell, C. (1914). Art. Chatto & Windus.

records.line: Art transports us from the world of human activity to a world of aesthetic exaltation; for a moment we are lifted above the stream of life.
records.reference: Bell, C. (1914). Art. Chatto & Windus.

records.line: The law is created by acts of human beings; it is independent of morality and similar norm systems based on the “will of nature” or “pure reason”.
records.reference: Kelsen, H. (1960/1967). Pure Theory of Law, M. Knight (trans.), Berkeley: University of California Press.

records.line: The law is basically a scheme of interpretation: we attach a legal-normative meaning to certain actions and events in the world.
records.reference: Marmor, A. (n.d.). “The Pure Theory of Law”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/lawphil-theory/

records.line: An act or an event gains its legal-normative meaning by another legal norm that confers this normative meaning on it.
records.reference: Marmor, A. (n.d.). “The Pure Theory of Law”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/lawphil-theory/

records.line: To get an “ought” type of conclusion from a set of “is” premises, one must point to some “ought” premise in the background.
records.reference: Marmor, A. (n.d.). “The Pure Theory of Law”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/lawphil-theory/

records.line: In every legal system, hierarchical derivations of validity must come to an end at a ‘basic norm’ that is presupposed to be legally valid.
records.reference: Marmor, A. (n.d.). “The Pure Theory of Law”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/lawphil-theory/

records.line: Every two norms that ultimately derive their validity from one basic norm belong to the same legal system.
records.reference: Marmor, A. (n.d.). “The Pure Theory of Law”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/lawphil-theory/

records.line: A norm can only be legally valid if it belongs to a system that is efficacious, i.e. by and large actually practiced by a certain population.
records.reference: Marmor, A. (n.d.). “The Pure Theory of Law”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/lawphil-theory/

records.line: A basic norm is legally valid if and only if it is actually followed in a given population; a successful revolution can change its content.
records.reference: Marmor, A. (n.d.). “The Pure Theory of Law”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/lawphil-theory/

records.line: Every type of “ought”, be it religious, moral or legal, must presuppose a certain point of view which is constituted by a basic norm.
records.reference: Marmor, A. (n.d.). “The Pure Theory of Law”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/lawphil-theory/

records.line: We are more certain of the truth of everyday things (‘common sense’) than we are of the general grounds for doubt introduced by sceptics.
records.reference: Baldwin, T. (2001). Contemporary Philosophy. Oxford University Press.

records.line: We can regard the unbelievable conclusions of sceptical arguments as reasons for rejecting their premises.
records.reference: Baldwin, T. (2001). Contemporary Philosophy. Oxford University Press.

records.line: To have a sensation is already to be outside the circle of our own ideas and sensations; it is to know something which is not a part of my experience.
records.reference: Moore, G. E. (1903). “The refutation of idealism”. Mind 12 (48):433-453.

records.line: ‘Sense-data’ (colors, sounds, etc. of which we are very often directly conscious) stand in a representational relation to physical objects.
records.reference: Hatfield, G. (n.d.). “Sense Data”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/sense-data/

records.line: One can understand, and know the truth of, various ‘common sense’ propositions even if one cannot give the analysis of them.
records.reference: Beaney, M., & Chapman, S. (n.d.). “Susan Stebbing”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/stebbing/

records.line: One could not judge the correctness of an analysis of a proposition if one did not understand the proposition in the first place.
records.reference: Beaney, M., & Chapman, S. (n.d.). “Susan Stebbing”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/stebbing/

records.line: Moral concepts (e.g. goodness) are neither reducible to nor derivable from non-moral (scientific or metaphysical) judgements. (‘naturalistic fallacy’)
records.reference: Hurka, T. (n.d.). “Moore’s Moral Philosophy”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/moore-moral/

records.line: Morality presupposes the existence of an irreducible domain of distinctive and objective facts about the intrinsic value of different things.
records.reference: Baldwin, T. (2001). Contemporary Philosophy. Oxford University Press.

records.line: Ethical knowledge rests on a capacity for an intuitive grasp of fundamental ethical truths.
records.reference: Baldwin, T. (n.d.). “George Edward Moore”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/moore/

records.line: What is right is always what most promotes the good.
records.reference: Hurka, T. (n.d.). “Moore’s Moral Philosophy”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/moore-moral/

records.line: The idea of ‘my own good’, what is ‘good for me’, makes no sense: nothing is added by saying that the pleasure constitutes my good, or is good for me.
records.reference: Crisp, R. (n.d.). “Well-being”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/well-being/

records.line: Ethical egoism (“Each person’s happiness is the sole good”) is contradictory; a number of different things cannot each of them be the sole good.
records.reference: Moore, G. E. (1903). Principia Ethica. Cambridge University Press.

records.line: Mathematical and logical truths must be true unconditionally; 2 + 2 equals 4 even if there are no intelligences or minds to think it.
records.reference: Carey, R. (n.d.). “Bertrand Russell: Metaphysics”. The Internet Encyclopedia of Philosophy. https://iep.utm.edu/russ-met/

records.line: Mathematics can be derived from logic.
records.reference: Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.

records.line: Naïve set theory is paradoxical: the set of all sets that are not members of themselves is and is not a member of itself.
records.reference: Irvine, A. D. (n.d.). “Russell’s Paradox”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/russell-paradox/

records.line: Epistemology is distinct from psychology; a belief is true when there is a corresponding fact, and is false when there is no corresponding fact.
records.reference: Russell, B. (1912). The problems of philosophy. Home University Library.

records.line: A true belief is not knowledge when it is deduced from a false belief or a fallacious process of reasoning.
records.reference: Russell, B. (1912). The problems of philosophy. Home University Library.

records.line: All words that possess meaning do so by denoting complex or simple, abstract or concrete objects.
records.reference: Carey, R. (n.d.). “Bertrand Russell: Metaphysics”. The Internet Encyclopedia of Philosophy. https://iep.utm.edu/russ-met/

records.line: There are particulars (objects, etc.) existing in only one place at a time, and universals (qualities, relations, etc.) existing in more than one place at a time.
records.reference: Carey, R. (n.d.). “Bertrand Russell: Metaphysics”. The Internet Encyclopedia of Philosophy. https://iep.utm.edu/russ-met/

records.line: We infer the existence of physical objects from what we are directly acquainted with, our sense data.
records.reference: Carey, R. (n.d.). “Bertrand Russell: Metaphysics”. The Internet Encyclopedia of Philosophy. https://iep.utm.edu/russ-met/

records.line: Transitive and asymmetrical relations are irreducible to properties of terms, as the quality of imposing order among terms is lost.
records.reference: Carey, R. (n.d.). “Bertrand Russell: Metaphysics”. The Internet Encyclopedia of Philosophy. https://iep.utm.edu/russ-met/

records.line: Since relations are external to terms, reality is composed of diverse entities, bound but not dissolved into wholes by external relations.
records.reference: Carey, R. (n.d.). “Bertrand Russell: Metaphysics”. The Internet Encyclopedia of Philosophy. https://iep.utm.edu/russ-met/

records.line: All that physics gives us is certain equations giving abstract properties of their changes, but as to what it is that changes, physics is silent.
records.reference: Russell, B. (1959). My Philosophical Development, London: Routledge. 1995.

records.line: The world consists of a complex of logical atoms and their properties and relations, forming the atomic facts which, in turn, form logically complex objects.
records.reference: Irvine, A. D. (n.d.). “Bertrand Russell”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/russell/

records.line: Inferred entities (e.g. physical objects) are in fact logical constructions (fictions) formed from the sense data (improved by science).
records.reference: Irvine, A. D. (n.d.). “Bertrand Russell”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/russell/

records.line: Analysis results in a logically perfect language consisting only of words that denote sense data, universals, and logical constants (words like “or” and “not”).
records.reference: Carey, R. (n.d.). “Bertrand Russell: Metaphysics”. The Internet Encyclopedia of Philosophy. https://iep.utm.edu/russ-met/

records.line: A sentence can be meaningful (and true/false) even if a description in it fails to describe anything, and ordinary proper names are disguised descriptions.
records.reference: Baldwin, T. (2001). Contemporary Philosophy. Oxford University Press.

records.line: The world consists of just one neutral type of substance which is neither exclusively mental nor exclusively physical.
records.reference: Irvine, A. D. (n.d.). “Bertrand Russell”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/russell/

records.line: The sensation we have when we see a patch of colour simply is that patch of colour; and since sensations are not intentional, they are non-mental.
records.reference: Stubenberg, L. (n.d.). “Neutral Monism”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/neutral-monism/

records.line: Things are bundles of co-existing properties; there is not a substratum as an unknowable ‘this’ distinct from and underlying its properties.
records.reference: Carey, R. (n.d.). “Bertrand Russell: Metaphysics”. The Internet Encyclopedia of Philosophy. https://iep.utm.edu/russ-met/

records.line: Empiricism uses principles of reason that cannot be proved by experience; induction involves a non-empirical logical principle, known a priori.
records.reference: Carey, R. (n.d.). “Bertrand Russell: Metaphysics”. The Internet Encyclopedia of Philosophy. https://iep.utm.edu/russ-met/

records.line: The notion of causation is a relic of a bygone age; scientific formulas make no difference between past and future and no reference to causes and effects.
records.reference: Russell, B. (1913). “On the Notion of Cause”. Proceedings of the Aristotelian Society 13:1-26.

records.line: Vagueness is a matter of representation (language); it is a mistake to attribute vagueness to the world simply because our language is vague.
records.reference: Russell, B. (1923). “Vagueness”. Australasian Journal of Philosophy and Psychology, 1.

records.line: Desires, emotions or passions are the only possible causes of action; reason is not a cause of action but only a regulator.
records.reference: Nath, R. (n.d.). “Bertrand Russell: Ethics”. The Internet Encyclopedia of Philosophy. https://iep.utm.edu/russ-eth/

records.line: Ethical knowledge is not possible and ethical statements are not fact-stating; they are optative or desire expressing.
records.reference: Nath, R. (n.d.). “Bertrand Russell: Ethics”. The Internet Encyclopedia of Philosophy. https://iep.utm.edu/russ-eth/

records.line: A great deal of harm is caused by the belief in the virtuousness of work; the road to happiness lies in an organized diminution of work.
records.reference: Russell, B. (1932). “In Praise of Idleness”.

records.line: Science has nothing to say about the intrinsic nature of matter; our knowledge in physics consists solely of readings of pointers on instruments.
records.reference: Eddington, A. (1928). The Nature of the Physical World. Cambridge University Press.

records.line: The sort of thing we know in science is structure of the kind defined and investigated in the mathematical theory of groups.
records.reference: Eddington, A. (1939). The Philosophy of Physical Science. Cambridge University Press.

records.line: Mind is the first and most direct thing in our experience, and all else is remote inference.
records.reference: Eddington, A. (1928). The Nature of the Physical World. Cambridge University Press.

records.line: It seems rather silly to prefer to postulate something of a “concrete” nature inconsistent with thought, and then to wonder where thought comes from.
records.reference: Eddington, A. (1928). The Nature of the Physical World. Cambridge University Press.

records.line: Consciousness is not sharply defined, but fades into subconsciousness; and beyond that we must postulate something continuous with our mental nature.
records.reference: Eddington, A. (1928). The Nature of the Physical World. Cambridge University Press.

records.line: The universe is of the nature of a thought or sensation in a universal Mind; the stuff of the world is mind-stuff.
records.reference: Eddington, A. (1928). The Nature of the Physical World. Cambridge University Press.

records.line: We cannot even talk about the part of reality beyond our experience – that talk becomes nonsense.
records.reference: Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.

records.line: The sense of the world must lie outside the world – in the world everything is as it is, and everything happens as it does happen, accidentally: in it no value exists.
records.reference: Wittgenstein, L. (1921). Tractatus Logico-Philosophicus.

records.line: Our understanding of the world is based on language; the limits of language define the limits of our conception.
records.reference: Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.

records.line: Language is like a picture, describing reality by having the same logical form with it.
records.reference: Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.

records.line: Language is a multifunctional tool; meaning depends on the context and the function being served (describing, ordering, asking, etc.).
records.reference: Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.

records.line: The meaning(s) of a word lacks essences and boundaries; it is a network of similarities, ‘family resemblances’, overlapping and criss-crossing.
records.reference: Wittgenstein, L. (1953). Philosophical Investigations. Oxford: Blackwell.

records.line: Language is a public, social thing, getting its meanings from rule-based ‘language-games’; there can be no private language.
records.reference: Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.

records.line: Much of philosophy consist of conceptual confusions due to misuse of language; the philosopher’s job should be to eliminate them by an analysis of language.
records.reference: Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.
records.keywords: ideal language philosophy, ordinary language philosophy

records.line: Since one cannot be mistaken or in doubt about one’s own sensations, one cannot really say that one knows of one’s own sensations either.
records.reference: Schroeder, S. (2017). “Wittgenstein and his legacy”. In Amy Kind (ed.), Philosophy of Mind in the Twentieth and Twenty-First Centuries: The History of the Philosophy of Mind, Volume 6. New York: Routledge.

records.line: Mental vocabulary is not descriptive: the verbal expression of pain does not describe the pain, it is a learned pain-behavior which replaces crying.
records.reference: Wittgenstein, L. (1953). Philosophical Investigations. Oxford: Blackwell.

records.line: What I think is what I can declare to be my thought and what can manifest itself in my behaviour – not the words/images going through my mind.
records.reference: Schroeder, S. (2017). “Wittgenstein and his legacy”. In Amy Kind (ed.), Philosophy of Mind in the Twentieth and Twenty-First Centuries: The History of the Philosophy of Mind, Volume 6. New York: Routledge.

records.line: Understanding cannot be identified with a state or a process in the mind; it is a capacity.
records.reference: Wittgenstein, L. (1953). Philosophical Investigations. Oxford: Blackwell.

records.line: Words like ‘voluntary’ or ‘willing’ do not stand for some distinctive mental occurrence that must precede or accompany a movement for it to be voluntary.
records.reference: Schroeder, S. (2017). “Wittgenstein and his legacy”. In Amy Kind (ed.), Philosophy of Mind in the Twentieth and Twenty-First Centuries: The History of the Philosophy of Mind, Volume 6. New York: Routledge.

records.line: Our common-sense understanding provides the context within which doubts are possible; scepticism is self-undermining.
records.reference: Baldwin, T. (2001). Contemporary Philosophy. Oxford University Press.

records.line: Philosophy has nothing to contribute at the level of empirical problems; they are to be solved by empirical methods.
records.reference: Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.

records.line: Objective truth is not the privileged achievement of any one language-game, such as the natural sciences; different language-games capture different aspects.
records.reference: Baldwin, T. (2001). Contemporary Philosophy. Oxford University Press.

records.line: The whole theory of particulars and universals is due to mistaking for a fundamental characteristic of reality, what is merely a characteristic of language.
records.reference: Ramsey, F. (1925). “Universals”. Mind, XXXIV: 401–17.

records.line: The subject-predicate distinction isn’t a semantic or representational distinction, but rather a tool to communicate the focus of a discussion.
records.reference: MacBride, F., et al. (n.d.). “Frank Ramsey”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/ramsey/

records.line: To say “It is true that [proposition]” means no more than to assert the proposition itself; this usage is for emphasis or stylistic reasons, or for indication.
records.reference: Ramsey, F. (1978). Foundations: Essays in Philosophy, Logic, Mathematics and Economics. London and Henley: Routledge and Kegan Paul.

records.line: The verb “is true” gives rise to artificial problems as to the nature of truth, which disappear when they are expressed in logical symbolism.
records.reference: Ramsey, F. (1991). “The Nature of Truth”. N. Rescher & U. Majer (Eds.), On Truth: Original Manuscript Materials (1927-1929) from the Ramsey Collection at the University of Pittsburgh. Dordrecht: Kluwer Academic Publishers.

records.line: A belief is true if it is a belief that p, and p.
records.reference: Ramsey, F. (1991). “The Nature of Truth”. N. Rescher & U. Majer (Eds.), On Truth: Original Manuscript Materials (1927-1929) from the Ramsey Collection at the University of Pittsburgh. Dordrecht: Kluwer Academic Publishers.

records.line: The meaning of a sentence is to be determined by reference to the actions to which asserting it would lead or by its possible causes and effects.
records.reference: Ramsey, F. (1927). “Facts and Propositions”. Proceedings of the Aristotelian Society (Supplementary Volume), 7.

records.line: In non-linguistic animals, beliefs are dispositions to act; e.g. a chicken abstaining from eating a sort of caterpillar based on unpleasant experiences.
records.reference: MacBride, F., et al. (n.d.). “Frank Ramsey”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/ramsey/

records.line: A belief is knowledge if it is true, certain, and obtained by a reliable process.
records.reference: Goldman, A., & Beddor, B. (n.d.). “Reliabilist Epistemology”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/reliabilism/

records.line: Inductive arguments are justified insofar as the world is so constituted that they lead on the whole to true opinions.
records.reference: Ramsey, F. (1929). “Truth and Probability”. R. B. Braithwaite (Ed.), The Foundations of Mathematics and Other Logical Essays. London: Kegan Paul, Trench, Trubner, & Co. 1931.

records.line: If two people are arguing “If p, will q?” and are both in doubt about p, they are fixing their degrees of belief in q given p.
records.reference: Ramsey, F. (1929). “General Propositions and Causality”. R. B. Braithwaite (Ed.), The Foundations of Mathematics and Other Logical Essays. London: Kegan Paul, Trench, Trubner, & Co. 1931.

records.line: Laws of nature are the axioms and theorems in the simplest axiomatization of all of our knowledge.
records.reference: MacBride, F., et al. (n.d.). “Frank Ramsey”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/ramsey/

records.line: Laws (‘variable hypotheticals’) are not propositions or judgments but rules for judging ‘If I meet a ϕ, I shall regard it as a ψ’.
records.reference: MacBride, F., et al. (n.d.). “Frank Ramsey”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/ramsey/

records.line: Definitions for theoretical terms hinder scientific development because they require conceptual decisions based on fiat.
records.reference: MacBride, F., et al. (n.d.). “Frank Ramsey”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/ramsey/

records.line: A theory’s observational implications can be listed relationally by replacing the non-observable theoretical terms with existentially quantified predicate variables.
records.reference: MacBride, F., et al. (n.d.). “Frank Ramsey”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/ramsey/; Ladyman, J. (n.d.). “Structural Realism”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/structural-realism/

records.line: The chief danger to philosophy is scholasticism: treating what is vague as if it were precise and trying to fit it into an exact logical category.
records.reference: Ramsey, F. (1925). “The Foundations of Mathematics”. Proceedings of the London Mathematical Society (Series 2), 25, 338-384.

records.line: We are part of the world we investigate; the central problem is not knowledge but the meaning of Being, which Western thinking has long trivialized or ignored.
records.reference: Heidegger, M. (1927). Being and Time. Translated by John Macquarrie & Edward Robinson. Blackwell Publishers. 1962.

records.line: Dismantling (‘Destruktion’) of the traditional history of ontology can lead us back to the primordial experiences that reveal the nature of Being.
records.reference: Heidegger, M. (1927). Being and Time. Translated by John Macquarrie & Edward Robinson. Blackwell Publishers. 1962.

records.line: Being is essentially different from beings (‘ontological difference’): rocks, people, numbers, universals are all beings, but they all have Being.
records.reference: Smith, J. (n.d.). “Phenomenology”. The Internet Encyclopedia of Philosophy. https://iep.utm.edu/phenom/

records.line: To uncover Being and its meaning, we should carry out a phenomenological analysis of our awareness of our own everyday human ‘existence’, or ‘Dasein’.
records.reference: Heidegger, M. (1927). Being and Time. Translated by John Macquarrie & Edward Robinson. Blackwell Publishers. 1962.

records.line: Dasein’s ‘Essence’ is grounded in its existence; our ‘substance’ is not spirit/soul, or an unchanging subject (“I”, Self, Ego), it is rather ‘existence’.
records.reference: Heidegger, M. (1927). Being and Time. Translated by John Macquarrie & Edward Robinson. Blackwell Publishers. 1962.

records.line: ‘Nothing’ is a positive ontological phenomenon – a necessary feature of the essential unfolding of Being.
records.reference: Wheeler, M. (n.d.). “Martin Heidegger”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/heidegger/

records.line: Our Being is ‘worldly’, with all the people, things, ideas, states of mind, moods, and situations that contextualize our everyday existence, ‘Being-in-the-world’.
records.reference: Heidegger, M. (1927). Being and Time. Translated by John Macquarrie & Edward Robinson. Blackwell Publishers. 1962.; Yoshimi, J. Unpublished lecture notes on Heidegger.

records.line: Our fundamental relation to the world is practical rather than cognitive: our understanding of the Being of beings is manifested in our acting with them.
records.reference: Smith, J. (n.d.). “Phenomenology”. The Internet Encyclopedia of Philosophy. https://iep.utm.edu/phenom/

records.line: Discourse is not always about assertion, it is ‘worldly’: we assent, refuse, demand, warn, consult, share moods; keep silent, intonate, listen to, etc.
records.reference: Heidegger, M. (1927). Being and Time. Translated by John Macquarrie & Edward Robinson. Blackwell Publishers. 1962.

records.line: Facticity is a characteristic of Dasein’s Being: we find ourselves in states of mind or moods, as if we had been thrown “there”, delivered over to the Being.
records.reference: Heidegger, M. (1927). Being and Time. Translated by John Macquarrie & Edward Robinson. Blackwell Publishers. 1962.

records.line: Dasein is temporal: we always ‘project’ forward into possibilities (future) against the background of our everyday life (present) and our facticity (past).
records.reference: Heidegger, M. (1927). Being and Time. Translated by John Macquarrie & Edward Robinson. Blackwell Publishers. 1962.; Yoshimi, J. Unpublished lecture notes on Heidegger.

records.line: Dasein is distinguished by the fact that, in its very Being, that Being is an issue for it: humans, unlike rocks, care about their and others’ existence.
records.reference: Heidegger, M. (1927). Being and Time. Translated by John Macquarrie & Edward Robinson. Blackwell Publishers. 1962.; Yoshimi, J. Unpublished lecture notes on Heidegger.

records.line: Dasein is historical, being born into a tradition that influences its choices; it can live unaware of this or discover this and make more conscious choices.
records.reference: Heidegger, M. (1927). Being and Time. Translated by John Macquarrie & Edward Robinson. Blackwell Publishers. 1962.

records.line: When we are absorbed in our everyday social contexts, we are ‘falling’ away from the projection of our authentic possibilities and our answerability.
records.reference: Heidegger, M. (1927). Being and Time. Translated by John Macquarrie & Edward Robinson. Blackwell Publishers. 1962.

records.line: One is never in control of one’s own Being from the ground up; with the call of conscience, one owns this fact (‘guilt’) and chooses to choose to be a self (‘resoluteness’).
records.reference: Heidegger, M. (1927). Being and Time. Translated by John Macquarrie & Edward Robinson. Blackwell Publishers. 1962.

records.line: Since Dasein is a Being which is ‘between’ birth and death, we cannot get it in our grasp as a whole; it has a constant ‘lack of totality’ which finds an end with death.
records.reference: Heidegger, M. (1927). Being and Time. Translated by John Macquarrie & Edward Robinson. Blackwell Publishers. 1962.

records.line: Society does not permit us the courage for anxiety in the face of death; it transforms anxiety into indifferent tranquillity (alienation) or into fear labeled as weakness.
records.reference: Heidegger, M. (1927). Being and Time. Translated by John Macquarrie & Edward Robinson. Blackwell Publishers. 1962.

records.line: Anxiety (not fear) in the face of death (“nothing”) is a liberating anticipation of one’s own potentiality, of an authentic existence; it individualizes us.
records.reference: Heidegger, M. (1927). Being and Time. Translated by John Macquarrie & Edward Robinson. Blackwell Publishers. 1962.

records.line: What distinguishes modernity from the Greeks and the Middle Ages is that the world becomes object (picture, represented) and man becomes subject.
records.reference: Heidegger, M. (1950). “The Age of the World Picture” in Off the Beaten Track. Edited & Translated by Julian Young & Kenneth Haynes. Blackwell Publishers. 2002.

records.line: The more objectively the object appears, the more inexorably do observations and teachings about the world transform into a subjective anthropology.
records.reference: Heidegger, M. (1950). “The Age of the World Picture” in Off the Beaten Track. Edited & Translated by Julian Young & Kenneth Haynes. Blackwell Publishers. 2002.

records.line: In the battle of world views, science as research is the indispensable form of self-establishment in the world via calculation, planning, and breeding.
records.reference: Heidegger, M. (1950). “The Age of the World Picture” in Off the Beaten Track. Edited & Translated by Julian Young & Kenneth Haynes. Blackwell Publishers. 2002.

records.line: With the beginnings of the battle for mastery of the earth, nature is willed out of the essence of being and appears as the object of technology.
records.reference: Heidegger, M. (1950). “Nietzsche’s Word: ‘God Is Dead’” in Off the Beaten Track. Edited & Translated by Julian Young & Kenneth Haynes. Blackwell Publishers. 2002.

records.line: When mastery begins to be exercised by the unconditional will to power, Being becomes value, deprecated as a mere condition set by the will to power itself.
records.reference: Heidegger, M. (1950). “Nietzsche’s Word: ‘God Is Dead’” in Off the Beaten Track. Edited & Translated by Julian Young & Kenneth Haynes. Blackwell Publishers. 2002.

records.line: The origin of the artwork – of creators and interpreters (‘preservers’) – is art, which is a poetic way beings are unconcealed and truth comes into being.
records.reference: Heidegger, M. (1950). “The Origin of the Work of Art” in Off the Beaten Track. Edited & Translated by Julian Young & Kenneth Haynes. Blackwell Publishers. 2002.

records.line: All legitimate knowledge is either scientific or logico-mathematical.
records.reference: Baldwin, T. (2001). Contemporary Philosophy. Oxford University Press.

records.line: Philosophy is to be replaced by the logic of science – by the logical analysis of the concepts and sentences of the sciences.
records.reference: Carnap, R. (1934). The Logical Syntax of Language. London: Routledge. 1937.

records.line: The meaning of a sentence is identical with the way we determine its truth or falsehood; it has meaning only if such a determination is possible.
records.reference: Carnap, R. (1936). “Testability and Meaning”. Philosophy of Science, 3(4), 419-471.

records.line: A definitive and final verification of a sentence is not possible; we can only confirm a sentence more and more.
records.reference: Carnap, R. (1936). “Testability and Meaning”. Philosophy of Science, 3(4), 419-471.

records.line: It is impossible to test a single hypothetical sentence; the test applies to the whole system of physics as a system of hypotheses.
records.reference: Carnap, R. (1934). The Logical Syntax of Language. London: Routledge. 1937.

records.line: We can reconstruct scientific statements as structural statements (in a phenomenalist constitution system, in a physicalist constitution system, etc.).
records.reference: Leitgeb, H. & Carus, A. (n.d.). “Rudolf Carnap”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/carnap/aufbau.html

records.line: Existence questions should be interpreted internally, relative to incommensurable ‘linguistic frameworks’ (mathematical or empirical).
records.reference: Bricker, P. (n.d.). “Ontological commitment”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/ontological-commitment/

records.line: When existence questions are interpreted externally, independent of any linguistic framework, they are pseudo-questions lacking cognitive meaning.
records.reference: Bricker, P. (n.d.). “Ontological commitment”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/ontological-commitment/

records.line: The pseudo-statements of metaphysics only serve to express the general attitude of a person towards life, like artworks do.
records.reference: Carnap, R. (1932). “The Elimination of Metaphysics through the Logical Analysis of Language”. Erkenntnis, 60-81.

records.line: The truths of logic are a by-product of the meaning of the language we use for reasoning and calculation.
records.reference: Baldwin, T. (2001). Contemporary Philosophy. Oxford University Press.

records.line: The truth of even what are supposed to be analytic truths may require revision in the light of experience.
records.reference: Baldwin, T. (2001). Contemporary Philosophy. Oxford University Press.

records.line: Analyticity should be defined within linguistic frameworks describing a subject-matter (physics, astronomy, etc.).
records.reference: Baldwin, T. (2001). Contemporary Philosophy. Oxford University Press.

records.line: The self is the class (not the collection) of the experiences; it is constructed only on a very high level.
records.reference: Carnap, R. (1928). The Logical Structure of the World: Pseudoproblems in Philosophy. Berkeley: University of California Press. 1967.

records.line: There is no contradiction between free choice and determinism: if the act springs from one’s own character and preferences, then that person acts freely.
records.reference: Carnap, R. (1958). An Introduction to the Philosophy of Science. Dover Publications.

records.line: What we ordinarily say, we say unclearly, because we think unclearly; it is the task of philosophy to render our thoughts clear.
