records.reference: Stebbing, S. (1933). “Logical positivism and analysis”. Proceedings of the British Academy, 19, 53-87.

records.line: Insofar as the logical forms that the science of logic discovers are norms of thinking, logic can be described as a normative science.
records.reference: Beaney, M., & Chapman, S. (n.d.). “Susan Stebbing”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/stebbing/

records.line: We cannot make everything clear at once using analysis; we must proceed step by step, beginning with propositions which we know to be true.
records.reference: Stebbing, S. (1933). “Logical positivism and analysis”. Proceedings of the British Academy, 19, 53-87.

records.line: The role of analysis is to understand something more clearly; simply replacing our ordinary vague concepts by precise ones does not always do that.
records.reference: Beaney, M., & Chapman, S. (n.d.). “Susan Stebbing”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/stebbing/

records.line: Even metaphysical (reductive) analysis directed towards ‘basic facts’ does not always analyse the meaning of a proposition.
records.reference: Beaney, M., & Chapman, S. (n.d.). “Susan Stebbing”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/stebbing/

records.line: “Logically constructed systems” with the fewest possible assumptions and primitive terms are too abstract; the world itself is not such a system.
records.reference: Beaney, M., & Chapman, S. (n.d.). “Susan Stebbing”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/stebbing/

records.line: Ordinary language accurately describes everyday experience; for the scientific understanding of matter, a different type of language is necessary.
records.reference: Beaney, M., & Chapman, S. (n.d.). “Susan Stebbing”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/stebbing/

records.line: The structures and principles of formal logic need not be seen as an isolated system; they can be applied to the problems and issues of modern life.
records.reference: Beaney, M., & Chapman, S. (n.d.). “Susan Stebbing”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/stebbing/

records.line: Every person needs to hone their critical skills in order to identify misleading uses of language by advertisers, journalists, clergymen, and politicians.
records.reference: Beaney, M., & Chapman, S. (n.d.). “Susan Stebbing”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/stebbing/

records.line: There is no abstract “human nature”, fixed and immutable; human nature is the totality of historically determined social relations.
records.reference: Gramsci, A. (1929–1935). Selections from the prison notebooks. Translated and Edited by Q. Hoare and G. N. Smith. International Publishers. 1971.

records.line: Each person is a “philosopher”, an artist: each person has a conscious line of moral conduct and sustains a conception of the world or modifies it.
records.reference: Gramsci, A. (1929–1935). Selections from the prison notebooks. Translated and Edited by Q. Hoare and G. N. Smith. International Publishers. 1971.

records.line: The real philosopher is the politician, the active person who modifies their environment, i.e. the relations which define our individualities.
records.reference: Gramsci, A. (1929–1935). Selections from the prison notebooks. Translated and Edited by Q. Hoare and G. N. Smith. International Publishers. 1971.

records.line: There is a minimum of creative intellectual activity even in physical work; all people are intellectuals, but not all have in society the function of intellectuals.
records.reference: Gramsci, A. (1929–1935). Selections from the prison notebooks. Translated and Edited by Q. Hoare and G. N. Smith. International Publishers. 1971.

records.line: Every social group creates, organically, intellectuals who give it an awareness of its own function in the economic, social, and political fields.
records.reference: Gramsci, A. (1929–1935). Selections from the prison notebooks. Translated and Edited by Q. Hoare and G. N. Smith. International Publishers. 1971.

records.line: Traditional intellectuals (scientists, philosophers, artists, etc.) see themselves as autonomous and independent of the dominant social group.
records.reference: Gramsci, A. (1929–1935). Selections from the prison notebooks. Translated and Edited by Q. Hoare and G. N. Smith. International Publishers. 1971.

records.line: Hegemony entails for a class its execution of a leadership role on the economic, political, moral, and intellectual levels vis-a-vis other classes.
records.reference: Ramos, V. (1982). “The concepts of ideology, hegemony, and organic intellectuals in Gramsci’s Marxism”. Theoretical Review, 27, March-April.

records.line: Organic intellectuals can be hegemonic (shaping the dominant frames of reference) or counter-hegemonic (calling those frames into question).
records.reference: O’Neill, D., & Wayne, M. (2018). “On intellectuals”. D. O’Neill & M. Wayne (Eds.), Considering class, theory, culture and the media in the 21st century. Leiden/Boston: Brill.

records.line: When a dominant class convinces all other classes that their interests can be met by aligning themselves with its rule, it becomes hegemonic.
records.reference: O’Neill, D., & Wayne, M. (2018). “On intellectuals”. D. O’Neill & M. Wayne (Eds.), Considering class, theory, culture and the media in the 21st century. Leiden/Boston: Brill.

records.line: An organic ideology, as a unified world-view, is diffused throughout the family, churches, the media, schools, the legal system, trade unions, etc.
records.reference: Ramos, V. (1982). “The concepts of ideology, hegemony, and organic intellectuals in Gramsci’s Marxism”. Theoretical Review, 27, March-April.

records.line: Hegemonic rule, characterized by the predominance of consensus over coercion, represents a balance between “civil society” and “political society”.
records.reference: Ramos, V. (1982). “The concepts of ideology, hegemony, and organic intellectuals in Gramsci’s Marxism”. Theoretical Review, 27, March-April.

records.line: An ideological struggle happens between two hegemonic principles for the “appropriation” of ideological elements, resulting in a new collective will.
records.reference: Ramos, V. (1982). “The concepts of ideology, hegemony, and organic intellectuals in Gramsci’s Marxism”. Theoretical Review, 27, March-April.

records.line: In the modern world, technical education, closely bound to industrial labor, must form the basis of the new type of intellectual.
records.reference: Gramsci, A. (1929–1935). Selections from the prison notebooks. Translated and Edited by Q. Hoare and G. N. Smith. International Publishers. 1971.

records.line: The mode of being of the new intellectual must consist in active participation in practical life, as constructor, organizer, “permanent persuader”.
records.reference: Gramsci, A. (1929–1935). Selections from the prison notebooks. Translated and Edited by Q. Hoare and G. N. Smith. International Publishers. 1971.

records.line: The working class must, before exercising state power, attain leadership in the political, cultural, and moral fields with its own organic intellectuals.
records.reference: Ramos, V. (1982). “The concepts of ideology, hegemony, and organic intellectuals in Gramsci’s Marxism”. Theoretical Review, 27, March-April.

records.line: Within major historical periods, there are changes in the manner of sense perception of the human collective.
records.reference: Benjamin, W. (1936). The work of art in the age of mechanical reproduction (J. A. Underwood, Trans.). Penguin Books. 2008.

records.line: The ‘one-of-a-kind’ value of the ‘genuine’ work of art has its underpinnings in the ritual in which it had its original, initial utility value.
records.reference: Benjamin, W. (1936). The work of art in the age of mechanical reproduction (J. A. Underwood, Trans.). Penguin Books. 2008.

records.line: What shrinks in an age where the work of art can be reproduced by technological means is its ‘aura’: its here-and-now, its uniqueness, its authority.
records.reference: Benjamin, W. (1936). The work of art in the age of mechanical reproduction (J. A. Underwood, Trans.). Penguin Books. 2008.

records.line: In manual reproduction the genuine article keeps its full authority; in technological reproduction that is not the case.
records.reference: Benjamin, W. (1936). The work of art in the age of mechanical reproduction (J. A. Underwood, Trans.). Penguin Books. 2008.

records.line: Its being reproducible by technological means frees the work of art, for the first time, from its existence as a parasite upon ritual.
records.reference: Benjamin, W. (1936). The work of art in the age of mechanical reproduction (J. A. Underwood, Trans.). Penguin Books. 2008.

records.line: Because of the weight placed on its display value, the work of art is becoming an image with new functions; the artistic function may be deemed incidental.
records.reference: Benjamin, W. (1936). The work of art in the age of mechanical reproduction (J. A. Underwood, Trans.). Penguin Books. 2008.

records.line: When the criterion of genuineness in art production failed, the social function of art came to be underpinned by politics instead of ritual.
records.reference: Benjamin, W. (1936). The work of art in the age of mechanical reproduction (J. A. Underwood, Trans.). Penguin Books. 2008.

records.line: Fascism seeks to give the masses a voice without changing the property relations; it leads logically to an aestheticization of political life.
records.reference: Benjamin, W. (1936). The work of art in the age of mechanical reproduction (J. A. Underwood, Trans.). Penguin Books. 2008.

records.line: All efforts to aestheticize politics culminate in war; war only makes it possible to give masses a goal while retaining the ownership structure.
records.reference: Benjamin, W. (1936). The work of art in the age of mechanical reproduction (J. A. Underwood, Trans.). Penguin Books. 2008.

records.line: We cannot explain the expressiveness of art merely in terms of associations set up between formal properties (color, etc.) and human feelings.
records.reference: Eaton, M. M. (1988). Basic Issues In Aesthetics. Waveland Press.

records.line: Art is a highly articulated form of expression symbolizing direct/intuitive knowledge of life patterns (feeling, motion, etc.) inexpressible in ordinary language.
records.reference: Britannica, T. Editors of Encyclopaedia (n.d.). “Susanne K. Langer”. Encyclopedia Britannica. https://www.britannica.com/biography/Susanne-K-Langer

records.line: We can fill the articulate forms of a music piece with any meaning that fits them; it may convey an idea of anything conceivable in its logical image.
records.reference: Langer, S. K. (1953). Feeling and form. Routledge & Kegan Paul.

records.line: Unlike language, in art we do not have to learn vocabulary or grammar; feeling emerges from the form and we get it directly.
records.reference: Eaton, M. M. (1988). Basic Issues In Aesthetics. Waveland Press.

records.line: Mental activity is a form of (inner) behavior; it is irrelevant (explanatorily circular) in a functional analysis of behavior.
records.reference: Graham, G. (n.d.). “Behaviorism”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/behaviorism/

records.line: The only non-circular way to explain behavior is to appeal to something non-behavioral: environmental stimuli and the organism’s interactions with it.
records.reference: Graham, G. (n.d.). “Behaviorism”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/behaviorism/

records.line: What are introspectively observed are not the causes of the behavior, but certain collateral products of a person’s genetic and environment histories.
records.reference: Skinner, B. F. (1974). About Behaviorism, New York: Vintage.

records.line: Neurobiological conditions sustain the functional/causal relations but they do not serve as ultimate or independent sources or explanations of behavior.
records.reference: Graham, G. (n.d.). “Behaviorism”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/behaviorism/

records.line: Organisms learn from their successes and mistakes, without being innately provided with implicit procedures by which to learn.
records.reference: Graham, G. (n.d.). “Behaviorism”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/behaviorism/

records.line: The analysis of behavior should strip away the functions assigned to a free or autonomous person and transfer them to the controlling environment.
records.reference: Skinner, B. F. (1971). Beyond Freedom and Dignity, New York: Knopf.

records.line: The main aim of science is explanation.
records.reference: Baldwin, T. (2001). Contemporary Philosophy. Oxford University Press.

records.line: A good scientific explanation is one that would have enabled the phenomenon to be predicted.
records.reference: Baldwin, T. (2001). Contemporary Philosophy. Oxford University Press.

records.line: The logic of science is a combination of the deductive refutation and the inductive confirmation of theoretical predictions by observation.
records.reference: Baldwin, T. (2001). Contemporary Philosophy. Oxford University Press.

records.line: In a scientific explanation, the explanandum must be a logical consequence of the explanans and the explanans must be true.
records.reference: Woodward, J. (n.d.). “Scientific explanation”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/scientific-explanation/

records.line: In a scientific explanation, the explanans must contain at least one law of nature and this must be an essential premise in the derivation.
records.reference: Woodward, J. (n.d.). “Scientific explanation”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/scientific-explanation/

records.line: The progress of science consists in the refinement of unrefuted theories.
records.reference: Baldwin, T. (2001). Contemporary Philosophy. Oxford University Press.

records.line: Predictions of a theory should be couched in an ‘observation-language’ whose meaning is independent of the theory.
records.reference: Baldwin, T. (2001). Contemporary Philosophy. Oxford University Press.

records.line: Theories which include reference to unobservable things are merely instruments for calculation.
records.reference: Baldwin, T. (2001). Contemporary Philosophy. Oxford University Press.

records.line: Explanations in history must conform to the model of scientific explanations, involving (psychological) laws, causes, and effects.
records.reference: Hempel, C. G. (1942). “The Function of General Laws in History”. The Journal of Philosophy, 39(2), 35–48.

records.line: All meaningful (verifiable) psychological statements are translatable into statements involving only the concepts of physics (about behaviour, neurology, etc.).
records.reference: Hempel, C. G. (1935). “The Logical Analysis of Psychology”. Revue de Synthese. 10: p. 21-42.

records.line: In order to characterize behavioral patterns, propensities, or capacities, we need not only a suitable behavioristic vocabulary, but psychological terms as well.
records.reference: Hempel, C. G. (1966). Philosophy of Natural Science, Englewood Cliffs, N. J.: Prentice-Hall.

records.line: Physical reality is of a very different nature from the human experience, and cannot be directly understood.
records.reference: Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.

records.line: Theories cannot be proved, but they can be disproved (falsified); criticism is the main method of progress.
records.reference: Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.

records.line: Theories are ‘corroborated’ when they survive several refutations; the better corroborated it is, the more it approximates to the truth.
records.reference: Baldwin, T. (2001). Contemporary Philosophy. Oxford University Press.

records.line: The progress of science consists in the replacement of refuted theories by theories that are only tentatively corroborated (conjectures).
records.reference: Baldwin, T. (2001). Contemporary Philosophy. Oxford University Press.

records.line: A statement that cannot be falsified by any observation is not scientific.
records.reference: Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.

records.line: The observations and experiments which test scientific theories are not independent of scientific theory.
records.reference: Baldwin, T. (2001). Contemporary Philosophy. Oxford University Press.

records.line: Unobservable things postulated by scientific theories are not intrinsically different from observable things; they are equally real.
records.reference: Baldwin, T. (2001). Contemporary Philosophy. Oxford University Press.

records.line: The question how it happens that a new idea or theory occurs to a person is irrelevant to the logical analysis of scientific knowledge.
records.reference: Popper, K. (1934). The Logic of Scientific Discovery, London and New York: Routledge. 2002.

records.line: New ideas and free-will decisions may be the results of selections from repertoires of random events, like natural selection and genetic mutations.
records.reference: Popper, K., & Eccles, J. C. (1977). The Self and Its Brain. Springer.

records.line: The mind is not like a passive bucket to be filled by sense-impressions, but like an active searchlight constantly testing hypotheses by trial and error.
records.reference: Popper, K. (1948). “The Bucket and the Searchlight: Two Theories of Knowledge” in Objective Knowledge. Oxford: Clarendon. 1972.

records.line: Three interacting realms: World 1 (objects of the natural sciences), World 2 (animal/human mental states and processes), World 3 (products of human thought).
records.reference: Popper, K. (1968). “Epistemology Without a Knowing Subject”. Third International Congress for Logic, Methodology and Philosophy of Science, 25 August 1967. Amsterdam.

records.line: World 2 emerged as a product of biological evolution in World 1, and World 3 emerged as a product of evolution within the human World 2.
records.reference: Popper, K. (1968). “Epistemology Without a Knowing Subject”. Third International Congress for Logic, Methodology and Philosophy of Science, 25 August 1967. Amsterdam.

records.line: World 3 objects (scientific theories, myths, artworks, etc.) may have an indirect but powerful effect on World 1, via World 2-based human action.
records.reference: Popper, K. (1968). “Epistemology Without a Knowing Subject”. Third International Congress for Logic, Methodology and Philosophy of Science, 25 August 1967. Amsterdam.

records.line: Certainty is not possible in politics; the imposition of a single viewpoint is never justified.
records.reference: Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.

records.line: Centralized planning without criticism is the most undesirable form of society.
records.reference: Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.

records.line: A perfect static society is a dream; we can only engage in perpetual improvement, reducing social evils.
records.reference: Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.

records.line: We should claim, in the name of tolerance, the right not to tolerate the intolerant who use violence to suppress rational argument.
records.reference: Popper, K. (1945). The Open Society and Its Enemies. Princeton University Press. 2013.

records.line: The analytical techniques of formal logic cannot resolve philosophical problems.
records.reference: Baldwin, T. (2001). Contemporary Philosophy. Oxford University Press.

records.line: Materialist mechanism cannot by itself explain mental phenomena; human affairs need explanations of different kinds and levels.
records.reference: Baldwin, T. (2001). Contemporary Philosophy. Oxford University Press.

records.line: Cartesian dualism (‘ghost in the machine’) is a category mistake: it represents mental life as belonging to the wrong logical type or category.
records.reference: Baldwin, T. (2001). Contemporary Philosophy. Oxford University Press.

records.line: If the rationality of any given performance required the rational execution of some anterior (mental) performance, no rational performance could ever begin.
records.reference: Ryle, G. (1946). “Knowing How and Knowing That”. Proceedings of the Aristotelian Society 46:1 - 16.

records.line: Our talk of “the mind” does not require the existence of a private space within which mental acts and happenings take place.
records.reference: Baldwin, T. (2001). Contemporary Philosophy. Oxford University Press.

records.line: Having a mind is a matter of possessing abilities (knowing how to do things), dispositions, inclinations (emotions).
records.reference: Baldwin, T. (2001). Contemporary Philosophy. Oxford University Press.

records.line: Knowing-how is not reducible to knowings-that; we can be instructed in truths, we can only be disciplined in methods, with exercises, criticisms, and examples.
records.reference: Ryle, G. (1946). “Knowing How and Knowing That”. Proceedings of the Aristotelian Society 46:1 - 16.

records.line: Knowing-that presupposes knowing-how: establishing truths is an operation requiring rules of method, checks, tests, criteria, etc.
records.reference: Ryle, G. (1946). “Knowing How and Knowing That”. Proceedings of the Aristotelian Society 46:1 - 16.

records.line: Logical rules, ethical or tactical maxims, and technical canons are educational tools for the half-trained; experts and saints do not need them.
records.reference: Ryle, G. (1946). “Knowing How and Knowing That”. Proceedings of the Aristotelian Society 46:1 - 16.

records.line: Motives are not happenings and are not therefore of the right type to be causes.
records.reference: Ryle, G. (1949). The Concept of Mind. Chicago: University of Chicago Press.

records.line: When we perceive or have sensations, we are attending to features of the world or of our bodies, not to our experiences.
records.reference: Baldwin, T. (2001). Contemporary Philosophy. Oxford University Press.

records.line: If our experiences have to be attended by us, that awareness experience also has to be attended – this sets off an absurd regress.
records.reference: Baldwin, T. (2001). Contemporary Philosophy. Oxford University Press.

records.line: We can choose to attend to the experiences of perception/sensation, but it will be retrospective and fallible.
records.reference: Baldwin, T. (2001). Contemporary Philosophy. Oxford University Press.

records.line: There is no difference in kind between what I can know about myself and what I can know about others, and differences in degree are not all in favor of self-knowledge.
records.reference: Ryle, G. (1949). The Concept of Mind. Chicago: University of Chicago Press.

records.line: Our first-person statements about intentions and decisions are ‘avowels’ and not descriptions of our mental states.
records.reference: Baldwin, T. (2001). Contemporary Philosophy. Oxford University Press.

records.line: The self is elusive; when we try to attend to ourselves, we put ourselves beyond our reach.
records.reference: Baldwin, T. (2001). Contemporary Philosophy. Oxford University Press.

records.line: In any sufficiently powerful logical system F, there are statements which can neither be proved nor disproved in F.
records.reference: Raatikainen, P. (n.d.). “Gödel’s Incompleteness Theorems”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/goedel-incompleteness/

records.line: For any sufficiently powerful logical system F, the consistency of F cannot be proved in F itself.
records.reference: Raatikainen, P. (n.d.). “Gödel’s Incompleteness Theorems”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/goedel-incompleteness/

records.line: Our everyday language contains semantic terms such as “true” referring to its own sentences; it is ‘semantically closed’ and this causes antinomies.
records.reference: Tarski, A. (1944). “The Semantic Conception of Truth: and the Foundations of Semantics”. Philosophy and Phenomenological Research, 4(3), 341-376.

records.line: The solution to the Antinomy of the Liar is to have two languages: a ‘meta-language’ in which we can talk about the sentences of an ‘object-language’.
records.reference: Tarski, A. (1944). “The Semantic Conception of Truth: and the Foundations of Semantics”. Philosophy and Phenomenological Research, 4(3), 341-376.

records.line: Equivalence (T): The sentence “snow is white” [object-language] is true if, and only if, snow is white. [meta-language]
records.reference: Tarski, A. (1944). “The Semantic Conception of Truth: and the Foundations of Semantics”. Philosophy and Phenomenological Research, 4(3), 341-376.

records.line: We can use the term “true” in such a way that all equivalences of the form (T) (“X (name of p) is true if, and only if, p.”) can be asserted.
records.reference: Tarski, A. (1944). “The Semantic Conception of Truth: and the Foundations of Semantics”. Philosophy and Phenomenological Research, 4(3), 341-376.

records.line: The problem of the definition of truth can be solved only for formal languages; natural, spoken languages are too vague.
records.reference: Tarski, A. (1944). “The Semantic Conception of Truth: and the Foundations of Semantics”. Philosophy and Phenomenological Research, 4(3), 341-376.

records.line: We should reconcile ourselves with the fact that there may not be a single “right” conception of truth.
records.reference: Tarski, A. (1944). “The Semantic Conception of Truth: and the Foundations of Semantics”. Philosophy and Phenomenological Research, 4(3), 341-376.

records.line: We cannot get rid of the word “true” in a sentence like “The first sentence written by Plato is true”; the conception of truth is not sterile or useless.
records.reference: Tarski, A. (1944). “The Semantic Conception of Truth: and the Foundations of Semantics”. Philosophy and Phenomenological Research, 4(3), 341-376.

records.line: Proximate questions ask ‘how’ a biological mechanism works; ultimate questions are evolutionary questions asking ‘why’.
records.reference: Mayr, E. (1961). “Cause and effect in biology: Kinds of causes, predictability, and teleology are viewed by a practicing biologist”. Science, 134, 1501-1506.

records.line: Species are groups of actually or potentially interbreeding natural populations, which are reproductively isolated from other such groups.
records.reference: Mayr, E. (1942). Systematics and the Origin of Species. Columbia University Press.

records.line: A ‘selecton’ is a discrete entity and a cohesive whole, an individual or a social group, the survival and successful reproduction of which is favored by selection.
records.reference: Mayr, E. (1997). “The objects of selection”. Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences.

records.line: Human beings are beings of excess with exorbitant energy, fantasies, drives, needs, and heterogeneous desire.
records.reference: Kellner, D. (n.d.). “Jean Baudrillard”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/baudrillard/

records.line: The capitalist imperatives of labor, utility, and savings are unnatural, and go against human nature.
records.reference: Kellner, D. (n.d.). “Jean Baudrillard”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/baudrillard/

records.line: We can escape the existing imperatives of utility (capitalism) by pursuing a ‘general economy’ of expenditure, giving, sacrifice, and destruction.
records.reference: Kellner, D. (n.d.). “Jean Baudrillard”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/baudrillard/

records.line: In the recognition of its mirror-image, the child is having its first anticipation of itself as a unified and separate individual.
records.reference: Sharpe, M. (n.d.). “Jacques Lacan”. The Internet Encyclopedia of Philosophy. https://iep.utm.edu/lacweb/

records.line: The ‘I’ is an ‘other’ from the ground up: an artificial projection of subjective unity modelled on how one is seen from other perspectives.
records.reference: Sharpe, M. (n.d.). “Jacques Lacan”. The Internet Encyclopedia of Philosophy. https://iep.utm.edu/lacweb/

records.line: ‘The Imaginary’ is our quotidian reality: who and what one imagines other persons to be, who and what one imagines oneself to be, etc.
records.reference: Johnston, A. (n.d.). “Jacques Lacan”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/lacan/

records.line: ‘The Symbolic’ is the customs, institutions, laws, mores, etc. of cultures and societies (the ‘Other’), entwined with language.
records.reference: Johnston, A. (n.d.). “Jacques Lacan”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/lacan/

records.line: ‘The Real’ is whatever is beyond phenomenal appearances; it is intrinsically elusive and uncapturable by Imaginary-Symbolic signs.
records.reference: Johnston, A. (n.d.). “Jacques Lacan”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/lacan/

records.line: The insertion into socio-symbolic contexts and the dependence on the foreignness of signifiers amount to a ‘symbolic castration’.
records.reference: Johnston, A. (n.d.). “Jacques Lacan”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/lacan/

records.line: ‘Master signifiers’ (those that the subject most deeply identifies with, like nationality) are actually signifiers without a signified.
records.reference: Sharpe, M. (n.d.). “Jacques Lacan”. The Internet Encyclopedia of Philosophy. https://iep.utm.edu/lacweb/

records.line: The unconscious is not an anarchic bundle of animalistic instincts like the id – it is structured like language.
records.reference: Johnston, A. (n.d.). “Jacques Lacan”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/lacan/

records.line: Demand – need = desire: vital requirements take on the excess baggage of meanings and become socio-symbolic demands for love.
records.reference: Johnston, A. (n.d.). “Jacques Lacan”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/lacan/

records.line: ‘Drive’ extracts enjoyment (‘jouissance’) from the dissatisfactions of desire, sometimes through repetitive or overwhelming experiences.
records.reference: Johnston, A. (n.d.). “Jacques Lacan”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/lacan/

records.line: The Real of sexuation creates incommensurable structural-psychical positions for the sexes; there is no sexual relationship.
records.reference: Johnston, A. (n.d.). “Jacques Lacan”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/lacan/

records.line: There is no such thing as pure thought: thinking is a socio-historical form of activity, and there is not a universal standpoint to discern “truth”.
records.reference: Fagan, A. (n.d.). “Theodor Adorno”. The Internet Encyclopedia of Philosophy. https://iep.utm.edu/adorno/

records.line: ‘Identity thinking’ is misrepresenting reality by putting specific phenomena under general, abstract classificatory headings.
records.reference: Fagan, A. (n.d.). “Theodor Adorno”. The Internet Encyclopedia of Philosophy. https://iep.utm.edu/adorno/

records.line: ‘Identity thinking’ is fundamentally concerned not to understand phenomena but to control and manipulate it.
records.reference: Fagan, A. (n.d.). “Theodor Adorno”. The Internet Encyclopedia of Philosophy. https://iep.utm.edu/adorno/

records.line: The source of today’s disaster is the domination of nature and human beings by other human beings, in a capitalist economy fed by science and technology.
records.reference: Zuidervaart, L. (n.d.). “Theodor W. Adorno”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/adorno/

records.line: A critique of modernity must also be a critique of premodernity; a turn toward the postmodern cannot simply be a return to the premodern.
records.reference: Zuidervaart, L. (n.d.). “Theodor W. Adorno”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/adorno/

records.line: The instrumentalization of reason and the supremacy of “facts” established a single order: enlightenment is totalitarian (like myth).
records.reference: Fagan, A. (n.d.). “Theodor Adorno”. The Internet Encyclopedia of Philosophy. https://iep.utm.edu/adorno/

records.line: With the instrumentalization of reason and positivism, morality is put under the invalid, “meaningless” category of subjective knowledge.
records.reference: Fagan, A. (n.d.). “Theodor Adorno”. The Internet Encyclopedia of Philosophy. https://iep.utm.edu/adorno/

records.line: Intelligence is a moral category; the separation of feeling and understanding dismembers people into function.
records.reference: Adorno, T. (1951). Minima Moralia.

records.line: The ‘culture industry’ promotes domination by subverting the psychological development of the mass of people in capitalist societies.
records.reference: Fagan, A. (n.d.). “Theodor Adorno”. The Internet Encyclopedia of Philosophy. https://iep.utm.edu/adorno/

records.line: The commodities of the culture industry are increasingly standardized, formulaic, and repetitive in character.
records.reference: Fagan, A. (n.d.). “Theodor Adorno”. The Internet Encyclopedia of Philosophy. https://iep.utm.edu/adorno/

records.line: The standardization of music promotes a general condition of immaturity, prohibiting the exercise of any critical or reflexive faculties.
records.reference: Fagan, A. (n.d.). “Theodor Adorno”. The Internet Encyclopedia of Philosophy. https://iep.utm.edu/adorno/

records.line: The appeal of totalitarian ideologies, mobilizing populations, rests upon the devastation of ordered and stable contexts in which people once lived.
records.reference: Yar, M. (n.d.). “Hannah Arendt”. The Internet Encyclopedia of Philosophy. https://iep.utm.edu/arendt/

records.line: One of the primary concerns of all tyrannical government is to isolate people against each other, thus rendering them powerless.
records.reference: Arendt, H. (1951). The Origins of Totalitarianism. New York: Harcourt Brace Jovanovich. 1973.

records.line: The ideal subject of totalitarian rule is not the convinced Nazi or Communist, but people for whom the distinction between fact and fiction no longer exists.
records.reference: Arendt, H. (1951). The Origins of Totalitarianism. New York: Harcourt Brace Jovanovich. 1973.

records.line: There are three types of human activities: labor, work, and action (in ascending hierarchy of importance).
records.reference: Yar, M. (n.d.). “Hannah Arendt”. The Internet Encyclopedia of Philosophy. https://iep.utm.edu/arendt/

records.line: Labor is the biological practices necessary for the maintenance of life: an animal-like, unfree, private activity with impermanent products.
records.reference: Yar, M. (n.d.). “Hannah Arendt”. The Internet Encyclopedia of Philosophy. https://iep.utm.edu/arendt/

records.line: Work is the fabrication of quasi-permanent physical and cultural artifacts: a non-animal, public activity with some degree of freedom.
records.reference: Yar, M. (n.d.). “Hannah Arendt”. The Internet Encyclopedia of Philosophy. https://iep.utm.edu/arendt/

records.line: The rise of ‘animal laborans’ threatens the extinction of ‘homo faber’ and what makes a community’s public life possible (‘world alienation’).
records.reference: Yar, M. (n.d.). “Hannah Arendt”. The Internet Encyclopedia of Philosophy. https://iep.utm.edu/arendt/

records.line: Authentic human action can initiate the wholly new – unanticipated, unconditioned by the laws of cause and effect.
records.reference: Yar, M. (n.d.). “Hannah Arendt”. The Internet Encyclopedia of Philosophy. https://iep.utm.edu/arendt/

records.line: Actions cannot be justified for their own sake, but only in light of their public recognition and the shared rules of a political community.
records.reference: Yar, M. (n.d.). “Hannah Arendt”. The Internet Encyclopedia of Philosophy. https://iep.utm.edu/arendt/

records.line: The raison d’être of politics is freedom, and its field of experience is action.
records.reference: Yar, M. (n.d.). “Hannah Arendt”. The Internet Encyclopedia of Philosophy. https://iep.utm.edu/arendt/

records.line: Evil does not always come from malevolence; failures of thinking and judgment, sometimes abused by political systems, can result in evil.
records.reference: Buckingham, W. et al. (2011). The Philosophy Book. DK Pub.

records.line: A disinterested and imaginatively publicly-minded form of political judgement is required to tackle the new circumstances of the modern era.
records.reference: Yar, M. (n.d.). “Hannah Arendt”. The Internet Encyclopedia of Philosophy. https://iep.utm.edu/arendt/

records.line: The object of consciousness exists in an independent way; however, consciousness itself is always consciousness “of something.”
records.reference: Onof, C. J. (n.d.). “Jean Paul Sartre: Existentialism”. The Internet Encyclopedia of Philosophy. https://www.iep.utm.edu/sartre-ex/

records.line: The Ego is the transcendent object of one’s reflective experience, and not the subject of the pre-reflective experience of the world.
records.reference: Reynolds, J. & Renaudie, P. (n.d.). “Jean-Paul Sartre”, The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/archives/sum2022/entries/sartre/

records.line: Since other minds are required to account for conscious states such as those of shame, this establishes their existence a priori.
records.reference: Onof, C. J. (n.d.). “Jean Paul Sartre: Existentialism”. The Internet Encyclopedia of Philosophy. https://www.iep.utm.edu/sartre-ex/

records.line: The objectification of the other corresponds to an affirmation of my self by distinguishing myself from the other.
records.reference: Onof, C. J. (n.d.). “Jean Paul Sartre: Existentialism”. The Internet Encyclopedia of Philosophy. https://www.iep.utm.edu/sartre-ex/

records.line: When I objectify the other, I deny its selfhood and therefore deny that with respect to which I want to affirm myself.
records.reference: Onof, C. J. (n.d.). “Jean Paul Sartre: Existentialism”. The Internet Encyclopedia of Philosophy. https://www.iep.utm.edu/sartre-ex/

records.line: The instability of the dependence upon the other is characteristic of the typically conflictual state of our relations with others.
records.reference: Onof, C. J. (n.d.). “Jean Paul Sartre: Existentialism”. The Internet Encyclopedia of Philosophy. https://www.iep.utm.edu/sartre-ex/

records.line: Consciousness can be directed at (imaginary) objects, values, meanings, facts that do not exist in the present; this non-being, nothingness, grounds our freedom.
records.reference: Green, K. (2022). Simone de Beauvoir (Elements on Women in the History of Philosophy). Cambridge: Cambridge University Press.

records.line: The fundamental mode of existence of consciousness is future-oriented; it does not have a stable identity like a chair.
records.reference: Reynolds, J. & Renaudie, P. (n.d.). “Jean-Paul Sartre”, The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/archives/sum2022/entries/sartre/

records.line: In being conscious of laws that determine how things will act, we transcend them; we always transcend what merely is, towards what is not.
records.reference: Sartre, J. P. (1943). Being and Nothingness, trans. Barnes, H. London: Routledge. 1993.

records.line: In this Godless world we are thrown into, we are condemned to be free and to create our own values, with resulting responsibility and anguish.
records.reference: Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.; Reynolds, J. & Renaudie, P. (n.d.). “Jean-Paul Sartre”, The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/archives/sum2022/entries/sartre/

records.line: Existence precedes essence; by choosing our values and our actions we determine the nature of our lives and personalities, we create ourselves and our destinies.
records.reference: Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.; Reynolds, J. & Renaudie, P. (n.d.). “Jean-Paul Sartre”, The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/archives/sum2022/entries/sartre/

records.line: Freedom can be terrifying; many run away from it in ‘bad faith’ and act inauthentically, choosing existing societal norms.
records.reference: Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.

records.line: The ongoing process of one’s existence, constantly reassumed so as to integrate new developments, cannot be fully self-conscious.
records.reference: Reynolds, J. & Renaudie, P. (n.d.). “Jean-Paul Sartre”, The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/archives/sum2022/entries/sartre/

records.line: Psychoanalysis must consist in understanding our conducts in light of one’s project of existence and free choice – not just external and past events.
records.reference: Reynolds, J. & Renaudie, P. (n.d.). “Jean-Paul Sartre”, The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/archives/sum2022/entries/sartre/

records.line: Emotions are intentional and strategic ways of coping with difficulties; we utilize them and control them, so we are free even in what we feel.
records.reference: Sartre, J. P. (1943). Sketch for a Theory of the Emotions. Routledge. 1971.

records.line: Many of us may not obviously be bigots or “settlers”, but we sustain an unjust system through our choices and sometimes wilful ignorance.
records.reference: Reynolds, J. & Renaudie, P. (n.d.). “Jean-Paul Sartre”, The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/archives/sum2022/entries/sartre/

records.line: In an unjust society, the writer must jolt the audience from their complacency, rather than simply be concerned with art for its own sake.
records.reference: Reynolds, J. & Renaudie, P. (n.d.). “Jean-Paul Sartre”, The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/archives/sum2022/entries/sartre/

records.line: We should not flee but assume the ambiguity of the human condition: we are both free subjects and situated objects with instincts, desires, etc.
records.reference: Green, K. (2022). Simone de Beauvoir (Elements on Women in the History of Philosophy). Cambridge: Cambridge University Press.

records.line: Not all situations are equally compatible with the exercise of freedom: the failure to adopt an authentic attitude can be the result of oppression.
records.reference: Green, K. (2022). Simone de Beauvoir (Elements on Women in the History of Philosophy). Cambridge: Cambridge University Press.

records.line: The separation of our subjectivities entails that a revolt of the oppressed must in turn constrain the freedom of the oppressors; the conflict never ends.
records.reference: Green, K. (2022). Simone de Beauvoir (Elements on Women in the History of Philosophy). Cambridge: Cambridge University Press.

records.line: Men find in women another consciousness that does not demand reciprocal recognition, allowing them to escape the dialectic of master-slave.
records.reference: Green, K. (2022). Simone de Beauvoir (Elements on Women in the History of Philosophy). Cambridge: Cambridge University Press.

records.line: Woman is consistently defined as the Other by man who takes on the role of the Self; she positions herself according to him and not to herself.
records.reference: Mussett, S. (n.d.). “Simone de Beauvoir”. The Internet Encyclopedia of Philosophy. https://iep.utm.edu/beauvoir/

records.line: Men have been privileged with expressing transcendence through projects; women have been forced into the uncreative life of immanence.
records.reference: Mussett, S. (n.d.). “Simone de Beauvoir”. The Internet Encyclopedia of Philosophy. https://iep.utm.edu/beauvoir/

records.line: One is not born, but rather becomes, a woman – determined not by hormones, instincts, or a mysterious essence, but by her social interactions.
records.reference: Beauvoir, S. (1949). Le deuxième sexe. Paris: Gallimard.

records.line: The myth of the ‘Eternal Feminine’ (the mother, the virgin, the motherland, nature, etc.) traps women into an impossible ideal by denying their individuality.
records.reference: Mussett, S. (n.d.). “Simone de Beauvoir”. The Internet Encyclopedia of Philosophy. https://iep.utm.edu/beauvoir/

records.line: The inferiority of women and the conflicts between women and men merely mark a transitional moment in human history.
records.reference: Beauvoir, S. (1949). Le deuxième sexe. Paris: Gallimard.

records.line: Many women living in patriarchal cultures are guilty of ‘bad faith’; they flee their responsibility into prefabricated values and beliefs.
records.reference: Mussett, S. (n.d.). “Simone de Beauvoir”. The Internet Encyclopedia of Philosophy. https://iep.utm.edu/beauvoir/

records.line: Happiness is too hard to define and measure; the fortunes of the individual must be defined not in terms of happiness but in terms of liberty.
records.reference: Beauvoir, S. (1949). Le deuxième sexe. Paris: Gallimard.

records.line: Woman does wrong in yielding to the temptation of conformity in the anxiety of liberty; but man is in no position to blame her, since he has led her into it.
records.reference: Beauvoir, S. (1949). Le deuxième sexe. Paris: Gallimard.

records.line: Social systems must be altered for equal treatment of women, but women themselves also should take on their existential responsibility and freedom.
records.reference: Mussett, S. (n.d.). “Simone de Beauvoir”. The Internet Encyclopedia of Philosophy. https://iep.utm.edu/beauvoir/

records.line: It is not certain that women’s “worlds of ideas” will be different from men’s; we just have to wait and see.
records.reference: Beauvoir, S. (1949). Le deuxième sexe. Paris: Gallimard.

records.line: There will always be differences between man and woman in their sexual worlds: their relations to their own bodies, to that of the other sex, to the child.
records.reference: Beauvoir, S. (1949). Le deuxième sexe. Paris: Gallimard.

records.line: We are not immaterial centers of consciousness; we cannot perceive or act without our bodies.
records.reference: Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.

records.line: Consciousness is in the first place not a matter of “I think that” but of “I can”.
records.reference: Merleau-Ponty, M. (1945). Phenomenology of perception. Translated by C. Smith. Routledge & Kegan Paul. 1962.

records.line: I am never a thing and never naked consciousness, we are objects and subjects at the same time; there is therefore never determinism.
records.reference: Merleau-Ponty, M. (1945). Phenomenology of perception. Translated by C. Smith. Routledge & Kegan Paul. 1962.

records.line: Perceptual experience (and its normativity, representing things that appear elliptical from an oblique angle as being round) is intertwined with our bodily form.
records.reference: Walsh, P. J. & Yoshimi, J. (2017). “Philosophy of Mind in the Phenomenological Tradition”. In Amy Kind (ed.), Philosophy of Mind in the Twentieth and Twenty-First Centuries: The History of the Philosophy of Mind, Volume 6. New York: Routledge.

records.line: The mind does not use the body, but realizes itself through it and comes into the world while transferring the body outside of physical space.
records.reference: Merleau-Ponty, M. (1942). The Structure of Behaviour.

records.line: My body has its world, or understands its world without having to go through ‘representations,’ or being subordinated to a ‘symbolic’ or ‘objectifying function’.
records.reference: Merleau-Ponty, M. (1945). Phenomenology of Perception. Translated by D. Landes. London: Routledge. 2012.

records.line: There is the body as defined by chemistry, by biology, by sociology, etc. – each of these degrees is soul for the preceding, body for the following.
records.reference: Merleau-Ponty, M. (1942). The Structure of Behaviour.

records.line: Each one of us has a unique position in space and time which affects how we perceive and experience the world.
records.reference: Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.

records.line: Our perspectives of the world slip into each other’s and we are brought together in a single world of anonymous subjects of perception.
records.reference: Merleau-Ponty, M. (1945). Phenomenology of perception. Translated by C. Smith. Routledge & Kegan Paul. 1962.

records.line: The objectification of each by the other’s gaze is felt as unbearable only because it takes the place of possible communication.
records.reference: Merleau-Ponty, M. (1945). Phenomenology of perception. Translated by C. Smith. Routledge & Kegan Paul. 1962.

records.line: Solipsism would be strictly true only of someone who is aware of their existence without being or doing anything, which is impossible.
records.reference: Merleau-Ponty, M. (1945). Phenomenology of perception. Translated by C. Smith. Routledge & Kegan Paul. 1962.

records.line: It is absurd that we ask for meaning for our lives in a universe without meaning and purpose.
records.reference: Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.

records.line: The Absurd is not in humans nor in the world, but in their presence together; it is the only bond uniting them.
records.reference: Simpson, D. (n.d.). “Albert Camus”. The Internet Encyclopedia of Philosophy: https://iep.utm.edu/camus/

records.line: There is but one truly serious philosophical problem and that is suicide.
records.reference: Camus, A. (1942). Le Mythe de Sisyphe. Éditions Gallimard.

records.line: To kill oneself is a capitulation; we should refuse to surrender unto the meaninglessness of the universe.
records.reference: Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.

records.line: The religious solution of positing a transcendent world of solace and meaning beyond the Absurd is philosophical suicide.
records.reference: Simpson, D. (n.d.). “Albert Camus”. The Internet Encyclopedia of Philosophy: https://iep.utm.edu/camus/

records.line: Science and rationalism always hit walls in their explanations of the world; the world is unreasonable.
records.reference: Camus, A. (1942). Le Mythe de Sisyphe. Éditions Gallimard.

records.line: The only authentic and valid solution to the Absurd is simply to accept it, or better yet to embrace it, and to continue living. (‘Revolt’)
records.reference: Simpson, D. (n.d.). “Albert Camus”. The Internet Encyclopedia of Philosophy: https://iep.utm.edu/camus/

records.line: True revolt is performed not just for the self but also in solidarity with and out of compassion for others.
records.reference: Simpson, D. (n.d.). “Albert Camus”. The Internet Encyclopedia of Philosophy: https://iep.utm.edu/camus/

records.line: Living with the Absurd brings revolt (against the meaninglessness of life), freedom (from roles, goals, and values), and passion (living life to its fullest).
records.reference: Camus, A. (1942). Le Mythe de Sisyphe. Éditions Gallimard.

records.line: An analysis of rebellion leads at least to the suspicion that, contrary to contemporary thought, a human nature does exist.
records.reference: Simpson, D. (n.d.). “Albert Camus”. The Internet Encyclopedia of Philosophy: https://iep.utm.edu/camus/

records.line: Of all the methods of revolt, creation is the most effective; to work and create “for nothing,” to know one’s creation has no future, is wisdom.
records.reference: Camus, A. (1942). Le Mythe de Sisyphe. Éditions Gallimard.

records.line: Art should not hope to make sense of life, but only to describe it without offering meaning or consolation.
records.reference: Camus, A. (1942). Le Mythe de Sisyphe. Éditions Gallimard.

records.line: Not all laws are orders by a sovereign: there are laws that confer powers on people, reflect custom, or bind the legislators themselves.
records.reference: Hart, H.L.A. (1961). The Concept of Law. Oxford: Oxford University Press, 2nd Edition. 1994.

records.line: A habit of obedience is not sufficient to explain the continuity of legal authority and the persistence of laws in a succession of different legislators.
records.reference: Hart, H.L.A. (1961). The Concept of Law. Oxford: Oxford University Press, 2nd Edition. 1994.

records.line: Law is the union of primary and secondary rules.
records.reference: Hart, H.L.A. (1961). The Concept of Law. Oxford: Oxford University Press, 2nd Edition. 1994.

records.line: Primary (basic) rules are concerned with the actions that individuals must or must not do; they impose duties.
records.reference: Hart, H.L.A. (1961). The Concept of Law. Oxford: Oxford University Press, 2nd Edition. 1994.

records.line: Secondary rules are concerned with the primary rules, specifying the ways in which they may be recognized, changed, and adjudicated.
records.reference: Hart, H.L.A. (1961). The Concept of Law. Oxford: Oxford University Press, 2nd Edition. 1994.

records.line: The rule of recognition is the ultimate rule, socially accepted to provide the criteria of validity for the other rules; it itself can neither be valid nor invalid.
records.reference: Hart, H.L.A. (1961). The Concept of Law. Oxford: Oxford University Press, 2nd Edition. 1994.

records.line: It is in no sense a necessary truth that laws reproduce or satisfy certain demands of morality, though in fact they have often done so.
records.reference: Hart, H.L.A. (1961). The Concept of Law. Oxford: Oxford University Press, 2nd Edition. 1994.

records.line: The general language of legislation needs interpretation and may prove indeterminate in some cases; it is open-textured like natural languages.
records.reference: Hart, H.L.A. (1961). The Concept of Law. Oxford: Oxford University Press, 2nd Edition. 1994.

records.line: It is possible to be concerned with the rules, either merely as an external observer, or as a member of the group which critically accepts and uses them.
records.reference: Hart, H.L.A. (1961). The Concept of Law. Oxford: Oxford University Press, 2nd Edition. 1994.

records.line: Reference to the internal point of view is required for the analysis of the concepts of obligation, duty, legislation, jurisdiction, validity, and legal powers.
records.reference: Hart, H.L.A. (1961). The Concept of Law. Oxford: Oxford University Press, 2nd Edition. 1994.

records.line: Certain facts about humans (vulnerability, approximate equality, limited altruism, etc.) result in common elements in the law and conventional morality.
records.reference: Hart, H.L.A. (1961). The Concept of Law. Oxford: Oxford University Press, 2nd Edition. 1994.

records.line: The painful facts of human history (e.g. slavery) show that a viable society need not offer the same protections and benefits to all of its members.
records.reference: Hart, H.L.A. (1961). The Concept of Law. Oxford: Oxford University Press, 2nd Edition. 1994.

records.line: Accepting the possibility of valid but morally iniquitous laws would make people clear sighted in confronting the official abuse of power.
records.reference: Hart, H.L.A. (1961). The Concept of Law. Oxford: Oxford University Press, 2nd Edition. 1994.

records.line: The function of philosophy is not factual speculation but logical clarification and analysis of (especially scientific) language.
records.reference: Ayer, A.J. (1936). Language, Truth, and Logic. London: Gollancz, 2nd Edition. 1946.

records.line: A statement has meaning iff it is either analytic or (weakly) verifiable – if it is possible for experience to render it probable.
records.reference: Ayer, A.J. (1936). Language, Truth, and Logic. London: Gollancz, 2nd Edition. 1946.

records.line: Analytic propositions are tautological, but there is a sense in which they give us new knowledge: they bring to light the implications of our linguistic usages.
records.reference: Ayer, A.J. (1936). Language, Truth, and Logic. London: Gollancz, 2nd Edition. 1946.

records.line: All metaphysical sentences (and many disputes in philosophy) are meaningless: they involve neither tautologies nor empirical hypotheses.
records.reference: Ayer, A.J. (1936). Language, Truth, and Logic. London: Gollancz, 2nd Edition. 1946.

records.line: The principles of logic and mathematics are true universally because we cannot abandon them without sinning against the rules of language use.
records.reference: Ayer, A.J. (1936). Language, Truth, and Logic. London: Gollancz, 2nd Edition. 1946.

records.line: All synthetic propositions, including the ones describing sense-contents, are hypotheses; there is no absolute validation/invalidation.
records.reference: Ayer, A.J. (1936). Language, Truth, and Logic. London: Gollancz, 2nd Edition. 1946.

records.line: It is never just a single hypothesis which an observation confirms or discredits, but always a system of hypotheses.
records.reference: Ayer, A.J. (1936). Language, Truth, and Logic. London: Gollancz, 2nd Edition. 1946.

records.line: The word ‘truth’ does not stand for anything; the terms ‘true’ and ‘false’ function in the sentence simply as marks of assertion and denial.
records.reference: Ayer, A.J. (1936). Language, Truth, and Logic. London: Gollancz, 2nd Edition. 1946.

records.line: The only test for forms of self-consistent scientific procedures (e.g. induction) is the test of success in practice – in predicting future experience.
records.reference: Ayer, A.J. (1936). Language, Truth, and Logic. London: Gollancz, 2nd Edition. 1946.

records.line: Material things (including people) are logical constructions out of sense-contents; belief in the existence of substances/substrata leads to confusion.
records.reference: Ayer, A.J. (1936). Language, Truth, and Logic. London: Gollancz, 2nd Edition. 1946.

records.line: An object being a logical construction out of sense-contents only means that all sentences referring to it are translatable into sentences referring to them.
records.reference: Ayer, A.J. (1936). Language, Truth, and Logic. London: Gollancz, 2nd Edition. 1946.

records.line: The substantive ego is a fictitious metaphysical entity; to say anything about the self is always to say something about sense-experiences.
records.reference: Ayer, A.J. (1936). Language, Truth, and Logic. London: Gollancz, 2nd Edition. 1946.

records.line: If I know that an object behaves in every way as a conscious being must, by definition, behave, then I know that it is really conscious.
records.reference: Ayer, A.J. (1936). Language, Truth, and Logic. London: Gollancz, 2nd Edition. 1946.

records.line: Statements of (ethical or aesthetic) value have no factual meaning; they only evince ‘emotive’ dis/approval.
records.reference: Ayer, A.J. (1936). Language, Truth, and Logic. London: Gollancz, 2nd Edition. 1946.

records.line: Ethical terms do not serve only to express feeling but also to arouse feeling, and so to stimulate action, even to command.
records.reference: Ayer, A.J. (1936). Language, Truth, and Logic. London: Gollancz, 2nd Edition. 1946.

records.line: The purpose of aesthetic criticism is not so much to give knowledge as to communicate emotion and make us share the critic’s attitude towards the work.
records.reference: Ayer, A.J. (1936). Language, Truth, and Logic. London: Gollancz, 2nd Edition. 1946.

records.line: ‘Basic propositions’ that refer solely to the content of a single experience can be verified conclusively by the occurrence of that experience.
records.reference: Ayer, A.J. (1936). Language, Truth, and Logic. London: Gollancz, 2nd Edition. 1946.

records.line: Ordinary language embodies the inherited experience and acumen of many generations; it is not the last word, but it is the first word.
records.reference: Baldwin, T. (2001). Contemporary Philosophy. Oxford University Press.

records.line: For the objects of perception, the reality-appearance duality is misconceived; the term ‘real’ has many different uses.
records.reference: Baldwin, T. (2001). Contemporary Philosophy. Oxford University Press.

records.line: The application of the positive term (reality, freedom, etc.) depends on its negative term, which is itself contextual.
records.reference: Baldwin, T. (2001). Contemporary Philosophy. Oxford University Press.

records.line: The search for absolute certainty is both fruitless and unwanted.
records.reference: Baldwin, T. (2001). Contemporary Philosophy. Oxford University Press.

records.line: Because a belief is about something, its truth is independent of the fact that it is believed; it must be possible for the thinker to be mistaken.
records.reference: Baldwin, T. (2001). Contemporary Philosophy. Oxford University Press.

records.line: What is evidence for what is always contextual; there is no limit to the kinds of evidence that might turn out to be relevant.
records.reference: Baldwin, T. (2001). Contemporary Philosophy. Oxford University Press.

records.line: There can’t be a theory providing an abstract recipe for the totality of evidence for a particular claim.
records.reference: Baldwin, T. (2001). Contemporary Philosophy. Oxford University Press.

records.line: In sceptical arguments we should locate our doubts only in specific contexts of inquiry.
records.reference: Baldwin, T. (2001). Contemporary Philosophy. Oxford University Press.

records.line: Utterances are not always descriptions (‘constatives’); they can be ‘performatives’ in which we do things with words (promises, threats, etc.).
records.reference: Baldwin, T. (2001). Contemporary Philosophy. Oxford University Press.

records.line: Three types of speech act: locutionary (what I say), illocutionary (what I do in saying that), perlocutionary (what effect it has on the listener).
records.reference: Baldwin, T. (2001). Contemporary Philosophy. Oxford University Press.

records.line: Claims to knowledge (“I know that S is P”) can also be performatives where we give others our authority (for saying that S is P).
records.reference: Baldwin, T. (2001). Contemporary Philosophy. Oxford University Press.

records.line: Representation, unlike resemblance, involves manipulating signs that will be recognized as standing for particular things.
records.reference: Eaton, M. M. (1988). Basic Issues In Aesthetics. Waveland Press.

records.line: The innocent eye is a myth, seeing is never just registering: all thinking is sorting, classifying, and all perceiving relates to expectations and comparisons.
records.reference: Gombrich, E. (1960). Art and Illusion: a Study in the Psychology of Pictorial Representation. London: Phaidon.

records.line: Pictorial realism is not just the faithful copying from an art-independent reality; it involves illusions using conventional perceptual ‘schemata’.
records.reference: Ambrosio, C. (2021). “Feyerabend on art and science”. In Karim Bschir & Jamie Shaw (eds.), Interpreting Feyerabend: Critical Essays. Cambridge University Press.

records.line: Artists cannot escape from the stylistic and pictorial conventions of their cultural background, but they can modify them.
records.reference: Schneider, N. (2009). “Form of Thought and Presentational Gesture in Karl Popper and E. H. Gombrich”. Human Affairs 19 (3).

records.line: The artistic process of first making and then matching and remaking (trial and error) is much like how science works.
records.reference: Gombrich, E. (1960). Art and Illusion: a Study in the Psychology of Pictorial Representation. London: Phaidon.

records.line: The viewer’s practised perception is taken into account during the creative process, so that they can interpret the conventional signs.
records.reference: Schneider, N. (2009). “Form of Thought and Presentational Gesture in Karl Popper and E. H. Gombrich”. Human Affairs 19 (3).

records.line: Visual experience is not entirely determined by cultural conventions: it is organized according to the laws of optics and perspective.
records.reference: Branko, M. (2010). “A defence of light: Ernst Gombrich, the Innocent Eye and seeing in perspective”. Journal of Art Historiography. 3.

records.line: Judgments about qualia are decrees; which ones are accepted is a matter of the overall coherence of my system of beliefs and my other qualia judgments.
records.reference: Cohnitz, D., & Rossberg, M. (n.d.). “Nelson Goodman”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/plato/

records.line: Two terms have the same meaning iff they have the same primary and secondary extensions (the extension of its compounds, like “P-picture”).
records.reference: Cohnitz, D., & Rossberg, M. (n.d.). “Nelson Goodman”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/plato/

records.line: P-descriptions that are not Q-descriptions (“is a P but not a Q”) are easy to construct for any P and Q; strict synonymy is impossible.
records.reference: Cohnitz, D., & Rossberg, M. (n.d.). “Nelson Goodman”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/plato/

records.line: Synonymy and analyticity are matters of degree, depending on how uninteresting the constructs that make a difference in secondary extension are.
records.reference: Cohnitz, D., & Rossberg, M. (n.d.). “Nelson Goodman”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/plato/

records.line: Positional predicates (with definitions referring to place/time constants) can generate mutually incompatible predictions, all confirmed by the past evidence.
records.reference: Cohnitz, D., & Rossberg, M. (n.d.). “Nelson Goodman”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/plato/

records.line: The positionality of a predicate is relative to a language; there is no semantic or syntactic criterion to choose predicates suitable for induction.
records.reference: Cohnitz, D., & Rossberg, M. (n.d.). “Nelson Goodman”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/plato/

records.line: The choice of predicates is a matter of ‘entrenchment’ – of what language we use and have used to describe the behaviour of our world.
records.reference: Cohnitz, D., & Rossberg, M. (n.d.). “Nelson Goodman”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/plato/

records.line: Instances of intuitive inferences and the rules of inference are slowly adjusted to justify each other in a process of reflective equilibrium.
records.reference: Cohnitz, D., & Rossberg, M. (n.d.). “Nelson Goodman”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/plato/

records.line: A hypothesis is ‘projectible’ (suitable for induction) iff it is supported, unviolated, and unexhausted, and all hypotheses conflicting with it are overridden.
records.reference: Cohnitz, D., & Rossberg, M. (n.d.). “Nelson Goodman”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/plato/

records.line: Counterfactual conditionals are a particular variety of strict conditional (restricting attention to those possibilities where all facts ‘co-tenable’ with p are true).
records.reference: Weatherson, B. (n.d.). “David Lewis”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/david-lewis/

records.line: There are conflicting truths that cannot be accommodated in a single world version: we need many worlds to accommodate the conflicting versions.
records.reference: Cohnitz, D., & Rossberg, M. (n.d.). “Nelson Goodman”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/plato/

records.line: Worlds are made by answering to right versions (with entrenchment, simplicity, usefulness, etc.); there are no worlds that correspond to false/possible versions.
records.reference: Cohnitz, D., & Rossberg, M. (n.d.). “Nelson Goodman”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/plato/

records.line: A Reality underlying the worlds must be fully neutral, and thus serves no purpose; it would have no spacetime, no relations, no structure at all.
records.reference: Cohnitz, D., & Rossberg, M. (n.d.). “Nelson Goodman”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/plato/

records.line: The world versions suffice, and are really the only things that are directly accessible; the versions can for many purposes be treated as worlds.
records.reference: Cohnitz, D., & Rossberg, M. (n.d.). “Nelson Goodman”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/plato/

records.line: There is no sharp division between art, science, and philosophy: they all use symbol systems to perceive and understand the world, to construct worlds.
records.reference: Giovannelli, A. (n.d.). “Goodman’s Aesthetics”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/goodman-aesthetics/

records.line: There are logical differences between representation and resemblance; e.g. two chairs resemble, but do not represent, one another.
records.reference: Eaton, M. M. (1988). Basic Issues In Aesthetics. Waveland Press.

records.line: Aesthetics is a branch of epistemology; artworks are made of symbols and require interpretation to understand what they refer to, in which ways and systems.
records.reference: Giovannelli, A. (n.d.). “Goodman’s Aesthetics”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/goodman-aesthetics/

records.line: Denotation (labeling) and exemplification (referring back to the label, like a color swatch) are the two fundamental forms of symbolization in art.
records.reference: Giovannelli, A. (n.d.). “Goodman’s Aesthetics”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/goodman-aesthetics/

records.line: A non-representational work of art expresses something when it metaphorically exemplifies it, by calling attention to some of its features.
records.reference: Giovannelli, A. (n.d.). “Goodman’s Aesthetics”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/goodman-aesthetics/

records.line: Since to be a work of art is to perform certain referential functions, the question “What is art?” should be replaced with the question “When is art?”.
records.reference: Giovannelli, A. (n.d.). “Goodman’s Aesthetics”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/goodman-aesthetics/

records.line: A work of art is ‘autographic’ iff the distinction between original and forgery of it is significant; iff even a perfect duplication does not count as genuine.
records.reference: Goodman, N. (1976). Languages of Art: An Approach to a Theory of Symbols. Hackett Publishing Company.

records.line: A work of art is ‘allographic’ iff it allows for instantiations of the work that are independent of the work’s history of production.
records.reference: Giovannelli, A. (n.d.). “Goodman’s Aesthetics”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/goodman-aesthetics/

records.line: Representational painting lacks the notational features of languages (and allographic arts) that make repetition possible; it is not a language.
records.reference: Eaton, M. M. (1988). Basic Issues In Aesthetics. Waveland Press.

records.line: When we know we are looking at an original painting and its perfect copy, there is an aesthetic difference as the awareness modifies our perception.
records.reference: Giovannelli, A. (n.d.). “Goodman’s Aesthetics”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/goodman-aesthetics/

records.line: There is no innocent eye, how and what it sees is regulated by need and prejudice; it selects, rejects, organizes, associates, classifies, analyzes, constructs.
records.reference: Goodman, N. (1976). Languages of Art: An Approach to a Theory of Symbols. Hackett Publishing Company.

records.line: Pictures can denote things because we have learned the visual conventions (including perspective), not just because they resemble those things.
records.reference: Eaton, M. M. (1988). Basic Issues In Aesthetics. Waveland Press.

records.line: There are red things and meaningful utterances but there may not be additional entities named “redness” or “meaning”.
records.reference: Quine, W. V. O. (1953). From a logical point of view. Harvard University Press.

records.line: The two useful ways in which people talk about meanings – significance and synonymy – could be defined in terms of behavior.
records.reference: Quine, W. V. O. (1953). From a logical point of view. Harvard University Press.

records.line: Necessity depends on our ways of referring to things; it is not a trait of things themselves.
records.reference: Quine, W. V. O. (1953). From a logical point of view. Harvard University Press.

records.line: Ontological controversy tends into controversy over language, but we must not jump to the conclusion that what there is depends on words.
records.reference: Quine, W. V. O. (1953). From a logical point of view. Harvard University Press.

records.line: There is no way to define analyticity without an appeal to synonymy (or semantical rules) defined analytically; it’s a vicious circle.
records.reference: Quine, W. V. O. (1953). From a logical point of view. Harvard University Press.

records.line: A boundary between analytic and synthetic statements cannot be drawn; truth depends both on language and on extra-linguistic fact.
records.reference: Quine, W. V. O. (1953). From a logical point of view. Harvard University Press.

records.line: Our whole knowledge is a network which impinges on experience only along the edges; centrally located statements (e.g. logic, physics) are more resistant.
records.reference: Quine, W. V. O. (1953). From a logical point of view. Harvard University Press.

records.line: There are no principled limits to the range of evidence for a statement; evidence and understanding are holistic.
records.reference: Baldwin, T. (2001). Contemporary Philosophy. Oxford University Press.

records.line: Nothing, including putative conceptual truths, logic, and mathematics, is immune from revision in the light of experience.
records.reference: Baldwin, T. (2001). Contemporary Philosophy. Oxford University Press.

records.line: Even the truths of elementary number theory are presumably not in general derivable by self-evident steps from self-evident truths.
records.reference: Raatikainen, P. (2005). “On the philosophical relevance of Gödel’s incompleteness theorems”. Revue Internationale de Philosophie.

records.line: Science as a whole network has its double dependence upon language and experience; this duality is not traceable into single statements.
records.reference: Quine, W. V. O. (1953). From a logical point of view. Harvard University Press.

records.line: In point of epistemological footing the physical objects and the gods differ only in degree and not in kind; they are both convenient myths.
records.reference: Quine, W. V. O. (1953). From a logical point of view. Harvard University Press.

records.line: The myth of physical objects is epistemologically superior to most in that it has proved more efficacious than other myths.
records.reference: Quine, W. V. O. (1953). From a logical point of view. Harvard University Press.

records.line: The indispensability of mathematics to empirical science gives us good reason to believe in the existence of mathematical entities.
records.reference: Colyvan, M. (n.d.). “Indispensability Arguments in the Philosophy of Mathematics”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/mathphil-indis/

records.line: Epistemology should be naturalized, by studying empirically (psychologically) how people transform sensory input into theoretical output.
records.reference: Quine, W. V. O. (1969). “Epistemology naturalized”. Ontological relativity and other essays. Columbia University Press.

records.line: There is redundancy in our belief networks, so we can question and change one part without compromising all the rest.
records.reference: Baldwin, T. (2001). Contemporary Philosophy. Oxford University Press.

records.line: Translation between languages is underdetermined: there are multiple equally good translations.
records.reference: Baldwin, T. (2001). Contemporary Philosophy. Oxford University Press.

records.line: Indeterminacy of translation implies indeterminacy of communication, even of thinking.
records.reference: Baldwin, T. (2001). Contemporary Philosophy. Oxford University Press.

records.line: When the deviant logician tries to deny the doctrine, they only change the subject; a change of logic entails a change of meaning.
records.reference: Quine, W. V. O. (1970). Philosophy of Logic. Oxforf University Press.

records.line: You can bathe in the same river twice, but not in the same river ‘stage’; ostension, identification, and induction convey spatio-temporal spread.
records.reference: Quine, W. V. O. (1953). From a logical point of view. Harvard University Press.

records.line: External moral requirements are ‘justifying’ reasons for action, different from internal ‘motivating’ reasons.
records.reference: Baldwin, T. (2001). Contemporary Philosophy. Oxford University Press.

records.line: The morality of duty and principles and the morality of virtues are not rivals, but two complementary aspects of the same morality.
records.reference: Frankena, W.K. (1973). Ethics. Prentice-Hall.

records.line: The essence of art coincides with all that is unique in the nature of its medium; effects that might belong to other mediums must be eliminated.
records.reference: Greenberg, C. (1960). “Modernist Painting”. Forum Lectures (Washington, D. C.: Voice of America).

records.line: It has been in search of the absolute that the avant-garde has arrived at abstract art; it is an imperative that comes from history.
records.reference: Greenberg, C. (1939). “Avant-Garde and Kitsch”.

records.line: The avant-garde (abstract) artist imitates God by creating something valid solely on its own terms: increate, independent of meanings, similars, or originals.
records.reference: Greenberg, C. (1939). “Avant-Garde and Kitsch”.
