records.line: Quality in art can be neither ascertained nor proved by logic or discourse; it depends on inspired, felt relations or proportions.
records.reference: Greenberg, C. (1961). “The Identity of Art”.; Greenberg, C. (1969). Avant-Garde Attitudes: New Art in the Sixties. Power Institute of Fine Arts, University of Sydney.

records.line: Things that purport to be art do not function, do not exist, as art until they are experienced through taste.
records.reference: Greenberg, C. (1969). Avant-Garde Attitudes: New Art in the Sixties. Power Institute of Fine Arts, University of Sydney.

records.line: There is a consensus of taste; the best taste is that of the people who spend the most time and trouble on art.
records.reference: Greenberg, C. (1961). “The Identity of Art”.

records.line: We should be stereoscopic with the ‘scientific image’ and the ‘manifest image’ of the world.
records.reference: Baldwin, T. (2001). Contemporary Philosophy. Oxford University Press.

records.line: The manifest image is the framework in which we first came to be aware of ourselves as persons.
records.reference: Baldwin, T. (2001). Contemporary Philosophy. Oxford University Press.

records.line: The conception of the physical world characteristic of the manifest image is incorrect insofar as it conflicts with the scientific image.
records.reference: Baldwin, T. (2001). Contemporary Philosophy. Oxford University Press.

records.line: Knowledge (justification) belongs within the ‘logical space of reasons’; it cannot be grounded in the bare facts of sense-experience. (‘The Myth of the Given’)
records.reference: Baldwin, T. (2001). Contemporary Philosophy. Oxford University Press.

records.line: Sense-experience (‘the Given’) cannot certify the authenticity of information but it can cause us to form reliable beliefs.
records.reference: Baldwin, T. (2001). Contemporary Philosophy. Oxford University Press.

records.line: The unobservables posited by a theory explain directly why observable entities behave the way they do and obey the empirical laws they do.
records.reference: Sellars, W. (1963). Science, Perception and Reality. Atascadero CA: Ridgeview P.C.

records.line: Our concepts of sensory states and psychological states (hope, regret, desire, etc.) are like theoretical concepts in the sciences.
records.reference: DeVries, W. (n.d.). “Wilfrid Sellars”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/sellars/

records.line: We can use the analogy of brain states as sentence-like structures and translate them into our own language to assess them rationally.
records.reference: Baldwin, T. (2001). Contemporary Philosophy. Oxford University Press.

records.line: The aim of philosophy is to understand how things in the broadest possible sense of the term hang together in the broadest possible sense of the term.
records.reference: Sellars, W. (1962). “Philosophy and the Scientific Image of Man”. R. Colodny (Ed.), Frontiers of Science and Philosophy. Pittsburgh, PA: University of Pittsburgh Press.

records.line: The meaning of a referring expression and its referent in a particular context are separate things.
records.reference: Baldwin, T. (2001). Contemporary Philosophy. Oxford University Press.

records.line: A sentence with a vacuous referring expression is meaningful, but is not a statement (true or false).
records.reference: Baldwin, T. (2001). Contemporary Philosophy. Oxford University Press.

records.line: There is a distinction between the contextual presuppositions of a sentence and its logical implications.
records.reference: Baldwin, T. (2001). Contemporary Philosophy. Oxford University Press.

records.line: Ascribing truth to a proposition is performative: the speaker is not characterizing the proposition itself but licensing our adoption of (the belief in) the proposition.
records.reference: Dowden, B., & Swartz, N. (n.d.). “Truth”. The Internet Encyclopedia of Philosophy. https://iep.utm.edu/truth/

records.line: Sceptical doubts amount to the rejection of the whole conceptual scheme within which such doubt make sense.
records.reference: Baldwin, T. (2001). Contemporary Philosophy. Oxford University Press.

records.line: To ask whether it is reasonable to rely on induction is like asking whether it is reasonable to proportion the degree of one’s convictions to the strength of the evidence.
records.reference: Strawson, P. (1952). Introduction to logical theory. Methuen.

records.line: Self-consciousness requires that there is a distinction between ourselves and others; thus, others really exist.
records.reference: Baldwin, T. (2001). Contemporary Philosophy. Oxford University Press.

records.line: The basic structure of our conceptual scheme lies so deep in our thought and practice that its legitimation is neither possible nor necessary.
records.reference: Baldwin, T. (2001). Contemporary Philosophy. Oxford University Press.

records.line: The primitive concept of a person allows us to see human life with physical and (irreducible) mental aspects without being dualistic.
records.reference: Baldwin, T. (2001). Contemporary Philosophy. Oxford University Press.

records.line: Meaning can be ‘natural’ (e.g. spots meaning measles) or ‘nonnatural’ (dependent on communicators’ intentions).
records.reference: Grice, P. (1957). “Meaning”. Philosophical Review 66 (3):377-388.

records.line: When something has natural meaning, what it means cannot be false; when something has nonnatural meaning, what it means can be false.
records.reference: Grice, P. (1957). “Meaning”. Philosophical Review 66 (3):377-388.

records.line: Not all things with meanings are signs (e.g. words are not), and some things with nonnatural meaning are not conventional (e.g. certain gestures).
records.reference: Grice, P. (1957). “Meaning”. Philosophical Review 66 (3):377-388.

records.line: “A meant something by x” is roughly equivalent to “A uttered x with the intention of inducing a belief by means of the recognition of this intention”.
records.reference: Grice, P. (1989). Studies in the Way of Words. Harvard University Press.

records.line: “x means (nonnatural) (timeless) that so-and-so” might be equated with some statement about what “people” (vague) intend to effect by x.
records.reference: Grice, P. (1957). “Meaning”. Philosophical Review 66 (3):377-388.

records.line: Intentions are transmitted perlocutionarily, by an effect produced by the speaker on the listener that is exterior to the functioning of linguistic conventions.
records.reference: Moati, R. (2014). Derrida/Searle. Columbia University Press.

records.line: The ‘conversational implicature’ of an utterance can be different from its strict meaning; it is central to humor, rudeness, etc.
records.reference: Baldwin, T. (2001). Contemporary Philosophy. Oxford University Press.

records.line: Two simultaneous experiences are had by the same person because they can be known by introspection to be simultaneous.
records.reference: Grice, P. (1941). “Personal Identity”, Mind, 50: 330–350.

records.line: All knowledge claims go beyond their evidence, are highly presumptive and corrigible.
records.reference: Campbell, D. T. (1969). “A phenomenology of the other one: Corrigible, hypothetical, and critical”. T. Mischel (Ed.), Human action: Conceptual and empirical issues. New York: Academic Press.

records.line: Knowledge needs to be explained using biological, psychological, sociological, historical knowledge; no first philosophy is required. (‘evolutionary epistemology’)
records.reference: Gontier, N. (n.d.). “Evolutionary epistemology”. The Internet Encyclopedia of Philosophy. https://iep.utm.edu/evo-epis/

records.line: An ‘epistemology of the other one’ must deal with how organisms come to know, not how a first-person knower acquires knowledge.
records.reference: Campbell, D. T. (1974). “Evolutionary epistemology”. P. A. Schlipp (Ed.), The philosophy of Karl Popper (Vol. 1, pp. 413-459). La Salle.

records.line: There are different vehicles carrying knowledge, with different physical structures: genes, paper, electronic chips, etc.
records.reference: Callebaut, W. (n.d.). “Campbell, Donald Thomas.” Encyclopedia.com. https://www.encyclopedia.com/science/dictionaries-thesauruses-pictures-and-press-releases/campbell-donald-thomas

records.line: There are different knowledge mechanisms: non-mnemonic problem solving, habit, instinct, cultural transmission, science, etc.
records.reference: Gontier, N. (n.d.). “Evolutionary epistemology”. The Internet Encyclopedia of Philosophy. https://iep.utm.edu/evo-epis/

records.line: A blind-variation-and-selective-retention (BVSR) process is fundamental to all inductive achievements, to all genuine increases in knowledge.
records.reference: Campbell, D. T. (1960). “Blind variation and selective retention in creative thought as in other knowledge processes”. Psychological Review, 67, 380-400.

records.line: The many processes (e.g. vision) which shortcut a more full BVSR process contain wisdom about the environment achieved originally by BVSR.
records.reference: Campbell, D. T. (1960). “Blind variation and selective retention in creative thought as in other knowledge processes”. Psychological Review, 67, 380-400.

records.line: The shortcut processes contain in their own operation a BVSR process at some level (e.g. vision substituting for overt locomotor exploration).
records.reference: Campbell, D. T. (1960). “Blind variation and selective retention in creative thought as in other knowledge processes”. Psychological Review, 67, 380-400.

records.line: Innovative thought is a BVSR process; “foresight” is a result, once the process has blindly stumbled into a thought trial that fits the selection criterion.
records.reference: Campbell, D. T. (1960). “Blind variation and selective retention in creative thought as in other knowledge processes”. Psychological Review, 67, 380-400.

records.line: Biological systems are hierarchically structured, and there is ‘downward causation’ when a higher structure operates causally upon its substructure.
records.reference: Callebaut, W. (n.d.). “Campbell, Donald Thomas.” Encyclopedia.com. https://www.encyclopedia.com/science/dictionaries-thesauruses-pictures-and-press-releases/campbell-donald-thomas

records.line: Aesthetics is meta-criticism; there would be no problems of aesthetics if no one ever talked about works of art.
records.reference: Beardsley, M. (1958). Aesthetics: Problems in the philosophy of criticism. Harcourt, Brace.

records.line: In aesthetic experience, we attend to the regional qualities (color, pitch, etc.) and the formal unity (patterns or the organization of parts) of an object.
records.reference: Eaton, M. M. (1988). Basic Issues In Aesthetics. Waveland Press.

records.line: The detachedness of aesthetic experience blocks artworks’ referential characteristics from referring to anything outside of the experience.
records.reference: Dickie, G. (2005). “Beardsley, Monroe”. Encyclopedia of Philosophy. https://www.encyclopedia.com/humanities/encyclopedias-almanacs-transcripts-and-maps/beardsley-monroe-1915-1985

records.line: A work of art is an arrangement of conditions intended to be capable of affording an experience with marked aesthetic character.
records.reference: Beardsley, M. (1982). The Aesthetic Point of View: Selected Essays. Cornell University Press.

records.line: Aesthetic experience, and thus aesthetic objects, are instrumentally valuable, being productive of human welfare.
records.reference: Dickie, G. (2005). “Beardsley, Monroe”. Encyclopedia of Philosophy. https://www.encyclopedia.com/humanities/encyclopedias-almanacs-transcripts-and-maps/beardsley-monroe-1915-1985

records.line: In aesthetic experience, the ‘presentation’ is one’s particular experience of a particular ‘performance’ of a particular ‘production’ of an ‘artifact’.
records.reference: Wreen, M. (n.d.). “Beardsley’s Aesthetics”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/beardsley-aesthetics/

records.line: In painting and sculpture, the distinction between artifact and production almost disappears.
records.reference: Beardsley, M. (1958). Aesthetics: Problems in the philosophy of criticism. Harcourt, Brace.

records.line: The aesthetic object, the primary object of critical attention, is the production.
records.reference: Wreen, M. (n.d.). “Beardsley’s Aesthetics”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/beardsley-aesthetics/

records.line: External evidence – evidence from the psychological and social background of the object – is forbidden in art criticism.
records.reference: Wreen, M. (n.d.). “Beardsley’s Aesthetics”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/beardsley-aesthetics/

records.line: A person’s affective responses to a work of art are irrelevant to its descriptive, interpretive, and evaluative properties.
records.reference: Wreen, M. (n.d.). “Beardsley’s Aesthetics”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/beardsley-aesthetics/

records.line: The artist’s intentions are irrelevant to the descriptive, interpretive, and evaluative properties of the work.
records.reference: Wreen, M. (n.d.). “Beardsley’s Aesthetics”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/beardsley-aesthetics/

records.line: What a sentence means depends not on the whim of the individual but upon conventions of usage; an author can be wrong about what their own work means.
records.reference: Wreen, M. (n.d.). “Beardsley’s Aesthetics”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/beardsley-aesthetics/

records.line: What poets and other authors of literary works do is represent the performance of illocutionary acts, not perform the illocutionary acts themselves.
records.reference: Wreen, M. (n.d.). “Beardsley’s Aesthetics”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/beardsley-aesthetics/

records.line: If we look at what we call “art”, we will find no common properties – only strands of similarities, “family resemblances”.
records.reference: Weitz, M. (1956). “The role of theory in aesthetics”. Journal of Aesthetics and Art Criticism, 15(1), 27-35.

records.line: The expansive, adventurous, innovative character of art makes it logically impossible to insure any set of defining properties; art is an open concept.
records.reference: Weitz, M. (1956). “The role of theory in aesthetics”. Journal of Aesthetics and Art Criticism, 15(1), 27-35.

records.line: Most literary works contain printed or suggested truth claims which are called upon to take as serious commentaries on life.
records.reference: Weitz, M. (1955). “Truth in literature”. Revue Internationale de Philosophie, 9(31), 116-129.

records.line: Sometimes truth is irrelevant to aesthetic assessment, but claims made in artworks can also be aesthetically relevant in some cases.
records.reference: Eaton, M. M. (1988). Basic Issues In Aesthetics. Waveland Press.

records.line: Myth is a second-order semiological system, a metalanguage: a sign (‘meaning’) in the first-order language becomes a signifier (‘form’) in the second.
records.reference: Barthes, R. (1957). Mythologies. Hill and Wang: New York. 1972.

records.line: Not only written discourse, but also photography, cinema, reporting, sport, shows, publicity, all these can serve as a support to mythical speech.
records.reference: Barthes, R. (1957). Mythologies. Hill and Wang: New York. 1972.

records.line: Myth hides nothing, its function is to steal and distort: it purifies things, it makes them innocent, it gives them a natural and eternal justification.
records.reference: Barthes, R. (1957). Mythologies. Hill and Wang: New York. 1972.

records.line: Myth in a bourgeois society is depoliticized speech: it suggests a world where bourgeois norms are experienced as the evident laws of a natural order.
records.reference: Barthes, R. (1957). Mythologies. Hill and Wang: New York. 1972.

records.line: There is one language which is not mythical: that of the oppressed, speaking in order to transform reality and not to preserve it as an image.
records.reference: Barthes, R. (1957). Mythologies. Hill and Wang: New York. 1972.

records.line: It is language which speaks, not the author; to write is to reach that point where only language acts, ‘performs’, and not ‘me’.
records.reference: Barthes, R. (1967). “The Death of the Author”. Image—Music—Text. Hill and Wang: New York. 1977.

records.line: The Author is thought to exist before the book, thinking, suffering, living for it, like a parent; the modern scriptor is born simultaneously with the text.
records.reference: Barthes, R. (1967). “The Death of the Author”. Image—Music—Text. Hill and Wang: New York. 1977.

records.line: A text does not have a single ‘theological’ meaning; it is a multi-dimensional space in which a variety of writings, none of them original, blend and clash.
records.reference: Barthes, R. (1967). “The Death of the Author”. Image—Music—Text. Hill and Wang: New York. 1977.

records.line: The work closes on a signified (evident or interpreted); the Text practises the infinite deferment of the signified: its field is that of the signifier.
records.reference: Barthes, R. (1971). “From Work to Text”. Image—Music—Text. Hill and Wang: New York. 1977.

records.line: The work grows by ‘development’, like an organism; the Text is like a network and it can extend itself as a result of a combinatory systematic.
records.reference: Barthes, R. (1971). “From Work to Text”. Image—Music—Text. Hill and Wang: New York. 1977.

records.line: The ‘writerly’ text makes the reader no longer a passive consumer (like the classic ‘readerly’ texts do), but a producer of the text.
records.reference: Barthes, R. (1970). “S/Z: An Essay”. Hill and Wang: New York. 1975.

records.line: The destination of a text cannot any longer be personal: the reader is without history, biography, psychology.
records.reference: Barthes, R. (1967). “The Death of the Author”. Image—Music—Text. Hill and Wang: New York. 1977.

records.line: The text itself plays and the reader plays twice over; playing the Text as one plays a game, but also playing it in the musical sense of the term.
records.reference: Barthes, R. (1971). “From Work to Text”. Image—Music—Text. Hill and Wang: New York. 1977.

records.line: The pleasure of the work remains a pleasure of consumption; as for the Text, it is bound to jouissance, that is to a pleasure without separation.
records.reference: Barthes, R. (1971). “From Work to Text”. Image—Music—Text. Hill and Wang: New York. 1977.

records.line: Historically, the reign of the Author has also been that of the Critic; criticism is today undermined along with the Author.
records.reference: Barthes, R. (1967). “The Death of the Author”. Image—Music—Text. Hill and Wang: New York. 1977.

records.line: Propositions must be distinguished from assertions: a predicate may be attached to a subject in a subordinate clause.
records.reference: Kenny, A. (2015). “Peter Thomas Geach”. Biographical Memoirs of Fellows of the British Academy, XIV, 185–203.

records.line: Names and predicables differ from each other: a name has a complete sense, a predicable does not, containing an empty place to be filled by a subject.
records.reference: Kenny, A. (2015). “Peter Thomas Geach”. Biographical Memoirs of Fellows of the British Academy, XIV, 185–203.

records.line: Not only proper names but also common nouns can occur as the subjects of sentences; we may tell a story switching between “Jemima” and “the cat”.
records.reference: Kenny, A. (2015). “Peter Thomas Geach”. Biographical Memoirs of Fellows of the British Academy, XIV, 185–203.

records.line: There is no such thing as absolute identity (without some general term): A and B may be the same F, but not the same G.
records.reference: Kenny, A. (2015). “Peter Thomas Geach”. Biographical Memoirs of Fellows of the British Academy, XIV, 185–203.

records.line: There is no such thing as being just good or bad, there is only being a good or bad so-and-so; ‘good’ and ‘bad’ are attributive, not predicative.
records.reference: Geach, P. (1956). “Good and Evil”. Analysis, 17(2), 33-42.

records.line: Sentences expressing moral judgments can be embedded in semantically complex sentences in a way that a noncognitivist cannot explain.
records.reference: Marturano, A. (n.d.). “Non-Cognitivism in Ethics”. The Internet Encyclopedia of Philosophy. https://iep.utm.edu/non-cogn/
records.keywords: Frege-Geach problem, embedding problem, moral cognitivism

records.line: An ought thesis can follow logically from factual premises together with a vacuous deontic premise.
records.reference: Geach, P. (1977). “Again the Logic of ‘Ought’”. Philosophy, 52(202), 473-476.

records.line: A mental state cannot be defined as a disposition for a behavior without referring to the other mental states (beliefs, desires) of the agent.
records.reference: Geach, P. (1957). Mental Acts: Their Content and Their Objects. London: Routledge & Kegan Paul.

records.line: The intentional modes of description needed for human activities are logically not derivable from natural science.
records.reference: Kenny, A. (2015). “Peter Thomas Geach”. Biographical Memoirs of Fellows of the British Academy, XIV, 185–203.

records.line: Humans have distinctive mental capacities (e.g. rationality) that cannot be explained by Darwinian processes; they are miraculously created.
records.reference: Murray, M. J. (2001). Review of “Peter Geach (1998) Truth and Hope”. University of Notre Dame Press.

records.line: Materialists (“We think with the brain”) and immaterialists (“We think with some non bodily part”) are both wrong: there is no such thing as an organ of thought.
records.reference: Kenny, A. (2015). “Peter Thomas Geach”. Biographical Memoirs of Fellows of the British Academy, XIV, 185–203.

records.line: The one reality, the divine creation ultimately rooted in God himself is the ultimate truthmaker; God is Truth.
records.reference: Murray, M. J. (2001). Review of “Peter Geach (1998) Truth and Hope”. University of Notre Dame Press.

records.line: God is not omnipotent but rather he is almighty: he has power over all things.
records.reference: Kenny, A. (2015). “Peter Thomas Geach”. Biographical Memoirs of Fellows of the British Academy, XIV, 185–203.

records.line: There is no future there for God to see: he knows the future not by seeing it but by controlling it.
records.reference: Kenny, A. (2015). “Peter Thomas Geach”. Biographical Memoirs of Fellows of the British Academy, XIV, 185–203.

records.line: Sympathy with animal suffering would not be a virtue in God: it is a virtue in humans only because we share an animal nature.
records.reference: Kenny, A. (2015). “Peter Thomas Geach”. Biographical Memoirs of Fellows of the British Academy, XIV, 185–203.

records.line: Since no mistake of reference is possible for ‘I’, it cannot be a referring expression at all; it is a word with a use but no reference.
records.reference: Richter, D. (n.d.). “G. E. M. Anscombe”. The Internet Encyclopedia of Philosophy. https://iep.utm.edu/anscombe/

records.line: Any causal law linking a cause with an effect will have to be of the form “If C then E, other things being equal” – very often other things are not equal.
records.reference: Richter, D. (n.d.). “G. E. M. Anscombe”. The Internet Encyclopedia of Philosophy. https://iep.utm.edu/anscombe/

records.line: We can know that one thing caused another without knowing any true law involving a necessary connection between events of general types.
records.reference: Richter, D. (n.d.). “G. E. M. Anscombe”. The Internet Encyclopedia of Philosophy. https://iep.utm.edu/anscombe/

records.line: Actions are identified and understood by reference to the intentions with which they were performed.
records.reference: Baldwin, T. (2001). Contemporary Philosophy. Oxford University Press.

records.line: The ‘practical knowledge’ of action is different from the ‘contemplative’ knowledge of science.
records.reference: Baldwin, T. (2001). Contemporary Philosophy. Oxford University Press.

records.line: Unintentional causes of action (‘mental causes’) are not reasons for action.
records.reference: Baldwin, T. (2001). Contemporary Philosophy. Oxford University Press.

records.line: The intention is not a cause: causes are distinct from their effects, whereas intentional actions are not distinct from the intentions they embody.
records.reference: Richter, D. (n.d.). “G. E. M. Anscombe”. The Internet Encyclopedia of Philosophy. https://iep.utm.edu/anscombe/

records.line: We know what we are doing because we know our intentions, and we do not have to look and see what we are doing to have this knowledge.
records.reference: Richter, D. (n.d.). “G. E. M. Anscombe”. The Internet Encyclopedia of Philosophy. https://iep.utm.edu/anscombe/

records.line: There are objects of perception in both a material and an intentional sense; idealism and ordinary language philosophy (direct realism) conflate them.
records.reference: Wiland, E. & Driver, J. (n.d.). “Gertrude Elizabeth Margaret Anscombe”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/anscombe/

records.line: Thinking of morality as a set of rules prescribing what we “ought” to do presupposes a religious conception about what is “owed” to God.
records.reference: Baldwin, T. (2001). Contemporary Philosophy. Oxford University Press.

records.line: A secular culture should construct a new morality based on our understanding of particular virtues and vices.
records.reference: Baldwin, T. (2001). Contemporary Philosophy. Oxford University Press.

records.line: A secular morality requires a new philosophy of mind which will break with the old ways of thinking about the roles of beliefs and desires in explaining action.
records.reference: Baldwin, T. (2001). Contemporary Philosophy. Oxford University Press.

records.line: Getting one another to do things without the application of physical force – through institutions like promising – is a necessity for human life.
records.reference: Anscombe, G. E. M. (1969). “On Promising and Its Justice, and Whether It Needs be Respected In Foro Interno”. Crítica: Revista Hispanoamericana de Filosofía, 3(7/8), 61–83.

records.line: The practice of the virtues should connect with a potentiality for ‘human flourishing’.
records.reference: Baldwin, T. (2001). Contemporary Philosophy. Oxford University Press.

records.line: Actions can be bad and can be known to be bad without observing them or their results.
records.reference: Richter, D. (n.d.). “G. E. M. Anscombe”. The Internet Encyclopedia of Philosophy. https://iep.utm.edu/anscombe/

records.line: Utilitarianism cannot sustain any strict prohibitions because it cannot distinguish between intended and merely foreseen consequences.
records.reference: Baldwin, T. (2001). Contemporary Philosophy. Oxford University Press.

records.line: Masturbation, sex with contraception, and gay sex are abuses of the sexual organs that can never lead to procreation – an insult to life itself.
records.reference: Richter, D. (n.d.). “G. E. M. Anscombe”. The Internet Encyclopedia of Philosophy. https://iep.utm.edu/anscombe/

records.line: Intention and action are not distinct existences; reasons are not causes.
records.reference: Baldwin, T. (2001). Contemporary Philosophy. Oxford University Press.

records.line: The relationship between an action and a reason does not have to be the same every time and for every agent, unlike a causal relationship.
records.reference: Baldwin, T. (2001). Contemporary Philosophy. Oxford University Press.

records.line: The study of society is about the logical relationships between the beliefs and intentions, and the rules of social institutions.
records.reference: Baldwin, T. (2001). Contemporary Philosophy. Oxford University Press.

records.line: Social studies are not causal inquiries; there cannot be a social science.
records.reference: Baldwin, T. (2001). Contemporary Philosophy. Oxford University Press.

records.line: The truth-conditions of a sentence provide its meaning: someone who knows under what conditions a sentence would be true understands that sentence.
records.reference: Davidson, D. (2005). Truth, Language, and History: Philosophical Essays Volume 5. Oxford, GB: Clarendon Press.

records.line: We get to meaning by interpreting others, by presuming that their beliefs are largely true and that they are generally rational.
records.reference: Baldwin, T. (2001). Contemporary Philosophy. Oxford University Press.

records.line: Language isn’t a network of social conventions; there are just the general grammatical capacities and individual communicative strategies.
records.reference: Baldwin, T. (2001). Contemporary Philosophy. Oxford University Press.

records.line: There is an autonomy of meaning whereby speakers can distance themselves from the literal meaning of their words (jokes, irony, etc.).
records.reference: Baldwin, T. (2001). Contemporary Philosophy. Oxford University Press.

records.line: Languages provide ways of thinking about and expressing truths about this world, so they are translatable.
records.reference: Baldwin, T. (2001). Contemporary Philosophy. Oxford University Press.

records.line: One cannot be a thinker at all unless one has beliefs.
records.reference: Baldwin, T. (2001). Contemporary Philosophy. Oxford University Press.

records.line: One cannot have a belief unless one recognizes that one might be in error.
records.reference: Baldwin, T. (2001). Contemporary Philosophy. Oxford University Press.

records.line: It is only through seeing others’ errors that one can understand the possibility of being in error oneself.
records.reference: Baldwin, T. (2001). Contemporary Philosophy. Oxford University Press.

records.line: A creature cannot have thoughts unless it is an interpreter of the speech of another; “no thought without talk.”
records.reference: Baldwin, T. (2001). Contemporary Philosophy. Oxford University Press.

records.line: A new replica of me could not be said to have thoughts or meaningful words, without the causal history to establish the connections between itself and the world.
records.reference: Malpas, J. (n.d.). “Donald Davidson”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/davidson/
records.keywords: semantic externalism, causal theory of reference, holism

records.line: The relation between a sensation and a belief cannot be logical, since sensations are not beliefs or other propositional attitudes; the relation is causal.
records.reference: Davidson, D. (1986). “A Coherence Theory of Truth and Knowledge”. Truth And Interpretation: Perspectives on the Philosophy of Donald Davidson (pp. 307-319). Oxford: Basil Blackwell.

records.line: At least some mental events interact causally with physical events.
records.reference: Davidson, D. (1970). “Mental events”. Essays on actions and events (pp. 207-225). Oxford: Clarendon Press.

records.line: Where there is causality, there must be a law: events related as cause and effect fall under strict deterministic laws.
records.reference: Davidson, D. (1970). “Mental events”. Essays on actions and events (pp. 207-225). Oxford: Clarendon Press.

records.line: Mental concepts, insofar as they involve propositional attitudes partly identified by their logical relations, are normative; physical concepts are not.
records.reference: Davidson, D. (1999). “Anomalous monism”. R. A. Wilson & F. C. Keil (Eds.), MIT encyclopedia of the cognitive sciences. Cambridge, MA: MIT Press.

records.line: Mental entities are neither definitionally nor nomologically reducible to the vocabulary of physics; there are no strict (‘homonomic’) psychophysical laws.
records.reference: Davidson, D. (1999). “Anomalous monism”. R. A. Wilson & F. C. Keil (Eds.), MIT encyclopedia of the cognitive sciences. Cambridge, MA: MIT Press.

records.line: Mental events (taken as unrepeatable, dated individuals) are identical with physical events.
records.reference: Davidson, D. (1970). “Mental events”. Essays on actions and events (pp. 207-225). Oxford: Clarendon Press.
records.keywords: anomalous monism, token physicalism, mind-brain identity theory

records.line: Reasons and actions are in causal relationships in their physical states but these relationships cannot be subsumed under law.
records.reference: Baldwin, T. (2001). Contemporary Philosophy. Oxford University Press.

records.line: Mental characteristics ‘supervene’ on physical characteristics: there cannot be two events alike in all physical respects but differing in mental respects.
records.reference: Davidson, D. (1970). “Mental events”. Essays on actions and events (pp. 207-225). Oxford: Clarendon Press.

records.line: Facts really are the true statements themselves; facts are not named by them.
records.reference: Dowden, B., & Swartz, N. (n.d.). “Truth”. The Internet Encyclopedia of Philosophy. https://iep.utm.edu/truth/

records.line: The relation between the wanting to do and the doing is logical (conceptual, a priori), so it cannot be a causal one.
records.reference: Melden, A. (1961). Free Action. London: Routledge and Kegan Paul.

records.line: Actions cannot be causally determined; the free will debate should not arise at all.
records.reference: Baldwin, T. (2001). Contemporary Philosophy. Oxford University Press.

records.line: The passage of time is an illusion: if it really passed, then we could ask at what rate it passes, but this requires a second time-dimension, and so on.
records.reference: Smart, J. J. C. (1949). “The River of Time”. Mind, 58(232), 483-494.

records.line: The universe is a four-dimensional space-time manifold; present, past and future are all equally real.
records.reference: Smart, J. J. C. (1968). Between science and philosophy. New York: Random House.

records.line: If the phenomenalist about theoretical entities is correct, we must believe in cosmic coincidence in our successful scientific predictions.
records.reference: Smart, J. J. C. (1963). Philosophy and Scientific Realism. Routledge.

records.line: States and processes of the mind are identical to states and processes of the brain.
records.reference: Smart, J. J. C. (2007). “The Mind/Brain Identity Theory”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/archives/spr2017/entries/mind-identity/

records.line: A person can talk about their sensations without knowing about their brain processes, just as they can talk about lightning without knowing anything of electricity.
records.reference: Smart, J. J. C. (1959). “Sensations and Brain Processes”, Philosophical Review, 68:141–156.

records.line: “I have a yellowish-orange after-image” only means “There is something going on which is like what is going on when I see an orange”.
records.reference: Smart, J. J. C. (1959). “Sensations and Brain Processes”, Philosophical Review, 68:141–156.

records.line: The apparent features of experience are not genuine properties “in the mind” or “in the world” but only the contents of mental representations.
records.reference: Rey, G. (n.d.). “Philosophy of mind”. Encyclopædia Britannica. https://www.britannica.com/topic/philosophy-of-mind/Qualitative-states

records.line: Neither determinism nor pure chance leaves room for free will and moral responsibility, and there is no third possibility.
records.reference: Smart, J. J. C. (1961). “Free will, praise and blame”. Mind 70 (279):291-306.

records.line: The metaphysical question of determinism is quite irrevelant to the rationality of our (pragmatic) ascription of responsibility.
records.reference: Smart, J. J. C. (1961). “Free will, praise and blame”. Mind 70 (279):291-306.

records.line: The rightness of individual actions is to be judged by their particular consequences; general rules, like “keep promises”, are mere rules of thumb.
records.reference: Smart, J. J. C. (1956). “Extreme and Restricted Utilitarianism”. The Philosophical Quarterly, 6(25), 344-354.

records.line: Obeying rules where we know that to break them will have better results is a form of superstitious rule-worship.
records.reference: Smart, J. J. C. (1956). “Extreme and Restricted Utilitarianism”. The Philosophical Quarterly, 6(25), 344-354.

records.line: We do not want to obtain our pleasures by operating electrodes stimulating the pleasure centers in our brains; we want to achieve things.
records.reference: Smart, J. J. C. (1961). An Outline of a System of Utilitarian Ethics. Carlton: Melbourne University Press.

records.line: Ethics is the logical study of the language of morals.
records.reference: Baldwin, T. (2001). Contemporary Philosophy. Oxford University Press.

records.line: There can be no ethical difference between two possible states of affairs or actions without there being some natural difference between them.
records.reference: McPherson, T. (n.d.). “Supervenience in Ethics”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/supervenience-ethics/

records.line: There is a logical distinction between facts and values; morality is ‘prescriptive’ and is not a domain of moral facts to be ‘described’.
records.reference: Baldwin, T. (2001). Contemporary Philosophy. Oxford University Press.

records.line: Value-terms have a special function in language, that of commending; they cannot be defined in terms of other words which do not perform this function.
records.reference: Hare, H. R. (1952). The Language of Morals. Oxford: Oxford University Press.

records.line: Moral imperatives are instances of principles that we are prepared to universalize.
records.reference: Baldwin, T. (2001). Contemporary Philosophy. Oxford University Press.

records.line: Actions are right if they maximize the satisfaction of preferences or desires, no matter what the preferences may be for.
records.reference: Britannica, T. Editors of Encyclopaedia (n.d.). “Consequentialism”. Encyclopedia Britannica. https://www.britannica.com/topic/consequentialism

records.line: Imaginative identification with others reveals principles that provide the best ways of fulfilling the preferences of all those who might be affected.
records.reference: Baldwin, T. (2001). Contemporary Philosophy. Oxford University Press.

records.line: If there were objective values, they would be very strange things, utterly different from anything else in the universe; they are metaphysically queer.
records.reference: Mackie, J. (1977). Ethics: Inventing right and wrong. Pelican Books.

records.line: If there were objective values, our awareness of them would require a strange kind of special faculty; they are epistemologically queer.
records.reference: Mackie, J. (1977). Ethics: Inventing right and wrong. Pelican Books.

records.line: The variations of moral codes and beliefs imply that they reflect ways of life, not that they express perceptions of objective values.
records.reference: Mackie, J. (1977). Ethics: Inventing right and wrong. Pelican Books.

records.line: Although most people making moral judgments implicitly claim to be pointing to something objectively prescriptive, these claims are all false.
records.reference: Mackie, J. (1977). Ethics: Inventing right and wrong. Pelican Books.
records.keywords: error theory, moral cognitivism, moral anti-realism

records.line: The final stage of the universalization of moral judgements, taking account of different tastes and rival ideals, is logically impossible.
records.reference: Mackie, J. (1977). Ethics: Inventing right and wrong. Pelican Books.

records.line: A logical or semantic truth is no real constraint on belief, action, prescription, or choice of policy.
records.reference: Mackie, J. (1977). Ethics: Inventing right and wrong. Pelican Books.

records.line: The utilitarian calculus is an unhelpful myth; there is no such common measure of all interests and purposes as happiness or utility is supposed to be.
records.reference: Mackie, J. (1977). Ethics: Inventing right and wrong. Pelican Books.

records.line: Concrete moral issues can be argued out without appeal to any mythical objective values or obligations, or to a fictitious unitary measurable happiness.
records.reference: Mackie, J. (1977). Ethics: Inventing right and wrong. Pelican Books.

records.line: Morality is not to be discovered but to be made and maintained (and in part remade as time passes); we have to decide what moral views to adopt.
records.reference: Mackie, J. (1977). Ethics: Inventing right and wrong. Pelican Books.

records.line: Our evolved moral sentiments and dispositions, and more formal rules along with politico-legal devices are complementary parts of the device of morality.
records.reference: Mackie, J. (1977). Ethics: Inventing right and wrong. Pelican Books.

records.line: Socially established – and socially necessary – patterns of behaviour are ‘objectified’ into moral values to put pressure on individuals.
records.reference: Mackie, J. (1977). Ethics: Inventing right and wrong. Pelican Books.

records.line: Egoism and self-referential altruism (concern for others who have some special connection with oneself) form a central part of the good life.
records.reference: Mackie, J. (1977). Ethics: Inventing right and wrong. Pelican Books.

records.line: A virtue is a disposition which is usually advantageous for its possessor and which harmonizes with knowledge, with seeing things as they are.
records.reference: Mackie, J. (1977). Ethics: Inventing right and wrong. Pelican Books.

records.line: The idea of a non-empirical free self that is not caused by one’s character, desires, etc. is incoherent; we shouldn’t base our concept of freedom on it.
records.reference: Mackie, J. (1977). Ethics: Inventing right and wrong. Pelican Books.

records.line: An agent is responsible for all and only their intentional actions; even if determinism holds, the moral principles will still have their points.
records.reference: Mackie, J. (1977). Ethics: Inventing right and wrong. Pelican Books.

records.line: A cause is an insufficient but necessary (non-redundant) part of an unnecessary but sufficient (‘INUS’) condition for the effect.
records.reference: Mackie, J. (1974). The Cement of the Universe. Oxford: Clarendon Press.

records.line: There could not be logically contingent states of affairs, prior to the creation of free beings, which an omnipotent god would have to accept.
records.reference: Mackie, J. (1982). The Miracle of Theism: Arguments for and Against the Existence of God. Oxford University Press.

records.line: The propositions that a good omnipotent thing exists, and that evil exists, are logically incompatible.
records.reference: Mackie, J. (1955). “Evil and omnipotence”. Mind 64 (254):200-212.

records.line: If an action is in our interests, we have reason to do it, and the virtues are always in one’s interest.
records.reference: Hacker-Wright, J. (n.d.). “Philippa Foot”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/philippa-foot/

records.line: The “categorical” commands of morality are like those of etiquette: one can rationally ignore them, lacking the purpose of being moral.
records.reference: Hacker-Wright, J. (n.d.). “Philippa Foot”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/philippa-foot/

records.line: Quite apart from thoughts of duty a person may care about the suffering of others, having a sense of identification with them and wanting to help.
records.reference: Foot, P. (1978). Virtues and vices and other essays in moral philosophy (2nd ed.). Oxford University Press. 2022.

records.line: It is sometimes permissible to bring about a bad result that one foresees as a consequence of one’s action but does not directly intend. (double effect)
records.reference: Foot, P. (1967). “The Problem of Abortion and the Doctrine of Double Effect”, Oxford Review, 5: 5–15.

records.line: There is a moral distinction between doing and allowing; we may not steal someone’s property, yet we may not be required to secure their possession of it.
records.reference: Hacker-Wright, J. (n.d.). “Philippa Foot”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/philippa-foot/

records.line: It is impermissible to kill (to initiate a causal sequence that kills) one person to save another; the negative rights have priority over the positive rights.
records.reference: Hacker-Wright, J. (n.d.). “Philippa Foot”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/philippa-foot/

records.line: In the Trolley Problem (killing one vs. five people), it is preferable for the driver to minimize the violation of negative rights by turning the trolley.
records.reference: Foot, P. (1967). “The Problem of Abortion and the Doctrine of Double Effect”, Oxford Review, 5: 5–15.

records.line: The distinction between avoiding injury and bringing aid seems more important than the ones between direct-indirect intention or between doing-allowing.
records.reference: Foot, P. (1967). “The Problem of Abortion and the Doctrine of Double Effect”, Oxford Review, 5: 5–15.

records.line: Virtue is a disposition of the will: good intentions are not sufficient, we must take the trouble to cultivate the desire to act well.
records.reference: Hacker-Wright, J. (n.d.). “Philippa Foot”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/philippa-foot/

records.line: The virtues are needed for humans as a corrective to general human characteristics that make it difficult for us to act well.
records.reference: Hacker-Wright, J. (n.d.). “Philippa Foot”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/philippa-foot/

records.line: Imperatives with no relevance to human interests do not become moral requirements simply by being universally prescribed.
records.reference: Baldwin, T. (2001). Contemporary Philosophy. Oxford University Press.

records.line: Description and prescription are not separate in morality; value-judgements enter into descriptions.
records.reference: Baldwin, T. (2001). Contemporary Philosophy. Oxford University Press.

records.line: There are objective and natural features of living things that make them good as members of their species; moral goodness is one of them for us.
records.reference: Hacker-Wright, J. (n.d.). “Philippa Foot”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/philippa-foot/

records.line: Evaluations of human will and action share a conceptual structure with evaluations of characteristics and operations of other living things.
records.reference: Foot, P. (2001). Natural Goodness. Oxford Clarendon Press.

records.line: Evaluations of human will and action should be determined – just like those of human sight, hearing, etc. – by facts about the nature of our species.
records.reference: Foot, P. (2001). Natural Goodness. Oxford Clarendon Press.

records.line: Mere pleasure or contentment is not identical with human good: objects of deep happiness (home, family, work, friendship, etc.) must be taken into account.
records.reference: Foot, P. (2001). Natural Goodness. Oxford Clarendon Press.

records.line: Moral action is rational action and goodness is a necessary condition of practical rationality.
records.reference: Foot, P. (2001). Natural Goodness. Oxford Clarendon Press.

records.line: To ask for a reason for acting rationally (and thus morally) is to ask for a reason where reasons must a priori have come to an end.
records.reference: Foot, P. (2001). Natural Goodness. Oxford Clarendon Press.

records.line: Virtues can be described in terms of practical rationality: the recognition of reasons for acting determined by general facts about human beings.
records.reference: Foot, P. (2001). Natural Goodness. Oxford Clarendon Press.

records.line: Moral considerations are on par with other sets of considerations (self-interest, desire-fulfillment, etc.) that generate reasons; one must weigh them all.
records.reference: Hacker-Wright, J. (n.d.). “Philippa Foot”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/philippa-foot/

records.line: Moral and political philosophy have no place for a rigid analytic/synthetic distinction.
records.reference: Baldwin, T. (2001). Contemporary Philosophy. Oxford University Press.

records.line: If the plurality of persons with separate ends is an essential feature of societies, the principles of social choice cannot be utilitarian.
records.reference: Rawls, J. (1971). A Theory of Justice. Harvard University Press.

records.line: The construction of justice is guided by our recognition of a range of primary, fundamental goods.
records.reference: Baldwin, T. (2001). Contemporary Philosophy. Oxford University Press.

records.line: Primary goods: basic rights and liberties, freedom of movement and choice, powers and positions of responsibility, income and wealth, the social bases of self-respect.
records.reference: Rawls, J. (2001). Justice as Fairness. Harvard University Press.

records.line: The principles of justice should be chosen from an ‘original position’ of equality without knowing our position in society, behind a ‘veil of ignorance’.
records.reference: Rawls, J. (1971). A Theory of Justice. Harvard University Press.

records.line: Each person is to have an equal right to the most extensive total system of equal basic liberties compatible with a similar system of liberty for all.
records.reference: Rawls, J. (1971). A Theory of Justice. Harvard University Press.

records.line: Social and economic inequalities are to be arranged so that they are attached to offices and positions open to all under conditions of fair equality of opportunity.
records.reference: Rawls, J. (1971). A Theory of Justice. Harvard University Press.

records.line: Social and economic inequalities are to be arranged so that they are to the greatest benefit of the least advantaged. (‘difference principle’)
records.reference: Rawls, J. (1971). A Theory of Justice. Harvard University Press.

records.line: Inequalities in income and wealth could be accepted when these work effectively to improve everyone’s situation starting from equal division.
records.reference: Rawls, J. (2001). Justice as Fairness. Harvard University Press.

records.line: All reasonable (or decent) persons would construct political ideals that benefit all liberal peoples. (‘overlapping consensus’)
records.reference: Kleist, C. (n.d.). “Global Ethics: Capabilities Approach”. The Internet Encyclopedia of Philosophy. https://www.iep.utm.edu/ge-capab/

records.line: The policies constructed by liberal peoples should direct non-liberal societies to (ideally) all become liberal.
records.reference: Kleist, C. (n.d.). “Global Ethics: Capabilities Approach”. The Internet Encyclopedia of Philosophy. https://www.iep.utm.edu/ge-capab/

records.line: While liberal societies should attempt to tolerate illiberal societies initially, an outlaw state eventually subjects itself to sanctions and intervention.
records.reference: Kleist, C. (n.d.). “Global Ethics: Capabilities Approach”. The Internet Encyclopedia of Philosophy. https://www.iep.utm.edu/ge-capab/

records.line: While citizens may have religious reasons that favor a policy, they must eventually “translate” these reasons into terms that non-believers could accept.
records.reference: Cherem, M. (n.d.). “Jürgen Habermas (1929—)”. The Internet Encyclopedia of Philosophy. https://iep.utm.edu/habermas/

records.line: Aesthetic properties are only perceived; they cannot be reasoned from lists of non-aesthetic properties – they are not condition-governed.
records.reference: Eaton, M. M. (1988). Basic Issues In Aesthetics. Waveland Press.

records.line: We experience things aesthetically when we exercise our taste – special perceptiveness, sensitivity, or powers of discrimination.
records.reference: Eaton, M. M. (1988). Basic Issues In Aesthetics. Waveland Press.

records.line: Agreement (convergence) among people with taste constitutes the basis from which objective standards can be generated in aesthetic cases.
records.reference: Eaton, M. M. (1988). Basic Issues In Aesthetics. Waveland Press.

records.line: Logic and observation are not enough to define scientific reasoning; we must look at its history and at scientists themselves.
records.reference: Baldwin, T. (2001). Contemporary Philosophy. Oxford University Press.

records.line: Sciences alternate between periods of ‘normal science’ (‘puzzle-solving’) and periods of ‘crisis’ followed by ‘revolution’.
records.reference: Baldwin, T. (2001). Contemporary Philosophy. Oxford University Press.

records.line: It’s only when a paradigm is established that empirical underdetermination is solved and normal science is possible.
records.reference: Baldwin, T. (2001). Contemporary Philosophy. Oxford University Press.

records.line: The paradigms eventually use up their capacity to solve empirical puzzles, and are replaced by new paradigms.
records.reference: Baldwin, T. (2001). Contemporary Philosophy. Oxford University Press.

records.line: A new paradigm is ‘incommensurable’ with the old one; there is no uncontested evidence and shared nomenclature to choose one.
records.reference: Baldwin, T. (2001). Contemporary Philosophy. Oxford University Press.

records.line: A procedure of judgement by the scientific community is required for paradigm-choice.
records.reference: Baldwin, T. (2001). Contemporary Philosophy. Oxford University Press.

records.line: If any and every failure to fit the facts were ground for theory rejection, all theories ought to be rejected at all times.
records.reference: Kuhn, T. (1962). The Structure of Scientific Revolutions. The University of Chicago Press.

records.line: There is no precise answer to the question how well a theory fits the facts, but we can ask which of two competing theories fits the facts better.
records.reference: Kuhn, T. (1962). The Structure of Scientific Revolutions. The University of Chicago Press.

records.line: Falsification consists in the triumph of a new paradigm; it might equally well be called verification.
records.reference: Kuhn, T. (1962). The Structure of Scientific Revolutions. The University of Chicago Press.

records.line: The immediate experience of a scientist is also paradigm-embodied: they see a pendulum when they look at a swinging stone.
records.reference: Kuhn, T. (1962). The Structure of Scientific Revolutions. The University of Chicago Press.

records.line: There is no absolute distinction between the context of discovery and the context of justification.
records.reference: Baldwin, T. (2001). Contemporary Philosophy. Oxford University Press.

records.line: We may have to relinquish the notion that paradigm changes carry scientists closer and closer to the truth.
records.reference: Kuhn, T. (1962). The Structure of Scientific Revolutions. The University of Chicago Press.

records.line: The development of science is like biological evolution: an increase in articulation and specialization, without an end goal.
records.reference: Kuhn, T. (1962). The Structure of Scientific Revolutions. The University of Chicago Press.

records.line: The unit of scientific evaluation is not the individual theory, but a sequence of theories, the ‘research program’ (RP).
records.reference: Musgrave, A., & Pigden, C. (n.d.). “Imre Lakatos”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/lakatos/

records.line: Each RP has a refutation-resistant hard core, stubbornly but rationally defended, and often devoid of empirical consequences.
records.reference: Musgrave, A., & Pigden, C. (n.d.). “Imre Lakatos”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/lakatos/

records.line: Each RP has a protective belt of auxiliary hypotheses which has to bear the brunt of tests and gets adjusted or replaced, to defend the hard core.
records.reference: Lakatos, I. (1970). “Falsification and the methodology of scientific research programmes”. Lakatos, I., & Musgrave, A. (Eds.). Criticism and the growth of knowledge (pp. 91-196). Cambridge: Cambridge University Press.

records.line: The RP as a whole (hard core plus auxiliary hypotheses) entails empirical predictions and is a falsifiable affair.
records.reference: Musgrave, A., & Pigden, C. (n.d.). “Imre Lakatos”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/lakatos/

records.line: Each RP, at any stage, has unsolved problems and undigested anomalies; all theories, in this sense, are born refuted and die refuted.
records.reference: Lakatos, I. (1974). “Science and Pseudoscience”. G. Vesey (Ed.), Philosophy in the Open. Open University Press.

records.line: It may be rational to put an inconsistency discovered in a RP into some temporary, ad hoc quarantine, and carry on.
records.reference: Lakatos, I. (1970). “Falsification and the methodology of scientific research programmes”. Lakatos, I., & Musgrave, A. (Eds.). Criticism and the growth of knowledge (pp. 91-196). Cambridge: Cambridge University Press.

records.line: A RP is progressive if its successive theories deliver novel predictions and some of those novel predictions turn out to be true.
records.reference: Musgrave, A., & Pigden, C. (n.d.). “Imre Lakatos”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/lakatos/

records.line: A degenerating RP either fails to predict novel facts at all, or makes novel predictions that are systematically falsified; it may in the end become pseudoscience.
records.reference: Musgrave, A., & Pigden, C. (n.d.). “Imre Lakatos”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/lakatos/

records.line: The demarcation of science/non-science is a matter of degree, and can vary with time: a RP might degenerate but later recover its scientific status.
records.reference: Musgrave, A., & Pigden, C. (n.d.). “Imre Lakatos”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/lakatos/

records.line: Marxism started out as theoretically progressive but empirically degenerate and ended up as theoretically degenerate as well.
records.reference: Musgrave, A., & Pigden, C. (n.d.). “Imre Lakatos”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/lakatos/

records.line: The development of mathematics is an evolutionary, dialectical process: concepts/proofs are initially open-ended and become sharper within a protracted debate.
records.reference: Musgrave, A., & Pigden, C. (n.d.). “Imre Lakatos”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/lakatos/

records.line: We cannot just confront a theory with “the observed facts”; the identity and the significance of these facts are underdetermined.
records.reference: Baldwin, T. (2001). Contemporary Philosophy. Oxford University Press.

records.line: The history of science includes revolutions; ‘incommensurable’ alternatives replace existing theories.
records.reference: Oberheim, E., & Hoyningen-Huene, P. (n.d.). “The Incommensurability of Scientific Theories”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/incommensurability/

records.line: Science flourishes best when there is a proliferation of different paradigms (‘epistemological anarchism’).
records.reference: Baldwin, T. (2001). Contemporary Philosophy. Oxford University Press.

records.line: Aesthetic criteria, personal whims, and social factors have a decisive role in the history of science.
records.reference: Preston, J. (n.d.). “Paul Feyerabend”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/feyerabend/

records.line: Conventions are not used only in the absence of the object, but exert their influence even when a direct account of the visible object is attempted.
records.reference: Feyerabend, P. (1965). Problems of empiricism. New York: Cambridge University Press.

records.line: The history of science is so complex that if we insist on a general methodology the only “rule” will be the useless suggestion: “anything goes”.
records.reference: Preston, J. (n.d.). “Paul Feyerabend”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/feyerabend/

records.line: Science is close to myth; it is one of the many forms of thought developed by humans, and not necessarily the best.
records.reference: Feyerabend, P. (1975). Against Method. London: Verso.

records.line: The separation of church and state should be supplemented by the separation of science and state, in order for us to achieve the humanity we are capable of.
records.reference: Preston, J. (n.d.). “Paul Feyerabend”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/feyerabend/

records.line: To defend society against science we should place science under democratic control and be intensely sceptical about scientific “experts”.
records.reference: Preston, J. (n.d.). “Paul Feyerabend”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/feyerabend/

records.line: Different ideologies are different representations of our social and imaginary “reality”, not representations of the Real itself.
records.reference: Felluga, D. F. (2015). Critical theory: The key concepts. Routledge.

records.line: ‘Theoretical practice’ of science weeds out ideological notions associated with the original concepts, allowing specific research programs to progress.
records.reference: Lewis, W. (n.d.). “Louis Althusser”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/althusser/

records.line: Genuine philosophy functions as a ‘Theory of theoretical practice’ helping science by distinguishing between ideological concepts and scientific ones.
records.reference: Lewis, W. (n.d.). “Louis Althusser”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/althusser/

records.line: ‘Epistemological breaks’, like Marx’s founding of Historical Materialism, are periods when ideological concepts are replaced by scientific ones.
records.reference: Lewis, W. (n.d.). “Louis Althusser”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/althusser/

records.line: Scientific, ideological, political, and philosophical practices are important parts of the mode of production, not reducible to economic production.
records.reference: Lewis, W. (n.d.). “Louis Althusser”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/althusser/

records.line: Whatever conceptions we have of the nature of human beings or about the proper function of the state are historically generated; they are ideological.
records.reference: Lewis, W. (n.d.). “Louis Althusser”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/althusser/

records.line: History has no subject (such as the economy or human agency) or no goal (such as communism or human freedom) and it does not necessarily “progress”.
records.reference: Lewis, W. (n.d.). “Louis Althusser”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/althusser/

records.line: There is no criterion sufficient to demarcate scientific from ideological concepts; all theoretical concepts are marked by ideology.
records.reference: Lewis, W. (n.d.). “Louis Althusser”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/althusser/

records.line: Through a rigorous “scientific” approach to society, economics, and history, we can come close to perceiving the ways that we are inscribed in ideology.
records.reference: Felluga, D. F. (2015). Critical theory: The key concepts. Routledge.

records.line: Philosophers’ communication of science to politicians and the public can lead to a modest shift from an idealist ideology to one that is more scientific.
records.reference: Lewis, W. (n.d.). “Louis Althusser”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/althusser/

records.line: Hegemony is reliant less on the Repressive State Apparatus than it is on ‘Ideological State Apparatuses’ (ISAs).
records.reference: Felluga, D. F. (2015). Critical theory: The key concepts. Routledge.

records.line: There are religious, educational, family, legal, political, trade-union, communications (press, TV, etc.), and cultural (arts, sports, etc.) ISAs.
records.reference: Althusser, L. (1970). “Ideology and Ideological State Apparatuses”. La Pensée.

records.line: ISAs provide the individuals with categories in which they can recognize themselves, believing that their positions within the society are natural.
records.reference: Lewis, W. (n.d.). “Louis Althusser”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/althusser/

records.line: The individuals are ‘interpellated’ as (free) ‘subjects’ by the ISAs in order that they shall make the actions of their subjection all by themselves.
records.reference: Felluga, D. F. (2015). Critical theory: The key concepts. Routledge.

records.line: A Dictatorship of the Proletariat is necessary to create ISAs productive of proletarian or communist subjects.
records.reference: Lewis, W. (n.d.). “Louis Althusser”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/althusser/

records.line: The applications of our concepts and words change over time; we need an archaeology of our thinking.
records.reference: Buckingham, W. et al. (2011). The Philosophy Book. DK Pub.

records.line: We are not shaped by inevitable anthropological constraints but by historical and political forces that can be changed or overthrown.
records.reference: May, T. (2005). Gilles Deleuze: An Introduction. Cambridge University Press.

records.line: Each society has its regime of truth: the types of discourse which it accepts as true, the procedures which are valorized for obtaining truth, etc.
records.reference: Foucault, M. (1972-1977). Power/Knowledge: Selected Interviews and Other Writings 1972-1977. Translated by C. Gordon. Vintage. 1980.

records.line: The concept of ‘man’ is a recent invention, arising at the beginning of the 19th century, and it may be close to its end.
records.reference: Buckingham, W. et al. (2011). The Philosophy Book. DK Pub.

records.line: ‘Man’ is a paradoxical idea, seen both as object and subject.
records.reference: Buckingham, W. et al. (2011). The Philosophy Book. DK Pub.

records.line: Every kind of discourse is an attempt to exercise power over others.
records.reference: Magee, B. (1998). The Story of Philosophy. DK Pub.

records.line: There are three primary techniques of control: hierarchical observation, normalizing judgment, and the examination.
records.reference: Gutting, G., & Oksala, J. (n.d.). “Michel Foucault”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/foucault/

records.line: The modern prison is a model for factories, hospitals, and schools; a model to control an entire society.
records.reference: Gutting, G., & Oksala, J. (n.d.). “Michel Foucault”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/foucault/

records.line: The categories of maleness and femaleness were founded and explained as “natural” in discourses claiming the status of scientific truth.
records.reference: Gutting, G., & Oksala, J. (n.d.). “Michel Foucault”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/foucault/

records.line: The mission to liberate our repressed sexuality is misguided because there is no authentic or natural sexuality to liberate.
records.reference: Gutting, G., & Oksala, J. (n.d.). “Michel Foucault”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/foucault/

records.line: Power is not repressive but productive; it produces, by cultural practices and scientific discourses, the ways we conceive of ourselves (sexuality, madness, etc.).
records.reference: Gutting, G., & Oksala, J. (n.d.). “Michel Foucault”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/foucault/

records.line: Individuals internalize the norms laid down by the sciences of sexuality, psychology, etc. and monitor themselves to conform to these norms.
records.reference: Gutting, G., & Oksala, J. (n.d.). “Michel Foucault”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/foucault/

records.line: We should analyze power relations from the bottom up (families, workplaces, etc.) instead of looking for a center of power.
records.reference: Gutting, G., & Oksala, J. (n.d.). “Michel Foucault”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/foucault/

records.line: One way of contesting power is by shaping oneself creatively, with new ways of being, new fields of experience, pleasures, relationships, etc.
records.reference: Gutting, G., & Oksala, J. (n.d.). “Michel Foucault”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/foucault/

records.line: Marxism exists in nineteenth-century thought like a fish in water: it is unable to breathe anywhere else.
records.reference: Foucault, M. (1966). Les mots et les choses: Une archéologie des sciences humaines. Éditions Gallimard.

records.line: With computers, knowledge has become information that can be stored in databases, and bought and sold.
records.reference: Buckingham, W. et al. (2011). The Philosophy Book. DK Pub.

records.line: Knowledge is becoming externalized; it is no longer something that helps towards the development of minds.
records.reference: Buckingham, W. et al. (2011). The Philosophy Book. DK Pub.

records.line: Knowledge is becoming disconnected from questions of truth; it becomes a commodity.
records.reference: Buckingham, W. et al. (2011). The Philosophy Book. DK Pub.

records.line: Postmodernism is incredulity towards meta-narratives – overarching stories that put all of our knowledge into a single framework.
records.reference: Buckingham, W. et al. (2011). The Philosophy Book. DK Pub.

records.line: In the computer age the question of knowledge is now more than ever a question of government, of power.
records.reference: Woodward, A. (n.d.). “Jean-François Lyotard”. The Internet Encyclopedia of Philosophy. https://iep.utm.edu/lyotard/

records.line: Science does not have any justification in claiming to be a more legitimate form of knowledge than narrative.
records.reference: Woodward, A. (n.d.). “Jean-François Lyotard”. The Internet Encyclopedia of Philosophy. https://iep.utm.edu/lyotard/

records.line: The principle of performativity (efficiency) merely subordinates science to capital.
records.reference: Woodward, A. (n.d.). “Jean-François Lyotard”. The Internet Encyclopedia of Philosophy. https://iep.utm.edu/lyotard/

records.line: Postmodern science is about the generation of new ideas rather than the efficient application of existing knowledge.
records.reference: Woodward, A. (n.d.). “Jean-François Lyotard”. The Internet Encyclopedia of Philosophy. https://iep.utm.edu/lyotard/

records.line: Modern art presents the fact that there is an unpresentable, while postmodern art attempts to present the unpresentable, creating the sublime.
records.reference: Woodward, A. (n.d.). “Jean-François Lyotard”. The Internet Encyclopedia of Philosophy. https://iep.utm.edu/lyotard/

records.line: Matter and life are dynamic; we should see being not in terms of stable identities but in terms of difference.
records.reference: May, T. (2005). Gilles Deleuze: An Introduction. Cambridge University Press.

records.line: Philosophy is the art of forming, inventing, and fabricating concepts palpating the difference that disrupts all projects of identification.
records.reference: May, T. (2005). Gilles Deleuze: An Introduction. Cambridge University Press.

records.line: Philosophy does not consist in knowing; rather, it is categories like Interesting, Remarkable, or Important that determine success or failure.
records.reference: Guattari, F., & Deleuze, G. (1991). Qu’est-ce que la philosophie? Les éditions de Minuit.

records.line: Being is univocal and immanent: it folds, unfolds, and refolds itself into the specific forms that constitute the world of our experience.
records.reference: May, T. (2005). Gilles Deleuze: An Introduction. Cambridge University Press.

records.line: The viewpoint of the human subject is not the only perspective; the lives (unfoldings) of other things (an organ, a group, an epoch, etc.) matter.
records.reference: May, T. (2005). Gilles Deleuze: An Introduction. Cambridge University Press.

records.line: Substance is temporal in character: the past is a realm of virtual differences that exist in the present in actualized form.
records.reference: May, T. (2005). Gilles Deleuze: An Introduction. Cambridge University Press.

records.line: Duration is a unity: not merely a unity of past and present, but of past, present, and future.
records.reference: May, T. (2005). Gilles Deleuze: An Introduction. Cambridge University Press.

records.line: The eternal return is the recurrence of difference itself; to affirm it is to set free the active and unpredictable forces of creativity.
records.reference: May, T. (2005). Gilles Deleuze: An Introduction. Cambridge University Press.

records.line: Tools, organs, individuals, groups, states, etc. and combinations of these can all be seen as ‘machines’ in terms of the creative connections they make.
records.reference: May, T. (2005). Gilles Deleuze: An Introduction. Cambridge University Press.

records.line: ‘Machines’ are not mechanisms; they evolve, mutate, and reconnect with other machines, in creative and unpredictable ways.
records.reference: May, T. (2005). Gilles Deleuze: An Introduction. Cambridge University Press.

records.line: Desire, seen machinically, is a creator of connections (sexual, political, etc.), not a lack that must be filled.
records.reference: May, T. (2005). Gilles Deleuze: An Introduction. Cambridge University Press.

records.line: Economic modes of production do not define machinic connections; it is the other way around.
records.reference: May, T. (2005). Gilles Deleuze: An Introduction. Cambridge University Press.

records.line: Our political thought must not be like a tree (with a definite structure) but like a rhizome with no beginning/end and no particular shape.
records.reference: May, T. (2005). Gilles Deleuze: An Introduction. Cambridge University Press.

records.line: We are composed of lines of rigid segmentarity (family-profession, etc.) and lines of flight carrying us across segments in unpredictable ways.
records.reference: May, T. (2005). Gilles Deleuze: An Introduction. Cambridge University Press.

records.line: The state does not mediate or liberate; it is parasitic, powerful, and oppressive – a force for conformity through abstract machines like discipline.
records.reference: May, T. (2005). Gilles Deleuze: An Introduction. Cambridge University Press.

records.line: Capitalism deterritorializes, but instead of freeing lines of flight, it captures and stifles them to serve its own purposes.
records.reference: May, T. (2005). Gilles Deleuze: An Introduction. Cambridge University Press.

records.line: Oedipus is an abstract machine of capitalism: it says that your desires are really directed toward your own family, placing them in the service of capital.
records.reference: May, T. (2005). Gilles Deleuze: An Introduction. Cambridge University Press.

records.line: There is a kind of transhistorical essence in art (independent of particular styles), but it only discloses itself through history.
records.reference: Danto, A. C. (1997). After the end of art: Contemporary art and the pale of history. Princeton University Press.

records.line: Artists live and produce within the horizon of a closed historical period; some of the limitations are technical, some stylistic.
records.reference: Danto, A. C. (1997). After the end of art: Contemporary art and the pale of history. Princeton University Press.

records.line: To see something as art requires something the eye cannot decry – an atmosphere of artistic theory, a knowledge of the history of art: an art world.
records.reference: Danto, A. C. (1964). “The art world”. The Journal of Philosophy, 61(19), 580-581.

records.line: The decisions of the art world concerning works of art are not merely fiats of arbitrary will; they are less a matter of declaration than of discovery.
records.reference: Danto, A. C. (1997). After the end of art: Contemporary art and the pale of history. Princeton University Press.

records.line: To see an artwork without knowing it is an artwork is comparable to what one’s experience of print is, before one learns to read.
records.reference: Danto, A. C. (1981). The transfiguration of the commonplace: A philosophy of art. Harvard University Press.

records.line: Art is naming less a practice than a movement or even a period, with marked temporal boundaries.
records.reference: Danto, A. C. (1997). After the end of art: Contemporary art and the pale of history. Princeton University Press.

records.line: Art came to an end (in the 1960s) when it became self-conscious and turned to philosophy for a definition which is not based on styles or narratives.
records.reference: Danto, A. C. (1997). After the end of art: Contemporary art and the pale of history. Princeton University Press.

records.line: To be a work of art is to be (i) ‘about’ something and (ii) to ‘embody its meaning’.
records.reference: Danto, A. C. (1981). The transfiguration of the commonplace: A philosophy of art. Harvard University Press.

records.line: There is a difference between the work of art and the physical body that embodies it; the same physical object might embody different artworks.
records.reference: Danto, A. C. (1981). The transfiguration of the commonplace: A philosophy of art. Harvard University Press.

records.line: Artworks and real things cannot any longer be told apart by visual inspection alone (e.g. pop art, conceptual art).
records.reference: Danto, A. C. (1997). After the end of art: Contemporary art and the pale of history. Princeton University Press.

records.line: In post-historical art without a master narrative, anything can be art; it is a stable period of freedom, pluralism, and happiness.
