records.reference: Danto, A. C. (1997). After the end of art: Contemporary art and the pale of history. Princeton University Press.

records.line: A general theory of quality might contain aesthetic goodness not as a defining trait but as a special case after the end of art.
records.reference: Danto, A. C. (1997). After the end of art: Contemporary art and the pale of history. Princeton University Press.

records.line: Everything is possible in that all forms are ours, but to mention, not to use; we must still relate to them in our own way, partly defining our period.
records.reference: Danto, A. C. (1997). After the end of art: Contemporary art and the pale of history. Princeton University Press.

records.line: The true heroes of the post-historical period are the artists who are masters of every style without having a painterly style at all.
records.reference: Danto, A. C. (1997). After the end of art: Contemporary art and the pale of history. Princeton University Press.

records.line: The possibility of transformative personal experiences justifies the production, the maintenance, and the exhibition (museums) of art.
records.reference: Danto, A. C. (1997). After the end of art: Contemporary art and the pale of history. Princeton University Press.

records.line: Post-historical art seeks a more immediate contact with people than the museum makes possible.
records.reference: Danto, A. C. (1992). Beyond the Brillo Box: The Visual Arts in Post-Historical Perspective. New York: Farrar, Straus and Giroux.

records.line: A pluralistic art world calls for a pluralistic art criticism which takes each work up on its own terms (history, causes, embodied meanings, references, etc.).
records.reference: Danto, A. C. (1997). After the end of art: Contemporary art and the pale of history. Princeton University Press.

records.line: A work of art is an artifact upon which the status of candidate for appreciation is conferred by a certain social institution (the artworld).
records.reference: Dickie, G. (1974). Art and the Aesthetic: An Institutional Analysis. Cornell University Press.

records.line: When an artwork’s status depends upon non-exhibited characteristics a simple look at the object will not necessarily reveal that status.
records.reference: Dickie, G. (1974). Art and the Aesthetic: An Institutional Analysis. Cornell University Press.

records.line: There are not various sorts of attending, but only attending or failing to attend; the concept of an aesthetic attitude is not viable.
records.reference: Eaton, M. M. (1988). Basic Issues In Aesthetics. Waveland Press.

records.line: Aesthetic experience is special because it involves attending to some things rather than others; it is not how we attend, but what we attend to that matters.
records.reference: Eaton, M. M. (1988). Basic Issues In Aesthetics. Waveland Press.

records.line: A speaker’s understanding of their own language comprises an ability to conceptualize the world.
records.reference: Baldwin, T. (2001). Contemporary Philosophy. Oxford University Press.

records.line: Psychology and linguistics can offer causal theories of language, but language is best seen as an activity of rational agents.
records.reference: Baldwin, T. (2001). Contemporary Philosophy. Oxford University Press.

records.line: Understanding one aspect of language doesn’t require an understanding of the rest of it.
records.reference: Baldwin, T. (2001). Contemporary Philosophy. Oxford University Press.

records.line: Words have meaning only insofar as they contribute to the meaning of sentences.
records.reference: Baldwin, T. (2001). Contemporary Philosophy. Oxford University Press.

records.line: Communication is of the essence of language.
records.reference: Baldwin, T. (2001). Contemporary Philosophy. Oxford University Press.

records.line: Speakers internalize conventional rules that are social norms; understanding requires reference to this social context.
records.reference: Baldwin, T. (2001). Contemporary Philosophy. Oxford University Press.

records.line: Understanding is based upon sensitivity to direct, canonical evidence for sentences, which is limited.
records.reference: Baldwin, T. (2001). Contemporary Philosophy. Oxford University Press.

records.line: Reality is constrained by the possibility of knowledge of it; it has gaps because the truths of some sentences are undecidable.
records.reference: Baldwin, T. (2001). Contemporary Philosophy. Oxford University Press.

records.line: The statements of science are either true or false and their truth/falsity does not consist in describing regularities in human experience.
records.reference: Putnam, H. (1979). Mathematics, Matter and Method. Cambridge University Press.

records.line: If science was not describing an objective reality, then it would be a miracle that scientific theories were successful in prediction and control.
records.reference: Putnam, H. (1978). “Realism and Reason” in Meaning and the Moral Sciences, London, Routledge and Kegan Paul, 123-38.
records.keywords: no miracles argument, abduction, scientific realism

records.line: Quantification over mathematical entities is indispensable for science; we should accept the existence of mathematical entities in question.
records.reference: Putnam, H. (1971). Philosophy of logic. Allen & Unwin.

records.line: “Truth” is some sort of ideal coherence of our beliefs with each other and with our experiences, not correspondence with mind-independent “states of affairs”.
records.reference: Putnam, H. (1981). Reason, Truth and History. Cambridge University Press.

records.line: “Objects” do not exist independently of conceptual schemes; we cut up the world into objects when we introduce one or another scheme of description.
records.reference: Putnam, H. (1981). Reason, Truth and History. Cambridge University Press.

records.line: There can be equally coherent but incompatible conceptual schemes which fit our experience equally well.
records.reference: Putnam, H. (1981). Reason, Truth and History. Cambridge University Press.

records.line: The meaning of a concept depends on its role in a network (‘cluster’) of concepts, which can change in the light of experience.
records.reference: Baldwin, T. (2001). Contemporary Philosophy. Oxford University Press.

records.line: If the analytic sentences are all consequences of some finite list of meaning postulates, then there must be synthetic truths in mathematics.
records.reference: Raatikainen, P. (2005). “On the philosophical relevance of Gödel’s incompleteness theorems”. Revue Internationale de Philosophie.

records.line: The meanings of words and the identities of our thoughts are not purely psychological, they are affected by their worldly context.
records.reference: Baldwin, T. (2001). Contemporary Philosophy. Oxford University Press.

records.line: Our scientific knowledge allows us to reach essentialist hypotheses such as “Water = H₂O”.
records.reference: Baldwin, T. (2001). Contemporary Philosophy. Oxford University Press.

records.line: One cannot refer to certain kinds of things, e.g. trees, if one has no causal interaction at all with them, or with things in terms of which they are described.
records.reference: Putnam, H. (1981). Reason, Truth and History. Cambridge University Press.
records.keywords: causal theory of reference, semantic externalism

records.line: A brain in a vat being deceived by mad scientists cannot think of brains or vats; they play no part in explaining its neural inputs.
records.reference: Baldwin, T. (2001). Contemporary Philosophy. Oxford University Press.

records.line: We cannot be brains in vats being deceived by mad scientists, because we can think of brains in vats.
records.reference: Baldwin, T. (2001). Contemporary Philosophy. Oxford University Press.

records.line: We can imagine people who develop the ability to suppress all involuntary pain behavior (and even talk of pain); logical behaviorism is mistaken.
records.reference: Putnam, H. (1965). “Brains and behavior”. In Sydney Shoemaker (ed.), Review of Analytical Philosophy, Ronald J. Butler (ed.). Blackwell.

records.line: Mental states are not identical to physical states because a mental state can be realized by multiple physical states (in different organisms).
records.reference: Baldwin, T. (2001). Contemporary Philosophy. Oxford University Press.

records.line: Mental states are computational states, not identified by their physical realizations but by their functional roles.
records.reference: Levin, J. (n.d.). “Functionalism”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/functionalism/

records.line: Truth cannot be defined in terms of verification; knowledge claims are responsible to reality.
records.reference: Putnam, H. (1995). Pragmatism: An open question. Blackwell.; Putnam, H. (2001). The threefold cord: Mind, body, and world. Columbia University Press.

records.line: Art does not give us empirical knowledge but conceptual knowledge of a possibility, of the world as it looks to someone else.
records.reference: Putnam, H. (1976). “Literature, science, and reflection”. New Literary History, 7(3), 483-491.

records.line: There are cases where a belief is true and justified without being knowledge; the “justified true belief” definition of knowledge is problematic.
records.reference: Gettier, E. (1963). “Is Justified True Belief Knowledge?”. Analysis 23(6). Oxford University Press.

records.line: The basic principles of all languages, and the basic range of concepts they are used to express, are innately represented in the human mind. (Universal grammar)
records.reference: McGilvray, J. A. (n.d.). “Noam Chomsky”. Encyclopedia Britannica. https://www.britannica.com/biography/Noam-Chomsky

records.line: Language learning consists of the unconscious construction of a grammar from the innate principles, with cues from the child’s linguistic environment.
records.reference: McGilvray, J. A. (n.d.). “Noam Chomsky”. Encyclopedia Britannica. https://www.britannica.com/biography/Noam-Chomsky

records.line: Linguistic behaviour is essentially uncaused – enabled by a distinct set of innate principles but innovative, or creative.
records.reference: McGilvray, J. A. (n.d.). “Noam Chomsky”. Encyclopedia Britannica. https://www.britannica.com/biography/Noam-Chomsky

records.line: The mind is a computational network of innate, automatic, independent modules (e.g. language) interacting to yield perception, cognition, and thought.
records.reference: McGilvray, J. A. (n.d.). “Noam Chomsky”. Encyclopedia Britannica. https://www.britannica.com/biography/Noam-Chomsky

records.line: The faculty of language in the broad sense (FLB) includes perceptual-articulatory systems (for sound and sign) and conceptual-intentional systems (for meaning).
records.reference: McGilvray, J. A. (n.d.). “Noam Chomsky”. Encyclopedia Britannica. https://www.britannica.com/biography/Noam-Chomsky

records.line: The faculty of language in the narrow sense (FLN) amounts to the recursive computational system alone.
records.reference: McGilvray, J. A. (n.d.). “Noam Chomsky”. Encyclopedia Britannica. https://www.britannica.com/biography/Noam-Chomsky

records.line: There are homologues and analogs in other species for the FLB; FLN is what makes human beings cognitively distinct from other creatures.
records.reference: McGilvray, J. A. (n.d.). “Noam Chomsky”. Encyclopedia Britannica. https://www.britannica.com/biography/Noam-Chomsky

records.line: Languages should not be defined as ‘E-languages’ (extensional, external) but as ‘I-languages’ (individual, internal, intensional).
records.reference: Scholz, B. C., Pelletier, F. J., & Pullum, G. K. (n.d.). “Philosophy of Linguistics”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/linguistics/

records.line: There are problems (topics/phenomena that fall under the scope of our capacity for comprehension or explanation) and mysteries (that fall outside of such a scope).
records.reference: Collins, J. (2021). “Chomsky’s Problem/Mystery Distinction”. In Nicholas Allott, Terje Lohndal & Georges Rey (eds.), A Companion to Chomsky. Wiley. pp. 557–566.

records.line: The media serve the interests of state and corporate power, supporting established privilege and limiting debate and discussion accordingly.
records.reference: Chomsky, N. (1989). Necessary illusions: Thought control in democratic societies. Pluto Press.

records.line: The responsibility of the writer as a moral agent is to try to bring the truth about matters of human significance to an audience that can do something about them.
records.reference: Chomsky, N. (1996). Powers and prospects. Pluto Press.

records.line: Marxism does not break radically enough with capitalism, offering itself merely as a more efficient and equitable organization of production.
records.reference: Kellner, D. (n.d.). “Jean Baudrillard”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/baudrillard/

records.line: Pre-capitalist societies were governed by laws of symbolic exchange rather than production and utility.
records.reference: Kellner, D. (n.d.). “Jean Baudrillard”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/baudrillard/

records.line: The catastrophe of modernity and eruption of postmodernity is produced by the unfolding of technological revolution.
records.reference: Kellner, D. (n.d.). “Jean Baudrillard”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/baudrillard/

records.line: In addition to their use-value and exchange-value, commodities have sign-value: the expression and mark of style, prestige, luxury, power, etc.
records.reference: Kellner, D. (n.d.). “Jean Baudrillard”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/baudrillard/

records.line: Postmodern societies are organized around representations that ‘simulate’ reality as in television, cyberspace, and virtual reality.
records.reference: Kellner, D. (n.d.). “Jean Baudrillard”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/baudrillard/

records.line: Simulation is no longer a question of imitation, nor duplication, nor even parody; it is substituting the signs of the real for the real.
records.reference: Baudrillard, J. (1981). Simulacra and simulation. University of Michigan Press. 1994.

records.line: Economics, politics, social life, and culture are all governed by the mode of simulation now.
records.reference: Kellner, D. (n.d.). “Jean Baudrillard”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/baudrillard/

records.line: Entertainment and information technologies provide experiences more intense and involving (‘hyperreality’) than the banal everyday life.
records.reference: Kellner, D. (n.d.). “Jean Baudrillard”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/baudrillard/

records.line: As simulations proliferate, they come to refer only to themselves: a carnival of mirrors reflecting images projected from other mirrors.
records.reference: Kellner, D. (n.d.). “Jean Baudrillard”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/baudrillard/

records.line: The masses are now bathed in a ‘media massage’; classes disappear, and politics is dead, as are the dreams of disalienation, liberation, and revolution.
records.reference: Kellner, D. (n.d.). “Jean Baudrillard”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/baudrillard/

records.line: In postmodern societies, the masses seek spectacle and not meaning; they implode into a ‘silent majority.’
records.reference: Kellner, D. (n.d.). “Jean Baudrillard”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/baudrillard/

records.line: Social theory is losing its very object as meanings, classes, and difference implode into a black hole of non-differentiation.
records.reference: Kellner, D. (n.d.). “Jean Baudrillard”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/baudrillard/

records.line: Scientists and science are molded and used by the dominant social and economic forces in society; science is part of ideology.
records.reference: Lewontin, R. (1991). Biology as Ideology: The Doctrine of DNA. Harper Perennial.

records.line: The reductionist view of the biological world is a reflection of the ideologies of the individualistic bourgeois revolutions of the eighteenth century.
records.reference: Lewontin, R. (1991). Biology as Ideology: The Doctrine of DNA. Harper Perennial.

records.line: It is a mistake to atomize an organism into a bundle of traits; the organism should be treated as an integrated whole.
records.reference: Gould, S. J., & Lewontin, R. C. (1979). “The spandrels of San Marco and the Panglossian paradigm: A critique of the adaptationist programme”. Proceedings of the Royal Society B: Biological Sciences, 205(1161), 581–598.

records.line: Any entities in nature (molecules, genes, organisms, populations, species, ecosystems) that have variation, reproduction, and heritability may evolve.
records.reference: Lewontin, R. (1970). “The units of selection”. Annual Review of Ecology and Systematics, 1, 1-18.

records.line: The genes and the environment work together with a division-of-labor model; separating out their causal contributions makes no sense.
records.reference: Rosenberg, A. (2008). Darwinian reductionism: Or, how to stop worrying and love molecular biology. University of Chicago Press.

records.line: The organism is not determined by its genes/environment or by their interaction; it bears a significant mark of random processes.
records.reference: Lewontin, R. (2000). The Triple Helix: Gene, Organism, and Environment. Harvard University Press.

records.line: ‘Spandrels’, hitchhiking, vestigial traits, and developmental constraints prove why assuming that every trait of every organism is adaptive is a mistake.
records.reference: Gould, S. J., & Lewontin, R. C. (1979). “The spandrels of San Marco and the Panglossian paradigm: A critique of the adaptationist programme”. Proceedings of the Royal Society B: Biological Sciences, 205(1161), 581–598.

records.line: It is not that organisms find environments/niches to adapt to, they construct them in a dialectical process; the environment of organisms is coded in their DNA.
records.reference: Lewontin, R. (1991). Biology as Ideology: The Doctrine of DNA. Harper Perennial.

records.line: The organism influences its own evolution, by being both the object of natural selection and the creator of the conditions of that selection.
records.reference: Levins, R., & Lewontin, R. (1985). The Dialectical Biologist. Harvard University Press.

records.line: The genes, in making possible the development of human consciousness, have surrendered their power to a new level of causation: social interaction.
records.reference: Lewontin, R. (1991). Biology as Ideology: The Doctrine of DNA. Harper Perennial.

records.line: A ‘person’ is someone who has second-order volitions – who cares about what desires they have.
records.reference: Frankfurt, H. G. (1971). “Freedom of the Will and the Concept of a Person”. The Journal of Philosophy, 68(1), 5–20.

records.line: Free action does not require that an agent “could have done otherwise”.
records.reference: Baldwin, T. (2001). Contemporary Philosophy. Oxford University Press.

records.line: An agent is free when their first-order intentions causing action are in harmony with their reflective second-order intentions.
records.reference: Baldwin, T. (2001). Contemporary Philosophy. Oxford University Press.

records.line: None of the modes of equality (of resources, welfare, opportunity, respect, rights, consideration, concern, etc.) is intrinsically valuable.
records.reference: Frankfurt, H. G. (2015). On Equality. Princeton, NJ: Princeton University Press.

records.line: What is morally important with regard to money is that everyone should have enough (to be content) – not that they should have the same.
records.reference: Frankfurt, H. G. (2015). On Equality. Princeton, NJ: Princeton University Press.

records.line: Egalitarian concerns can be alienating, distracting people from recognizing their authentic ambitions deriving from the character of their own lives.
records.reference: Frankfurt, H. G. (2015). On Equality. Princeton, NJ: Princeton University Press.

records.line: Economic egalitarianism may be instrumentally valuable as a necessary condition for the attainment of goods that do genuinely possess intrinsic value.
records.reference: Frankfurt, H. G. (2015). On Equality. Princeton, NJ: Princeton University Press.

records.line: Unlike liars, bullshitters have a complete disregard and carelessness for the truth; they are more harmful.
records.reference: Frankfurt, H. G. (2005). On Bullshit. Princeton, NJ: Princeton University Press.

records.line: The possibility of knowledge of any kind depends upon the possibility of an ‘absolute conception’ of the world, maximally independent of human peculiarities.
records.reference: Baldwin, T. (2001). Contemporary Philosophy. Oxford University Press.

records.line: The aim of objectivity in science is to approach as closely as possible to the absolute conception of the world, compensating for the distortions of observers.
records.reference: Nagel, T. & Duignan, B. (n.d.). “Bernard Williams”. Encyclopædia Britannica. https://www.britannica.com/biography/Bernard-Williams

records.line: There can be no external reasons for action; once they enter into our explanations of our actions, they become internal.
records.reference: Baldwin, T. (2001). Contemporary Philosophy. Oxford University Press.

records.line: If there can be no external reasons for action, no absolute conception of ethics will be available to us.
records.reference: Chappell, S., & Smyth, N. (n.d.). “Bernard Williams”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/williams-bernard/

records.line: Ethical truth can only be local and historically contingent (not timeless or universal), based on reasons deriving from people’s actual motives and practices.
records.reference: Nagel, T. and Duignan, B. (n.d.). “Bernard Williams”. Encyclopedia Britannica. https://www.britannica.com/biography/Bernard-Williams

records.line: Vulgar moral relativism is inconsistent: it attaches a nonrelative morality of toleration or non-interference to a view of morality as relative.
records.reference: Williams, B. (1972). Morality: An Introduction to Ethics. Cambridge: Cambridge University Press.

records.line: We blame people not only for what they have voluntarily done, but also for what they have done as a matter of luck (‘moral luck’).
records.reference: Chappell, S., & Smyth, N. (n.d.). “Bernard Williams”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/williams-bernard/

records.line: A utilitarian assessment of duty is a form of alienation because the agent’s own welfare counts simply as someone’s welfare.
records.reference: Baldwin, T. (2001). Contemporary Philosophy. Oxford University Press.

records.line: My freedom is reasonably not taken to include freedom from my past, my character, and my desires: I must see them as within the boundaries of the self.
records.reference: Williams, B. (1973). “The Makropulos Case: Reflections on the Tedium of Immortality”. In Problems of the Self, Cambridge: Cambridge University Press.

records.line: Immortality is undesirable: one would either run out of aims to pursue and get bored, or pursue new aims that have nothing to do with one’s current self.
records.reference: Williams, B. (1973). “The Makropulos Case: Reflections on the Tedium of Immortality”. In Problems of the Self, Cambridge: Cambridge University Press.

records.line: There is a distinction between the history of ideas (focusing on the historical context) and the history of philosophy which is part of philosophy.
records.reference: Williams, B. (1978). Descartes: The Project of Pure Enquiry. Penguin Books.

records.line: Historically impoverished, reductionist styles of philosophy and science stand in the way of our understanding who we are.
records.reference: Williams, B. (2002, October 17). “Why philosophy needs history”. London Review of Books, 24(20).

records.line: There is such a property as being a good K only if K is a ‘goodness-fixing’ kind (e.g. toasters, knifes, but not smudges).
records.reference: Lutz, M. (n.d.). “Moral Naturalism”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/naturalism-moral/

records.line: Things can be ‘good-modified’ as in “good for use in making cheesecake” or “good for Alfred” – a smudge can be “good for using in a Rorschach test”.
records.reference: Lutz, M. (n.d.). “Moral Naturalism”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/naturalism-moral/

records.line: There is no such thing as a good act, but only acts that are good in some respect (e.g. a moral respect).
records.reference: Lutz, M. (n.d.). “Moral Naturalism”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/naturalism-moral/

records.line: There are five kinds of goodness that are all natural properties: the useful, the skilful, the enjoyable, the beneficial, and the moral.
records.reference: Lutz, M. (n.d.). “Moral Naturalism”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/naturalism-moral/

records.line: Moral goodness is a matter of having character traits that promote the other kinds of goodness (particularly by being beneficial to oneself and others).
records.reference: Lutz, M. (n.d.). “Moral Naturalism”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/naturalism-moral/

records.line: The idea that we should act in such a way as to make the world better than it otherwise would have been is meaningless: there is no such relation as “better than”.
records.reference: McGrath, S. (2005) “Thomson, Judith Jarvis”. Encyclopedia of Philosophy. https://www.encyclopedia.com/humanities/encyclopedias-almanacs-transcripts-and-maps/thomson-judith-jarvis-1929

records.line: Our evidence that moral hypotheses are true is that they would be explained by observation; the data explain the hypotheses.
records.reference: McGrath, S. (2005) “Thomson, Judith Jarvis”. Encyclopedia of Philosophy. https://www.encyclopedia.com/humanities/encyclopedias-almanacs-transcripts-and-maps/thomson-judith-jarvis-1929

records.line: The right to life does not include the right to be given the bare minimum one needs for continued life, nor the right not to be killed.
records.reference: Thomson, J. (1971). “A defense of abortion”. Philosophy and Public Affairs, 1(1), 47-66.

records.line: Having a right to life does not guarantee having a right to be given the use of another person’s body; abortion is not always morally impermissible.
records.reference: Thomson, J. (1971). “A defense of abortion”. Philosophy and Public Affairs, 1(1), 47-66.

records.line: There are some cases in which the unborn person has a right to the use of its mother’s body, in which abortion is impermissible.
records.reference: Thomson, J. (1971). “A defense of abortion”. Philosophy and Public Affairs, 1(1), 47-66.

records.line: There is no Principle of Moral Inertia – no prima facie duty to refrain from interfering with existing states of affairs just because they are so.
records.reference: Thomson, J. (1976). “Killing, letting die, and the trolley problem”. The Monist, 59(2), 204-217.

records.line: When a passenger has to make the choice in the Trolley Problem, the doing/allowing status changes but our intuitions about the right choice do not.
records.reference: Thomson, J. (1976). “Killing, letting die, and the trolley problem”. The Monist, 59(2), 204-217.

records.line: We may be allowed to better distribute a threat/good by doing something to it, but not by doing something to a person.
records.reference: Thomson, J. (1976). “Killing, letting die, and the trolley problem”. The Monist, 59(2), 204-217.

records.line: The thesis that killing is worse than letting die cannot be used unconditionally; there are circumstances in which one may choose to kill.
records.reference: Thomson, J. (1976). “Killing, letting die, and the trolley problem”. The Monist, 59(2), 204-217.

records.line: The thesis of temporal parts is absurd because it implies that material objects are constantly coming into existence ex nihilo.
records.reference: Thomson, J. (1983). “Parthood and identity across time”. The Journal of Philosophy, 80(4), 201-220.

records.line: Artifacts are not identical to but rather constituted by quantities of stuff; the material parts can change while the artifact remains by keeping its shape.
records.reference: Thomson, J. (1998). “The statue and the clay”. Noûs, 32(2), 149-173.

records.line: Reality is like an over-crowded attic, some of its contents interesting, and most junk – there is no need to deny the junk; we can simply leave it to gather dust.
records.reference: Thomson, J. (1998). “The statue and the clay”. Noûs, 32(2), 149-173.

records.line: Writing is different from speech; the reader is absent when we write, and the author and his/her intentions are absent when we read.
records.reference: Moati, R. (2014). Derrida/Searle. Columbia University Press.

records.line: The legibility of the text is indifferent to the communicational parameters (context, speaker, receiver, message) it first had.
records.reference: Moati, R. (2014). Derrida/Searle. Columbia University Press.

records.line: The intended meaning of a text is incapable of being stabilized; rereading may activate new meanings (‘iteration’).
records.reference: Moati, R. (2014). Derrida/Searle. Columbia University Press.

records.line: Every text is riddled with gaps, holes, and contradictions (‘aporias’).
records.reference: Buckingham, W. et al. (2011). The Philosophy Book. DK Pub.

records.line: The meaning of the words we use depends on their relationship to (difference from) the words we are not using.
records.reference: Buckingham, W. et al. (2011). The Philosophy Book. DK Pub.

records.line: Meaning is always deferred in language; it depends on what else we say, which depends on what else we say, and so on.
records.reference: Buckingham, W. et al. (2011). The Philosophy Book. DK Pub.

records.line: Meaning is not self-contained within the text, not even present to the author, because it is a matter of deferring and differing (‘différance’).
records.reference: Buckingham, W. et al. (2011). The Philosophy Book. DK Pub.

records.line: We should expose the Platonistic dual hierarchies (good/evil, mind/body, etc.) in texts and mark them with new terms (‘deconstruction’).
records.reference: Lawlor, L. (n.d.). “Jacques Derrida”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/derrida/

records.line: Many rational (perceptual, logical, memory, etc.) beliefs do not require justification; similarly, theistic belief without justification is not irrational.
records.reference: Plantinga, A. (2000). Warranted Christian Belief. New York, US: Oxford University Press USA.

records.line: Warrant, not justification, is the property – or quantity – enough of which is what makes the difference between knowledge and mere true belief.
records.reference: Plantinga, A. (2000). Warranted Christian Belief. New York, US: Oxford University Press USA.

records.line: A belief has warrant just if it is produced by properly functioning cognitive faculties in an environment propitious for that exercise according to a design plan.
records.reference: Plantinga, A. (2000). Warranted Christian Belief. New York, US: Oxford University Press USA.

records.line: We humans may have a God-given faculty (“sensus divinitatis”) which produces in us theistic beliefs that are properly basic with respect to warrant.
records.reference: Plantinga, A. (2000). Warranted Christian Belief. New York, US: Oxford University Press USA.

records.line: There is a possible world in which maximal greatness is instantiated.
records.reference: Plantinga, A. (1974). God, Freedom, and Evil. Grand Rapids: Michigan.

records.line: Necessarily, a being is maximally great only if it has maximal excellence in every world.
records.reference: Plantinga, A. (1974). God, Freedom, and Evil. Grand Rapids: Michigan.

records.line: Necessarily, a being has maximal excellence in every world only if it has omniscience, omnipotence, and moral perfection in every world.
records.reference: Plantinga, A. (1974). God, Freedom, and Evil. Grand Rapids: Michigan.

records.line: God did not bring into existence any states of affairs; God actualizes states of affairs, and could not have actualized logically impossible states of affairs.
records.reference: Plantinga, A. (1974). God, Freedom, and Evil. Grand Rapids: Michigan.

records.line: A world containing creatures who are morally free is more valuable, all else being equal, than a world containing no free creatures at all.
records.reference: Plantinga, A. (1974). God, Freedom, and Evil. Grand Rapids: Michigan.

records.line: If a person suffers from ‘transworld depravity’, then it isn’t within God’s power to actualize any world in which that person is free but does no wrong.
records.reference: Plantinga, A. (1974). God, Freedom, and Evil. Grand Rapids: Michigan.

records.line: It’s possible that every essence suffers from transworld depravity; so it’s possible that God could not have created a world with moral good but no moral evil.
records.reference: Plantinga, A. (1974). God, Freedom, and Evil. Grand Rapids: Michigan.

records.line: It’s possible that natural evil is due to the free actions of nonhuman persons (Satan) and it was not within God’s power to create a world with less evil.
records.reference: Plantinga, A. (1974). God, Freedom, and Evil. Grand Rapids: Michigan.

records.line: The fact that the theist doesn’t know why God permits evil is a fact about the theist; it isn’t by itself relevant to the rationality of belief in God.
records.reference: Plantinga, A. (1974). God, Freedom, and Evil. Grand Rapids: Michigan.

records.line: A successful atheological objection must be to the truth of theism (de facto), not to its rationality, justification, intellectual respectability, etc. (de jure).
records.reference: Plantinga, A. (2000). Warranted Christian Belief. New York, US: Oxford University Press USA.

records.line: According to the conjunction of naturalism and evolution, there would be many ways in which our beliefs could be false, but adaptive nonetheless.
records.reference: Plantinga, A. (1994). “Naturalism defeated”. Unpublished manuscript.

records.line: The devotees of the conjunction of naturalism and evolution (N&E) have a defeater for just any belief they hold; N&E is self-defeating.
records.reference: Plantinga, A. (1994). “Naturalism defeated”. Unpublished manuscript.

records.line: Only one form of subjectivity exists in Western culture (religion, science, language, philosophy, psychoanalysis, etc.) and it is male.
records.reference: Donovan, S. K. (n.d.). “Luce Irigaray”. The Internet Encyclopedia of Philosophy. https://iep.utm.edu/irigaray/

records.line: Women have historically been seen as “mother” (and “the other”) associated with nature, body, irrationality, and unthinking matter.
records.reference: Donovan, S. K. (n.d.). “Luce Irigaray”. The Internet Encyclopedia of Philosophy. https://iep.utm.edu/irigaray/

records.line: Separating mind and body is unethical insofar as it perpetuates the division in culture between man/mind and woman/body.
records.reference: Donovan, S. K. (n.d.). “Luce Irigaray”. The Internet Encyclopedia of Philosophy. https://iep.utm.edu/irigaray/

records.line: Science itself is biased towards categories typically personified as masculine (e.g. solids as opposed to fluids).
records.reference: Donovan, S. K. (n.d.). “Luce Irigaray”. The Internet Encyclopedia of Philosophy. https://iep.utm.edu/irigaray/

records.line: Language systems are malleable, and largely determined by power relationships that are in flux.
records.reference: Donovan, S. K. (n.d.). “Luce Irigaray”. The Internet Encyclopedia of Philosophy. https://iep.utm.edu/irigaray/

records.line: Inclusion of women in the current form of subjectivity is not the solution; there should be more than one subject position in language.
records.reference: Donovan, S. K. (n.d.). “Luce Irigaray”. The Internet Encyclopedia of Philosophy. https://iep.utm.edu/irigaray/

records.line: Both sexes have to reconfigure their subjectivity so that they both understand themselves as belonging equally to nature and culture.
records.reference: Donovan, S. K. (n.d.). “Luce Irigaray”. The Internet Encyclopedia of Philosophy. https://iep.utm.edu/irigaray/

records.line: Women are not, strictly speaking, a class and their dispersion in several classes makes their political struggle and their demands sometimes contradictory.
records.reference: Irigaray, L. (1977). This Sex Which Is Not One.

records.line: Five types of speech acts: assertives (e.g. statements), directives (e.g. orders), commissives (e.g. promises), expressives (e.g. thanks), and declarations (e.g. hirings).
records.reference: Fotion, N. (n.d.). “John Searle”. Encyclopædia Britannica. https://www.britannica.com/biography/John-Searle

records.line: There are indirect speech acts where the speaker performs one kind of speech act by means of performing another. (“You are standing on my foot.”)
records.reference: Fotion, N. (n.d.). “John Searle”. Encyclopædia Britannica. https://www.britannica.com/biography/John-Searle

records.line: Mental states are intrinsic and irreducible features (emergent properties) of certain very complex kinds of biological system.
records.reference: Fotion, N. (n.d.). “John Searle”. Encyclopædia Britannica. https://www.britannica.com/biography/John-Searle

records.line: Unless a mental state has the potential to be conscious, it does not qualify as genuinely mental.
records.reference: Jacob, P. (n.d.). “Intentionality”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/intentionality/

records.line: Consciousness is an on/off switch: you are either conscious or not, but once conscious, there can be different degrees of it, from drowsiness to alertness.
records.reference: Searle, J. (1990). “Who is computing with the brain?” Behavioral and Brain Sciences, 13:4:632-642

records.line: The syntactic structure of computer programs is not sufficient (or necessary) for semantics; they do not have minds, understanding, or consciousness.
records.reference: Searle, J. (1980). “Minds, Brains and Programs”. Behavioral and Brain Sciences, 3 (3): 417–457.

records.line: Because mental states are biological, they can cause and be caused by physical changes in human bodies.
records.reference: Fotion, N. (n.d.). “John Searle”. Encyclopædia Britannica. https://www.britannica.com/biography/John-Searle

records.line: Our experiences of free action contain both indeterminism and rationality; quantum mechanics must enter into the explanation of consciousness.
records.reference: Searle, J. (2007). Freedom & Neurobiology. Columbia University Press.

records.line: It is false that every intentional state must have an object; the existence of hallucinations does not refute direct realism.
records.reference: Searle, J. (2015). Seeing Things as They Are: A Theory of Perception. Oxford University Press.

records.line: Meanings are in the head of the speaker; they do not depend upon external, causal or contextual determinations.
records.reference: Chakraborty, S. (2016). Understanding Meaning and World. Cambridge Scholars Publishing.

records.line: Language and other artifacts have intentionality derived from the ‘original/intrinsic intentionality’ of our conscious mental states.
records.reference: Searle, J. (1999). Mind, Language and Society. Basic Books.

records.line: Signification is illocutionary; linguistic conventions are sufficient to endow intentional meaning, independent of the presence of the sender.
records.reference: Moati, R. (2014). Derrida/Searle. Columbia University Press.

records.line: Proper names function not as descriptions, but as pegs on which to hang (a sufficient but unspecified number of) descriptions.
records.reference: Searle, J. (1958). “Proper Names”, Mind, 67(266): 166–173.

records.line: We-intentions (collective intentions) are held by individuals but they are not reducible to I-intentions.
records.reference: Fotion, N. (n.d.). “John Searle”. Encyclopædia Britannica. https://www.britannica.com/biography/John-Searle

records.line: Objective social reality (games, money, language, etc.) is created by means of we-intentions.
records.reference: Fotion, N. (n.d.). “John Searle”. Encyclopædia Britannica. https://www.britannica.com/biography/John-Searle

records.line: Human beings have the capacity to be motivated by desire-independent reasons for action (such as promises).
records.reference: Searle, J. (2007). Freedom & Neurobiology. Columbia University Press.

records.line: It is possible to derive ‘ought’ from ‘is’ if we start from facts which concern human institutions (such as promises).
records.reference: Baldwin, T. (2001). Contemporary Philosophy. Oxford University Press.

records.line: Many animals have perceptual organs and behaviors similar to ours, so we find it irresistible to suppose that they have intentional states.
records.reference: Lurz, R. (n.d.). “Animal minds”. The Internet Encyclopedia of Philosophy. https://www.iep.utm.edu/ani-mind/

records.line: Philosophy must engage in a fully cooperative relationship with the social sciences and the empirical disciplines in general.
records.reference: Bohman, J. (n.d.). “Jürgen Habermas”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/habermas/

records.line: The ‘public sphere’ (modern European cafés, newspapers, etc.) is where all citizens engage in free critical debate and ‘public opinion’ is formed.
records.reference: Habermas, J. (1962). The Structural Transformation of the Public Sphere. Burger, T. & Lawrence, F. (trans.). Polity, Cambridge. 1989.

records.line: Formal legislative institutions must accurately represent and address deliberations going on in the informal public sphere.
records.reference: Cherem, M. (n.d.). “Jürgen Habermas (1929—)”. The Internet Encyclopedia of Philosophy. https://iep.utm.edu/habermas/

records.line: Thanks to the ‘communicative power’ emerging from the public sphere, a community can both author and rationally abide by the law.
records.reference: Cherem, M. (n.d.). “Jürgen Habermas (1929—)”. The Internet Encyclopedia of Philosophy. https://iep.utm.edu/habermas/

records.line: Mass consumer capitalism led to the ‘re-feudalization’ of the public sphere; corporations control the media and citizens become atomized consumers.
records.reference: Cherem, M. (n.d.). “Jürgen Habermas (1929—)”. The Internet Encyclopedia of Philosophy. https://iep.utm.edu/habermas/

records.line: Reaching understanding is the inherent telos of speech; when actors speak with this sort of practical attitude, they engage in ‘communicative action’.
records.reference: Bohman, J. (n.d.). “Jürgen Habermas”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/habermas/

records.line: We understand a speech act when we know the kinds of reasons that a speaker could provide in order to convince a hearer.
records.reference: Habermas, J. (1998). On the Pragmatics of Communication, M. Cooke (ed.) Cambridge, MA: MIT Press.

records.line: Publicly defendable validity claims include claims to empirical truth, moral rightness, ethical authenticity, personal sincerity, and aesthetic value.
records.reference: Bohman, J. (n.d.). “Jürgen Habermas”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/habermas/

records.line: Many speech acts, including non-constatives, involve a set of tacit validity claims regarding their own sincerity, (social) rightness, and truth.
records.reference: Bohman, J. (n.d.). “Jürgen Habermas”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/habermas/

records.line: When disagreement persists we may transition to ‘discourse’ in which the validity claims that are always immanent within speech become explicit.
records.reference: Cherem, M. (n.d.). “Jürgen Habermas (1929—)”. The Internet Encyclopedia of Philosophy. https://iep.utm.edu/habermas/

records.line: The ‘lifeworld’ is the domains of action (family, social interactions, culture, etc.) in which consensual modes of action coordination predominate.
records.reference: Bohman, J. (n.d.). “Jürgen Habermas”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/habermas/

records.line: ‘Systems’ are the predefined situations (markets, bureaucracies, etc.) in which the demands of communicative action are relaxed, within legal limits.
records.reference: Bohman, J. (n.d.). “Jürgen Habermas”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/habermas/

records.line: The self-perpetuating logic of systems can, if unchecked, colonize and destroy the lifeworld: strategic action must be balanced by communicative action.
records.reference: Cherem, M. (n.d.). “Jürgen Habermas (1929—)”. The Internet Encyclopedia of Philosophy. https://iep.utm.edu/habermas/

records.line: Ideal pragmatic presuppositions for dialectical procedures are full inclusion, equality, honesty, and non-coercion.
records.reference: Bohman, J. (n.d.). “Jürgen Habermas”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/habermas/

records.line: Only those action norms are valid to which all possibly affected persons could agree as participants in rational discourse. (Discourse principle)
records.reference: Habermas, J. (1992). Between Facts and Norms: Contributions to a Discourse Theory of Law and Democracy. W. Rehg (trans.). Cambridge, MA: MIT Press. 1996.

records.line: For empirical truth claims, the objective world is the truth-maker; moral rightness, unlike truth, means ideal warranted assertibility.
records.reference: Cherem, M. (n.d.). “Jürgen Habermas (1929—)”. The Internet Encyclopedia of Philosophy. https://iep.utm.edu/habermas/

records.line: Both believers and non-believers are involved in a complementary learning process, and translation makes demands on both sides.
records.reference: Bohman, J. (n.d.). “Jürgen Habermas”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/habermas/

records.line: Enlightenment is an unfinished project but the best hope for human emancipation is in a correction and completion of Enlightenment.
records.reference: Calhoun, C., Gerteis, J., Moody, J., Pfaff, S., & Virk, I. (Eds.). (2012). Contemporary sociological theory. Wiley-Blackwell.

records.line: There is no absolute distinction between the contributions of language and the world to the truth of what we say.
records.reference: Rorty, R. (1979). Philosophy and the Mirror of Nature. Princeton University Press.

records.line: Tools (a hammer or a belief or a statement) cannot take one out of touch with reality; no organism is ever more or less in touch with reality than others.
records.reference: Rorty, R. (1999). Philosophy and Social Hope. Penguin.

records.line: Knowledge is a matter of conversation and of social practice, rather than an attempt to mirror nature.
records.reference: Baldwin, T. (2001). Contemporary Philosophy. Oxford University Press.

records.line: Our acculturation is what makes certain options live, or momentous, or forced, while leaving others dead, or trivial, or optional.
records.reference: Rorty, R. (1991). Objectivity, Relativism, and Truth: Philosophical Papers, Volume 1, New York: Cambridge University Press.

records.line: While “truth” has various important uses, it does not itself name a goal towards which we can strive, over and above warrant or justification.
records.reference: Ramberg, B. (n.d.). “Richard Rorty”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/rorty/

records.line: Philosophy is not any longer a constructive discipline; it is an edifying one concerned to expose illusion and to keep the conversation going.
records.reference: Baldwin, T. (2001). Contemporary Philosophy. Oxford University Press.

records.line: We should look forward to a post-Philosophical culture in which philosophy amounts to a study of the advantages of the various ways of talking.
records.reference: Baldwin, T. (2001). Contemporary Philosophy. Oxford University Press.

records.line: We need different non-essentialist descriptions of ourselves for predicting and controlling ourselves, for deciding what to do, what meaning our lives shall have.
records.reference: Rorty, R. (1982). “Contemporary philosophy of mind”. Synthese 53 (November):323-48.

records.line: Intellectual and moral progress are best described by metaphors of (creative) evolutionary development, not metaphors of progressively less distorted perception.
records.reference: Rorty, R. (1991). “Feminism and Pragmatism”, Radical Philosophy 059, Autumn 1991.

records.line: Social-political movements do not need philosophical foundations; all philosophers can do is to offer pieces of special-purpose ammunition.
records.reference: Rorty, R. (1991). “Feminism and Pragmatism”, Radical Philosophy 059, Autumn 1991.

records.line: Our incorrigibility about our mental states is not infallibility, but only systematic invulnerability to correction by others under most circumstances.
records.reference: Rorty, R. (1970). “Incorrigibility as the Mark of the Mental”. The Journal of Philosophy, 67(12), 399–424.

records.line: “What is the nature of the mind?” is not a good question; what needs to be explained is simply our behavior, our perceptual/introspective reports.
records.reference: Rorty, R. (1982). “Contemporary philosophy of mind”. Synthese 53 (November):323-48.

records.line: The mental-physical distinction is a cultural artifact; our concept of mind is a blur to be discarded in favor of smaller, handier, more useful concepts.
records.reference: Rorty, R. (1982). “Contemporary philosophy of mind”. Synthese 53 (November):323-48.

records.line: The ineffability of the mental serves the same cultural function as the ineffability of the divine – it vaguely suggests that science does not have the last word.
records.reference: Rorty, R. (1982). “Contemporary philosophy of mind”. Synthese 53 (November):323-48.

records.line: Every event that falls under a special science predicate also falls under a physical predicate, but not vice versa.
records.reference: Rives, B. (n.d.). “Jerry A. Fodor”. The Internet Encyclopedia of Philosophy. https://iep.utm.edu/fodor/

records.line: The special sciences are ‘autonomous’ with their irreducible generalizations about irreducible and casually efficacious higher-level properties.
records.reference: Rives, B. (n.d.). “Jerry A. Fodor”. The Internet Encyclopedia of Philosophy. https://iep.utm.edu/fodor/

records.line: Brain states are like computer states, capable of representation in virtue of causal connections with the world.
records.reference: Baldwin, T. (2001). Contemporary Philosophy. Oxford University Press.
records.keywords: causal theory of representation, computationalism, representationalism

records.line: Thought draws upon brain states which function as sentence-like representations – an innate ‘language of thought’ (‘mentalese’).
records.reference: Baldwin, T. (2001). Contemporary Philosophy. Oxford University Press.

records.line: The causal roles that define mental properties are provided by empirical psychology – not by commonsense psychology or analyses of the meanings of mental terms.
records.reference: Rives, B. (n.d.). “Jerry A. Fodor”. The Internet Encyclopedia of Philosophy. https://iep.utm.edu/fodor/

records.line: Linguistic form recognition can’t be context-driven because context doesn’t determine form; it must be carried out by largely encapsulated processes.
records.reference: Fodor, J. (1983). The Modularity of Mind. The MIT Press.

records.line: Cognitive input systems (for perception and language) are modules: domain-specific computational systems with informational encapsulation.
records.reference: Fodor, J. (1983). The Modularity of Mind. The MIT Press.

records.line: Confirmation in science is isotropic: everything that the scientist knows is, in principle, relevant to determining what else they ought to believe.
records.reference: Fodor, J. (1983). The Modularity of Mind. The MIT Press.

records.line: Confirmation in science is Quineian: the degree of confirmation of a hypothesis is sensitive to global properties of the entire belief system.
records.reference: Fodor, J. (1983). The Modularity of Mind. The MIT Press.

records.line: The brain’s central systems of belief fixation, thought, etc. must be isotropic and Quineian, thus domain-neutral, unencapsulated, non-modular.
records.reference: Fodor, J. (1983). The Modularity of Mind. The MIT Press.

records.line: It is only the modular cognitive systems that we have any hope of understanding; there is nothing to know about the central systems with universal connectivity.
records.reference: Fodor, J. (1983). The Modularity of Mind. The MIT Press.

records.line: All cognitive psychologies thus far proposed, modular or not, imply epistemic boundedness (constraints on the beliefs that we can entertain).
records.reference: Fodor, J. (1983). The Modularity of Mind. The MIT Press.

records.line: The complexity of our minds is irrelevant to the question of whether they’re the products of natural selection.
records.reference: Rives, B. (n.d.). “Jerry A. Fodor”. The Internet Encyclopedia of Philosophy. https://iep.utm.edu/fodor/

records.line: The theory of natural selection cannot predict/explain what traits are selected-for and what traits are free-riders.
records.reference: Fodor, J., & Piattelli-Palmarini, M. (2010). What Darwin Got Wrong. Profile Books.

records.line: We cannot altogether abandon our subjectivity and fully objectify our knowledge (from a ‘view from nowhere’).
records.reference: Baldwin, T. (2001). Contemporary Philosophy. Oxford University Press.

records.line: Objective knowledge about an organism’s behaviour and physiology does not allow us to form a conception of its subjective experience.
records.reference: Baldwin, T. (2001). Contemporary Philosophy. Oxford University Press.

records.line: Materialism is incomplete even as a theory of the physical world, since the physical world includes conscious organisms.
records.reference: Nagel, T. (2012). Mind and Cosmos. Oxford University Press.

records.line: Any evolutionary account of the place of reason presupposes reason’s validity and cannot confirm it without circularity.
records.reference: Nagel, T. (2012). Mind and Cosmos. Oxford University Press.

records.line: Consciousness, reason, and value cannot be products of evolution; they might be among the basic aspects of the universe, guiding evolution.
records.reference: Nagel, T. (2012). Mind and Cosmos. Oxford University Press.

records.line: Ethics is a branch of psychology; susceptibility to certain motivational influences, including altruism, is a condition of rationality.
records.reference: Nagel, T. (1970). The Possibility of Altruism. Oxford,: Clarendon P.

records.line: We do not have to choose between utilitarianism, theories of individual rights, and egalitarianism; a plausible social morality will show the influence of them all.
records.reference: Nagel, T. (1979). Mortal questions. Cambridge University Press.

records.line: There are prudential, rational, timeless, internal reasons for action, independent of our current concerns.
records.reference: Baldwin, T. (2001). Contemporary Philosophy. Oxford University Press.

records.line: Reasons for action are impersonal; if I act in some way on my own behalf, I should act the same way on behalf of others similarly situated.
records.reference: Baldwin, T. (2001). Contemporary Philosophy. Oxford University Press.

records.line: Only beings capable of practical reason can recognize value but once they recognize it, they find it in the lives of creatures without reason or consciousness.
records.reference: Nagel, T. (2012). Mind and Cosmos. Oxford University Press.

records.line: There is no crucial experiment to establish/refute moral realism; its validity is a comparative matter, as it is in other domains (science, history, etc.).
records.reference: Nagel, T. (2012). Mind and Cosmos. Oxford University Press.

records.line: There are four types of moral luck: resultant luck, circumstantial (situational) luck, causal luck, and constitutive luck.
records.reference: Nagel, T. (1993). “Moral Luck”. D. Statman (Ed.), Moral Luck (pp. 57-71). Albany, NY: State University of New York Press.

records.line: When we take an external view of ourselves and recognize what we do as arbitrary, it does not disengage us from life; there lies our absurdity.
records.reference: Nagel, T. (1971). “The Absurd”. Journal of Philosophy 68 (20):716-727.

records.line: If there is no reason to believe that anything matters, then that doesn’t matter either; we can approach our absurd lives with irony instead of heroism or despair.
records.reference: Nagel, T. (1971). “The Absurd”. Journal of Philosophy 68 (20):716-727.

records.line: Given a knockdown argument for an intuitively unacceptable conclusion, one should assume there is probably something wrong with the argument.
records.reference: Nagel, T. (1979). Mortal questions. Cambridge University Press.

records.line: Subjects of knowledge are embodied and socially located; ‘strong objectivity’ requires that they be placed on the same critical plane as the objects of knowledge.
records.reference: Harding, S. (1992). “Rethinking Standpoint Epistemology: What Is ‘Strong Objectivity?’”. In: The Centennial Review, 36, 1992, 3, 437-470.

records.line: The “scientific world view” is, in fact, a view of (dominant groups in) modern, Western societies.
records.reference: Harding, S. (1992). “Rethinking Standpoint Epistemology: What Is ‘Strong Objectivity?’”. In: The Centennial Review, 36, 1992, 3, 437-470.

records.line: Trees, rocks, and electrons appear to natural scientists only as they are already socially constituted, like humans appear to the social scientist.
records.reference: Harding, S. (1992). “Rethinking Standpoint Epistemology: What Is ‘Strong Objectivity?’”. In: The Centennial Review, 36, 1992, 3, 437-470.

records.line: A maximally critical study of scientist communities can be done only from the perspective of those who have been marginalized by such communities.
records.reference: Harding, S. (1992). “Rethinking Standpoint Epistemology: What Is ‘Strong Objectivity?’”. In: The Centennial Review, 36, 1992, 3, 437-470.

records.line: A detached (gender-free) position fails to do justice to facts that women are epistemically privileged with (such as sexual harassment).
records.reference: Baldwin, T. (2001). Contemporary Philosophy. Oxford University Press.

records.line: Starting off research from women’s lives will generate less partial and distorted accounts, not only of women’s lives, but also of the whole social order.
records.reference: Harding, S. (1992). “Rethinking Standpoint Epistemology: What Is ‘Strong Objectivity?’”. In: The Centennial Review, 36, 1992, 3, 437-470.

records.line: Feminist knowledge has started off from women’s lives, but there is no typical or essential woman’s life from which feminisms start their thought.
records.reference: Harding, S. (1992). “Rethinking Standpoint Epistemology: What Is ‘Strong Objectivity?’”. In: The Centennial Review, 36, 1992, 3, 437-470.

records.line: The notion of objectivity is useful in deciding which feminisms generate less partial and distorted accounts of nature and social life.
records.reference: Harding, S. (1992). “Rethinking Standpoint Epistemology: What Is ‘Strong Objectivity?’”. In: The Centennial Review, 36, 1992, 3, 437-470.

records.line: Men, too, must contribute forms of feminist knowledge from their particular social situation, beginning first from women’s lives.
records.reference: Harding, S. (1992). “Rethinking Standpoint Epistemology: What Is ‘Strong Objectivity?’”. In: The Centennial Review, 36, 1992, 3, 437-470.

records.line: Women tend to assign more importance to the responsibilities arising from personal relationships than to the more abstract claims of justice.
records.reference: Baldwin, T. (2001). Contemporary Philosophy. Oxford University Press.

records.line: We should accept an ‘ethics of care’ which structures private life in addition to the more ‘masculine’ ethics of rights and duties.
records.reference: Baldwin, T. (2001). Contemporary Philosophy. Oxford University Press.

records.line: We cannot simply apply notions of mathematics and physics to the life sciences; we must think in terms of change, of development.
records.reference: Ruse, M. (2010). “David Hull: a memoir”. Biology & Philosophy, 25(5), 739-747.

records.line: Each species is an individual rather than a kind; the relation between an organism and a species is that of part to whole, not member to kind.
records.reference: Okasha, S. (2019). Philosophy of biology (A very short introduction). Oxford University Press.

records.line: Since ‘Homo sapiens’, like all species, is an individual and not a kind, there is no property that defines the essence of humanity.
records.reference: Okasha, S. (2019). Philosophy of biology (A very short introduction). Oxford University Press.

records.line: The logical empiricist analysis of reduction is not very instructive in the case of understanding the relationship between Mendelian and molecular genetics.
records.reference: Hull, D. (1974). Philosophy of biological science. Prentice-Hall.

records.line: The appropriate levels in biology are not genes, organisms, and species, but replicators, interactors, and lineages.
records.reference: Hull, D. (2001). Science and selection: Essays on biological evolution and the philosophy of science. Cambridge University Press.

records.line: ‘Interactors’ are those entities that interact as cohesive wholes with their environments in such a way as to make replication differential.
records.reference: Hull, D. (2001). Science and selection: Essays on biological evolution and the philosophy of science. Cambridge University Press.

records.line: Genes are both replicators and interactors; they are not riding around in helpless and hapless “vehicles”.
records.reference: Hull, D. (2001). Science and selection: Essays on biological evolution and the philosophy of science. Cambridge University Press.

records.line: Organisms are not as discrete, unitary, and well organized as we assume; entities more inclusive than single organisms can act as interactors.
records.reference: Hull, D. (2001). Science and selection: Essays on biological evolution and the philosophy of science. Cambridge University Press.

records.line: Many features of science stem from the interaction between the reward system of citation and its social context (competition, cooperation, etc.).
records.reference: Godfrey-Smith, P. (2010). “David Hull: obituary”. Biology and Philosophy, 25(5), 749-753.

records.line: Scientists, like organisms and genes, act altruistically and cooperatively, because altruism at one level is inclusive self-interest at another.
records.reference: Wilkins, J. (2010). “David Hull’s philosophy”. Evolving Thoughts. http://evolvingthoughts.net/2010/08/12/david-hulls-philosophy/

records.line: True belief is knowledge only if those aspects of the world which make it true are appropriately involved in causing it to exist.
records.reference: Hetherington, S. (n.d.). “Gettier Problems”. The Internet Encyclopedia of Philosophy. https://iep.utm.edu/gettier/
records.keywords: causal theory of knowledge, externalism, naturalistic epistemology

records.line: The causal theory of knowledge allows cases in which there is an appropriate causal connection while there is a lack of knowledge.
records.reference: Nagel, J. (2014). Knowledge: A very short introduction. Oxford University Press.

records.line: Knowledge is true belief that has been generated by a reliable belief-producing mechanism.
records.reference: Nagel, J. (2014). Knowledge: A very short introduction. Oxford University Press.

records.line: Social structures may either contribute to, or interfere with, the discovery and dissemination of truths.
records.reference: Kornblith, H. (2005) “Goldman, Alvin”. Encyclopedia of Philosophy. https://www.encyclopedia.com/humanities/encyclopedias-almanacs-transcripts-and-maps/goldman-alvin-1938

records.line: In mental state attribution, one’s own mental states are being used to simulate the workings of the target mental state.
records.reference: Kornblith, H. (2005) “Goldman, Alvin”. Encyclopedia of Philosophy. https://www.encyclopedia.com/humanities/encyclopedias-almanacs-transcripts-and-maps/goldman-alvin-1938

records.line: We have many possible existential quantifiers used in different frameworks in natural language.
records.reference: Bricker, P. (n.d.). “Ontological commitment”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/ontological-commitment/

records.line: There is a single reality but it consists of unstructured facts that we carve up differently with our different existential quantifiers.
records.reference: Bricker, P. (n.d.). “Ontological commitment”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/ontological-commitment/

records.line: The strongest reason to advocate a libertarian society is that such advocacy follows from a respect for individual rights.
records.reference: Feser, E. (n.d.). “Robert Nozick”. The Internet Encyclopedia of Philosophy. https://iep.utm.edu/nozick/

records.line: Individuals are self-owners; they have rights to their lives, liberty, and the fruits of their labor.
records.reference: Feser, E. (n.d.). “Robert Nozick”. The Internet Encyclopedia of Philosophy. https://iep.utm.edu/nozick/

records.line: The endorsement of the imposition of sacrifices on individuals for social good fails to recognize the status of individuals as ends-in-themselves.
records.reference: Mack, E. (n.d.). “Robert Nozick’s Political Philosophy”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/nozick-political/

records.line: Patterned conceptions of distributive justice cannot be reconciled with the individual liberty to make voluntary, consensual transactions.
records.reference: Long, R. (n.d.). “Egalitarianism”. The Internet Encyclopedia of Philosophy. https://iep.utm.edu/egalitarianism/

records.line: Just outcomes are those arrived at by the separate just actions of individuals; a particular distributive pattern is not required for justice.
records.reference: Lamont, J., Favor, C. (n.d.). “Distributive Justice”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/justice-distributive/

records.line: The various programs of the modern liberal welfare state (e.g. taxation) are immoral because they make slaves of the citizens of such a state.
records.reference: Feser, E. (n.d.). “Robert Nozick”. The Internet Encyclopedia of Philosophy. https://iep.utm.edu/nozick/

records.line: Only a ‘minimal state’, limited to the narrow functions of protection against force, theft, fraud, enforcement of contracts, and so on, is justified.
records.reference: Nozick, R. (1974). Anarchy, State, and Utopia. Basic Books.

records.line: We would not want to live as plugged into a machine that would give us preprogrammed pleasurable experiences; what we desire is to live in contact with reality.
records.reference: Nozick, R. (1974). Anarchy, State, and Utopia. Basic Books.

records.line: Knowledge is true belief that tracks the truth by varying according to the state of things in close possible worlds.
records.reference: Baldwin, T. (2001). Contemporary Philosophy. Oxford University Press.

records.line: The descriptions that are candidates for the putative descriptive meaning of a name do not have the same modal profile as the name.
records.reference: Green, M. S. (2005). “Kripke, Saul Aaron (1940– )” in Shook, J. R. & Hull, R. T. (eds.) The dictionary of modern American philosophers. Bristol: Thoemmes Continuum.

records.line: There are rigid designators, names/descriptions that refer to the same thing in all possible worlds, which allow essentialist theses.
records.reference: Baldwin, T. (2001). Contemporary Philosophy. Oxford University Press.

records.line: Ordinary proper names are rigid designators of ordinary things, thanks to fixations by ‘initial baptisms’.
records.reference: Baldwin, T. (2001). Contemporary Philosophy. Oxford University Press.
records.keywords: causal theory of reference, direct reference theory

records.line: Natural kind terms are rigid designators: if “water” refers in the actual world to H₂O then it refers in all counterfactual situations to stuff with the same structure.
records.reference: Green, M. S. (2005). “Kripke, Saul Aaron (1940– )” in Shook, J. R. & Hull, R. T. (eds.) The dictionary of modern American philosophers. Bristol: Thoemmes Continuum.

records.line: There are empirical necessary truths. (e.g. “Water is H₂O.”)
records.reference: Baldwin, T. (2001). Contemporary Philosophy. Oxford University Press.

records.line: There are contingent a priori truths. (e.g. “Jack the Ripper is the man responsible for the East End murders.”)
records.reference: Baldwin, T. (2001). Contemporary Philosophy. Oxford University Press.

records.line: There are three types of essential necessity: origin, substance, and form.
records.reference: Baldwin, T. (2001). Contemporary Philosophy. Oxford University Press.

records.line: A has the “identity” property of being necessarily identical with A; if A = B, they share all their properties, so the identity must also be necessarily true.
records.reference: Green, M. S. (2005). “Kripke, Saul Aaron (1940– )” in Shook, J. R. & Hull, R. T. (eds.) The dictionary of modern American philosophers. Bristol: Thoemmes Continuum.

records.line: Pain without physical realization (and vice versa) is conceptually possible; mental states are not identical to states of the nervous system.
records.reference: Green, M. S. (2005). “Kripke, Saul Aaron (1940– )” in Shook, J. R. & Hull, R. T. (eds.) The dictionary of modern American philosophers. Bristol: Thoemmes Continuum.

records.line: For any given world W, there is some subset W' of W such that each element of W' is accessible from W; these are the worlds that are possible relative to W.
records.reference: Green, M. S. (2005). “Kripke, Saul Aaron (1940– )” in Shook, J. R. & Hull, R. T. (eds.) The dictionary of modern American philosophers. Bristol: Thoemmes Continuum.

records.line: There is a single, semantically coherent and meaningful Liar Sentence, but it is neither true nor false; it falls into a ‘truth value gap’.
records.reference: Dowden, B. (n.d.). “Liar Paradox”. The Internet Encyclopedia of Philosophy. https://iep.utm.edu/par-liar/

records.line: The history of science is full of empirically successful theories that are shown to be false/non-referential; therefore, our current theories are likely to be false/non-referential.
records.reference: Laudan, L. (1981). “A Confutation of Convergent Realism”. Philosophy of Science 48: 19–49.

records.line: The aim of science is to secure theories with a high problem-solving effectiveness – a goal which (unlike truth) is not transcendent and unassessable.
records.reference: Laudan, L. (1981). “A Problem-Solving Approach to Scientific Progress.” In Hacking, I. (ed.), Scientific Revolutions, pp. 144–55. Oxford: Oxford University Press.

records.line: A change from an empirically well-supported theory to a less well-supported one could be progressive if the latter resolved more conceptual problems.
records.reference: Laudan, L. (1981). “A Problem-Solving Approach to Scientific Progress.” In Hacking, I. (ed.), Scientific Revolutions, pp. 144–55. Oxford: Oxford University Press.

records.line: The logic of acceptance/rejection is too restrictive: a theory may not yet be worthy of acceptance but its progress may well warrant further pursuit.
records.reference: Laudan, L. (1981). “A Problem-Solving Approach to Scientific Progress.” In Hacking, I. (ed.), Scientific Revolutions, pp. 144–55. Oxford: Oxford University Press.

records.line: The co-existence of rival research traditions is the rule rather than the exception; there is a continuous struggle taking place at any time.
records.reference: Laudan, L. (1981). “A Problem-Solving Approach to Scientific Progress.” In Hacking, I. (ed.), Scientific Revolutions, pp. 144–55. Oxford: Oxford University Press.

records.line: There is no fundamental difference in kind between scientific and other forms of intellectual inquiry; all are subject to empirical and conceptual constraints.
records.reference: Laudan, L. (1981). “A Problem-Solving Approach to Scientific Progress.” In Hacking, I. (ed.), Scientific Revolutions, pp. 144–55. Oxford: Oxford University Press.

records.line: Aims, methods, and theories in science justify each other in no hierarchical order within an ever-changing network. (‘reticulated model’)
records.reference: Laudan, L. (1984). Science and Values, University of California Press, Berkeley.

records.line: The displacement of the idea that facts and evidence matter by the idea that everything is subjective is the most pernicious manifestation of anti-intellectualism.
records.reference: Laudan, L. (1990). Science and Relativism. Chicago: The University of Chicago Press.

records.line: The aim of science is not to be identified with individual scientists’ motives.
records.reference: Van Fraassen, B. (1980). The Scientific Image. Oxford University Press.

records.line: Science aims to give us theories which are empirically adequate; and acceptance of a theory involves as belief only that it is empirically adequate.
records.reference: Van Fraassen, B. (1980). The Scientific Image. Oxford University Press.

records.line: Scientific activity is one of construction of models that must be adequate to observable phenomena, and agnostic about truth concerning the unobservable.
records.reference: Van Fraassen, B. (1980). The Scientific Image. Oxford University Press.
records.keywords: constructive empiricism, semantic approach, scientific anti-realism

records.line: Using a theory involves “immersing” oneself in its world and language; it need not entail a commitment to its entire ontology.
records.reference: Monton, B. & Chad, M. (n.d.). “Constructive Empiricism”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/constructive-empiricism/

records.line: What counts as observable depends on the epistemic community: observable is observable-to-us by unaided perception (without a microscope, etc.).
records.reference: Van Fraassen, B. (1980). The Scientific Image. Oxford University Press.

records.line: The pragmatic virtues (elegance, simplicity, scope, explanatory power) of a theory can be guides but they are not indicators of its truth.
records.reference: Van Fraassen, B. (1980). The Scientific Image. Oxford University Press.

records.line: The success of scientific theories is no miracle: they are born into a life of fierce competition, and only the successful ones survive.
records.reference: Van Fraassen, B. (1980). The Scientific Image. Oxford University Press.

records.line: It may be that all the potential explanations we have are bad, and hence we would be unwise to believe that one of those is the true one.
records.reference: Monton, B. & Chad, M. (n.d.). “Constructive Empiricism”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/constructive-empiricism/

records.line: Explanation goes beyond what science reveals to us: it cannot occur unless an appropriate question, offered in a particular context, is provided.
records.reference: Monton, B. & Chad, M. (n.d.). “Constructive Empiricism”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/constructive-empiricism/

records.line: Universality is not enough to make a truth a law of nature; there are merely incidental or accidental regularities. (e.g. “There are no rivers of milk”.)
records.reference: Van Fraassen, B. (1989). Laws and Symmetry. Oxford University Press.

records.line: Science does not certify that claims about causation, laws of nature, and other counterfactuals represent objective, context-independent truths.
records.reference: Monton, B. & Chad, M. (n.d.). “Constructive Empiricism”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/constructive-empiricism/

records.line: Even complete physical knowledge about color vision cannot contain the sensual information (qualia) we get when we experience colors: physicalism is false.
records.reference: Jackson, F. (1982). “Epiphenomenal qualia”. Philosophical Quarterly 32 (April):127-136.

records.line: It is possible that certain properties (qualia) of certain mental states make a difference to other mental states though not to anything physical.
records.reference: Jackson, F. (1982). “Epiphenomenal qualia”. Philosophical Quarterly 32 (April):127-136.

records.line: To be in a phenomenal state is to represent objective properties; a complete physicalist account for seeing colors is possible.
records.reference: Nida-Rümelin, M., & O Conaill, D. (n.d.). “Qualia: The Knowledge Argument”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/qualia-knowledge/

records.line: Using mental state terms increases our ability to make successful predictions and explanations; mental states are functional properties.
records.reference: Jackson, F. (2020). “The Theory–Theory Approach to Ethics”. A. Burgess, H. Cappelen, & D. Plunkett (Eds.), Conceptual Engineering and Conceptual Ethics. Oxford University Press.

records.line: We make moral decisions based on factual information, we debate ethical issues, etc.; ethical sentences express truth-apt propositions.
records.reference: Jackson, F. (2020). “The Theory–Theory Approach to Ethics”. A. Burgess, H. Cappelen, & D. Plunkett (Eds.), Conceptual Engineering and Conceptual Ethics. Oxford University Press.

records.line: No two completely specified situations that differ in their ethical properties can be exactly alike in their descriptive properties.
