records.reference: Lutz, M. (n.d.). “Moral Naturalism”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/naturalism-moral/

records.line: Supervenience entails that ethical properties are descriptive; they can be picked out without using ethical expressions.
records.reference: Jackson, F. (2020). “The Theory–Theory Approach to Ethics”. A. Burgess, H. Cappelen, & D. Plunkett (Eds.), Conceptual Engineering and Conceptual Ethics. Oxford University Press.

records.line: Ethical terms pick out the properties that play a certain role in the conceptual network determined by ‘mature folk morality’ (after critical reflection).
records.reference: Lutz, M. (n.d.). “Moral Naturalism”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/naturalism-moral/

records.line: Worlds that are merely possible are just as real as the actual world we happen to live in; these worlds are separate space-times.
records.reference: Baldwin, T. (2001). Contemporary Philosophy. Oxford University Press.

records.line: Essentialist claims draw upon similarities between different, but similar, things (counterparts) in different worlds.
records.reference: Baldwin, T. (2001). Contemporary Philosophy. Oxford University Press.

records.line: The truth condition of an indicative sentence is the set of possible worlds in which it is true.
records.reference: Weatherson, B. (n.d.). “David Lewis”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/david-lewis/

records.line: Counterfactuals are claims to the effect that the consequent is true in the possible worlds which are ‘closest to’ the actual world (except for the antecedent).
records.reference: Baldwin, T. (2001). Contemporary Philosophy. Oxford University Press.

records.line: Our beliefs about what is possible/impossible concern the worlds that are altogether separate from ours, about which we can have quasi-inductive knowledge.
records.reference: Baldwin, T. (2001). Contemporary Philosophy. Oxford University Press.

records.line: All there is to the world is a vast mosaic of local matters of particular fact, just one little thing and another. (‘Humean supervenience’)
records.reference: Lewis, D. (1986). Philosophical Papers, Vol. II. Oxford: Oxford University Press.

records.line: The fundamental facts about the actual world are physical facts; no fundamental fact about actual reality would be “left out” by an ideal physics.
records.reference: Hawthorne, J. (2005). “Lewis, David Kellogg (1941–2001)” in Shook, J. R. & Hull, R. T. (eds.) The dictionary of modern American philosophers. Bristol: Thoemmes Continuum.

records.line: Laws of nature are the generalizations within collections of truths that perform better than other collections in terms of simplicity and strength.
records.reference: Weatherson, B. (n.d.). “David Lewis”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/david-lewis/

records.line: Where C and E are two distinct actual events, E causally depends on C if and only if, if C were not to occur E would not occur.
records.reference: Lewis, D. (1973). “Causation”, Journal of Philosophy, 70: 556–67.

records.line: Shape properties must be explained in terms of non-indexed properties of temporal parts.
records.reference: Sider, T. (2007). “Temporal parts”. T. Sider, J. Hawthorne & D. W. Zimmerman (eds.), Contemporary Debates in Metaphysics. Blackwell. pp. 241-262.

records.line: Insofar as our mental states are causes of our physical actions, they too are physical, because physics is a ‘closed’ science.
records.reference: Baldwin, T. (2001). Contemporary Philosophy. Oxford University Press.

records.line: Supervenience guarantees the reduction of the mental to the physical.
records.reference: Baldwin, T. (2001). Contemporary Philosophy. Oxford University Press.

records.line: Mental-physical state identities should be restricted to species or even particular organisms.
records.reference: Baldwin, T. (2001). Contemporary Philosophy. Oxford University Press.

records.line: Mental state terms can be implicitly defined by the Ramsey sentences of our folk theory, specifying causal relations without any mental state terms.
records.reference: Levin, J. (n.d.). “Functionalism”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/functionalism/

records.line: Having the ability to do otherwise in a deterministic world does not entail having the ability to break the laws of nature.
records.reference: Lewis, D. (1981). “Are We Free to Break the Laws?” Theoria, 47: 113–21.

records.line: When you have all the objective knowledge about color vision, seeing a new color is not new factual knowledge but a new skill.
records.reference: Baldwin, T. (2001). Contemporary Philosophy. Oxford University Press.

records.line: Language is grounded in convention (regularities in action and belief that solve coordination problems) of truthfulness (for speakers) and trust (for hearers).
records.reference: Weatherson, B. (n.d.). “David Lewis”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/david-lewis/

records.line: The historical progress of the mature sciences is a matter of successively more accurate approximations to the truth about both observable and unobservable phenomena.
records.reference: Boyd, R. (1984). “The Current Status of Scientific Realism” in Leplin, J. (ed.), Scientific Realism. University of California.

records.line: Purely physical objects, states, properties, etc. need not have definitions in “the vocabulary of physics” or in any other reductive vocabulary.
records.reference: Boyd, R. (1989). “What Realism Implies and What it Does Not”. Dialectica 43 (1‐2):5-29.

records.line: “Cause” refers to natural phenomena whose analysis is a matter for scientists rather than a matter of reductive conceptual analysis.
records.reference: Boyd, R. (1989). “What Realism Implies and What it Does Not”. Dialectica 43 (1‐2):5-29.

records.line: Like healthiness, goodness is a complex natural property that is not directly observable, but nonetheless has a robust causal profile.
records.reference: Lutz, M. (n.d.). “Moral Naturalism”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/naturalism-moral/
records.keywords: Cornell realism, moral naturalism, moral realism

records.line: Moral values are best conceived as natural properties to be identified by inquiries in anthropology and sociology.
records.reference: Baldwin, T. (2001). Contemporary Philosophy. Oxford University Press.

records.line: Moral values are explanatory properties. (e.g. Hitler’s wickedness is part of the explanation of the Holocaust.)
records.reference: Baldwin, T. (2001). Contemporary Philosophy. Oxford University Press.

records.line: The explanatory role of moral values is to guide the identification of the natural properties which underpin this role.
records.reference: Baldwin, T. (2001). Contemporary Philosophy. Oxford University Press.

records.line: Our moral theories and background beliefs are justified together by their overall coherence, like scientific theories and background beliefs.
records.reference: Lutz, M. (n.d.). “Moral Naturalism”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/naturalism-moral/

records.line: The utilitarian principle provides the basis for a naturalistic treatment of moral values.
records.reference: Baldwin, T. (2001). Contemporary Philosophy. Oxford University Press.

records.line: Experiences must have propositional contents given that they justify beliefs about the external world.
records.reference: Silins, N. (n.d.). “Perceptual Experience and Perceptual Justification”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/perception-justification/

records.line: The conceptual content in experiences is (Kantian) intuitional, not propositional; it entitles us to take things to be so – whether we do is a further question.
records.reference: McDowell, J. (2013). “Avoiding the Myth of the Given”. Having the World in View. Harvard University Press.

records.line: The epistemic grounds one has for one’s world-directed judgments are different than those available to one when one hallucinates.
records.reference: Soteriou, M. (n.d.). “The Disjunctive Theory of Perception”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/perception-disjunctive/

records.line: The infallibly knowable fact of a seeming can be taken disjunctively, as constituted either by the fact that things are thus and so or by a mere seeming.
records.reference: McDowell, J. (1986). “Singular thought and the extent of ‘inner space’”. In Philip Pettit (ed.), Subject, Thought, And Context. NY: Clarendon Press.

records.line: All true ‘thinkables’ (possible objects of thought) are (identical with) facts (at the level of sense).
records.reference: Gaskin, R. (n.d.). “The Identity Theory of Truth”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/truth-identity/

records.line: Language is not just for communication; it is also a repository of tradition, a store of historically accumulated wisdom about what is a reason for what.
records.reference: McDowell, J. (1994). Mind and World. Cambridge: Harvard University Press.

records.line: We are animals who develop an irreducible, rational ‘second nature’ through language and upbringing, exercising spontaneity and agency.
records.reference: McDowell, J. (1994). Mind and World. Cambridge: Harvard University Press.
records.keywords: dual aspect theory, free will libertarianism

records.line: There are cases where the requirement imposed by the situation and detected by the agent must exhaust their reason for acting as they do.
records.reference: Baldwin, T. (2001). Contemporary Philosophy. Oxford University Press.

records.line: ‘Properly brought up’ people are motivated to do what is required simply by recognizing an obligation, without deliberation.
records.reference: Baldwin, T. (2001). Contemporary Philosophy. Oxford University Press.

records.line: In moral conversions, a new awareness of external reasons for action creates a shift in motivation.
records.reference: Baldwin, T. (2001). Contemporary Philosophy. Oxford University Press.

records.line: The fact that the identity of moral values is dependent (like colors) upon a human point of view does not undermine their reality.
records.reference: Baldwin, T. (2001). Contemporary Philosophy. Oxford University Press.

records.line: An act is wrong if it is disallowed by any set of principles that no one could reasonably reject as a basis for informed, unforced, general agreement.
records.reference: Scanlon, T. (1998). What We Owe to Each Other. Cambridge, MA: Harvard University Press.

records.line: It is reasons for action that are fundamental in the construction of justice.
records.reference: Baldwin, T. (2001). Contemporary Philosophy. Oxford University Press.

records.line: In order for there to be truths about reasons, it is not necessary for normative facts to have a kind of metaphysical reality.
records.reference: Bagnoli, C. (n.d.). “Constructivism in Metaethics”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/constructivism-metaethics/

records.line: Reasons for action are not dependent upon desires (in the directed-attention sense); finding such reasons motivates desires.
records.reference: Baldwin, T. (2001). Contemporary Philosophy. Oxford University Press.

records.line: Reasons for action are sensitive to contextual considerations; they can sometimes be undermined.
records.reference: Baldwin, T. (2001). Contemporary Philosophy. Oxford University Press.

records.line: We start from our ordinary common-sense moral judgements and then refine them with our understanding of the world.
records.reference: Baldwin, T. (2001). Contemporary Philosophy. Oxford University Press.

records.line: There is a core of fundamental human rights, and there are judgements which exhibit a degree of cultural relativity.
records.reference: Baldwin, T. (2001). Contemporary Philosophy. Oxford University Press.

records.line: The objectivity of reasons for belief and action does not require uniformity of thought and practice; there can be different priorities.
records.reference: Baldwin, T. (2001). Contemporary Philosophy. Oxford University Press.

records.line: The concept of well-being has less importance than is commonly supposed and there is little to be gained by constructing a theory of it.
records.reference: Scanlon, T. (1996). The status of well-being. The Tanner Lectures on Human Values, delivered at University of Michigan.

records.line: Equality is an important political goal but most of the reasons for favoring it can be traced back to fundamental values other than equality itself.
records.reference: Scanlon, T. (1996). The Diversity of Objections to Inequality. The Lindley Lecture.

records.line: A moral idea of substantive equality may be required to prevent stigmatizing differences in status, to preserve procedural fairness, etc.
records.reference: Scanlon, T. (1996). The Diversity of Objections to Inequality. The Lindley Lecture.

records.line: To be a person, a being must be self-conscious, aware of its identity and its continued existence over time.
records.reference: Parfit, D. (1984). Reasons and persons. Oxford: Oxford University Press.

records.line: Persons are constituted by, but not identical with, a physiology, a psychology, and the occurrence of a series of causal and cognitive relations.
records.reference: Korfmacher, C. (n.d.). “Personal Identity”. The Internet Encyclopedia of Philosophy: https://iep.utm.edu/person-i/

records.line: Personal identity is not what matters in survival over time; what matters is psychological connectedness and/or continuity (‘Relation R’).
records.reference: Parfit, D. (1984). Reasons and persons. Oxford: Oxford University Press.

records.line: Since my much later self would be more like a different person than me, how I treat him should fall under the rubric of morality, not self-interest.
records.reference: Shoemaker, D. (n.d.). “Personal Identity and Ethics”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/identity-ethics/

records.line: Only my current (not past or future) desires/aims that are not intrinsically irrational directly generate reasons for my acting.
records.reference: Kagan, S. (1986). “The Present-Aim Theory of Rationality”. Ethics, 96(4).

records.line: My death only involves that after a certain time, none of the thoughts and experiences that occur will be directly causally related to this brain.
records.reference: Parfit, D. (1984). Reasons and persons. Oxford: Oxford University Press.

records.line: If any particular person had not been conceived when they were in fact conceived, it is in fact true that they would never have existed.
records.reference: Parfit, D. (1984). Reasons and persons. Oxford: Oxford University Press.

records.line: A change in the identity of the involved parties in the compared outcomes should not affect our moral evaluation.
records.reference: Arrhenius, G., Ryberg, J., & Tännsjö, T. (n.d.). “The Repugnant Conclusion”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/repugnant-conclusion/

records.line: For any population with members having good lives, there can be some larger population whose total happiness is higher, but with members with terrible lives.
records.reference: Parfit, D. (1984). Reasons and persons. Oxford: Oxford University Press.

records.line: Consequentialism, Kantianism, and Contractualism ultimately converge on the same answers; they can be incorporated into a single Triple Theory.
records.reference: Parfit, D. (2011). On What Matters. Oxford: Oxford University Press.

records.line: An act is wrong just when such acts are disallowed by some principle that is optimific, uniquely universally willable, and not reasonably rejectable.
records.reference: Parfit, D. (2011). On What Matters. Oxford: Oxford University Press.

records.line: Normative truths and our knowledge of them are of a distinctive kind; we should not expect them to be like ordinary, empirical truths and their knowledge.
records.reference: Parfit, D. (1984). Reasons and persons. Oxford: Oxford University Press.

records.line: Our wealth is legally ours, but the poorest people have much stronger moral claims to some of this wealth.
records.reference: Parfit, D. (1984). Reasons and persons. Oxford: Oxford University Press.

records.line: The fundamental units of natural selection, the things that survive or fail to survive, that form lineages of copies with random mutations, are genes.
records.reference: Dawkins, R. (1976). The Selfish Gene. Oxford University Press.

records.line: We are survival machines – robot ‘vehicles’ blindly programmed to preserve the ‘selfish’ molecules (‘replicators’) known as genes.
records.reference: Dawkins, R. (1976). The Selfish Gene. Oxford University Press.

records.line: An animal’s behaviour tends to maximize the survival of the genes “for” that behaviour, whether or not those genes are in its body.
records.reference: Dawkins, R. (1982). The Extended Phenotype. Oxford University Press.

records.line: Genes can have ‘extended’ phenotypic effects when ‘vehicles’ modify their environment (nests, dams, etc.) or manipulate other organisms.
records.reference: Dawkins, R. (1982). The Extended Phenotype. Oxford University Press.

records.line: Human culture evolves thanks to ‘memes’: information units replicating, mutating and being selected.
records.reference: Dawkins, R. (1976). The Selfish Gene. Oxford University Press.

records.line: A cultural trait may have evolved in the way that it has, simply because it is advantageous to itself.
records.reference: Dawkins, R. (1976). The Selfish Gene. Oxford University Press.

records.line: We, alone on earth, can rebel against the tyranny of the selfish replicators.
records.reference: Dawkins, R. (1976). The Selfish Gene. Oxford University Press.

records.line: The idea of evolution by natural selection unifies the realm of meaning and purpose with the realm of mechanism and physical law.
records.reference: Dennett, D. (1995). Darwin’s Dangerous Idea: Evolution and the meanings of life. Simon & Schuster.

records.line: Reasons gradually emerged out of mere causes, ‘what for’s out of ‘how come’s, with no ‘essential’ dividing line between them.
records.reference: Dennett, D. (2017). From Bacteria to Bach and Back. Allen Lane.

records.line: There were reasons tracked by evolution (‘free-floating rationales’) long before there were reason-representers like us.
records.reference: Dennett, D. (2017). From Bacteria to Bach and Back. Allen Lane.

records.line: Adaptationist reasoning is the heart and soul of evolutionary biology, it is not optional.
records.reference: Dennett, D. (1995). Darwin’s Dangerous Idea: Evolution and the meanings of life. Simon & Schuster.

records.line: Comprehension is not the source of competence; it is composed of competences, and it comes in degrees.
records.reference: Dennett, D. (2017). From Bacteria to Bach and Back. Allen Lane.

records.line: Our intentionality is gradually derived from the intentionality of our “selfish genes”, which is derived from the functional context of evolution.
records.reference: Dennett, D. (2013). Intuition Pumps. Penguin Books.

records.line: With ‘the intentional stance’ we can treat something as an agent without committing to hypotheses about its internal structures.
records.reference: Dennett, D. (1987). The Intentional Stance. MIT Press.

records.line: Mental states/events and content have no independent and concrete existence; they are ‘real patterns’ interpreted within ‘the intentional stance’.
records.reference: Dennett, D. (1991). “Self Portrait”. In A Companion to the Philosophy of Mind, edited by S. Guttenplan. 236-244. Oxford: Blackwell Press, 1994.

records.line: There could be different, but equally real, patterns discernible in the same noisy world; the choice of a pattern/theory is pragmatic.
records.reference: Dennett, D. (1991). “Real patterns”. Journal of Philosophy 88 (1):27-51.

records.line: Minds are composed of simpler subsystems that are composed of teams of still simpler agents so stupid that they can be replaced by a machine.
records.reference: Dennett, D. (1978). Brainstorms. MIT Press.

records.line: Attributions of mental content are founded on an appreciation of the functional roles of the items in question in the biological (engineering) economy of the organism (robot).
records.reference: Dennett, D. (1998). Brainchildren. MIT Press.

records.line: There is not a finish line or a center (a ‘Cartesian theater’) in the brain where the content is “presented” to the conscious subject.
records.reference: Dennett, D. (1991). Consciousness Explained. Little, Brown & Company.

records.line: A self is like a center of (narrative) gravity: an abstract object, a theorist’s useful fiction – not a thing in the brain.
records.reference: Dennett, D. (1991). Consciousness Explained. Little, Brown & Company.

records.line: All mental activity is accomplished in the brain by parallel, competitive processes of interpretation, under continuous editorial revision. (‘Multiple Drafts Model’)
records.reference: Dennett, D. (1991). Consciousness Explained. Little, Brown & Company.

records.line: Consciousness is a system of user-illusions evolved to make versions of our cognitive processes accessible to us for purposes of communication.
records.reference: Dennett, D. (2017). From Bacteria to Bach and Back. Allen Lane.

records.line: We can study consciousness objectively by getting the subjects’ verbal accounts of their conscious experiences (‘heterophenomenology’).
records.reference: Dennett, D. (1991). Consciousness Explained. Little, Brown & Company.

records.line: Qualia (ineffable, intrinsic, private, directly apprehensible mental properties) are the intentional objects of mistaken beliefs – they simply don’t exist, anywhere.
records.reference: Dennett, D. (2017). From Bacteria to Bach and Back. Allen Lane.; Dennett, D. (1988). “Quining qualia”. In Anthony J. Marcel & Edoardo Bisiach, Consciousness in Contemporary Science. New York: Oxford University Press.

records.line: There might be variations of consciousness from poet to possum to protozoon, and no ‘essence’ of consciousness to discover.
records.reference: Dennett, D. (2017). From Bacteria to Bach and Back. Allen Lane.

records.line: If the competence observed can be explained without appeal to comprehension, we must not indulge in extravagant anthropomorphism for animals.
records.reference: Dennett, D. (2017). From Bacteria to Bach and Back. Allen Lane.

records.line: Memes turned our brains into minds capable of dealing with ideas, thanks to the apps installed in our ‘necktops’.
records.reference: Dennett, D. (2017). From Bacteria to Bach and Back. Allen Lane.

records.line: Brains are computers, but unlike our computers and flip-flops, organisms and neurons are competitive agents; this may be their key difference.
records.reference: Dennett, D. (2017). From Bacteria to Bach and Back. Allen Lane.

records.line: The traditional view of free will, as a personal power isolated from physical causation, is both incoherent and unnecessary as a ground for morality.
records.reference: Dennett, D. (2017). From Bacteria to Bach and Back. Allen Lane.

records.line: ‘Evitability’ requires predictability by agents, hence a deterministic world; freedom evolves at the design level, it doesn’t belong at the physical level.
records.reference: Dennett, D. (2003). Freedom Evolves. New York: Viking Press.

records.line: Free will (like colors, dollars, promises) is part of the user-illusion of the manifest image that we shouldn’t want to dismantle; it’s where we live.
records.reference: Dennett, D. (2017). From Bacteria to Bach and Back. Allen Lane.

records.line: The counterfeit people created with AI are the most dangerous artifacts in human history, undermining the trust on which society depends.
records.reference: Dennett, D. (2023, May 16). “The Problem With Counterfeit People”. The Atlantic.

records.line: Philosophy is not like Euclid’s geometry: there are almost no indubitable truths or axioms, and we must expect vagueness at the penumbra on every concept.
records.reference: Warburton, N. (2023, September 25). “Favorite Books recommended by Daniel Dennett”. Five Books. https://fivebooks.com/best-books/daniel-dennett-book-recommendations/

records.line: A reductionistic but incomprehensible explanation of the brain exists; it needs ‘soft’ concepts like levels, mappings, and meanings in order to be comprehensible.
records.reference: Hofstadter, D. (1979). Gödel, Escher, Bach: An Eternal Golden Braid. Basic Books.

records.line: Emergent phenomena like the self and consciousness are ‘Strange Loops’ – paradoxical feedback loops between the bottom and the top levels.
records.reference: Hofstadter, D. (1979). Gödel, Escher, Bach: An Eternal Golden Braid. Basic Books.

records.line: Consciousness and intelligence can be lifted right out of the hardware in which they reside; they are high-level, software properties.
records.reference: Hofstadter, D. (1979). Gödel, Escher, Bach: An Eternal Golden Braid. Basic Books.

records.line: Intelligence arises out of the composition of unintelligent neurons, just as ‘intelligent’ colony behaviour emerges from unintelligent ants.
records.reference: Hofstadter, D. (1979). Gödel, Escher, Bach: An Eternal Golden Braid. Basic Books.

records.line: The “I” is a tremendously effective, indispensable illusion, and falling for it has great survival value; it is the most precious thing we own.
records.reference: Hofstadter, D. (2007). I Am a Strange Loop. Basic Books.

records.line: Each human head mirrors, and thus houses, a large number of other “I”s – other human beings at vastly differing levels of detail and fidelity.
records.reference: Hofstadter, D. (2007). I Am a Strange Loop. Basic Books.

records.line: Although any individual’s consciousness is primarily resident in one particular brain, it is also somewhat present in other brains as well.
records.reference: Hofstadter, D. (2007). I Am a Strange Loop. Basic Books.

records.line: When the central brain is destroyed, tiny fragments of the living individual remain alive in other brains, in personal notebooks, etc.
records.reference: Hofstadter, D. (2007). I Am a Strange Loop. Basic Books.

records.line: Our decisions are made in the brain by an analogue to a voting process in a democracy as our desires chime in, constrained by external factors.
records.reference: Hofstadter, D. (2007). I Am a Strange Loop. Basic Books.

records.line: Our will, quite the opposite of being free, is steady and stable, like an inner gyroscope, and it is the stability of our non-free will that makes us who we are.
records.reference: Hofstadter, D. (2007). I Am a Strange Loop. Basic Books.

records.line: Common-sense psychology appears to be stagnant; there are many mental phenomena that folk psychology does not allow us to explain.
records.reference: Ramsey, W. (n.d.). “Eliminative Materialism”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/materialism-eliminative/

records.line: Since folk theories (folk physics, folk biology, etc.) generally turn out to be mistaken, it seems quite improbable that folk psychology will turn out true.
records.reference: Ramsey, W. (n.d.). “Eliminative Materialism”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/materialism-eliminative/

records.line: Introspection may be largely determined by our folk psychological framework; “introspecting” beliefs may be just like “seeing” demonic spirits or celestial spheres.
records.reference: Ramsey, W. (n.d.). “Eliminative Materialism”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/materialism-eliminative/

records.line: As our neuroscientific understanding grows, we will come to see that our current mental state concepts (belief, sensation, qualia, etc.) need to be discarded.
records.reference: Kind, A. (n.d.). “Qualia”. The Internet Encyclopedia of Philosophy. https://www.iep.utm.edu/qualia/

records.line: There is an evolved connection between the faithfulness of the brain’s “world-model” and the propriety of the organism’s behavior but it is not direct.
records.reference: Churchland, P. M. (1982). “The Anti‐Realist Epistemology of Van Fraassen's The Scientific Image”. Pacific Philosophical Quarterly 63 (3):226-235.

records.line: Our observational concepts are just as theory-laden as any others; our observational ontology is as dubious or real as our non-observational ontology.
records.reference: Churchland, P. M. (1982). “The Anti‐Realist Epistemology of Van Fraassen's The Scientific Image”. Pacific Philosophical Quarterly 63 (3):226-235.

records.line: Specifying some point where neuroscience ends and philosophy of science begins is hopeless because the boundaries are poorly defined.
records.reference: Bickle, J., Mandik, P., & Landreth, A. (n.d.). “The Philosophy of Neuroscience”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/neuroscience/

records.line: Activation vectors are the central kind of representations, and vector-to-vector transformations are the central kind of computations, in the brain.
records.reference: Bickle, J., Mandik, P., & Landreth, A. (n.d.). “The Philosophy of Neuroscience”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/neuroscience/

records.line: A person’s declarative utterance is a one-dimensional projection of a four- or five-dimensional “solid” that is an element in their true kinematical state.
records.reference: Churchland, P. M. (1981). “Eliminative Materialism and the Propositional Attitudes”. The Journal of Philosophy, 78(2), 67–90.

records.line: The more one learns about the brain, the harder it becomes to imagine creatures with brain processes like ours but lacking consciousness.
records.reference: Bickle, J., Mandik, P., & Landreth, A. (n.d.). “The Philosophy of Neuroscience”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/neuroscience/

records.line: Conscience is a brain construct rooted in our neural circuitry; it is not infallible, it develops over time, and is sensitive to social norms.
records.reference: Churchland, P. (2019). Conscience: The Origins of Moral Intuition. W. W. Norton & Company.

records.line: Moral judgment is a constraint satisfaction process undergirded by instincts, habits, norms, social skills, values, and problem solving.
records.reference: Churchland, P. (2019). Conscience: The Origins of Moral Intuition. W. W. Norton & Company.

records.line: If you intend your action, know what you’re doing, are of sound mind, and are not coerced, then you are responsible for your action.
records.reference: Churchland, P. (2019). Conscience: The Origins of Moral Intuition. W. W. Norton & Company.

records.line: Since we know that we have brains, and that we have qualia, we know that brain-headed systems can have qualia.
records.reference: Block, N. (1978). “Troubles with functionalism”. Minnesota Studies in the Philosophy of Science 9:261-325.

records.line: Something (a homunculi-headed or machine simulation) can be functionally equivalent to you, yet there can be prima facie doubt about its qualia.
records.reference: Block, N. (1978). “Troubles with functionalism”. Minnesota Studies in the Philosophy of Science 9:261-325.

records.line: The capacity to emit sensible responses is not sufficient for intelligence; whether behavior is intelligent is in part a matter of how it is internally produced.
records.reference: Block, N. (1981). “Psychologism and behaviorism”. Philosophical Review 90 (1):5-43.

records.line: Specifying inputs and outputs in behavioristic terms is chauvinist; it denies mentality to brains in vats and to paralytics.
records.reference: Block, N. (1978). “Troubles with functionalism”. Minnesota Studies in the Philosophy of Science 9:261-325.

records.line: Functionalism is guilty of either liberalism (classifying systems that lack mentality as having mentality) or chauvinism (the opposite, e.g. species-specific).
records.reference: Block, N. (1978). “Troubles with functionalism”. Minnesota Studies in the Philosophy of Science 9:261-325.

records.line: Since there is a doubt that qualia are in the domain of psychology, there is a doubt that qualitative states are empirical functional states.
records.reference: Block, N. (1978). “Troubles with functionalism”. Minnesota Studies in the Philosophy of Science 9:261-325.

records.line: Consciousness is a mongrel concept: there are a number of very different “consciousnesses” that are often conflated, with bad results.
records.reference: Block, N. (1995). “On a confusion about a function of consciousness”. Brain and Behavioral Sciences 18 (2):227–247.

records.line: ‘Access-consciousness’ (A-consciousness) is representational; it is used in reasoning and rationally guiding speech and action.
records.reference: Block, N. (1995). “On a confusion about a function of consciousness”. Brain and Behavioral Sciences 18 (2):227–247.

records.line: ‘Phenomenal consciousness’ (P-consciousness) is experience; it is distinct from any cognitive, intentional, or functional property.
records.reference: Block, N. (1995). “On a confusion about a function of consciousness”. Brain and Behavioral Sciences 18 (2):227–247.

records.line: A-conscious states must always be states of consciousness of; P-conscious states sometimes are and sometimes are not transitive.
records.reference: Block, N. (1995). “On a confusion about a function of consciousness”. Brain and Behavioral Sciences 18 (2):227–247.

records.line: A-consciousness and P-consciousness interact, and there are cases of P-consciousness without A-consciousness (e.g. color perception in babies).
records.reference: Block, N. (2023). The Border Between Seeing and Thinking. New York, US: OUP.

records.line: Perception is constitutively nonpropositional, nonconceptual, and iconic, and cognition does not constitutively have any of these properties.
records.reference: Block, N. (2023). The Border Between Seeing and Thinking. New York, US: OUP.

records.line: Fundamental laws abstract from all the detailed ceteris paribus conditions that give phenomenological laws a chance of being true within their specific domains.
records.reference: Dupré, J. (2005). “Cartwright, Nancy (1944–)”. Encyclopedia.com. https://www.encyclopedia.com/humanities/encyclopedias-almanacs-transcripts-and-maps/cartwright-nancy-1944

records.line: While fundamental laws may play an essential role in the formulation of phenomenological laws, the former are not themselves true.
records.reference: Dupré, J. (2005). “Cartwright, Nancy (1944–)”. Encyclopedia.com. https://www.encyclopedia.com/humanities/encyclopedias-almanacs-transcripts-and-maps/cartwright-nancy-1944

records.line: A “nomological machine” is a fixed (enough) arrangement of components that will give rise to the kind of regular behaviour represented in laws.
records.reference: Cartwright, N. (1999). The Dappled World: A Study of the Boundaries of Science. Cambridge University Press.

records.line: Laws of nature (as regular associations) hold only ceteris paribus – only relative to the successful repeated operation of a nomological machine.
records.reference: Cartwright, N. (1999). The Dappled World: A Study of the Boundaries of Science. Cambridge University Press.

records.line: Nomological machines might occur naturally in nature, but more often they are engineered by us, as in a laboratory experiment.
records.reference: Cartwright, N. (1999). The Dappled World: A Study of the Boundaries of Science. Cambridge University Press.

records.line: Laws in different domains are not reducible to laws of a more fundamental science because the world does not have a unitary underlying lawlike pattern.
records.reference: Dupré, J. (2005). “Cartwright, Nancy (1944–)”. Encyclopedia.com. https://www.encyclopedia.com/humanities/encyclopedias-almanacs-transcripts-and-maps/cartwright-nancy-1944

records.line: Science is not searching for laws but constructing models for which the question of truth does not arise.
records.reference: Dupré, J. (2005). “Cartwright, Nancy (1944–)”. Encyclopedia.com. https://www.encyclopedia.com/humanities/encyclopedias-almanacs-transcripts-and-maps/cartwright-nancy-1944

records.line: Physics works as part of a motley assembly of (natural, biomedical, and social) scientific and engineering disciplines along with practical knowledge.
records.reference: Cartwright, N. (1999). The Dappled World: A Study of the Boundaries of Science. Cambridge University Press.

records.line: A cause is a factor that increases the probability of its effect in every background context.
records.reference: Hitchcock, C. (n.d.). “Probabilistic Causation”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/causation-probabilistic/

records.line: There is a great variety of different kinds of causes and causes of the same kind can operate in different ways; there can be no universal account of causality.
records.reference: Cartwright, N. (1999). The Dappled World: A Study of the Boundaries of Science. Cambridge University Press.

records.line: Singular rather than generic causes are fundamental.
records.reference: Dupré, J. (2005). “Cartwright, Nancy (1944–)”. Encyclopedia.com. https://www.encyclopedia.com/humanities/encyclopedias-almanacs-transcripts-and-maps/cartwright-nancy-1944

records.line: The fundamental facts about nature that ensure that regularities can obtain are not again themselves regularities/laws; they are capacities.
records.reference: Morrison, M. (1995). “Capacities, tendencies, and the problem of singular causes”. Philosophy and Phenomenological Research, 55(1), 163-168.

records.line: The higher causal level (depending on capacities) is indispensable for the explanation of what regularities there are in the world.
records.reference: Psillos, S. (2009). “Cartwright’s Realist Toil: From Entities to Capacities”. Knowing the Structure of Nature. Palgrave Macmillan.

records.line: Capacities remove the ceteris paribus clause from the causal laws: aspirin always carries the capacity to relieve headache.
records.reference: Psillos, S. (2009). “Cartwright’s Realist Toil: From Entities to Capacities”. Knowing the Structure of Nature. Palgrave Macmillan.

records.line: Capacities are of properties and not of individuals: the property of being an aspirin carries with it the capacity to cure headaches.
records.reference: Cartwright, N. (1989). Nature’s Capacities and Their Measurement. Oxford: Clarendon Press.

records.line: In our moral judgements we express our feelings (which are a species of desire), not describe them.
records.reference: Baldwin, T. (2001). Contemporary Philosophy. Oxford University Press.

records.line: Our talk of ethics is quasi-realist; our feelings color our experience of the world by projecting values onto it.
records.reference: Baldwin, T. (2001). Contemporary Philosophy. Oxford University Press.

records.line: Perhaps we do not need a conception of truth, but only a conception of the particular truths in the plural, the ones that make issues for us from time to time.
records.reference: Blackburn, S. (2005). Truth: A guide for the perplexed. Penguin.

records.line: Western dualisms (self/other, mind/body, male/female, whole/part, maker/made, etc.) have been used to dominate all constituted as others.
records.reference: Haraway, D. (1985). “A Cyborg Manifesto: Science, Technology, and Socialist-Feminism in the Late Twentieth Century,” in Simians, Cyborgs and Women: The Reinvention of Nature. Routledge. 1990.

records.line: There is nothing about being “female” that naturally binds women; “being” female is constructed in contested scientific discourses and social practices.
records.reference: Haraway, D. (1985). “A Cyborg Manifesto: Science, Technology, and Socialist-Feminism in the Late Twentieth Century,” in Simians, Cyborgs and Women: The Reinvention of Nature. Routledge. 1990.

records.line: The feminist dream of a common language, of perfectly faithful naming of “women’s experience,” is a totalizing and imperialist one.
records.reference: Haraway, D. (1985). “A Cyborg Manifesto: Science, Technology, and Socialist-Feminism in the Late Twentieth Century,” in Simians, Cyborgs and Women: The Reinvention of Nature. Routledge. 1990.

records.line: Feminisms do not need to use the perspective of a latent position of moral superiority, victimhood, innocence, and greater closeness to nature.
records.reference: Haraway, D. (1985). “A Cyborg Manifesto: Science, Technology, and Socialist-Feminism in the Late Twentieth Century,” in Simians, Cyborgs and Women: The Reinvention of Nature. Routledge. 1990.

records.line: We must refuse an anti-science metaphysics, a demonology of technology, and embrace the task of reconstructing the boundaries of daily life.
records.reference: Haraway, D. (1985). “A Cyborg Manifesto: Science, Technology, and Socialist-Feminism in the Late Twentieth Century,” in Simians, Cyborgs and Women: The Reinvention of Nature. Routledge. 1990.

records.line: High-tech culture challenges dualisms like human/machine, mind/body, natural/artificial; we find ourselves to be cyborgs, hybrids, mosaics, chimeras.
records.reference: Haraway, D. (1985). “A Cyborg Manifesto: Science, Technology, and Socialist-Feminism in the Late Twentieth Century,” in Simians, Cyborgs and Women: The Reinvention of Nature. Routledge. 1990.

records.line: The cyborg subverts the Western myths of original unity and final completion in an organic whole (Garden of Eden, Marxism, psychoanalysis).
records.reference: Haraway, D. (1985). “A Cyborg Manifesto: Science, Technology, and Socialist-Feminism in the Late Twentieth Century,” in Simians, Cyborgs and Women: The Reinvention of Nature. Routledge. 1990.

records.line: A cyborg world can be about a grid of control, but it can also be about a joint kinship with animals and machines, and permanently partial identities.
records.reference: Haraway, D. (1985). “A Cyborg Manifesto: Science, Technology, and Socialist-Feminism in the Late Twentieth Century,” in Simians, Cyborgs and Women: The Reinvention of Nature. Routledge. 1990.

records.line: Cyborg myth and politics are a matter of survival; a cyborg identity can be a potent subjectivity synthesized from fusions of “outsider” identities.
records.reference: Haraway, D. (1985). “A Cyborg Manifesto: Science, Technology, and Socialist-Feminism in the Late Twentieth Century,” in Simians, Cyborgs and Women: The Reinvention of Nature. Routledge. 1990.

records.line: Cyborg feminism does not search for a new essential unity and a common language; it prefers irony and coalition – affinity, not identity.
records.reference: Haraway, D. (1985). “A Cyborg Manifesto: Science, Technology, and Socialist-Feminism in the Late Twentieth Century,” in Simians, Cyborgs and Women: The Reinvention of Nature. Routledge. 1990.

records.line: Relativism is the perfect mirror twin of totalization in the ideologies of objectivity; both deny the stakes in location, embodiment, and partial perspective.
records.reference: Haraway, D. (1988). “Situated Knowledges: The Science Question in Feminism and the Privilege of Partial Perspective”. Feminist Studies 14 (3):575-599.

records.line: Rational knowledge is a process of ongoing critical interpretation among communities; it is not the product of isolated individuals.
records.reference: Haraway, D. (1988). “Situated Knowledges: The Science Question in Feminism and the Privilege of Partial Perspective”. Feminist Studies 14 (3):575-599.

records.line: Feminist objectivity is about embodied and situated knowledges, and against transcendent, unlocatable, and so irresponsible, knowledge claims.
records.reference: Haraway, D. (1988). “Situated Knowledges: The Science Question in Feminism and the Privilege of Partial Perspective”. Feminist Studies 14 (3):575-599.

records.line: To lose authoritative biological accounts of sex seems to be to lose too much – to lose the body itself as anything but a blank page for social inscriptions.
records.reference: Haraway, D. (1988). “Situated Knowledges: The Science Question in Feminism and the Privilege of Partial Perspective”. Feminist Studies 14 (3):575-599.

records.line: When science engages with its objects of knowledge as actors and agents, faithful knowledge can be imagined and can make claims on us.
records.reference: Haraway, D. (1988). “Situated Knowledges: The Science Question in Feminism and the Privilege of Partial Perspective”. Feminist Studies 14 (3):575-599.

records.line: Justification of knowledge is not like mathematical proof; it is gradational, like how one determines the reasonableness of entries in a crossword.
records.reference: Haack, S. (1993). “Double-aspect foundherentism: A new theory of empirical justification”. Philosophy and Phenomenological Research 53 (1):113-128.

records.line: Justification of knowledge requires both foundational experience (like clues in a crossword) and coherent reasons (like intersecting entries in a crossword).
records.reference: Haack, S. (1993). “Double-aspect foundherentism: A new theory of empirical justification”. Philosophy and Phenomenological Research 53 (1):113-128.

records.line: How good A’s evidence for p is depends on how favorable A’s direct evidence is; how secure A’s direct reasons are; how comprehensive A’s evidence is.
records.reference: Haack, S. (1993). “Double-aspect foundherentism: A new theory of empirical justification”. Philosophy and Phenomenological Research 53 (1):113-128.

records.line: Gettier-type “paradoxes” are inevitable, and harmless, given the mismatch between the gradational character of justification and the categorical character of knowledge.
records.reference: Haack, S. (2009). Evidence and Inquiry: A Pragmatist Reconstruction of Epistemology. Amherst, N.Y.: Prometheus Books.

records.line: More than one logic is a correct representation of validity, in the sense that it is inaccurate but good enough relative to its goals.
records.reference: Haack, S. (1978). Philosophy of Logics. London and New York: Cambridge University Press.

records.line: There’s no “scientific method,” i.e., no method used by all, and only, scientists; scientific inquiry is continuous with everyday empirical inquiry.
records.reference: Haack, S. (2011). Defending Science – Within Reason: Between Scientism and Cynicism. Prometheus Books.

records.line: Scientific inquiry is like an extension of everyday inquiry with more rigour, more tools and procedures, and more people (within and across generations).
records.reference: Haack, S. (2011). Defending Science – Within Reason: Between Scientism and Cynicism. Prometheus Books.

records.line: For science to be even possible, there must be real kinds and real laws, but there is no guarantee that current scientific vocabulary matches real kinds.
records.reference: Haack, S. (2011). Defending Science – Within Reason: Between Scientism and Cynicism. Prometheus Books.

records.line: The changes of meaning in the language of science need not impede inquiry, but can actually advance it when they come closer to real kinds in the world.
records.reference: Haack, S. (2011). Defending Science – Within Reason: Between Scientism and Cynicism. Prometheus Books.

records.line: Vulgar pragmatism (of Rorty) undermines the possibility of real inquiry of any kind, and the hope of knowing what would truly improve the condition of society.
records.reference: Haack, S. (1997). “Vulgar Rortyism”. The New Criterion.

records.line: “Feminist epistemology” (“women’s ways of knowing”) reintroduces old, indefensible sexist stereotypes and undermines inquiry by politicizing it.
records.reference: Haack, S. (1999). Manifesto of a Passionate Moderate: Unfashionable Essays. University Of Chicago Press.

records.line: The idea that what is to women’s advantage should determine theory-choice undermines the possibility even of determining what is to women’s advantage.
records.reference: Haack, S. (1999). Manifesto of a Passionate Moderate: Unfashionable Essays. University Of Chicago Press.

records.line: There is one real world, varied but also integrated, including physical stuff, kinds, laws, etc. and human artifacts, physical, social, intellectual, and imaginative.
records.reference: Haack, S. (2023). “What a World! The Pluralistic Universe of Innocent Realism”. Journal of Philosophical Investigations 17 (45):29-35.

records.line: An organism is an autopoietic machine: a network of processes of production of components which continuously regenerate and realize the network.
records.reference: Maturana, H. & Varela, F. (1980/1972). Autopoiesis and Cognition: The Realization of the Living. Dordrecht: D. Reidel.

records.line: The domain of problems for a species does not exist “out there” in a pre-given world; living beings and their environments determine each other.
records.reference: Varela, F. J., Thompson, E., & Rosch, E. (1991). The Embodied Mind: Cognitive Science and Human Experience. Cambridge: MIT Press.

records.line: The domain of all the interactions in which an autopoietic system can enter without loss of identity is its cognitive domain.
records.reference: Maturana, H. & Varela, F. (1980/1972). Autopoiesis and Cognition: The Realization of the Living. Dordrecht: D. Reidel.

records.line: Traditional notions of representation and computation, and decompositions of the cognitive system into inner modules (“boxology”) are misleading.
records.reference: Thompson, E. & Varela, F. (2001). “Radical embodiment: Neural dynamics and consciousness”. Trends in Cognitive Sciences 5(10), 418–425.

records.line: Perception consists in perceptually guided action.
records.reference: Varela, F. J., Thompson, E., & Rosch, E. (1991). The Embodied Mind: Cognitive Science and Human Experience. Cambridge: MIT Press.

records.line: Cognitive structures emerge from the recurrent sensorimotor patterns that allow action to be perceptually guided.
records.reference: Varela, F. J., Thompson, E., & Rosch, E. (1991). The Embodied Mind: Cognitive Science and Human Experience. Cambridge: MIT Press.

records.line: Cognitive (sensorimotor) capacities are embedded in a more surrounding biological, psychological and cultural context.
records.reference: Varela, F. J., Thompson, E., & Rosch, E. (1991). The Embodied Mind: Cognitive Science and Human Experience. Cambridge: MIT Press.

records.line: Understanding the complex interplay of brain, body, and world requires the tools and methods of nonlinear dynamical systems theory.
records.reference: Thompson, E. & Varela, F. (2001). “Radical embodiment: Neural dynamics and consciousness”. Trends in Cognitive Sciences 5(10), 418–425.

records.line: A theory’s predictive success only supplies a plausibility argument in favour of its somehow or other having latched onto the truth.
records.reference: Worrall, J. (1989). “Structural realism: The best of both worlds?” Dialectica 43 (1-2):99-124.

records.line: Assuming that the realist is not talking in some conjectural sense, realism is not compatible with the existence of radical theoretical changes in science.
records.reference: Worrall, J. (1989). “Structural realism: The best of both worlds?” Dialectica 43 (1-2):99-124.

records.line: The successful empirical content of a once-accepted theory is in general carried over to the new theory, but its basic theoretical claims are not.
records.reference: Worrall, J. (1989). “Structural realism: The best of both worlds?” Dialectica 43 (1-2):99-124.

records.line: There is continuity or accumulation in the theory-changes in science, but the continuity is one of form or structure (equations), not of content (ontology).
records.reference: Worrall, J. (1989). “Structural realism: The best of both worlds?” Dialectica 43 (1-2):99-124.

records.line: It is a mistake to think that we can ever “understand” the nature of the basic furniture of the universe.
records.reference: Worrall, J. (1989). “Structural realism: The best of both worlds?” Dialectica 43 (1-2):99-124.

records.line: Evolution has no moral direction; an evolutionary understanding can explain our differing moral intuitions, but it does not justify them.
records.reference: Singer, P. (2009). The Life You Can Save. Random House.

records.line: Putting yourself in the place of others is what thinking ethically is all about.
records.reference: Singer, P. (2009). The Life You Can Save. Random House.

records.line: An action contrary to the preference of any being is, unless this preference is outweighed by contrary preferences, wrong.
records.reference: Singer, P. (1993). Practical Ethics. Second Edition. Cambridge University Press.

records.line: All the arguments to prove the superiority of humans cannot shatter this hard fact: in suffering the animals are our equals.
records.reference: Singer, P. (1975). Animal liberation. HarperCollins.

records.line: We must make radical changes in our treatment of animals involving our diet, farming, scientific experiments, hunting, zoos, etc. to reduce their suffering.
records.reference: Singer, P. (1975). Animal liberation. HarperCollins.

records.line: For ‘impersonal’ animals who are not self-conscious beings aspiring to longer life, the case against killing (without causing pain) is weaker.
records.reference: Singer, P. (1979). Practical Ethics. Cambridge University Press.

records.line: The fact that a being is human, and alive, does not in itself tell us whether it is wrong to take that being’s life; personhood matters.
records.reference: Singer, P. (1995). Rethinking life and death. St. Martin’s Press.

records.line: To give preference to the life of a being simply because that being is a member of our species would put us in the same position as racists.
records.reference: Singer, P. (1993). Practical Ethics. Second Edition. Cambridge University Press.

records.line: If we can prevent something bad without sacrificing anything of comparable significance, we ought to do it.
records.reference: Singer, P. (1993). Practical Ethics. Second Edition. Cambridge University Press.

records.line: It makes no moral difference whether the person I can help is a neighbor ten yards from me or a stranger ten thousand miles away.
records.reference: Singer, P. (1972). “Famine, Affluence, and Morality”. Philosophy and Public Affairs, 1(1).

records.line: Reason is capable of presenting us with objective, impartial, non-natural reasons for action.
records.reference: Singer, P. & Lazari-Radek, K. (2014). The Point of View of the Universe: Sidgwick and Contemporary Ethics. New York: Oxford University Press.

records.line: Human beings are soft, tender, and capable of being wounded; but it is because of this plant-like nature that we are capable of flourishing.
records.reference: Wilkinson, H., & Dunford, C. (Sen. Ed.) (2019). Philosophers: Their Lives and Works. DK.

records.line: Emotions are not just the fuel that powers the psychological mechanism of a creature, they are highly complex and messy parts of the creature’s reasoning.
records.reference: Nussbaum, M. (2001). Upheavals of thought: The intelligence of emotions. Cambridge, UK: Cambridge University Press.

records.line: Emotions are ‘geological upheavals of thought’: judgments in which people acknowledge their vulnerability and neediness, for their own flourishing.
records.reference: Nussbaum, M. (2001). Upheavals of thought: The intelligence of emotions. Cambridge, UK: Cambridge University Press.

records.line: Instead of viewing morality as a system of principles to be grasped by the detached intellect, we have to include emotions in ethical reasoning.
records.reference: Nussbaum, M. (2001). Upheavals of thought: The intelligence of emotions. Cambridge, UK: Cambridge University Press.

records.line: Disgust and shame are hierarchical and connected with restrictions on liberty; the appeal to these emotions in law and public policy is problematic.
records.reference: Sanchez, J. (2004) “Discussing Disgust”. Interview with Martha Nussbaum. https://reason.com/2004/07/15/discussing-disgust-2/

records.line: The arts play a central role in human self-understanding: the understanding of an emotion is incomplete unless its narrative history is grasped.
records.reference: Nussbaum, M. (2001). Upheavals of thought: The intelligence of emotions. Cambridge, UK: Cambridge University Press.

records.line: The theory that best represents our intuition of a dignified human life is the capabilities approach.
records.reference: Kleist, C. (n.d.). “Global Ethics: Capabilities Approach”. The Internet Encyclopedia of Philosophy. https://www.iep.utm.edu/ge-capab/

records.line: A capability is a real possibility or opportunity for an individual to perform certain functions, in a truly human way.
records.reference: Kleist, C. (n.d.). “Global Ethics: Capabilities Approach”. The Internet Encyclopedia of Philosophy. https://www.iep.utm.edu/ge-capab/

records.line: Central capabilities: life; bodily health; bodily integrity; senses, imagination, thought; emotions; practical reason; affiliation; other species; play; control over one’s environment.
records.reference: Kleist, C. (n.d.). “Global Ethics: Capabilities Approach”. The Internet Encyclopedia of Philosophy. https://www.iep.utm.edu/ge-capab/

records.line: Citizens should be guaranteed, via institutions, a social minimum whereby central capabilities can be realized.
records.reference: Kleist, C. (n.d.). “Global Ethics: Capabilities Approach”. The Internet Encyclopedia of Philosophy. https://www.iep.utm.edu/ge-capab/

records.line: Personal, symbolic subversive gestures offer only a false hope; feminism demands and deserves lasting political, legal, institutional change.
records.reference: Nussbaum, M. (1999). “The Professor of Parody: The hip defeatism of Judith Butler”. The New Republic.

records.line: We can no longer draw the usual line between humans and other animals; experiments reveal that they have sentience, emotion, and various types of intelligence.
records.reference: Nussbaum, M. (2023). Justice for Animals: Our Collective Responsibility. ‎Simon & Schuster.

records.line: Animals cannot bring a legal claim, but neither can most humans – we need a lawyer to press our claims; animals should also have this simple privilege.
records.reference: Nussbaum, M. (2023). Justice for Animals: Our Collective Responsibility. ‎Simon & Schuster.

records.line: Gender is produced through ways in which language, bodily gestures, movements, and styles constitute the illusion of an abiding gendered self.
records.reference: Butler, J. (1990). Gender trouble. Routledge.

records.line: “I” only exists within signifying practices; gender acts are not performed by the subject, they performatively constitute the subject.
records.reference: Butler, J. (1990). Gender trouble. Routledge.

records.line: Gender has no essence, no ontological status apart from the various repetitive acts which constitute its reality.
records.reference: Butler, J. (1990). Gender trouble. Routledge.

records.line: The very notion of an essential, “natural” sex is constituted as part of the strategy that conceals gender’s performative character.
records.reference: Butler, J. (1990). Gender trouble. Routledge.

records.line: It is impossible to separate gender from the political and cultural intersections (racial, class, ethnic, sexual, regional) in which it is produced.
records.reference: Butler, J. (1990). Gender trouble. Routledge.

records.line: Drag subverts the distinction between inner and outer psychic space and mocks both the expressive model of gender and the notion of a true gender identity.
records.reference: Butler, J. (1990). Gender trouble. Routledge.

records.line: Experience is itself the fundamental given natural fact: full recognition of its reality is the obligatory starting point for any realistic version of physicalism.
records.reference: Strawson, G. (2006). “Realistic monism: why physicalism entails panpsychism”. In Anthony Freeman (ed.), Consciousness and Its Place in Nature: Does Physicalism Entail Panpsychism? Exeter: Imprint Academic.

records.line: We have no reason to think that we know anything about the physical that contradicts the idea that experiential phenomena are physical phenomena.
records.reference: Strawson, G. (2006). “Realistic monism: why physicalism entails panpsychism”. In Anthony Freeman (ed.), Consciousness and Its Place in Nature: Does Physicalism Entail Panpsychism? Exeter: Imprint Academic.

records.line: Emergence can’t be brute: just as the spatial can’t arbitrarily emerge from the non-spatial, the experiential can’t arbitrarily emerge from the non-experiential.
records.reference: Strawson, G. (2006). “Realistic monism: why physicalism entails panpsychism”. In Anthony Freeman (ed.), Consciousness and Its Place in Nature: Does Physicalism Entail Panpsychism? Exeter: Imprint Academic.

records.line: If experience emerges from something that is not experience, then that something must already be experiential however primitively or strangely.
records.reference: Strawson, G. (2006). “Realistic monism: why physicalism entails panpsychism”. In Anthony Freeman (ed.), Consciousness and Its Place in Nature: Does Physicalism Entail Panpsychism? Exeter: Imprint Academic.

records.line: Real physicalists must accept that at least some ultimates are intrinsically experience-involving; real physicalism entails panpsychism.
records.reference: Strawson, G. (2006). “Realistic monism: why physicalism entails panpsychism”. In Anthony Freeman (ed.), Consciousness and Its Place in Nature: Does Physicalism Entail Panpsychism? Exeter: Imprint Academic.

records.line: You do what you do because of the way you are.
records.reference: Strawson, G. (1994). “The impossibility of moral responsibility”. Philosophical Studies 75 (1-2):5-24.

records.line: To be truly responsible for the way you are, you must have intentionally brought it about that you are the way you are, and this sets off an infinite regress.
records.reference: Strawson, G. (1994). “The impossibility of moral responsibility”. Philosophical Studies 75 (1-2):5-24.

records.line: You cannot be truly responsible for the way you are, so you cannot be truly responsible for what you do.
records.reference: Strawson, G. (1994). “The impossibility of moral responsibility”. Philosophical Studies 75 (1-2):5-24.

records.line: It is exactly as just to punish or reward people for their actions as it is to punish or reward them for the (natural) colour of their hair.
records.reference: Strawson, G. (1994). “The impossibility of moral responsibility”. Philosophical Studies 75 (1-2):5-24.

records.line: Human beings differ deeply in a number of ways that affect their experience of the mental self as diachronically continuous or as a narrative.
records.reference: Strawson, G. (1997). “‘the Self’”. Journal of Consciousness Studies 4 (5-6):405-428.; Strawson, G. (2004). “Against Narrativity”. Ratio 17 (4):428-452.

records.line: One can have a full sense of the single mental self at any given time without thinking of the self as something that has long-term continuity.
records.reference: Strawson, G. (1997). “‘the Self’”. Journal of Consciousness Studies 4 (5-6):405-428.

records.line: Many mental selves exist for a human being, one at a time and one after another for considerably different lengths of time, like pearls on a string.
records.reference: Strawson, G. (1997). “‘the Self’”. Journal of Consciousness Studies 4 (5-6):405-428.

records.line: Prior to the introduction of data, naturalism trumps theism on grounds of simplicity (fewer and less complex primitive entities, features, and principles).
records.reference: Oppy, G. (2013). The Best Argument Against God. New York: Palgrave-Macmillan.

records.line: Considerations about global causal order, cosmic fine-tuning, and the accumulated scientific and a priori knowledge do not favour theism over naturalism.
records.reference: Oppy, G. (2013). The Best Argument Against God. New York: Palgrave-Macmillan.

records.line: Considerations about morality – moral truth, moral knowledge, conscience, the distribution of virtue, etc. – do not favour theism over naturalism.
records.reference: Oppy, G. (2013). The Best Argument Against God. New York: Palgrave-Macmillan.

records.line: Considerations about consciousness and reason do not favour theism over naturalism.
records.reference: Oppy, G. (2013). The Best Argument Against God. New York: Palgrave-Macmillan.

records.line: Considerations about horrendous evil favour naturalism over (standard) theism (with an omnipotent, omniscient, omnibenevolent God).
records.reference: Oppy, G. (2013). The Best Argument Against God. New York: Palgrave-Macmillan.

records.line: The ways in which theism can be rendered consistent with the amount of evil in our world further increases the simplicity advantage of naturalism.
records.reference: Oppy, G. (2013). The Best Argument Against God. New York: Palgrave-Macmillan.

records.line: Our lives can have meaning and purpose without an afterlife or an epic cosmic melodrama; the goods of the Aristotelian good life are intrinsically valuable.
records.reference: Oppy, G. (2013). The Best Argument Against God. New York: Palgrave-Macmillan.

records.line: Given that none of the evidence favours theism over naturalism and that naturalism is simpler, naturalism is to be preferred to theism.
records.reference: Oppy, G. (2013). The Best Argument Against God. New York: Palgrave-Macmillan.

records.line: We should not be too confident that there is a compelling evidential argument in favour of naturalism over theism; we may not have all the data.
records.reference: Oppy, G. (2013). The Best Argument Against God. New York: Palgrave-Macmillan.

records.line: The decision between naturalism and theism is a matter for judgement; not all intelligent, reflective, informed people must agree.
records.reference: Oppy, G. (2013). The Best Argument Against God. New York: Palgrave-Macmillan.

records.line: The human mind is not all in the head; it emerges at the productive interface of brain, body, and social and material world.
records.reference: Clark, A. (2008). Supersizing the mind: Embodiment, action, and cognitive extension. Oxford: Oxford University Press.

records.line: If an external object performs a function that we’d regard as mental if it were performed by your brain, then that object is a part of your mind.
records.reference: Clark, A., & Chalmers, D. (1998). “The extended mind”. Analysis, 58(1).

records.line: Animals and humans construct cognitive niches – physical structures that transform problem spaces in ways that aid thinking and reasoning.
records.reference: Clark, A. (2008). Supersizing the mind: Embodiment, action, and cognitive extension. Oxford: Oxford University Press.

records.line: My mental states might be partly constituted by the mental states of other thinkers – embodied in my secretary, my accountant, or my collaborator.
records.reference: Clark, A., & Chalmers, D. (1998). “The extended mind”. Analysis, 58(1).

records.line: External resources can be included as part of the cognitive system as long as they are reliable, trustworthy, and accessible.
records.reference: Clark, A. (2010). “Coupling, constitution, and the cognitive kind: A reply to Adams and Aizaway”. In R. Menary (ed.), The Extended Mind. Cambridge, MA: MIT Press, pp. 81–100.

records.line: Conscious experience flows from ‘generative entanglements’ that mix predictions about the world, the body, and our own reactive dispositions.
records.reference: Clark, A. (2019). “Consciousness as Generative Entanglement”. Journal of Philosophy, 116(12), 645-662.

records.line: The predictive brain is not an insulated inference engine so much as an action-oriented engagement machine.
records.reference: Clark, A. (2016). Surfing uncertainty: Prediction, Action, and the Embodied Mind. Oxford: Oxford University Press.

records.line: The tasks of explaining the performance of the cognitive functions (perception, attention, memory, etc.) are easy problems.
records.reference: Chalmers, D. (1995). “Facing up to the problem of consciousness.” Journal of Consciousness Studies, 2(3), 200-19.

records.line: The task of explaining why the performance of cognitive functions are accompanied by experience is the unique, hard problem of consciousness.
records.reference: Chalmers, D. (1995). “Facing up to the problem of consciousness.” Journal of Consciousness Studies, 2(3), 200-19.

records.line: Even when we have solved all the easy problems of cognitive function, the hard problem of consciousness may still remain unsolved.
records.reference: Chalmers, D. (1995). “Facing up to the problem of consciousness.” Journal of Consciousness Studies, 2(3), 200-19.

records.line: ‘Zombies’ (creatures physically and functionally identical to us, but lacking consciousness) are conceivable.
records.reference: Weisberg, J. (n.d.). “The Hard Problem of Consciousness”. The Internet Encyclopedia of Philosophy. https://iep.utm.edu/hard-con/

records.line: A natural kind term has a primary intension (its sense, description) and a secondary intension (its reference in this world, and thus in every world).
records.reference: Chalmers, D. (1996). The Conscious Mind. Oxford University Press.

records.line: Two-dimensional semantics bridge the epistemic and modal domains, in arguing from conceivability to metaphysical possibility.
records.reference: Chalmers, D. (2010). The character of consciousness. Oxford University Press.

records.line: The conceivability of ‘zombies’ (and spectrum inversion) and the “knowledge argument” demonstrate that consciousness cannot be functionally analyzed.
records.reference: Weisberg, J. (n.d.). “The Hard Problem of Consciousness”. The Internet Encyclopedia of Philosophy. https://iep.utm.edu/hard-con/

records.line: We may have to take experience itself as a fundamental feature of the world, alongside mass, charge, and space-time.
records.reference: Chalmers, D. (1995). “Facing up to the problem of consciousness.” Journal of Consciousness Studies, 2(3), 200-19.

records.line: Fundamental entities bearing micro(proto)phenomenal properties may be constituting the macrophenomenal realm.
records.reference: Chalmers, D. (2013). “Panpsychism and panprotopsychism”. The Amherst Lecture in Philosophy 8.

records.line: We need to discover the psychophysical principles that connect the properties of physical processes to the properties of experience.
records.reference: Chalmers, D. (1995). “Facing up to the problem of consciousness.” Journal of Consciousness Studies, 2(3), 200-19.

records.line: Consciousness is not a functional state but it arises from (is determined by) functional organization, independently of what it is made of.
records.reference: Chalmers, D. (1996). The Conscious Mind. Oxford University Press.

records.line: “Information” may be fundamental for experience, with its two aspects corresponding to the physical and the phenomenal features of the world.
records.reference: Chalmers, D. (1995). “Facing up to the problem of consciousness.” Journal of Consciousness Studies, 2(3), 200-19.

records.line: Even a thermostat might have maximally simple phenomenal experience as a maximally simple information-processing structure.
records.reference: Chalmers, D. (1995). “Facing up to the problem of consciousness.” Journal of Consciousness Studies, 2(3), 200-19.

records.line: If an external object performs a function that we’d regard as mental if it were performed by your brain, then that object is a part of your mind.
records.reference: Clark, A., & Chalmers, D. (1998). “The extended mind”. Analysis, 58(1).

records.line: My mental states might be partly constituted by the mental states of other thinkers – embodied in my secretary, my accountant, or my collaborator.
records.reference: Clark, A., & Chalmers, D. (1998). “The extended mind”. Analysis, 58(1).

records.line: One can survive in a post-singularity world by gradually uploading their brain onto new technology, preserving their consciousness.
records.reference: Chalmers, D. (2010). “The singularity: A philosophical analysis”. Journal of Consciousness Studies 17 (9-10).

records.line: Since physical reality can be characterized by its causal structure involving objects and our experience, virtual reality is in principle on a par with it.
records.reference: Chalmers, D. (2017). “The Virtual and the Real”. Disputatio 9 (46):309-352.

records.line: Virtual reality is a sort of genuine reality, virtual objects are real (digital) objects (with causal powers), and life in VR can be valuable.
records.reference: Chalmers, D. (2017). “The Virtual and the Real”. Disputatio 9 (46):309-352.

records.line: Metaphysics should be naturalized: it should be informed by science (instead of a priori inquiry, intuitions, or common sense) and contribute to the scientific image.
records.reference: Ladyman, J. (2017). “An Apology for Naturalized Metaphysics”. In Matthew Slater & Zanja Yudell (eds.), Metaphysics and the Philosophy of Science.

records.line: The institutional filters of science need not mirror individual cognitive capacities: they have a truth-tracking power – partly thanks to mathematics.
records.reference: Ladyman, J. & Ross, D. (2007). Every Thing Must Go: Metaphysics Naturalized. New York: Oxford University Press. Edited by Don Ross, David Spurrett & John G. Collier.

records.line: The nature of space, time and matter are not compatible with the ontological priority conferred on individuals in most traditional metaphysics.
records.reference: Ladyman, J. (n.d.). “Structural Realism”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/structural-realism/

records.line: Structural realism should be thought of as metaphysically rather than merely epistemically revisionary: there are no “things” and structure is all there is.
records.reference: Ladyman, J. (n.d.). “Structural Realism”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/structural-realism/

records.line: To be is to be a real pattern: one that indispensibly figures in a projectible generalization allowing us to predict and explain the behavior of the world.
records.reference: Ladyman, J. (2017). “An Apology for Naturalized Metaphysics”. In Matthew Slater & Zanja Yudell (eds.), Metaphysics and the Philosophy of Science.

records.line: The world has an objective modal structure that is represented by causal claims and laws in the special sciences using real patterns.
records.reference: Ladyman, J. (2017). “An Apology for Naturalized Metaphysics”. In Matthew Slater & Zanja Yudell (eds.), Metaphysics and the Philosophy of Science.

records.line: Ontology is scale-relative: different energy levels and regimes, and different length and time scales, feature different emergent structures of causation and law.
records.reference: Ladyman, J. (2018). “Scientific Realism Again”. Spontaneous Generations 9 (1):99-107.

records.line: Particles are not composed by some general part-whole relation, but are the products of dynamics of the real patterns of more fundamental physics.
records.reference: Ladyman, J. (2017). “An Apology for Naturalized Metaphysics”. In Matthew Slater & Zanja Yudell (eds.), Metaphysics and the Philosophy of Science.

records.line: Physics is not defined in terms of fundamental building blocks but as the science that holds at the widest range of scales and across all of space and time.
records.reference: Ladyman, J. (2017). “An Apology for Naturalized Metaphysics”. In Matthew Slater & Zanja Yudell (eds.), Metaphysics and the Philosophy of Science.

records.line: If there is not, or might not be, a fundamental level of reality, nor ultimate individuals of which everything else is made, then all real patterns are on a par.
records.reference: Ladyman, J. (2018). “Scientific Realism Again”. Spontaneous Generations 9 (1):99-107.

records.line: Composition is a real feature of the world and is in general diachronic, dynamical, and domain specific, since it depends on the interaction among parts.
records.reference: Ladyman, J. (2017). “An Apology for Naturalized Metaphysics”. In Matthew Slater & Zanja Yudell (eds.), Metaphysics and the Philosophy of Science.

records.line: The problems arising from Ramsification do not arise for ontic structural realism since it eschews an extensional understanding of relations.
records.reference: Ladyman, J. (n.d.). “Structural Realism”. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Edward N. Zalta (ed.). https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/structural-realism/
